with virtual machines.

120
00:08:21,890 --> 00:08:28,220
So we're going to save the settings, and we can go ahead and start this virtual machine by selecting

121
00:08:28,220 --> 00:08:31,130
it and clicking on Power on in here.

122
00:08:32,380 --> 00:08:37,180
We're going to say, I copied it and we're just going to give it some time to load.

123
00:08:38,200 --> 00:08:44,890
And as you can see, if everything goes as expected, you should get the default login screen for Kali.

124
00:08:45,100 --> 00:08:50,530
So I'm going to click in here and I'm going to put the username as root rot.

125
00:08:50,830 --> 00:08:54,040
Hit enter and the password is the reverse of that.

126
00:08:54,040 --> 00:08:56,290
So it's t o o r.

127
00:08:56,980 --> 00:09:00,040
We're going to hit enter and give it some time.

128
00:09:00,600 --> 00:09:02,220
And great as you can see.

129
00:09:02,220 --> 00:09:10,350
Now we have another operating system, Kali Linux, installed as a virtual machine inside our own main

130
00:09:10,350 --> 00:09:12,150
host operating system.

131
00:09:12,720 --> 00:09:19,530
Now we can put this in full screen by going to Virtual machine in here and clicking on enter full screen.

132
00:09:20,280 --> 00:09:22,470
The screen should automatically resize.

133
00:09:22,470 --> 00:09:27,630
As you can see in here, and in most cases the icons should look normal.

134
00:09:27,630 --> 00:09:32,310
But in my case they look small because I have a 4K display.

135
00:09:32,700 --> 00:09:36,240
So we're going to need to manually modify the display settings.

136
00:09:36,240 --> 00:09:40,260
And to do that we're going to right click go to settings.

137
00:09:41,830 --> 00:09:45,490
And we're gonna set the scale to 200.

138
00:09:46,280 --> 00:09:48,320
I'm going to click on apply.

139
00:09:49,380 --> 00:09:52,140
We're going to keep the changes and perfect.

140
00:09:52,140 --> 00:09:52,440
Now.

141
00:09:52,440 --> 00:09:54,090
Carly looks normal for me.

142
00:09:54,090 --> 00:09:55,500
You don't have to do this.

143
00:09:55,530 --> 00:09:57,330
If Carly looked normal from the start.

144
00:09:57,330 --> 00:10:02,100
Only if you have a high resolution screen, you might have to do this now.

145
00:10:02,100 --> 00:10:08,910
I know this operating system might look a little bit confusing and scary, but don't worry about it

146
00:10:08,910 --> 00:10:09,480
at all.

147
00:10:09,480 --> 00:10:09,870
We.

148
00:10:09,870 --> 00:10:14,550
I will teach you how to use it step by step as we go through the course.

149
00:10:14,820 --> 00:10:19,920
If you face any issues while installing Carly, check out the featured questions first.

150
00:10:19,920 --> 00:10:25,920
And if you don't see your answer, feel free to ask us in the Q&amp;A or in the forums, and we will respond

151
00:10:25,920 --> 00:10:29,190
to you within a maximum of 15 hours.

152
00:10:29,970 --> 00:10:36,720
And to turn off Carly, you can simply click here, go to Power off and Power Off.

153
00:10:37,480 --> 00:10:41,470
Click on Power-off one more time and the machine will shut down.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================

# Section 3: Preparation - Linux Basics

1
00:00:00,200 --> 00:00:06,350
Now that we have Kali Linux installed, I want to give you a basic overview of this operating system

2
00:00:06,350 --> 00:00:09,050
in this lecture so that you're comfortable with it.

3
00:00:09,050 --> 00:00:11,810
And we're going to be using it throughout the whole course.

4
00:00:11,810 --> 00:00:16,100
So everything is going to become easier for you as we go through the course.

5
00:00:17,010 --> 00:00:20,970
Now, the first thing that I want to talk about is the status bar in here.

6
00:00:20,970 --> 00:00:24,630
So you can see we have an applications menu in here.

7
00:00:24,630 --> 00:00:29,400
And inside it you can see all the applications are categorized.

8
00:00:29,940 --> 00:00:37,200
So we have information gathering applications vulnerability analysis, web application analysis reverse

9
00:00:37,200 --> 00:00:39,330
engineering and so on.

10
00:00:39,900 --> 00:00:45,270
So you can go on each one of these entries and just select any program that you want to run.

11
00:00:45,900 --> 00:00:52,890
And the next menu that we have is the places, which shows you the most common places in the file system.

12
00:00:52,890 --> 00:00:56,520
So we have home, we have the documents downloads and so on.

13
00:00:57,240 --> 00:00:59,460
So you can access the places from here.

14
00:00:59,460 --> 00:01:01,020
And then you click on home.

15
00:01:01,020 --> 00:01:04,680
Or you can just click on the files icon in here on the left.

16
00:01:04,680 --> 00:01:10,650
And both of these will bring you to the home directory which is actually the root directory.

17
00:01:10,650 --> 00:01:13,020
So it says home in here.

18
00:01:13,020 --> 00:01:19,860
But the actual location of this if I do control L you'll see it is forward slash root.

19
00:01:20,640 --> 00:01:26,550
So inside this you can see we have our downloads music pictures similar to the way that you'd have it

20
00:01:26,550 --> 00:01:28,290
in windows and OS x.

21
00:01:29,100 --> 00:01:34,410
We also have the time in here and in here on the right we have more controls.

22
00:01:34,410 --> 00:01:38,310
So the first thing that we have in here is the workspaces.

23
00:01:38,310 --> 00:01:42,390
So Linux comes with multiple workspaces by default.

24
00:01:42,390 --> 00:01:48,810
Basically what this allows us to do is we can have this desktop in here for example, we can open the

25
00:01:48,810 --> 00:01:53,550
places and we can for example navigate to downloads and do some work in here.

26
00:01:54,060 --> 00:02:02,040
But if we go here to workplaces and go to the second one, you can see that I have an empty desktop,

27
00:02:02,040 --> 00:02:03,660
that I can do whatever I want.

28
00:02:03,660 --> 00:02:06,420
And for example, I can launch Firefox in here.

29
00:02:07,910 --> 00:02:14,480
And then again, if I want to go back to the first workspace, click on number one and I'm back in here.

30
00:02:14,480 --> 00:02:20,810
And because I've already used up two spaces, the system is already creating a third empty space.

31
00:02:20,810 --> 00:02:22,250
So I can click on that.

32
00:02:22,250 --> 00:02:26,270
And as you can see, I'm in the third space where I can do more stuff.

33
00:02:27,010 --> 00:02:30,820
The next part of the status bar is this last part in here.

34
00:02:31,120 --> 00:02:36,100
And if you click on it, basically the first thing it will allow you to do is control the sound.

35
00:02:36,160 --> 00:02:38,860
Next you'll see your connections.

36
00:02:38,860 --> 00:02:44,230
So as you can see right now and as I showed you before, this is showing up as if we're connected to

37
00:02:44,230 --> 00:02:49,240
a wired network because I configured this network to use a Nat network.

38
00:02:49,840 --> 00:02:55,540
If you have a wireless adapter connected to this computer, you should be able to see it here.

39
00:02:55,540 --> 00:03:02,080
If you don't see it, then follow the link in the resources of this lecture to see how to properly connect

40
00:03:02,080 --> 00:03:07,120
your wireless adapter or any USB device to a virtual machine.

41
00:03:07,780 --> 00:03:16,210
Now, I should note you can only use external USB adapters in order to access Wi-Fi networks from Cali.

42
00:03:16,210 --> 00:03:23,380
You cannot use the built in Wi-Fi card, but Cali should have internet connection anyway because it

43
00:03:23,380 --> 00:03:25,420
is configured to use a Nat network.

44
00:03:25,420 --> 00:03:29,230
And as you can see in here, it's telling me my wired is connected.

45
00:03:29,410 --> 00:03:33,430
So if I actually go down to Firefox, I'll close this.

46
00:03:35,000 --> 00:03:41,660
And if I just go to google.com, you can see it is connected to the internet.

47
00:03:43,320 --> 00:03:46,020
So let's go back to where we were.

48
00:03:46,710 --> 00:03:52,560
And if we click here we also have the battery settings.

49
00:03:52,560 --> 00:03:55,200
You have this button to turn off the machine.

50
00:03:55,500 --> 00:03:57,360
This is to lock the machine.

51
00:03:57,360 --> 00:04:02,280
And this will show the preferences where you can modify the preferences of the machine.

52
00:04:02,280 --> 00:04:06,750
So you have Bluetooth Wi-Fi change the background and so on.

53
00:04:07,260 --> 00:04:10,200
So you can spend some time getting familiar with this.

54
00:04:10,230 --> 00:04:12,540
It's very easy and very intuitive.

55
00:04:12,540 --> 00:04:15,090
Same goes to the file manager in here.

56
00:04:15,090 --> 00:04:15,960
Very intuitive.

57
00:04:15,960 --> 00:04:17,310
Double click to go in.

58
00:04:17,310 --> 00:04:21,270
You can right click and archive and extract here to uncompress it.

59
00:04:21,270 --> 00:04:24,570
You can navigate throughout these files by clicking them.

60
00:04:24,570 --> 00:04:28,500
And then you have the trash here which contains the deleted files.

61
00:04:28,500 --> 00:04:33,150
Now we also have the dock in here which contains the favorite applications.

62
00:04:33,150 --> 00:04:39,390
And you can also access all the other applications by clicking on this icon right here.

63
00:04:39,390 --> 00:04:40,770
The show applications.

64
00:04:40,770 --> 00:04:44,760
And this will show you a menu with the most frequent applications.

65
00:04:44,760 --> 00:04:48,600
You can click through these arrows to load all of the applications.

66
00:04:48,600 --> 00:04:53,280
You can also use the search box to search through these applications.

67
00:04:53,280 --> 00:04:58,050
For example, if I wanted to search for this program right here nicto.

68
00:04:58,080 --> 00:05:03,510
All I have to do is type an IK and as you can see, it's filtering it for me.

69
00:05:03,600 --> 00:05:09,870
And last but not least, you can also switch between the desktops that I showed you in here through

70
00:05:09,870 --> 00:05:12,630
this menu by clicking on these desktops.

71
00:05:13,480 --> 00:05:19,150
And if you want to exit out of this, you can simply press the escape button on your keyboard or press

72
00:05:19,150 --> 00:05:22,120
the Show Apps icon in here on the left.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,240 --> 00:00:06,440
Now that we had a quick overview of Kali Linux, I want to spend some time talking about the Linux terminal.

2
00:00:06,960 --> 00:00:10,840
So you can access the terminal through this application right here.

3
00:00:10,840 --> 00:00:14,480
So if we click on that it will open the terminal window for us.

4
00:00:14,840 --> 00:00:19,360
And as you can see all it is is a black screen where you can type commands.

5
00:00:20,720 --> 00:00:27,120
Now the Linux terminal is actually very very powerful because it can be used to do anything that you

6
00:00:27,120 --> 00:00:27,840
can think of.

7
00:00:27,840 --> 00:00:35,040
Really a lot of the applications in Linux that have a graphical interface where command prompt programs

8
00:00:35,040 --> 00:00:38,960
first, and then people made a graphical interface for it.

9
00:00:39,360 --> 00:00:44,960
So a lot of the time, maybe the graphical interface will be buggy or crash, and the terminal program

10
00:00:44,960 --> 00:00:45,960
will still work.

11
00:00:47,000 --> 00:00:52,560
Also, a lot of the penetration testing tools do not even have a graphical interface.

12
00:00:52,600 --> 00:00:56,040
A lot of them can only be used throughout the terminal.

13
00:00:57,000 --> 00:01:04,800
Not only that, but in many scenarios you might only have an SSH or a command prompt access to a machine.

14
00:01:05,080 --> 00:01:09,980
So you need to know how to use this command prompt in order to achieve your goals.

15
00:01:10,940 --> 00:01:14,380
So throughout the course we're going to be using the terminal a lot.

16
00:01:14,580 --> 00:01:20,860
And that's why I want to spend some time just showing you the basics of it and making sure that you're

17
00:01:20,860 --> 00:01:22,820
going to be comfortable using it.

18
00:01:23,900 --> 00:01:29,740
Now the basic idea is you type a command and the result will be displayed for you on screen.

19
00:01:30,660 --> 00:01:34,300
So let's have a look on a very, very simple command which is P.

20
00:01:36,340 --> 00:01:40,500
Now this command prints the current working directory hence the name.

21
00:01:42,300 --> 00:01:49,380
So if I hit enter you can see it's printing forward slash root which means right now I am in the root

22
00:01:49,380 --> 00:01:50,180
directory.

23
00:01:50,740 --> 00:01:53,180
So basically I am in here.

24
00:01:53,340 --> 00:01:54,140
This is the root.

25
00:01:54,140 --> 00:01:54,900
It's home.

26
00:01:56,260 --> 00:02:03,100
So if I do LHS which is a command to list all the directories and files in the current working directory,

27
00:02:03,300 --> 00:02:06,740
we should get all of these directories that we see in here.

28
00:02:08,140 --> 00:02:14,720
So if I hit enter As you can see, I can see all these directories in the current working directory.

29
00:02:15,720 --> 00:02:19,040
Now another very useful command is the CD command.

30
00:02:19,440 --> 00:02:23,800
This command allows us to navigate into another directory.

31
00:02:24,280 --> 00:02:28,200
So for example let's say I want to navigate into the downloads.

32
00:02:29,080 --> 00:02:35,920
All we have to do is type CD followed by the name of the directory that I want to navigate to.

33
00:02:36,320 --> 00:02:37,960
So I'm going to type downloads.

34
00:02:39,680 --> 00:02:44,200
Now if I hit enter I should be inside the downloads now.

35
00:02:44,400 --> 00:02:51,440
So if I do PWD to see my current working directory you'll see that it's saying I'm in root forward slash

36
00:02:51,600 --> 00:02:52,320
downloads.

37
00:02:53,120 --> 00:02:58,760
So if I do LS here it should show me all the directories and files inside downloads.

38
00:02:59,080 --> 00:03:04,280
So if I do enter, as you can see I have a directory and a file in here.

39
00:03:04,480 --> 00:03:08,960
And these are the exact same files that you'll see if you double click the downloads here.

40
00:03:11,240 --> 00:03:18,380
Now if you want to go back one directory so similar to pressing the back button in here, all you have

41
00:03:18,380 --> 00:03:19,580
to do is do CD.

42
00:03:19,620 --> 00:03:23,940
Again, the command to change the working directory followed by dot dot.

43
00:03:25,220 --> 00:03:29,060
And now if I do PWD you'll see I'm back in route.

44
00:03:29,300 --> 00:03:33,340
And if I do LZ you'll see all the directories and files in route.

45
00:03:34,620 --> 00:03:40,500
Now if you are using a command and you're not sure about how this command works, you can just use the

46
00:03:40,500 --> 00:03:44,460
man command to display the manual of this command.

47
00:03:45,220 --> 00:03:50,220
For example, we've used the LS command here to list the files and directories in the current working

48
00:03:50,220 --> 00:03:50,940
directory.

49
00:03:51,420 --> 00:03:59,220
But if I do man followed by lz this basically means I'm requesting the manual of LZ.

50
00:03:59,220 --> 00:04:01,820
So I'm asking how can I use the LZ command.

51
00:04:02,780 --> 00:04:07,900
So if I hit enter you'll see I'll get a screen similar to a text file.

52
00:04:08,260 --> 00:04:13,620
And basically it's given me a lot of information on how to use the LZ command.

53
00:04:14,420 --> 00:04:19,040
So you can see that it's telling us that this command will list the directory contents.

54
00:04:19,680 --> 00:04:24,480
You can see the way it works by typing LZ, followed by the options followed by a file.

55
00:04:24,480 --> 00:04:24,960
If you.

56
00:04:24,960 --> 00:04:31,680
If you want to run it on a file, you can see a longer description of the command.

57
00:04:32,880 --> 00:04:39,240
And then you can see all the options and the arguments that we can use with this command.

58
00:04:40,480 --> 00:04:45,920
Now in Linux, most of the time the options will always follow the same syntax.

59
00:04:46,120 --> 00:04:52,320
So you either use dash letter or dash dash award to specify the argument.

60
00:04:52,680 --> 00:04:59,600
For example, in here the dash a and dash dash all will ignore entries starting with dot.

61
00:05:00,800 --> 00:05:07,160
So if you keep going down in here, you'll see all the options and arguments you can use with the LS

62
00:05:07,160 --> 00:05:07,800
command.

63
00:05:08,280 --> 00:05:09,680
We have another example here.

64
00:05:09,680 --> 00:05:17,160
We have the dash L, which means it's going to use a long listing format which will display more information

65
00:05:17,160 --> 00:05:19,960
about the files in the current working directory.

66
00:05:20,960 --> 00:05:22,120
So let's have a look on that.

67
00:05:22,120 --> 00:05:24,260
I'm going to press Q to exit this.

68
00:05:24,980 --> 00:05:27,260
And then we're going to do LZ as usual.

69
00:05:27,580 --> 00:05:33,820
And since we read the manual, we know we can do dash L to see more information about the files.

70
00:05:34,260 --> 00:05:38,780
And if I hit enter now you can see I'm still getting the same directories.

71
00:05:38,780 --> 00:05:44,140
But it's also showing me the permissions, the users, the date created and so on.

72
00:05:45,260 --> 00:05:50,260
So you can use the man command on any command you want, not only on the LZ.

73
00:05:50,540 --> 00:05:52,540
So you can use it on the PWD.

74
00:05:52,820 --> 00:05:56,300
You can use it on the CD or any other command.

75
00:05:56,420 --> 00:06:02,380
And it will show you full description or the manual page of how to use this command.

76
00:06:04,100 --> 00:06:07,460
Now I'm going to clear the screen by typing clear.

77
00:06:09,020 --> 00:06:11,660
And the next thing that I want to show you is the dash.

78
00:06:11,660 --> 00:06:12,460
Dash help.

79
00:06:12,940 --> 00:06:18,940
So this is something that you can use again in almost all commands in all programs in Linux.

80
00:06:19,180 --> 00:06:24,980
So you can just type the program name or the command name followed by dash dash help.

81
00:06:26,620 --> 00:06:32,440
As you might think, this will show you a help message telling you what this command is or what this

82
00:06:32,440 --> 00:06:39,240
program is, the arguments that it takes, how to use these arguments and examples at the bottom.

83
00:06:40,360 --> 00:06:42,400
Now another useful thing with the terminal.

84
00:06:42,400 --> 00:06:45,160
So I'm going to clear this again is the arrows.

85
00:06:45,360 --> 00:06:50,320
So you can press up to go up to see all the commands that we executed before.

86
00:06:50,480 --> 00:06:56,000
And again you can go down to navigate between the commands that you executed previously.

87
00:06:57,120 --> 00:07:00,320
You can also use the tab for autocomplete.

88
00:07:00,720 --> 00:07:03,880
So again let's do ls and you can see all the files.

89
00:07:04,040 --> 00:07:06,640
And let's say we want to go into documents.

90
00:07:07,520 --> 00:07:10,640
So we can do CD followed by documents.

91
00:07:10,640 --> 00:07:12,240
You can type documents.

92
00:07:12,520 --> 00:07:17,720
Or if you're lazy like me you can just do doc and press tab.

93
00:07:18,200 --> 00:07:23,040
And as you can see it's automatically completing the rest of the word for me.

94
00:07:24,000 --> 00:07:29,280
So this is something that comes very, very handy when you're using the terminal for a long time.

95
00:07:33,300 --> 00:07:34,660
So that's all good.

96
00:07:35,060 --> 00:07:39,220
And there's actually a huge number of commands that you can use.

97
00:07:39,220 --> 00:07:45,380
So I'm going to include a link in the resources of this lecture of all the Linux commands that you can

98
00:07:45,380 --> 00:07:45,820
use.

99
00:07:46,220 --> 00:07:47,980
You don't need to know them by heart.

100
00:07:48,020 --> 00:07:50,860
We're actually going to be using a lot of them throughout the course.

101
00:07:50,860 --> 00:07:54,980
So you're going to naturally learn them as you go through the course.

102
00:07:56,420 --> 00:08:02,100
Now, before I let you go, I just want to show you two websites that I'm going to include in the resources

103
00:08:02,100 --> 00:08:03,060
of this lecture.

104
00:08:03,620 --> 00:08:09,340
First of all, we have a website here that includes the most common Linux commands, if not all.

105
00:08:09,620 --> 00:08:12,860
So you can go through them and familiarize yourself with them.

106
00:08:12,860 --> 00:08:16,220
But keep in mind you don't need to know them all by heart.

107
00:08:16,260 --> 00:08:22,180
Like I said, as you go through my course and as you continue on with your journey, you will learn

108
00:08:22,180 --> 00:08:24,500
the commands that you need as you go.

109
00:08:25,980 --> 00:08:29,420
Another really useful resource that I'm going to include is.

110
00:08:31,500 --> 00:08:35,100
This website is designed to explain Linux commands to you.

111
00:08:35,260 --> 00:08:37,840
So all you have to do is type the commands in here.

112
00:08:37,840 --> 00:08:46,960
So for example if you just type ls and hit explain, it will explain that LS will list directory content.

113
00:08:47,280 --> 00:08:50,600
You could make it more complex and do ls la.

114
00:08:51,160 --> 00:08:57,120
And if we hit enter now, it will break all the arguments and explain every single argument.

115
00:08:57,120 --> 00:08:59,400
So again ls discontents.

116
00:08:59,400 --> 00:09:00,720
If we highlight the L.

117
00:09:00,760 --> 00:09:06,440
As you can see, it's telling us that it's going to use a long listing format, as we've seen earlier,

118
00:09:06,640 --> 00:09:13,640
and the A at the end is the same as Dash A, which is all, which means that we do not want to ignore

119
00:09:13,640 --> 00:09:15,440
entries starting with a dot.

120
00:09:15,520 --> 00:09:17,800
So we do not want to ignore hidden files.

121
00:09:18,840 --> 00:09:20,600
So you can do any command in here.

122
00:09:20,600 --> 00:09:26,480
You can do apt get install for example, and it will actually explain it to you, so it'll explain.

123
00:09:26,520 --> 00:09:31,280
APT is a package manager install can be used to install packages and so on.

124
00:09:31,600 --> 00:09:35,960
So if you ever see a Linux command that you don't understand, you can always just come in here, put

125
00:09:35,960 --> 00:09:40,640
the command and it will break it down to you and explain it really nicely.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,600 --> 00:00:06,160
Now, if you're using the custom Kali that we made for this course, you will also notice that we have

2
00:00:06,160 --> 00:00:08,000
two more terminal programs.

3
00:00:08,400 --> 00:00:10,920
The first one in here called Terminator.

4
00:00:11,120 --> 00:00:15,280
It's identical to the normal terminal that I just showed you.

5
00:00:15,320 --> 00:00:19,040
You can write commands in here and see the results as a response.

6
00:00:19,280 --> 00:00:25,080
The only difference is this terminal window can be split into many terminal windows.

7
00:00:25,560 --> 00:00:27,880
So I can right click and split horizontally.

8
00:00:27,880 --> 00:00:32,680
And I get another terminal in here when I where I can run another command and see the result.

9
00:00:32,680 --> 00:00:38,080
And then this one, I can split it for example vertically, and then split this one horizontally.

10
00:00:38,080 --> 00:00:44,880
And be able to run multiple terminal applications in all of these different terminal instances at the

11
00:00:44,880 --> 00:00:45,600
same time.

12
00:00:45,840 --> 00:00:51,080
So this will become very, very useful as we go through the course when we want to execute a number

13
00:00:51,080 --> 00:00:52,840
of commands at the same time.

14
00:00:52,840 --> 00:00:57,640
And you will see that this would be the terminal that I'll be using most of the time.

15
00:00:58,480 --> 00:01:04,440
The main point is the commands that we're using in here will work on any other Kali terminal.

16
00:01:05,040 --> 00:01:11,200
The third terminal that I want to show you is this one right here, warp, which is actually an application

17
00:01:11,200 --> 00:01:16,240
that is designed for developers to write code, but also comes in with a really nice terminal.

18
00:01:16,640 --> 00:01:20,520
Now, the main feature of this terminal is that it has AI features.

19
00:01:20,600 --> 00:01:25,360
So first of all, it's a terminal that looks identical to the terminal that I showed you before.

20
00:01:25,440 --> 00:01:26,760
You can write commands in here.

21
00:01:26,760 --> 00:01:28,160
So again I can do LS.

22
00:01:28,600 --> 00:01:31,000
And that will list all the files and directories.

23
00:01:31,000 --> 00:01:36,280
As you can see in here I can do PWD and then see the result as well.

24
00:01:36,560 --> 00:01:38,520
With this one you can have multiple tabs.

25
00:01:38,520 --> 00:01:41,520
So you can open a new tab and execute commands in here.

26
00:01:41,640 --> 00:01:46,520
Or you could right click and again split the screen horizontally and vertically.

27
00:01:47,280 --> 00:01:51,320
The main feature of this terminal however is the AI features.

28
00:01:51,320 --> 00:01:55,480
So in here we can actually ask questions and execute commands.

29
00:01:55,480 --> 00:01:58,960
And it will automatically determine what it should do with my prompt.

30
00:01:59,320 --> 00:02:04,470
And it's going to use whatever AI model that is selected in here to answer the question.

31
00:02:04,470 --> 00:02:16,470
For example, I can ask it how do I scan the network I am connected to for vulnerabilities?

32
00:02:17,150 --> 00:02:21,670
Now you'll notice that this terminal is not the fastest because of the AI features.

33
00:02:21,670 --> 00:02:26,990
And you'll also notice that sometimes it might not answer you, but in this example it's actually answering

34
00:02:26,990 --> 00:02:29,390
you if you're asking hacking related questions.

35
00:02:29,390 --> 00:02:33,990
But I actually show how to bypass that censorship in the hacking masterclass.

36
00:02:34,590 --> 00:02:39,910
If you don't want it to automatically determine what it should do with the prompt or the command that

37
00:02:39,910 --> 00:02:43,150
you type in here, you can actually switch in here.

38
00:02:43,150 --> 00:02:48,870
So if you click the terminal icon in here, this will allow you to only execute terminal commands.

39
00:02:48,870 --> 00:02:55,270
If you click the A icon in here which is the agent mode, it will actually go in agent mode and even

40
00:02:55,270 --> 00:03:01,230
execute the commands on your behalf so you can ask it to install packages or literally go ahead and

41
00:03:01,230 --> 00:03:04,670
scan the network for you or generate backdoors and so on.

42
00:03:04,990 --> 00:03:08,950
But to be able to do this, you still need to learn the basics yourself.

43
00:03:08,950 --> 00:03:12,310
And that's why this course is still relevant and still exists.

44
00:03:12,350 --> 00:03:17,670
You need to learn everything, everything that is in this course properly so that you can direct AI

45
00:03:17,830 --> 00:03:18,990
in the right direction.

46
00:03:19,670 --> 00:03:25,030
So, for example, if I wanted to make a backdoor, which is something that you'll learn in this course

47
00:03:25,030 --> 00:03:27,790
in details, I cannot simply just tell it.

48
00:03:27,830 --> 00:03:33,350
Make me a backdoor, because there are hundreds of tools to make backdoors and there are hundreds of

49
00:03:33,350 --> 00:03:34,470
types of backdoors.

50
00:03:35,310 --> 00:03:37,510
So I have to specify the tool.

51
00:03:37,510 --> 00:03:43,390
For example, use MSF venom, and then I have to specify the type of the backdoor.

52
00:03:43,390 --> 00:03:46,910
So I want a reverse https windows executable.

53
00:03:47,510 --> 00:03:50,110
And I have to specify how it connects back to my machine.

54
00:03:50,110 --> 00:03:53,230
So I wanted a reverse connection that connects back to my machine.

55
00:03:53,230 --> 00:03:55,070
And I have to specify the port.

56
00:03:55,550 --> 00:04:00,550
Once you give it all this relevant information, you'll see that it's gonna craft the correct command,

57
00:04:00,550 --> 00:04:05,460
as you can see in here, which you'll learn how to do manually because, like I said, it's very important

58
00:04:05,460 --> 00:04:07,620
to be able to prompt the AI.

59
00:04:08,100 --> 00:04:13,220
And then if we're happy with this, we can simply tell it to run it and it will create the backdoor

60
00:04:13,220 --> 00:04:13,860
for us.

61
00:04:15,500 --> 00:04:21,420
So we're able to carry out hacking actions using natural language without actually having to write any

62
00:04:21,420 --> 00:04:22,020
commands.

63
00:04:22,340 --> 00:04:25,620
And please don't be scared of all of the terminology that I used.

64
00:04:25,620 --> 00:04:29,460
You will learn all of this and much more as you go through the course.

65
00:04:29,460 --> 00:04:33,220
I just want to show you why you still need to learn hacking.

66
00:04:33,260 --> 00:04:39,340
Even though we have AI tools and AI agents like this that can carry out actions on our behalf.

67
00:04:39,900 --> 00:04:45,500
But if you think this is really cool and you want to learn more about how to use AI for hacking, then

68
00:04:45,500 --> 00:04:52,500
check out my hacking Masterclass as we have a full series within it covering how to use AI and AI agents

69
00:04:52,500 --> 00:04:55,900
and AI frameworks for hacking, bug hunting, and so on.

70
00:04:56,660 --> 00:05:02,500
You can find out more information about the Hacking masterclass in the last lecture of this course,

71
00:05:02,500 --> 00:05:03,580
the bonus Lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================

# Section 4: Information Gathering

1

00:00:01,020  -->  00:00:04,760
Now that you have the needed software configured and installed properly,

2

00:00:05,220  -->  00:00:09,440
we can move to the first penetration test and section, of this course.

3

00:00:10,340  -->  00:00:15,200
Like I said before, the penetration test and sections in this course, correspond to the step

4

00:00:15,440  -->  00:00:19,560
that you need to take, when you're targeting a system using social engineering.

5

00:00:20,480  -->  00:00:23,180
So, the first step is 'information gathering'.

6

00:00:24,200  -->  00:00:29,640
Now I can't stress, how important information gathering is, when it comes to hacking in general

7

00:00:30,100  -->  00:00:33,220
and specifically, when it comes to social engineering.

8

00:00:35,140  -->  00:00:37,600
Because the better that you know your target,

9

00:00:37,820  -->  00:00:42,380
the better that you're going to be able to come with an effective attack strategy.

10

00:00:43,400  -->  00:00:48,720
So you're going to learn, how to start with any entity. So, you can start with a person, with a website,

11

00:00:48,860  -->  00:00:51,220
with a company, with a phone number or an email

12

00:00:51,640  -->  00:00:56,020
and you'll see, how you can gather associated info from that.

13

00:00:56,360  -->  00:00:59,940
So, you're going to be able to discover friends, employees,

14

00:01:00,240  -->  00:01:02,080
emails and numbers of these friends,

15

00:01:02,580  -->  00:01:06,320
companies that the target person or company is associated with,

16

00:01:06,520  -->  00:01:11,940
websites, links, people that they interact with and much much more.

17

00:01:13,060  -->  00:01:16,940
It's actually hard to explain, how much information you'll be able to gather.

18

00:01:17,680  -->  00:01:21,340
From there, we'll graph all this information in a nice place,

19

00:01:21,640  -->  00:01:26,180
so we can look at it and we'll see all the links and connections between these entities

20

00:01:26,400  -->  00:01:31,600
and from there we're going to sit down, analyze it and come up with an effective attack strategy.

21

00:01:32,820  -->  00:01:38,540
And this is why information gathering is very important because from the information that you gathered,

22

00:01:38,780  -->  00:01:43,120
you can come up with something that's very, very smart and it will definitely work,

23

00:01:43,120  -->  00:01:48,240
it'll almost guarantee that the idea that you have or the strategy that you have will work

24

00:01:48,580  -->  00:01:51,820
and you'll see how we're going to do that by the end of the section.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================

# Section 5: Information Gathering - Gathering Info About A CompanyWebsite

1
00:00:00,110 --> 00:00:05,030
Now, in this lecture, I'd like to give you an overview of a tool that we're going to be using often

2
00:00:05,030 --> 00:00:05,990
in this course.

3
00:00:06,110 --> 00:00:12,500
This tool is great for information gathering, and it allows you to gather information just about anything.

4
00:00:12,500 --> 00:00:14,390
You can gather information about people.

5
00:00:14,390 --> 00:00:21,170
You can gather information about websites, computers, companies, phone numbers, everything really

6
00:00:21,170 --> 00:00:27,770
everything you can think of, You can add it to this tool and try to extract information related to

7
00:00:27,800 --> 00:00:28,760
that entity.

8
00:00:29,830 --> 00:00:35,560
The tool is called Maltego and it's going to become your best friend when it comes to information gathering.

9
00:00:35,680 --> 00:00:41,290
And since this course is a social engineering course, information gathering is really, really important

10
00:00:41,290 --> 00:00:48,130
and it's very important to know how to use this tool to gather as much information as possible on your

11
00:00:48,130 --> 00:00:48,820
target.

12
00:00:49,470 --> 00:00:54,990
Now, like I said, the tool can be used to gather information about anything, but using the tool is

13
00:00:54,990 --> 00:00:55,440
the same.

14
00:00:55,440 --> 00:01:01,050
So it doesn't matter if your target is a website, if it's a person, if it's a phone number, if it's

15
00:01:01,050 --> 00:01:03,050
a company, it doesn't really matter.

16
00:01:03,060 --> 00:01:08,610
Using the tool is exactly the same, but only the information that you'll be getting is going to be

17
00:01:08,610 --> 00:01:09,320
different.

18
00:01:09,330 --> 00:01:13,560
So in this lecture, we're going to have a quick overview over this tool and then we're going to be

19
00:01:13,560 --> 00:01:15,930
using it more in the next lectures.

20
00:01:16,260 --> 00:01:23,130
So first of all, to run the tool, just go to the more applications right here and then just type in

21
00:01:23,130 --> 00:01:23,970
Maltego.

22
00:01:25,910 --> 00:01:29,660
And you can see that the tool comes up in here and it's called Maltego.

23
00:01:31,640 --> 00:01:36,470
The first time you run the tool, you'll be asked to log in with a username and a password.

24
00:01:36,500 --> 00:01:40,760
If you don't have one, you'll have to register through the wizard that you'll see.

25
00:01:40,760 --> 00:01:46,430
So just create a new username and new password and then they'll send you an activation link to your

26
00:01:46,430 --> 00:01:50,960
email, activate your account, and then you'll be able to log in and use the tool.

27
00:01:51,500 --> 00:01:57,110
Now, as you can see, I've already logged in and it's offering me to use one of the already made templates

28
00:01:57,110 --> 00:01:59,570
for gathering information.

29
00:01:59,600 --> 00:02:03,230
I'm not going to use any of that, so I'm just going to click on the cancel right here.

30
00:02:03,920 --> 00:02:07,610
In here you can see that we have the home page for the for the tool.

31
00:02:08,030 --> 00:02:11,660
And from here you can add more transformers to the tool.

32
00:02:11,810 --> 00:02:18,560
Basically, transformers are plugins that allow you to gather information about specific things.

33
00:02:18,560 --> 00:02:25,250
So you can literally just click on install on any of these and it'll allow you to it'll add basically

34
00:02:25,250 --> 00:02:29,660
more transformers or more things that you can do with Maltego.

35
00:02:29,990 --> 00:02:36,300
A lot of these extra transformers will ask you to log in with a username and a password or use a certain

36
00:02:36,300 --> 00:02:37,110
API.

37
00:02:37,500 --> 00:02:45,300
For now we're just going to use the built in Transformers and I'm just going to go on right here to

38
00:02:45,300 --> 00:02:46,770
create a new graph.

39
00:02:49,060 --> 00:02:52,330
And this is the main workplace of Maltego.

40
00:02:52,870 --> 00:02:55,510
So in the middle we have our graph.

41
00:02:55,510 --> 00:03:00,190
So here is where you're going to be seeing your entities, where you're going to see, be seeing the

42
00:03:00,190 --> 00:03:01,120
information.

43
00:03:01,570 --> 00:03:04,390
And here you'll have an overview of the graph.

44
00:03:04,660 --> 00:03:09,220
And in here you'll have details about each entity in the graph.

45
00:03:09,430 --> 00:03:15,340
And in here at the bottom right, you'll be able to change the properties for each of these entities.

46
00:03:15,760 --> 00:03:17,140
All of this is empty now.

47
00:03:17,140 --> 00:03:19,180
And all of this is vague, I know, for you.

48
00:03:19,180 --> 00:03:23,320
But once we start using the tool, it will start making sense straight away.

49
00:03:24,370 --> 00:03:27,520
On the left, we have our entities.

50
00:03:27,940 --> 00:03:33,430
They're organized in categories right here, depending on the type of these entities.

51
00:03:33,640 --> 00:03:39,820
So, for example, if we click on the infrastructure, it will allow you to add a domain name.

52
00:03:40,180 --> 00:03:44,980
You can add MX records, you can add URLs, or you can add a website.

53
00:03:45,280 --> 00:03:48,220
So you can literally just drag.

54
00:03:49,030 --> 00:03:50,980
And drop the entity that you want.

55
00:03:52,730 --> 00:03:54,800
And now we have our website in the graph.

56
00:03:54,800 --> 00:03:59,360
And from here we can start gathering information about this website.

57
00:04:00,110 --> 00:04:02,750
There is a lot of different types of entities.

58
00:04:03,330 --> 00:04:07,500
So you can add a device from here.

59
00:04:07,620 --> 00:04:14,280
You can, for example, if we go to personal, you'll see that you can actually just add the person.

60
00:04:14,280 --> 00:04:18,960
So literally you can just add the person, give their first name, their second name, and then you'll

61
00:04:18,990 --> 00:04:22,110
be able to gather information about this person.

62
00:04:22,890 --> 00:04:27,750
You can also add a phone number, again, put the phone number and start gathering information about

63
00:04:27,750 --> 00:04:28,230
it.

64
00:04:28,560 --> 00:04:36,480
And one of the really cool categories right here is the social links, which will allow you to add Facebook

65
00:04:36,510 --> 00:04:37,290
entities.

66
00:04:37,290 --> 00:04:44,010
It will allow you to add GitHub, Foursquare, LinkedIn, Instagram and other social networks.

67
00:04:44,100 --> 00:04:49,680
And with that, once you add them, you'll be able to gather information about these entities.

68
00:04:49,680 --> 00:04:56,700
And obviously this information will really help you when it comes to trying to exploit that person and

69
00:04:56,700 --> 00:04:58,260
hack into their system.

70
00:04:58,920 --> 00:05:04,770
Now, once you add the entity in here, if we click on the website, for example, we I'm just going

71
00:05:04,770 --> 00:05:12,950
to go on the property view and you'll see that in here I can modify the properties for this website.

72
00:05:12,960 --> 00:05:19,680
So for example, the first thing that we need to change is put the name of our target website in here.

73
00:05:19,860 --> 00:05:27,150
And once we do that, we can right click and select what type of information we want to gather.

74
00:05:29,700 --> 00:05:32,610
Now, I'm not going to run any transformers in this video.

75
00:05:32,610 --> 00:05:34,140
We're going to do it in next videos.

76
00:05:34,140 --> 00:05:40,440
For now, I'm just showing you a quick overview of the tool, how to add entities, how to run transformers

77
00:05:40,440 --> 00:05:43,140
and what do we mean by all of these things.

78
00:05:44,180 --> 00:05:49,310
I know a lot of this is still a bit vague, but we're going to be using this tool a lot in the course

79
00:05:49,310 --> 00:05:51,800
and it's going to become very easy for you.

80
00:05:51,800 --> 00:05:57,710
And as I said, you're going to be able to use it to gather information about anything, and it's really

81
00:05:57,710 --> 00:06:01,190
going to help you enhance your social engineering skills.

82
00:06:01,840 --> 00:06:06,700
Please note that right now you are using the free version of Maltego.

83
00:06:06,880 --> 00:06:14,140
The free version is great at gathering all sorts of information, but it doesn't allow you to use search

84
00:06:14,140 --> 00:06:16,510
engines and a few other features.

85
00:06:16,540 --> 00:06:20,740
That's why in the next lectures I'm going to be using the paid version.

86
00:06:20,980 --> 00:06:26,260
You can actually request a free trial from the link in the resources of this lecture.

87
00:06:26,260 --> 00:06:32,260
Just explain to them that you are a student and they'll happily give you trial access which you can

88
00:06:32,260 --> 00:06:36,580
use to do everything that I'm going to show you in the next lectures.

89
00:06:37,120 --> 00:06:44,230
I know there are a lot of other free information gathering tools, but in my opinion they do not even

90
00:06:44,230 --> 00:06:45,820
come close to Maltego.

91
00:06:45,850 --> 00:06:52,660
Maltego is the best at information gathering and that's why I use it and I teach my students to use

92
00:06:52,660 --> 00:06:53,110
it.

93
00:06:53,140 --> 00:06:59,410
Think of it this way If you are learning how to design, would you rather learn how to do it using paint

94
00:06:59,410 --> 00:07:00,370
which is free?

95
00:07:00,370 --> 00:07:05,060
Or would you want to use the best design software such as Photoshop?


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:00,020  -->  00:00:04,440
Now in this lecture we're gonna have a
deeper view in Maltego

2

00:00:04,440  -->  00:00:08,260
and we'll see how we can run Transformers on an entity.

3

00:00:09,140  -->  00:00:11,780
So let's say, our target is a website

4

00:00:11,780  -->  00:00:16,500
and this is not a web application hacking
course, it's a social engineering course,

5

00:00:16,500  -->  00:00:20,279
so we're not going to be talking about
how to discover vulnerabilities

6

00:00:20,280  -->  00:00:23,540
and exploit them in the website in the web application itself.

7

00:00:23,540  -->  00:00:27,420
I have a full course on that and that's a completely different topic.

8

00:00:27,420  -->  00:00:29,853
For now, we're interested in social engineering.

9

00:00:29,860  -->  00:00:33,720
So we're assuming that the target website is completely secure.

10

00:00:33,720  -->  00:00:36,880
It does not have any vulnerabilities that we can use

11

00:00:36,880  -->  00:00:41,000
and the only way into it is through the people that run this website.

12

00:00:41,840  -->  00:00:46,220
So we're going to try to social engineer these people to gain access to the website.

13

00:00:47,500  -->  00:00:51,280
So we don't know anything about this website other than its URL

14

00:00:51,500  -->  00:00:54,280
and we'll see how we can gather information from that.

15

00:00:55,020  -->  00:00:56,820
Now, this also goes for company.

16

00:00:56,820  -->  00:01:00,840
So let's say your target is a company and you know the name of the company,

17

00:01:01,060  -->  00:01:04,960
it's very easy, all you have to do is just put the name of the company in Google

18

00:01:04,960  -->  00:01:10,180
and you'll find the website for that company and then
from there you can follow this lecture.

19

00:01:10,180  -->  00:01:15,080
So let's say my target is 'iSecurity' and if I google 'iSecurity'...

20

00:01:18,540  -->  00:01:23,340
the first thing that I'll get is a website and the website is 'isecurity.org'.

21

00:01:24,460  -->  00:01:31,320
So, I'm gonna go to Maltego and I'm gonna go to Infrastructure,

22

00:01:31,320  -->  00:01:37,780
I'm gonna go to the Website Entity and I'm going to drag and drop it in here in the Graph.

23

00:01:40,220  -->  00:01:42,520
Then I'm going to go to the Properties
right here

24

00:01:43,020  -->  00:01:48,660
and I'm gonna set change the website URL to i security dot org to my target.

25

00:01:55,860  -->  00:02:01,360
I'm gonna hit Enter and as you can see now the website name changed in here.

26

00:02:02,120  -->  00:02:08,720
So right now I can right-click this and I
can run Transformers that will allow you

27

00:02:08,720  -->  00:02:11,540
to gather information about this website.

28

00:02:12,580  -->  00:02:15,000
So I'm gonna go on Paterva CTAS

29

00:02:16,760  -->  00:02:21,300
and if we click on any of these categories it will show you the Transformers

30

00:02:21,300  -->  00:02:23,500
that allow you to do this specific thing.

31

00:02:24,080  -->  00:02:30,040
So for example, if we wanted to get a domain name from this website we can click on this

32

00:02:30,040  -->  00:02:35,060
which is convert to domain name and you can see that there is only one method to do that.

33

00:02:35,600  -->  00:02:39,800
So we can just click on the Play or the
Run button right here

34

00:02:41,180  -->  00:02:46,340
and that'll try to come up or to get us the domain name that's associated to this website.

35

00:02:46,940  -->  00:02:51,280
And as you can see we got a domain name called 'I security dot org'.

36

00:02:51,780  -->  00:02:55,780
Now this is added as another entity and from here I can actually,

37

00:02:55,780  -->  00:02:59,220
use this domain to get even more information.

38

00:02:59,900  -->  00:03:05,760
So I can right-click this and I can look for domain owner details,

39

00:03:05,760  -->  00:03:09,520
I can look for email addresses associated with this domain

40

00:03:09,700  -->  00:03:13,580
and I can look for Files and Documents from this domain.

41

00:03:14,060  -->  00:03:18,200
Now all of this can be really, really useful when it comes to social engineering.

42

00:03:18,380  -->  00:03:21,280
For example, if you look for Files and Documents,

43

00:03:21,300  -->  00:03:27,040
you might be able to find sensitive files such as passwords or user names and then you'll be able

44

00:03:27,040  -->  00:03:31,160
to compromise the users and then maybe login to the website.

45

00:03:32,740  -->  00:03:37,400
Now let's have an example and let's try to get the domain owner details.

46

00:03:38,060  -->  00:03:43,580
Now if we click on this you'll see that there's three other Transformers in that category.

47

00:03:43,580  -->  00:03:48,980
So we can get the email, we can get the email using another method using the Hugh's information

48

00:03:49,280  -->  00:03:51,140
and we can also get the phone number.

49

00:03:51,640  -->  00:03:55,940
Now instead of clicking on Run and all of these, I'm interested in all of that.

50

00:03:55,940  -->  00:04:00,920
So I'm interested into everything that is related to the domain owner.

51

00:04:00,920  -->  00:04:07,740
So I'm just gonna click on the two arrows in here which will run all the Transformers in this category.

52

00:04:08,440  -->  00:04:15,500
So I'm just gonna click on these two arrows to get all the information about the domain owner.

53

00:04:17,760  -->  00:04:23,600
And that's cool, now if you go down, you
can see that we should have got the information

54

00:04:23,600  -->  00:04:28,740
about the domain owner but if you look closely you'll see the emails that we got

55

00:04:28,740  -->  00:04:35,400
is I security dot org ad protected domain administrator and you can see all the information here

56

00:04:35,400  -->  00:04:39,840
is actually masked off so for example if we click at the location you'll see that the location

57

00:04:39,840  -->  00:04:43,080
you'll see that the location is showing up as Denver.

58

00:04:44,860  -->  00:04:48,820
So you can see all the information in here is not really useful

59

00:04:48,820  -->  00:04:53,780
and it's not really related to any person so we can see that this information is wrong.

60

00:04:53,780  -->  00:05:00,260
The reason for this is because iSecurity uses domain protection and it basically, uses another layer

61

00:05:00,260  -->  00:05:06,300
of security so that the domain honor information is not exposed to tools like this.

62

00:05:06,880  -->  00:05:11,800
So in other cases you'll be able to get very useful information with these Transformers.

63

00:05:11,800  -->  00:05:15,160
In this case, we didn't really get anything useful.

64

00:05:15,160  -->  00:05:18,240
So what I'm gonna do is I'm just going to select all of this

65

00:05:20,420  -->  00:05:24,480
and hit the delete button to delete all these Transformers.

66

00:05:25,640  -->  00:05:28,540
Now, let's see what else can we get from
this domain.

67

00:05:28,540  -->  00:05:36,620
So I'm gonna right click it again and I'm gonna try to find email addresses associated with this domain.

68

00:05:36,620  -->  00:05:41,040
Again, if I click on this category, you'll see that there is three methods

69

00:05:41,040  -->  00:05:43,880
to get email addresses associated with this domain

70

00:05:44,260  -->  00:05:49,060
but for me right now I want to get the email addresses regardless of the method.

71

00:05:49,080  -->  00:05:54,200
So I literally, want to get as many email addresses as possible that's associated with this domain.

72

00:05:54,580  -->  00:05:58,220
So I'm going to click on these two arrows to run all the transformations

73

00:05:59,820  -->  00:06:05,660
and you'll see that we managed to get two email addresses, the first one is the same email

74

00:06:05,660  -->  00:06:11,280
that we gathered at the start and this is not a useful email it's used by the domain protection service

75

00:06:11,720  -->  00:06:17,120
so I'm going to delete that and then the second email is actually very interesting

76

00:06:17,120  -->  00:06:19,310
because it refers to a person.

77

00:06:20,160  -->  00:06:24,000
Now the name of this person is M.Askar@isecurity.org

78

00:06:24,000  -->  00:06:28,300

a fake login page.

1

00:00:00,360  -->  00:00:03,540
Now, look in on all the data that we gathered so far,

2

00:00:03,700  -->  00:00:06,800
we can start building up an attack strategy.

3

00:00:07,460  -->  00:00:12,200
So, that's why information gathering is very important because without it will--

4

00:00:12,560  -->  00:00:14,880
Our attack is just not going to be convincing.

5

00:00:15,420  -->  00:00:19,860
The more information that you gather, the more effective your attack is going to be.

6

00:00:20,460  -->  00:00:23,260
So, we can see the first thing is we have our targets.

7

00:00:23,480  -->  00:00:26,440
So, we have three people that work for iSecurity.

8

00:00:27,180  -->  00:00:29,780
We have m.askar@isecurity.org,

9

00:00:30,060  -->  00:00:35,580
we have info@isecurity.org and we have mustafa@albazy.com.

10

00:00:36,360  -->  00:00:39,920
Now, these three people are the ones that we can start targeting.

11

00:00:40,560  -->  00:00:44,260
We can target them based on the other info that we gathered.

12

00:00:45,080  -->  00:00:47,620
So, we can see, we know now the company,

13

00:00:48,100  -->  00:00:49,940
the hosting company that they're using.

14

00:00:50,480  -->  00:00:52,920
We know the phone number for that hosting company.

15

00:00:53,540  -->  00:00:57,780
And we know the email structure that hosting company uses.

16

00:00:58,420  -->  00:01:02,660
So, we can just write an email pretending to be 'abuse@leaseweb.com'

17

00:01:03,100  -->  00:01:08,220
and tell them that someone has filed a complaint about their website saying,

18

00:01:08,480  -->  00:01:11,120
"This website teaches Hacking and this is not legal."

19

00:01:11,460  -->  00:01:16,540
And we'd ask 'm.askar.org' to log into the account to solve this issue.

20

00:01:17,120  -->  00:01:22,840
Now in that email, if the emails going to look exactly as if it's sent from abuse @leaseweb.com

21

00:01:23,420  -->  00:01:25,060
and in it,

22

00:01:25,220  -->  00:01:30,780
we're going to have a link to a fake login page that looks exactly like their login page.

23

00:01:31,260  -->  00:01:34,740
So when M. Askar clicks on that link, they're actually go to a page

24

00:01:34,740  -->  00:01:39,300
that looks exactly like the login page that they're used to use, when they log into leaseweb.

25

00:01:39,960  -->  00:01:43,320
But when they put the password, the password will be sent to us

26

00:01:43,500  -->  00:01:45,660
and we'll know their username and password.

27

00:01:46,460  -->  00:01:48,640
Now, this is just one attack idea.

28

00:01:49,240  -->  00:01:51,080
Another attack idea is,

29

00:01:51,820  -->  00:01:56,560
we can just send an email pretending to be a normal person and send them a book

30

00:01:56,580  -->  00:02:01,600
because we are seeing that this website posts books that's related to information technology.

31

00:02:03,080  -->  00:02:06,020
So, we can just send them an interesting article in PDF

32

00:02:06,420  -->  00:02:09,140
and have our backdoor combined with that PDF.

33

00:02:09,620  -->  00:02:14,160
So, once one of these people open that PDF, we'll hack into their computer.

34

00:02:14,860  -->  00:02:17,320
We can combine the PDF with,

35

00:02:17,800  -->  00:02:21,320
a keylogger or a virus or anything that we want really.

36

00:02:21,860  -->  00:02:24,780
We can also exploit the friendship between these people.

37

00:02:25,180  -->  00:02:29,320
So, there is a very high possibility or actually it's definite

38

00:02:29,480  -->  00:02:34,960
that mustafa@albazy.com is friends with m.askar@isecurity.org.

39

00:02:35,600  -->  00:02:39,980
So, we can send an email, pretending to be one of these guys to the other

40

00:02:40,100  -->  00:02:42,900
and again ask them to open a file or an image

41

00:02:43,260  -->  00:02:46,460
and once they open it, we'll gain full access to their computer.

42

00:02:47,220  -->  00:02:52,840
We can also ask them to login to the admin, telling them, "Oh, someone hacked into our website, let's fix it."

43

00:02:53,180  -->  00:02:55,860
Give them a login link and the login link is fake.

44

00:02:56,040  -->  00:03:00,220
And again, capture their password and then you'll be able to login to their website.

45

00:03:02,600  -->  00:03:07,550
Now these, just ideas on top of my head on how to use this information

46

00:03:07,760  -->  00:03:11,300
to gain access to the website or to one of these people.

47

00:03:11,740  -->  00:03:14,740
But the possibilities are limitless

48

00:03:15,000  -->  00:03:19,880
and I'm sure, you can already think of other ways to exploit these people

49

00:03:20,080  -->  00:03:23,060
based on the information that we gathered, so far.

50

00:03:23,700  -->  00:03:27,300
Also keep in mind, this is not all the information that you can get.

51

00:03:27,740  -->  00:03:31,340
I'm just showing you an example, I can keep going like this for ages

52

00:03:31,520  -->  00:03:33,340
but I don't want to make the course boring.

53

00:03:33,880  -->  00:03:37,660
So, you can literally keep going each on, each one of these entities

54

00:03:37,920  -->  00:03:39,360
and just right click it,

55

00:03:39,700  -->  00:03:42,200
see what information you can gather, click it

56

00:03:42,580  -->  00:03:50,060
and see what you're going to get and think about ways to exploit, to use that information to exploit your targets.

57

00:03:52,320  -->  00:03:58,780
Now, as I said before, don't worry about the technical aspect of how to say, make the backdoors,

58

00:03:59,080  -->  00:04:02,340
how to send the backdoors, how to pretend to be someone else,

59

00:04:02,640  -->  00:04:05,180
how to combine the backdoors with normal files,

60

00:04:05,420  -->  00:04:08,400
we're going to go into all of that, later on in the course.

61

00:04:08,720  -->  00:04:13,480
Right now, we're just going to be talking about how to gather as much information as possible

62

00:04:13,720  -->  00:04:15,780
and build up an attack strategy.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================

# Section 6: Information Gathering - Gathering Info About A Person

1

00:00:01,460  -->  00:00:04,300
So far we've seen how powerful Maltego is

2

00:00:04,540  -->  00:00:07,940
and how to use it to gather information about a company.

3

00:00:08,440  -->  00:00:10,080
We started with a company,

4

00:00:10,280  -->  00:00:14,140
then we gathered information about the servers that it used,

5

00:00:14,380  -->  00:00:17,520
the links that it used, the websites that it collaborates with.

6

00:00:17,840  -->  00:00:21,360
We were able to get the emails for their hosting company.

7

00:00:21,640  -->  00:00:27,800
We were able to get the emails for the admins and we were able to build up three attack strategies

8

00:00:27,800  -->  00:00:29,340
and I'm sure you can come up with more.

9

00:00:29,980  -->  00:00:35,560
Now at the first lecture of Maltego, I said, you can use Maltego to gather information about anything

10

00:00:35,960  -->  00:00:40,760
and using Maltego is the same, all you have to do is just use the right entities

11

00:00:40,940  -->  00:00:43,340
and the right, and the right transformers.

12

00:00:44,200  -->  00:00:48,580
In this video, I'd like to show you how to target a person.

13

00:00:48,700  -->  00:00:52,920
So we're going to start with literally just a name, just a person, just a person's name

14

00:00:53,220  -->  00:00:59,720
and then we'll see how we can gather information about that person and then build up an attack strategy as well.

15

00:01:00,580  -->  00:01:03,960
And as we do that I'm going to show you some more features of Maltego

16

00:01:04,200  -->  00:01:06,240
and how to configure a few more settings.

17

00:01:07,760  -->  00:01:11,960
Now, I have right here our graph that we did in the previous lectures

18

00:01:12,540  -->  00:01:16,740
and we can literally transfer each of these email addresses to a person.

19

00:01:17,180  -->  00:01:21,460
For example, I can just right click m.askar@isecurity.org

20

00:01:21,780  -->  00:01:24,980
and transfer that, transform that to a person.

21

00:01:28,320  -->  00:01:30,580
And you'll see that I have a person named Askar.

22

00:01:31,840  -->  00:01:33,740
If I go on the Property list.

23

00:01:35,360  -->  00:01:40,600
You'll see that the Full Name is set to Askar, so we still don't really know the First name.

24

00:01:40,980  -->  00:01:47,000
If you go on google and just look for this email address, you'll see that the first name is Mohammad

25

00:01:47,410  -->  00:01:53,530
and you can modify the First Name here to Mohammad and start gathering information about this person

26

00:01:53,530  -->  00:01:54,720
that's called Mohammad Askar.

27

00:01:55,500  -->  00:01:57,700
In today's lecture, I'm not going to do that.

28

00:01:57,840  -->  00:02:00,760
I want to start a new graph and I want to start from scratch,

29

00:02:00,760  -->  00:02:05,900
so we're going to assume that we don't know anything about iSecurity and  iSecurity is not our target.

30

00:02:06,760  -->  00:02:12,700
So right now, our target is not going to be iSecurity and my target is going to be a person

31

00:02:13,280  -->  00:02:17,100
and that person is going to be me, it's going to be a person named Zaid Sabih.

32

00:02:18,760  -->  00:02:22,600
So, I'm going to start a new graph by clicking on the plus sign, right here.

33

00:02:24,620  -->  00:02:26,600
I'm going to look for Personal.

34

00:02:28,640  -->  00:02:31,060
And I'm going to look for a person.

35

00:02:34,660  -->  00:02:36,780
And right here, we have a person entity.

36

00:02:37,380  -->  00:02:42,460
Now as I said, in this example I'm going to assume that I have a target and I know their name,

37

00:02:42,460  -->  00:02:44,240
I know their first name and their second name.

38

00:02:44,720  -->  00:02:49,300
And again that target is me and my First name is 'Zaid' and my Second name is 'Sabih'.

39

00:02:49,860  -->  00:02:51,800
So, I'm going to go here on the Property list,

40

00:02:52,160  -->  00:02:54,500
I'm going to set the First Name, just double click it.

41

00:02:55,380  -->  00:02:56,520
I'm going to set it to 'Zaid'

42

00:02:57,020  -->  00:02:58,700
and then I'm going to set the Surname

43

00:02:59,600  -->  00:03:00,360
to 'Sabih'.

44

00:03:03,000  -->  00:03:04,840
And I'm just going to set this capital,

45

00:03:06,320  -->  00:03:08,620
doesn't really matter but it just looks better.

46

00:03:10,060  -->  00:03:13,440
Now, let's see what information can we gather about this entity.

47

00:03:13,620  -->  00:03:16,640
Again as usual, all we have to do is just right click it.

48

00:03:17,780  -->  00:03:19,480
And see what we can get.

49

00:03:20,000  -->  00:03:24,520
So, I'm already in the PATERVA category.

50

00:03:25,920  -->  00:03:28,560
And I'm going to go to 'All Transformers'.

51

00:03:29,740  -->  00:03:32,480
And you can see, we can get associated emails,

52

00:03:32,480  -->  00:03:36,140
we can transfer this, we're already a person, we can try to get a phone number,

53

00:03:36,140  -->  00:03:37,760
we can try to get a Twitter account.

54

00:03:38,700  -->  00:03:39,820
You can try all of this.

55

00:03:40,280  -->  00:03:44,180
For now, I'm going to try to get a website or websites for this person.

56

00:03:45,760  -->  00:03:49,460
Now this is going to ask you, if you want to look for a specific Domain name.

57

00:03:49,600  -->  00:03:51,420
I'm going to assume that I know nothing.

58

00:03:51,600  -->  00:03:53,260
So, I'm just going to put a space in here,

59

00:03:53,440  -->  00:03:54,800
in both entries.

60

00:03:55,300  -->  00:03:59,640
So that just means, look up for any websites that are associated with this person.

61

00:04:01,060  -->  00:04:04,480
Now, you can see that we managed to get a number of websites, right here

62

00:04:05,060  -->  00:04:09,700
and all of these websites are associated with Zaid Sabih.

63

00:04:10,300  -->  00:04:14,780
Now, that doesn't really mean that these websites are actually associated with your target

64

00:04:14,920  -->  00:04:17,440
because there could be another person named Zaid Sabih.

65

00:04:17,860  -->  00:04:23,120
So, you'll have to go on each one of these and see which ones actually are related to that person.

66

00:04:24,060  -->  00:04:27,320
For example, if we click on the Facebook here, I'm going to double click it,

67

00:04:28,200  -->  00:04:29,580
to see what information is there

68

00:04:30,160  -->  00:04:31,980
and I'm going to go on the Properties.

69

00:04:33,500  -->  00:04:39,340
And you'll see that we have the Facebook URLs that are associated with that person, with that name.

70

00:04:39,920  -->  00:04:45,560
Now, I'm going to copy all of these and I'm just going to put them in a text file, so we can read them better.

71

00:04:48,740  -->  00:04:50,800
And you can see, we have three profiles.

72

00:04:51,500  -->  00:04:55,940
Now in a real life situation, you should be going into each of these profiles

73

00:04:56,320  -->  00:04:58,720
and see which ones actually related to your person.

74

00:04:59,180  -->  00:05:03,060
I'm not going to do that because the three of them are actually not related to me at all.

75

00:05:03,560  -->  00:05:07,100
So, this is really not useful and you'll just move in.

76

00:05:07,100  -->  00:05:11,580
In this case you'll just come back, move into the next entity and see what's useful.

77

00:05:12,260  -->  00:05:15,160
And I highly recommend you, delete the ones that are not useful

78

00:05:15,160  -->  00:05:17,800
because they'll just make it harder to look through things,

79

00:05:17,800  -->  00:05:21,080
so you can just click it, press delete and that'll delete it for you.

80

00:05:21,600  -->  00:05:26,640
Now, the LinkedIn profile right here again, you can double click it and see which profile it links you to.

81

00:05:26,920  -->  00:05:30,860
But again, it's not my own profile and you can see that once you actually go to the URL.

82

00:05:31,580  -->  00:05:33,240
So for now, I'm just going to delete that.

83

00:05:34,680  -->  00:05:38,740
And you can see the rest of the stuff in here is actually all related to me.

84

00:05:39,220  -->  00:05:40,940
So, it's all related to your target

85

00:05:41,260  -->  00:05:45,000
and you can try and double click each one of them, go to the properties,

86

00:05:45,380  -->  00:05:47,980
look for the URL and open it in your browser.

87

00:05:48,740  -->  00:05:51,980
When you do that, you'll get the information that's related to that person

88

00:05:52,340  -->  00:05:57,860
and hopefully, it'll help you to form some sort of an attack strategy or help you get even more information.

89

00:05:58,840  -->  00:06:00,940
For now, because I've already looked on all of them,

90

00:06:01,220  -->  00:06:04,560
I'm going to focus on one of them, which is the Udemy link.

91

00:06:05,200  -->  00:06:07,860
So, the information related to me on Udemy.

92

00:06:08,360  -->  00:06:10,460
I'm going to double click this like we did before.

93

00:06:11,240  -->  00:06:12,960
And I'm going to go on Properties.

94

00:06:14,160  -->  00:06:17,900
And you can see that we have a URL or a description here, I'm going to copy that.

95

00:06:21,590  -->  00:06:23,970
I'm going to put it in my text file, right here.

96

00:06:25,840  -->  00:06:27,790
And I'm just going to copy the URL.

97

00:06:31,200  -->  00:06:33,140
And open it in my browser, right here.

98

00:06:38,540  -->  00:06:43,800
So, you can see that this URL is related to our target person and you can see that it's leading us to our course,

99

00:06:43,800  -->  00:06:44,960
that's taught by myself.

100

00:06:45,680  -->  00:06:50,340
Now, this is not really useful, you can see that the target person is teaching online courses, whatever,

101

00:06:50,940  -->  00:06:52,160
not very useful.

102

00:06:52,600  -->  00:06:57,680
But if we go back and look at the other URLs, so this has two URLs, if I scroll back to the left,

103

00:06:58,160  -->  00:07:00,720
we can see we have the first URL, right here

104

00:07:01,320  -->  00:07:03,880
and this URL is my profile URL.

105

00:07:04,540  -->  00:07:07,760
So, I'm going to copy it again and paste it in the browser.

106

00:07:11,000  -->  00:07:14,660
And you can see this is showing us the profile for the target person.

107

00:07:15,040  -->  00:07:17,780
You can see that it's the same name, you can see their picture,

108

00:07:17,780  -->  00:07:19,400
so you know this is your target.

109

00:07:19,820  -->  00:07:22,980
You can see a description about what they do but you probably already know that.

110

00:07:23,380  -->  00:07:25,571
But you can see some useful stuff, for example, you can see,

111

00:07:25,580  -->  00:07:28,300
that they used to work with a company called iSecurity.

112

00:07:28,760  -->  00:07:35,000
So again, you can go to what we did previously, research 'isecurity' at their website as a target

113

00:07:35,120  -->  00:07:37,960
and then gather all the information that we were able to do

114

00:07:38,120  -->  00:07:41,860
and then use that to build up a strategy against Zaid.

115

00:07:42,920  -->  00:07:45,640
Now, we're not going to do that because I've already done something like that.

116

00:07:46,000  -->  00:07:48,960
So, we're going to try to see what other information we can get.

117

00:07:49,960  -->  00:07:53,560
What's really useful and here we can see that the person is sharing a few links,

118

00:07:53,860  -->  00:07:55,820
so they're sharing their YouTube channel,

119

00:07:56,160  -->  00:08:01,680
they're sharing their LinkedIn profile, they're sharing their Facebook and they're sharing their own blog.

120

00:08:02,980  -->  00:08:08,700
All of these, you can go ahead and browse all of these things to gather more information about that person.

121

00:08:09,400  -->  00:08:13,400
Now, you need to keep in mind that I'm setting my targets to myself,

122

00:08:13,500  -->  00:08:17,400
which is an aware person about computer and information technology

123

00:08:17,620  -->  00:08:23,260
and even the target I targeted in my previous video 'isecurity', it's an information technology company

124

00:08:23,820  -->  00:08:27,200
and even with that we were able to gather so much information.

125

00:08:27,760  -->  00:08:30,780
So, when you're targeting normal companies or normal people,

126

00:08:31,080  -->  00:08:33,560
it will be so much easier to get effective information.

127

00:08:34,660  -->  00:08:39,620
So right now, if you actually go look at the YouTube, look at the LinkedIn and look at the Facebook,

128

00:08:39,840  -->  00:08:42,740
you really won't get much, like even if you click on the Facebook,

129

00:08:42,960  -->  00:08:44,880
you'll see the Facebook won't lead you to anything,

130

00:08:44,940  -->  00:08:49,400
you need to login and even after logging in, you won't get too much useful information.

131

00:08:50,560  -->  00:08:53,480
What's useful in my case is, if we go on the blog.

132

00:08:58,260  -->  00:09:00,720
And we're going to go to the 'About'.

133

00:09:02,820  -->  00:09:07,980
And what's useful in this is, we have the email address of the target person

134

00:09:08,840  -->  00:09:13,460
and we have their Twitter account, so these information were not included on Udemy.

135

00:09:13,880  -->  00:09:17,120
And now we have two really useful pieces of information.

136

00:09:17,960  -->  00:09:24,180
So in the next video, we'll see how we can use this information to gather even more info about our target

137

00:09:24,340  -->  00:09:27,560
and hopefully, be able to build up an attack strategy.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:01,440  -->  00:00:06,660
Right. So now, we have the email address of the target person and we have their Twitter account.

2

00:00:07,720  -->  00:00:10,440
So, let's start with Twitter and see what we can get from that.

3

00:00:11,020  -->  00:00:14,300
So, I'm going to open the Twitter account for the target person, right here.

4

00:00:16,920  -->  00:00:18,340
I'm going to copy the link.

5

00:00:22,740  -->  00:00:24,400
And we're going to come here.

6

00:00:25,420  -->  00:00:28,060
And we're going to add a Twitter entity.

7

00:00:30,360  -->  00:00:32,880
So, I'm just going to move all of this to the side.

8

00:00:35,520  -->  00:00:40,880
And we're going to add a Twitter entity from the Social Network category.

9

00:00:44,080  -->  00:00:49,040
The problem here, is you'll see that there is a 'Tweet' entity but there is no Twitter entity

10

00:00:49,880  -->  00:00:54,920
but Maltego actually has an entity for Twitter, it's just not been shown in here

11

00:00:55,180  -->  00:00:59,540
and that's why I said, I want to spend more time using Maltego with you,

12

00:01:00,000  -->  00:01:02,280
to show you how to access the settings.

13

00:01:03,320  -->  00:01:05,720
So, we're going to go to 'Entities'.

14

00:01:06,460  -->  00:01:08,580
We're going to go to 'Manage Entities'.

15

00:01:11,280  -->  00:01:17,740
And right here, you can add all of these entities, so all of these entities are not added to the palette,

16

00:01:17,740  -->  00:01:18,960
to the left in here.

17

00:01:20,020  -->  00:01:25,740
So, if you see something in here that's interesting to you, you can just click on it.

18

00:01:25,940  -->  00:01:28,537
So the one I'm interested in right now is

19

00:01:28,537  -->  00:01:33,160
the 'Twitter Affiliation - the Membership of the Twitter Social Network'.

20

00:01:33,880  -->  00:01:35,980
I'm going to click on the three little dots here.

21

00:01:37,640  -->  00:01:39,440
I'm going to go on 'Advanced Settings'.

22

00:01:40,900  -->  00:01:43,960
And I'm going to check this box, which says 'Palette Item'.

23

00:01:45,740  -->  00:01:47,140
Now, if I click on OK.

24

00:01:47,960  -->  00:01:48,960
And close this.

25

00:01:49,860  -->  00:01:53,020
You'll see that I have a Twitter entity showing up in  here.

26

00:01:53,820  -->  00:01:57,280
So again, I'm going to use this as normal, I'm just going to drag and drop it.

27

00:01:58,120  -->  00:02:00,280
I'm going to set the name of it here.

28

00:02:01,060  -->  00:02:03,260
So, that's going to be myself which is Zaid.

29

00:02:05,660  -->  00:02:08,220
And we're going to put the URL in here.

30

00:02:08,880  -->  00:02:10,500
So, that's the URL of the profile.

31

00:02:11,280  -->  00:02:13,340
And I've already copied that, so it's this one.

32

00:02:14,360  -->  00:02:17,680
And my user ID is 'Zaid_alq'.

33

00:02:18,160  -->  00:02:19,720
So, I'm going to put that in here.

34

00:02:22,660  -->  00:02:23,560
And we're good to go.

35

00:02:23,920  -->  00:02:27,940
Now, we can gather information about this person based on their Twitter account.

36

00:02:29,180  -->  00:02:30,260
So, I'm going to right click it.

37

00:02:32,120  -->  00:02:35,240
And see what we can get, so we can get their tweets,

38

00:02:35,560  -->  00:02:38,740
we can see the tweets they've, they sent to people,

39

00:02:39,020  -->  00:02:42,820
we can transfer this to person, we can get more details,

40

00:02:43,020  -->  00:02:44,560
we can get the followers.

41

00:02:45,200  -->  00:02:51,780
What I really want to get in this, is to get their friends, so that I can actually target them through their friends.

42

00:02:52,700  -->  00:02:55,100
So again, I'm just going to click on the Play button here.

43

00:02:57,460  -->  00:03:02,380
And this particular transformer requires you to log in to Twitter.

44

00:03:02,640  -->  00:03:04,600
So, as you can see now Maltego is telling me

45

00:03:04,600  -->  00:03:09,080
that I have to log in to Twitter to be able to gather information about this person.

46

00:03:10,260  -->  00:03:12,380
So, I'm going to click Yes or Log in, no problem

47

00:03:13,040  -->  00:03:15,540
and then I'm going to click on the 'Sign in', right here.

48

00:03:19,540  -->  00:03:22,320
And I'm just going to log in with a username and a password.

49

00:03:22,900  -->  00:03:27,000
So, I'm going to actually use a different account than the one that we'll gather information about.

50

00:03:30,940  -->  00:03:33,600
And now it's asking me, "Do I want to authorize this up?"

51

00:03:33,860  -->  00:03:35,570
I'm going to say, "Yes, authorize it."

52

00:03:35,570  -->  00:03:36,060
No problem.

53

00:03:38,040  -->  00:03:38,700
And that's it.

54

00:03:38,700  -->  00:03:41,820
Now we're logged in to Maltego. I'm going to go back to Maltego.

55

00:03:42,340  -->  00:03:45,540
And you can see that it's telling me, I can sign out using this button,

56

00:03:45,540  -->  00:03:47,080
so now I'm logged in to it.

57

00:03:47,800  -->  00:03:50,160
And if I close this, the search will start.

58

00:03:50,940  -->  00:03:55,840
So, it's now starting to look for friends, on Twitter, of Zaid.

59

00:03:56,740  -->  00:04:01,660

Now, let's zoom in a little bit.

00:02:50,280  -->  00:02:51,050
Okay. Perfect.

35

00:02:51,050  -->  00:02:53,740
So, our target is Zaid.

36

00:02:54,200  -->  00:02:57,800
And we know Zaid uses Udemy and teaches courses there.

37

00:02:58,180  -->  00:03:00,580

38

00:03:00,960  -->  00:03:05,000
