# Section 7: Windows Malware

1
00:00:00,110 --> 00:00:00,530
Okay.

2
00:00:00,530 --> 00:00:06,920
Now that we have a number of attack strategies, let's see how we can do the practical aspect of that.

3
00:00:06,950 --> 00:00:10,600
So we are in the Evil Files section.

4
00:00:10,610 --> 00:00:17,530
And in this section you're going to learn how to generate evil files for windows, Linux and OS x.

5
00:00:17,540 --> 00:00:21,680
So this section is going to be split into a number of subsections.

6
00:00:21,680 --> 00:00:26,720
In each you're going to learn how to generate evil files for that operating system.

7
00:00:26,900 --> 00:00:31,080
The first section the first subsection is going to cover windows.

8
00:00:31,100 --> 00:00:38,060
So in here we're going to learn how to generate backdoors keyloggers, credential harvesters, spying

9
00:00:38,060 --> 00:00:40,370
tools and so on for windows.

10
00:00:40,940 --> 00:00:45,830
And all of these examples I'm going to be targeting my virtual windows machine.

11
00:00:45,830 --> 00:00:52,550
This virtual machine is connected to the same Nat network as the Kali machine.

12
00:00:52,550 --> 00:00:58,700
And at the end of this section, I'm going to show you how to configure your computer so that you can

13
00:00:58,700 --> 00:01:04,430
run these attacks and use these evil files on the internet outside your network.

14
00:01:04,550 --> 00:01:07,910
So for now, we're going to be doing this stuff in our network.

15
00:01:07,910 --> 00:01:13,940
And later on in the course, we'll know how to configure our network so that we can use these attacks

16
00:01:13,940 --> 00:01:15,980
on any computer that we want.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,200 --> 00:00:04,160
Now to practice some of the attacks that we're going to learn in the future.

2
00:00:04,160 --> 00:00:08,720
Lectures you need to have a target machine or a victim machine.

3
00:00:08,720 --> 00:00:15,020
So this is a computer that you're going to target and try to hack and test the attacks that we're going

4
00:00:15,020 --> 00:00:16,460
to learn against.

5
00:00:17,100 --> 00:00:19,470
So you can do this against any machine.

6
00:00:19,470 --> 00:00:25,380
But I highly recommend installing target machines as virtual machines.

7
00:00:25,590 --> 00:00:30,660
This is great because it won't break a real computer for you if you actually break something.

8
00:00:30,690 --> 00:00:35,240
It's also very easy to fix even if you have a computer that you don't care about.

9
00:00:35,250 --> 00:00:37,440
It's very easy to fix virtual machines.

10
00:00:37,440 --> 00:00:42,060
You can just remove it and reinstall it or store a snapshot and go back to it.

11
00:00:43,270 --> 00:00:47,140
Now, as we all know, Windows is a very common operating system.

12
00:00:47,140 --> 00:00:49,490
It's used by a lot of people.

13
00:00:49,510 --> 00:00:56,260
So in this video, I'm going to show you how to install Windows as a virtual machine on all operating

14
00:00:56,260 --> 00:01:01,090
systems so that we can test the attacks that we're going to learn against it.

15
00:01:01,120 --> 00:01:07,900
If you have an Apple computer that uses an MX chip, then skip this lecture and watch the next lecture.

16
00:01:07,900 --> 00:01:12,710
That one will show you how to install windows on an Apple computer.

17
00:01:12,730 --> 00:01:18,760
For Windows Linux and Apple computers that use the Intel chip, you can continue watching this lecture

18
00:01:18,760 --> 00:01:21,130
to learn how to install windows on it.

19
00:01:21,370 --> 00:01:29,050
Now, Microsoft has released 3D virtual images for people to test windows so we can actually download

20
00:01:29,050 --> 00:01:33,160
a virtual image of Windows for free from Microsoft.

21
00:01:33,640 --> 00:01:37,630
I'm going to include the download links in the resources of this lecture.

22
00:01:37,660 --> 00:01:42,830
Now I've already done that and I already have it in here in my downloads.

23
00:01:42,830 --> 00:01:44,660
As you can see, we have it here.

24
00:01:44,780 --> 00:01:47,360
Ms. Edge Windows 10, VMware.

25
00:01:47,360 --> 00:01:51,170
And as you can see from the icon, this is a zip archive.

26
00:01:51,200 --> 00:01:57,080
Therefore, in order to use the contents inside it, we have to uncompressed this file.

27
00:01:57,260 --> 00:02:01,520
So to do that on Windows simply we're going to have to double click it.

28
00:02:02,470 --> 00:02:04,090
Copy the content.

29
00:02:04,180 --> 00:02:07,540
Go back one directory and paste it here.

30
00:02:07,690 --> 00:02:11,050
We're going to give it some time to uncompressed the content.

31
00:02:11,080 --> 00:02:13,060
On Mac you can do the same.

32
00:02:13,060 --> 00:02:14,140
Simply double clicking.

33
00:02:14,140 --> 00:02:20,500
It will uncompressed the content and on Linux you'll have to right click and click on extract here.

34
00:02:21,040 --> 00:02:27,130
Now I'm doing these steps from a Windows host computer, but you should be able to follow them on Apple,

35
00:02:27,130 --> 00:02:34,120
Mac OS and Linux because we are essentially using the same virtualization software which is VMware.

36
00:02:35,090 --> 00:02:40,820
Once this is done, we're going to have another directory in here with the contents of the virtual Windows

37
00:02:40,820 --> 00:02:41,550
image.

38
00:02:41,570 --> 00:02:45,980
To import this to VMware, we're going to, first of all, run VMware.

39
00:02:47,290 --> 00:02:53,230
We're going to click on Open Virtual Machine or go to player file open.

40
00:02:54,130 --> 00:02:58,660
We're going to navigate to the location where we have this file in here.

41
00:02:58,660 --> 00:03:07,630
So it's going to be in my downloads in edge Windows 10 VMware, and we're going to select the only file

42
00:03:07,630 --> 00:03:14,890
that we can actually select from the list we're going to click on Open and we can set the name of the

43
00:03:14,890 --> 00:03:15,880
machine in here.

44
00:03:15,880 --> 00:03:18,760
So for example, I can set it to Windows 10.

45
00:03:18,910 --> 00:03:23,590
You can change the path where it's going to be stored, but we're going to keep that the same and we're

46
00:03:23,590 --> 00:03:26,440
going to click on import to import it.

47
00:03:27,020 --> 00:03:31,610
You're going to have to give it a bit of time to import this machine into VMware.

48
00:03:33,720 --> 00:03:34,500
And perfect.

49
00:03:34,500 --> 00:03:36,990
As you can see now, it's imported in here.

50
00:03:36,990 --> 00:03:41,910
And as usual, we can simply start it by clicking on the green play button.

51
00:03:41,910 --> 00:03:46,800
But before doing that, I actually want to modify the amount of memory it has.

52
00:03:46,800 --> 00:03:50,100
So we're going to right click and go to settings.

53
00:03:50,340 --> 00:03:55,230
And as you can see by default, it's set to have four gigabytes of memory.

54
00:03:55,230 --> 00:03:57,060
I think that's a little bit too much.

55
00:03:57,060 --> 00:03:58,500
It doesn't need that much.

56
00:03:58,500 --> 00:04:03,240
So we're going to set that to two by dragging the slider down to two.

57
00:04:03,420 --> 00:04:09,330
We're going to click on okay and start it by clicking on the green play button.

58
00:04:10,580 --> 00:04:11,420
And perfect.

59
00:04:11,420 --> 00:04:13,790
As you can see now, the machine started.

60
00:04:13,820 --> 00:04:18,260
We're going to click inside and click again to log in.

61
00:04:18,740 --> 00:04:23,680
The default password in here is Capital P, a, s.

62
00:04:23,680 --> 00:04:27,470
SW0 or de escalation.

63
00:04:27,470 --> 00:04:28,070
Mark.

64
00:04:28,190 --> 00:04:29,840
I'm going to hit enter.

65
00:04:31,260 --> 00:04:32,340
And here we go.

66
00:04:32,340 --> 00:04:40,500
We have a fully working virtual Windows 10 computer installed inside our main machine.

67
00:04:40,500 --> 00:04:48,750
And we did that so we can test attacks against this virtual machine without affecting our host computer.

68
00:04:48,900 --> 00:04:53,970
Now we can go in full screen by clicking here and going into full screen.

69
00:04:54,360 --> 00:05:02,310
The screen should automatically resize if it doesn't, right click display settings and you can set

70
00:05:02,310 --> 00:05:04,900
it to the right resolution for your screen.

71
00:05:04,920 --> 00:05:11,790
I'm going to set it to three 840 by two 160 because I have a 4K screen.

72
00:05:11,820 --> 00:05:17,330
You can see that the icons are actually really small because I have such a high resolution screen.

73
00:05:17,340 --> 00:05:21,960
In your case, you'll only face this issue if you have a 4K screen.

74
00:05:21,960 --> 00:05:26,670
So you'll also probably need to set the scaling in here to 200.

75
00:05:27,030 --> 00:05:32,020
And as you can see now, the icon size has changed to the normal size.

76
00:05:32,350 --> 00:05:36,310
Now, if you face any other issues, check out the featured questions.

77
00:05:36,310 --> 00:05:42,280
And if you don't find an answer, feel free to ask us and we will respond to you within a maximum of

78
00:05:42,280 --> 00:05:43,390
15 hours.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,050 --> 00:00:05,840
As mentioned in the previous lecture, installing windows on Apple computers that use the arm chips,

2
00:00:05,840 --> 00:00:07,610
the M1, M2 and so on.

3
00:00:07,610 --> 00:00:11,540
The M series is different, so the method shown in the previous lecture will not work.

4
00:00:11,540 --> 00:00:17,450
And that's why we have this lecture to show you how to install Windows 11 on Apple computers that use

5
00:00:17,450 --> 00:00:20,450
the arm chips, the M1, M2 and so on.

6
00:00:20,450 --> 00:00:25,100
If you already got windows installed following the instructions in the previous lecture, then you can

7
00:00:25,100 --> 00:00:26,030
skip this lecture.

8
00:00:26,030 --> 00:00:30,980
You should only watch this lecture if you have not installed windows yet as a virtual machine because

9
00:00:30,980 --> 00:00:36,920
you have an Apple computer that uses an arm chip such as the M1, M2 and so on.

10
00:00:37,280 --> 00:00:42,740
So first of all, you're going to need to download the Windows 11 for ARM processors.

11
00:00:42,740 --> 00:00:45,470
You can download it from the resources of this lecture.

12
00:00:45,470 --> 00:00:48,080
As you can see, I already have it downloaded in here.

13
00:00:48,080 --> 00:00:51,650
It's in a seven zip format, so it's compressed.

14
00:00:51,650 --> 00:00:54,980
And in order to decompress it, we're going to double click it.

15
00:00:56,150 --> 00:00:58,370
And give it some time to decompress.

16
00:00:59,240 --> 00:01:00,020
And perfect.

17
00:01:00,050 --> 00:01:04,100
Now that the decompression is complete, we have a new file in here.

18
00:01:04,100 --> 00:01:11,780
And as you can see, the extension is VMware VM, which is basically a ready virtual machine for VMware.

19
00:01:11,810 --> 00:01:16,670
Now we've already covered how to install VMware previously, so if you don't know or if you haven't

20
00:01:16,670 --> 00:01:18,980
done it already, just go back and revise it.

21
00:01:18,980 --> 00:01:24,920
So all I'm going to do is simply double click the file to open it in VMware, click on.

22
00:01:24,920 --> 00:01:30,710
I copied it because I created this virtual machine for you and now the virtual machine is going to start.

23
00:01:31,840 --> 00:01:32,620
And perfect.

24
00:01:32,620 --> 00:01:39,040
As you can see, we are logged in into our Windows 11 computer, which is running as a virtual machine

25
00:01:39,040 --> 00:01:43,260
inside VMware player inside my Apple silicon computer.

26
00:01:43,270 --> 00:01:49,390
So if I go ahead here on the right and click on the Virtual Machine library, as you can see, I have

27
00:01:49,420 --> 00:01:56,080
Kali installed in here and now I have windows which I can run by simply clicking on Start, but right

28
00:01:56,080 --> 00:01:58,660
now it's already running, so I can't stop it.

29
00:01:59,520 --> 00:02:00,960
So I'm going to close this.

30
00:02:00,960 --> 00:02:07,350
And as you can see in here, it's saying it is not connected to the Internet because we need to install

31
00:02:07,350 --> 00:02:14,070
the VMware tools on it, which will install the drivers for the virtual interfaces so that we can access

32
00:02:14,070 --> 00:02:15,000
virtual networks.

33
00:02:15,000 --> 00:02:20,130
And it will also install the display drivers and enable a lot of usability features.

34
00:02:20,130 --> 00:02:25,410
To do that, we're going to go to the virtual machine here and we're going to click reinstall VMware

35
00:02:25,440 --> 00:02:26,160
Tools.

36
00:02:26,490 --> 00:02:28,170
We're going to click install.

37
00:02:29,070 --> 00:02:33,210
And this will basically mount a DVD drive on this computer.

38
00:02:33,210 --> 00:02:39,300
And as you can see, we have a notification in here telling us that a new DVD drive is detected.

39
00:02:39,300 --> 00:02:41,730
And it's asking me, what do I want to do?

40
00:02:42,090 --> 00:02:46,090
I'm going to click it and I'm going to say, open the folder for me, please.

41
00:02:46,110 --> 00:02:52,340
I'm going to scroll down and we're going to use the setup script in here to install the tools.

42
00:02:52,350 --> 00:02:56,610
So I'm going to right click it and choose to run it with PowerShell.

43
00:02:57,460 --> 00:03:01,750
It's going to ask you if you want to allow this application to make changes.

44
00:03:01,750 --> 00:03:07,780
We're going to say yes and we're going to give this some time in order to install VMware Tools on this

45
00:03:07,780 --> 00:03:08,830
virtual machine.

46
00:03:10,000 --> 00:03:15,010
Once the PowerShell blue screen disappears, it means that everything got installed properly.

47
00:03:15,010 --> 00:03:20,320
And if you look here at the bottom, you'll see that we have network connection through Ethernet and

48
00:03:20,320 --> 00:03:26,530
that's because this machine is configured to use the virtual network that VMware starts and I have my

49
00:03:26,530 --> 00:03:29,380
virtual machines configured to get internet access.

50
00:03:29,380 --> 00:03:34,900
And like we said before, this way, these machines think that the host machine is the router and they

51
00:03:34,900 --> 00:03:40,210
connect to the virtual network through it and get internet access through it.

52
00:03:40,210 --> 00:03:43,690
And to confirm this, we can simply open up a web browser.

53
00:03:45,620 --> 00:03:48,560
Get started and just go to Google.com.

54
00:03:50,340 --> 00:03:51,210
And perfect.

55
00:03:51,210 --> 00:03:55,230
As you can see now we have Internet access on this virtual machine.

56
00:03:56,360 --> 00:04:00,640
And we can also go into full screen by clicking this green button in here.

57
00:04:00,650 --> 00:04:06,530
The screen will adjust, but sometimes, as you can see, is not going to fully go into the right resolution.

58
00:04:06,530 --> 00:04:13,700
So to fix this, you can simply right click anywhere on the desktop, go to the display settings and

59
00:04:13,700 --> 00:04:15,590
select the resolution that you want.

60
00:04:15,590 --> 00:04:19,010
So in my case, it's the one 920 by 1080.

61
00:04:20,300 --> 00:04:21,050
And perfect.

62
00:04:21,050 --> 00:04:24,230
As you can see right now, we got the right resolution.

63
00:04:24,260 --> 00:04:29,750
If you want to exit the full screen, you can simply just take the mouse to the top and click the green

64
00:04:29,750 --> 00:04:30,380
button.

65
00:04:31,600 --> 00:04:32,860
And we are out of it.

66
00:04:32,860 --> 00:04:39,010
And as you can see right now, we have this Windows machine running as a virtual machine through VMware,

67
00:04:39,010 --> 00:04:45,370
inside my Apple silicon computer alongside my Kali machine, which we can access in here in the virtual

68
00:04:45,370 --> 00:04:46,420
machine library.

69
00:04:46,540 --> 00:04:53,140
So now we can try to hack into this Windows machine and test attacks against it using the Kali machine.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================

# Section 8: Windows Malware - Generating Undetectable Backdoors

1
00:00:00,330 --> 00:00:06,000
In this lecture and the next few lectures, I'm going to teach you how to create your own backdoors.

2
00:00:06,000 --> 00:00:12,570
As an ethical hacker, it is very important to understand and learn how to do this properly, because

3
00:00:12,570 --> 00:00:18,390
you will find yourself in so many situations where you have to generate backdoors to gain full control

4
00:00:18,390 --> 00:00:20,040
over the target computer.

5
00:00:20,280 --> 00:00:23,940
So first of all, what do we mean by a backdoor?

6
00:00:24,780 --> 00:00:32,460
A backdoor is simply a program that gives us remote control over the system that it gets executed on.

7
00:00:32,460 --> 00:00:39,300
So it allows us to remotely execute system commands, access resources such as the storage, upload

8
00:00:39,300 --> 00:00:42,000
and download files, and read them on the target system.

9
00:00:42,030 --> 00:00:47,370
See what they type on their keyboard, access their webcam, even access their desktop, and so much

10
00:00:47,370 --> 00:00:47,850
more.

11
00:00:47,850 --> 00:00:54,720
Basically, you'll be able to remotely do anything that a normal person can do on their computer once

12
00:00:54,720 --> 00:00:56,400
they execute your backdoor.

13
00:00:56,400 --> 00:00:58,980
So a backdoor is simply a program.

14
00:00:58,980 --> 00:01:01,410
It's simply an executable.

15
00:01:01,410 --> 00:01:08,250
But what makes it special is part of its code that we refer to as a payload.

16
00:01:08,250 --> 00:01:15,750
So the payload is the part of the code inside the backdoor, inside the executable that allows us to

17
00:01:15,750 --> 00:01:22,020
remotely control that computer, execute system commands, access its resources, and so on.

18
00:01:22,290 --> 00:01:26,820
So the program that we're going to use to generate payloads is called MSF venom.

19
00:01:26,820 --> 00:01:31,980
And I'm choosing to go with this program because it's kind of the industry standard to learn how to

20
00:01:31,980 --> 00:01:33,150
use MSF venom.

21
00:01:33,150 --> 00:01:39,780
And then there is more advanced tools that modify the payloads generated by MSF venom and make them

22
00:01:39,780 --> 00:01:45,570
bypass antivirus programs and do fancy stuff with it to allow you to bypass security and so on.

23
00:01:45,570 --> 00:01:51,000
But as an ethical hacker, and especially in this course, it is very important to learn how to use

24
00:01:51,000 --> 00:01:57,330
MSF venom first before moving on to more advanced techniques, which I cover in my other courses, such

25
00:01:57,330 --> 00:02:02,430
as the Social Engineering course and the Cloud Course, where I show you how to use proper C2 frameworks

26
00:02:02,430 --> 00:02:05,670
to control the computers that you hacked from within the cloud.

27
00:02:05,670 --> 00:02:10,770
But before we can do that, it is very important to have a strong foundation in this topic.

28
00:02:10,770 --> 00:02:13,260
And that's why we're starting with MSF venom.

29
00:02:13,650 --> 00:02:21,180
So if we actually just go ahead to Kali and type MSF venom followed by dash dash list to list all of

30
00:02:21,180 --> 00:02:23,310
the payloads that we can use.

31
00:02:23,640 --> 00:02:28,980
So like I said, MSF venom is the program that we're going to use to generate our backdoors, and we're

32
00:02:28,980 --> 00:02:32,610
simply asking it to list all of the payloads that it has.

33
00:02:32,610 --> 00:02:37,560
And if I hit enter and give it a few seconds, because you'll see we're going to get a huge list of

34
00:02:37,560 --> 00:02:38,310
payloads.

35
00:02:38,310 --> 00:02:42,390
As you can see, there are so many payloads to pick from.

36
00:02:42,390 --> 00:02:46,530
And as you can see, the first part is the name of the payload.

37
00:02:46,530 --> 00:02:51,930
And the second part in here is a description of how this payload works.

38
00:02:51,930 --> 00:02:55,710
And obviously you can Google the name of the payload to learn more about it.

39
00:02:55,710 --> 00:03:02,640
But before we do all of that, I want to spend this lecture to explain to you the naming pattern that

40
00:03:02,640 --> 00:03:03,600
we have in here.

41
00:03:03,600 --> 00:03:09,480
Because as you can see, most of these payloads are made up of three parts, as you can see in here,

42
00:03:09,480 --> 00:03:11,730
separated by forward slashes.

43
00:03:12,120 --> 00:03:18,510
Now, these parts, first of all, refer to the platform that the payload works on, the type of payload

44
00:03:18,510 --> 00:03:24,360
and the communication channel or method that the payload is going to use in order to give you remote

45
00:03:24,360 --> 00:03:26,100
control over that computer.

46
00:03:26,100 --> 00:03:31,050
So an example would be windows shell reverse HTTP.

47
00:03:31,230 --> 00:03:34,980
And as I said, the first part in here is the platform.

48
00:03:35,310 --> 00:03:39,840
So this specific payload is designed to work on windows computers.

49
00:03:40,260 --> 00:03:44,250
Now MSF venom as you saw earlier has so many different payloads.

50
00:03:44,250 --> 00:03:50,730
So you've got Linux payloads, OS x, Apple iOS for iPads and iPhones.

51
00:03:50,730 --> 00:03:52,410
You've got Android payloads.

52
00:03:52,410 --> 00:03:56,730
The platform can also be a programming language such as Python or Java.

53
00:03:56,730 --> 00:04:03,120
So payloads that start with Python or Java mean that they will work on any computer that is able to

54
00:04:03,120 --> 00:04:06,630
run Python code or Java, regardless of the operating system.

55
00:04:06,630 --> 00:04:12,000
It doesn't matter if it runs windows, Linux, or Apple OS x if you create a Python payload, it will

56
00:04:12,000 --> 00:04:16,890
work on any computer that has a Python interpreter regardless of its operating system.

57
00:04:16,890 --> 00:04:22,740
And then we even have generic payloads that are designed without thinking about the operating system.

58
00:04:22,740 --> 00:04:28,020
They're designed to work in different scenarios and different operating system, regardless of that

59
00:04:28,020 --> 00:04:29,070
operating system.

60
00:04:29,900 --> 00:04:33,530
So that's the first part of the payload, the platform.

61
00:04:33,560 --> 00:04:36,980
Now the next part is the type of the payload.

62
00:04:36,980 --> 00:04:39,020
And in this example it's shell.

63
00:04:39,020 --> 00:04:43,820
And what that means is that it's going to give you remote shell control.

64
00:04:43,820 --> 00:04:50,090
So you'll be able to do anything that you can do on that computer using its shell using commands.

65
00:04:50,860 --> 00:04:54,160
Now again, there are so many other types than shell payloads.

66
00:04:54,160 --> 00:04:59,980
We have multiple payloads which are payloads specifically designed by the Metasploit team.

67
00:04:59,980 --> 00:05:06,490
They run in memory, they use encrypted communication, and they are designed to be modular and extendable.

68
00:05:06,490 --> 00:05:08,440
So they offer so, so many features.

69
00:05:08,440 --> 00:05:12,610
And these are actually the kind of payloads that we're going to use ourselves later on.

70
00:05:12,610 --> 00:05:18,940
You also have payloads designed to inject their code into other files, such as the DLL inject that

71
00:05:18,940 --> 00:05:25,630
injects itself into DLL files, and the PE that is designed to be injected into portable executable

72
00:05:25,630 --> 00:05:26,410
files.

73
00:05:26,710 --> 00:05:32,920
You got the VNC inject payloads that allow you to access the desktop of the computer that you've hacked.

74
00:05:32,920 --> 00:05:36,880
You even have very simple payloads that simply display a message box.

75
00:05:36,880 --> 00:05:39,970
And you might think that this is not useful, but it actually is.

76
00:05:39,970 --> 00:05:44,530
If you're just testing and trying to test an exploit, not a backdoor, you don't really want to hack

77
00:05:44,560 --> 00:05:45,430
the target computer.

78
00:05:45,430 --> 00:05:49,360
You just want to see if your exploit works because you're running the code yourself.

79
00:05:49,360 --> 00:05:53,380
So a lot of the time you want to keep things simple, and you want to go with a very simple payload

80
00:05:53,380 --> 00:05:55,300
that will simply display a message box.

81
00:05:55,300 --> 00:06:00,670
And if you see the message box, it means that you actually manage to exploit whatever vulnerability

82
00:06:00,670 --> 00:06:02,170
that you're trying to exploit.

83
00:06:02,650 --> 00:06:07,030
So the first part is the platform, and the second part is the type.

84
00:06:07,030 --> 00:06:12,730
And the third part of the payload name is the communication method or channel, like we said.

85
00:06:12,730 --> 00:06:19,090
And if you look closely, you can see that even this part is almost made out of two parts.

86
00:06:19,090 --> 00:06:22,450
So the first part is the direction of the connection.

87
00:06:22,450 --> 00:06:24,490
And I'll explain that to you in a second.

88
00:06:24,490 --> 00:06:29,260
And the second part is the protocol used in this connection.

89
00:06:29,620 --> 00:06:35,710
So when it comes to the direction, the most common types are going to be either bind or reverse.

90
00:06:35,710 --> 00:06:38,020
And let me explain to you what I mean by that.

91
00:06:38,020 --> 00:06:40,240
Let's say that you have two computers.

92
00:06:40,240 --> 00:06:45,850
This is you, the hacker, and this is the target computer in a bind or a direct connection.

93
00:06:45,850 --> 00:06:51,880
When the backdoor is executed on the target computer, it opens up what we call a port.

94
00:06:51,880 --> 00:06:56,050
So you can think of this as if it's a socket on the wall.

95
00:06:56,050 --> 00:07:03,370
And then as a hacker, you would go in whenever you want and connect to the socket using your plug.

96
00:07:03,370 --> 00:07:10,240
Now the problem with this is opening a port in here at the target computer could potentially raise a

97
00:07:10,240 --> 00:07:11,170
lot of alarms.

98
00:07:11,170 --> 00:07:14,470
So if they have a firewall installed, it will raise an alarm.

99
00:07:14,470 --> 00:07:19,930
And even when you connect to it, the connection is not a typical connection that a normal personal

100
00:07:19,930 --> 00:07:21,280
computer would do.

101
00:07:21,280 --> 00:07:25,540
However, if we go with a reverse connection, we do the reverse.

102
00:07:25,540 --> 00:07:33,820
So instead of opening a port at the target computer, we actually open a port in our own computer.

103
00:07:33,820 --> 00:07:39,820
Therefore, even if our firewall complains about this, we can disable the firewall because we are doing

104
00:07:39,820 --> 00:07:42,430
this ourselves and we know what we are doing.

105
00:07:42,550 --> 00:07:48,700
And then when we execute the backdoor at the target computer, the backdoor is going to connect from

106
00:07:48,700 --> 00:07:51,850
the target computer to our computer.

107
00:07:51,850 --> 00:07:57,640
So this connection is much less suspicious because first of all, we're not opening any ports at the

108
00:07:57,640 --> 00:08:04,780
target computer, and connecting from the target computer to any other destination is very normal,

109
00:08:04,780 --> 00:08:10,240
because even if a target goes ahead and loads a normal website, they are basically connecting to that

110
00:08:10,240 --> 00:08:12,550
website on port 80 or 8080.

111
00:08:12,550 --> 00:08:14,740
And this is exactly what we can do.

112
00:08:14,770 --> 00:08:22,240
We can actually open port 80 or 8080 so that this connection looks less suspicious, and even use a

113
00:08:22,240 --> 00:08:28,870
protocol that is used by web servers so that our connection seems like normal web traffic.

114
00:08:29,400 --> 00:08:33,570
So remember we said the second part of the communication is a protocol.

115
00:08:33,570 --> 00:08:39,150
So when you load a website, you either load it over HTTP or Https.

116
00:08:39,150 --> 00:08:46,650
So we can actually use a reverse connection on a port that websites use, which is port 80 or 8080 and

117
00:08:46,650 --> 00:08:50,010
set the protocol to HTTP or Https.

118
00:08:50,010 --> 00:08:56,100
And that way the connection is going to seem very similar to a connection that a computer would make

119
00:08:56,100 --> 00:09:02,040
when they load a website, thus allowing us to bypass many firewalls and security measurements.

120
00:09:03,170 --> 00:09:07,910
So this is a very common naming convention when it comes to payloads.

121
00:09:07,910 --> 00:09:13,790
We have the platform in the first part, the type of the payload in the second part and the communication

122
00:09:13,790 --> 00:09:15,380
method in the last part.

123
00:09:15,860 --> 00:09:21,530
So let's just have a quick look at a few examples just to make sure that we understand this properly.

124
00:09:21,530 --> 00:09:26,480
So these are very common payloads that you might see or use yourself in many places.

125
00:09:26,480 --> 00:09:31,340
As you can see, the first three in here will only run on windows as the platform.

126
00:09:31,340 --> 00:09:35,270
You can see the type in this one is shell and Meterpreter.

127
00:09:35,270 --> 00:09:36,920
And we explained what these are.

128
00:09:36,920 --> 00:09:40,130
And both of these are used in reverse connection.

129
00:09:40,130 --> 00:09:42,560
This one is used in the TCP protocol.

130
00:09:42,560 --> 00:09:45,020
This one is used in the HTTP protocol.

131
00:09:45,020 --> 00:09:50,690
And this one is actually using a bind or a direct connection over the TCP protocol.

132
00:09:50,690 --> 00:09:56,300
Now as mentioned earlier, there are payloads that would work on any computer as long as they have a

133
00:09:56,300 --> 00:09:58,340
specific programming language installed.

134
00:09:58,340 --> 00:10:01,250
So we have an example of a Python payload in here.

135
00:10:01,250 --> 00:10:03,020
So the platform here is Python.

136
00:10:03,020 --> 00:10:08,720
It's not an operating system because it will run on any operating system that has Python installed.

137
00:10:08,720 --> 00:10:12,320
Now I also wanted to point out these two payloads in here.

138
00:10:12,320 --> 00:10:16,580
And as you can see, they don't follow the exact naming convention that we spoke about.

139
00:10:16,580 --> 00:10:18,440
You will see outliers in the payloads.

140
00:10:18,440 --> 00:10:22,070
And there, because not all payloads can actually be used as backdoors.

141
00:10:22,070 --> 00:10:25,790
And you'll see that as you go through this course and maybe my other courses.

142
00:10:25,790 --> 00:10:28,670
But these payloads can actually be used as backdoors.

143
00:10:28,670 --> 00:10:33,680
But you can see in this one, the first two parts are actually referring to the platform.

144
00:10:33,680 --> 00:10:38,570
So it's an Apple OS x x64, not the ARM computers.

145
00:10:38,570 --> 00:10:42,410
And then the next part is actually the type.

146
00:10:42,410 --> 00:10:45,170
So it's not really separated with a forward slash.

147
00:10:45,170 --> 00:10:48,680
And then the last part is the communication method in here.

148
00:10:48,830 --> 00:10:51,110
Same with this Apple payload.

149
00:10:51,110 --> 00:10:54,860
So this is for Apple iOS and it's for ARM chips.

150
00:10:54,860 --> 00:10:56,990
So this one is for x64 chips.

151
00:10:56,990 --> 00:10:58,460
This one is for ARM chips.

152
00:10:58,460 --> 00:11:01,640
Again the first two parts are referring to the platform.

153
00:11:01,640 --> 00:11:04,550
And then the next part is referring to the type.

154
00:11:04,550 --> 00:11:08,570
And the final part is referring to the communication.

155
00:11:08,660 --> 00:11:15,140
So sometimes the forward slashes could be a little bit misleading, but the order is always the same.

156
00:11:15,140 --> 00:11:21,320
The first part of the payload indicates the platform, the second part indicates the type of the payload,

157
00:11:21,320 --> 00:11:24,230
and the last part is the communication method.

158
00:11:24,470 --> 00:11:29,270
Now we're actually going to probably be using only 1 or 2 types of payloads throughout the course.

159
00:11:29,270 --> 00:11:35,240
But like I said, this course is designed to teach you the basics of hacking, and it is very important

160
00:11:35,240 --> 00:11:39,860
to learn how to create your own backdoors and payloads using MSF venom.

161
00:11:39,860 --> 00:11:45,050
And to do that, it's very important to understand the naming convention that the payloads use.

162
00:11:45,050 --> 00:11:49,940
And in the next lecture, I'm going to show you how to put this in practice, create a backdoor and

163
00:11:49,940 --> 00:11:52,370
use it to hack a windows computer.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,340 --> 00:00:06,610
Now that we understand what we mean by a backdoor, a payload, the naming convention and so on, in

2
00:00:06,610 --> 00:00:12,400
this lecture, we're going to use all of this knowledge in order to create a backdoor that can be used

3
00:00:12,400 --> 00:00:16,000
to remotely hack and control windows computers.

4
00:00:16,450 --> 00:00:21,310
So as mentioned previously, the tool that we're going to be using is called MSF venom.

5
00:00:21,310 --> 00:00:27,400
And it's always a good idea to do dash dash help the first time that you use a tool in order to see

6
00:00:27,400 --> 00:00:30,640
all of the options and arguments that you can use with it.

7
00:00:31,180 --> 00:00:35,110
So as you can see, this gives us a list of everything that we can do.

8
00:00:35,110 --> 00:00:36,520
And straight away we can see.

9
00:00:36,520 --> 00:00:42,610
The first option is the dash L or dash dash list option, which is what we use previously to list all

10
00:00:42,610 --> 00:00:44,890
of the payloads available in this tool.

11
00:00:44,890 --> 00:00:47,200
So I'm actually going to split the screen.

12
00:00:48,150 --> 00:00:49,920
And we're just going to run it quickly.

13
00:00:49,920 --> 00:00:57,180
We're going to do MSF venom Dash dash list payloads in order to get a list of all of the payloads that

14
00:00:57,180 --> 00:00:58,110
we can use.

15
00:00:58,110 --> 00:01:01,620
And we saw previously that we got a really big list of payloads.

16
00:01:01,620 --> 00:01:06,930
So once you find the payload that you like and that you'd want to use, we can go ahead.

17
00:01:06,930 --> 00:01:13,410
And if we look at the help menu in here, you'll see that we can select the payload using the Dash P

18
00:01:13,410 --> 00:01:15,360
or dash dash payload option.

19
00:01:15,480 --> 00:01:18,660
So I'm going to go ahead and do MSF venom again.

20
00:01:18,660 --> 00:01:24,750
And I'm going to do dash dash payload to select the payload that I want to use inside of my backdoor.

21
00:01:24,750 --> 00:01:28,680
And the payload that I'm going to want to use is a windows payload.

22
00:01:28,680 --> 00:01:33,750
So the platform is windows and the type is M interpreter payload.

23
00:01:33,750 --> 00:01:40,290
So this is the type of payloads that I said in the previous lecture that is specifically designed by

24
00:01:40,290 --> 00:01:41,970
the Metasploit team.

25
00:01:41,970 --> 00:01:43,320
It runs in memory.

26
00:01:43,320 --> 00:01:47,040
It encrypts its communication between the backdoor and the target.

27
00:01:47,040 --> 00:01:52,320
And it has so many modules and so many options, and you're going to see how useful it is as we go through

28
00:01:52,320 --> 00:01:53,010
the course.

29
00:01:53,010 --> 00:01:57,150
And finally, as we know, the last part is the communication method.

30
00:01:57,150 --> 00:02:03,030
So we're going to use a reverse payload because as mentioned in the previous lecture, reverse payloads

31
00:02:03,030 --> 00:02:09,450
connect from the target computer to us, allowing us to bypass firewalls and many security measurements.

32
00:02:09,450 --> 00:02:16,980
And the last part, we're going to set it to use the Https protocol again, so that the traffic looks

33
00:02:16,980 --> 00:02:23,280
less suspicious and looks similar to a normal traffic that the target would create when they load normal

34
00:02:23,280 --> 00:02:25,440
websites over Https.

35
00:02:26,010 --> 00:02:32,490
So now that we selected the payload, we can actually do dash dash list options to see the options that

36
00:02:32,490 --> 00:02:37,080
we can set for this payload so we can instruct it how it should work.

37
00:02:37,650 --> 00:02:44,010
So we're going to go ahead in here and do dash dash list dash options.

38
00:02:44,010 --> 00:02:45,540
We're going to hit enter.

39
00:02:47,420 --> 00:02:54,800
And as you can see, we get a big list of all the potential options that we can use along with the description

40
00:02:54,800 --> 00:02:56,090
of this option.

41
00:02:56,960 --> 00:02:59,240
Now these are all the advanced options.

42
00:02:59,240 --> 00:03:01,700
So we're not actually going to be setting any of these.

43
00:03:01,700 --> 00:03:03,890
They're not mandatory at all.

44
00:03:04,130 --> 00:03:08,780
What we're interested in is the basic options that we have in here.

45
00:03:09,080 --> 00:03:11,990
So as you can see these options are required.

46
00:03:11,990 --> 00:03:15,080
And we can also see the description of these options.

47
00:03:15,410 --> 00:03:19,940
So the El host specifies the local listener host name.

48
00:03:19,940 --> 00:03:25,910
So that basically means the IP of the computer that you want to receive the connection on.

49
00:03:25,910 --> 00:03:28,850
So this is this computer our hacker computer.

50
00:03:28,850 --> 00:03:33,500
And the el port is the port that we want to listen on.

51
00:03:33,740 --> 00:03:35,660
It's this part of the diagram.

52
00:03:35,660 --> 00:03:37,280
It's the plug in the wall.

53
00:03:37,280 --> 00:03:44,000
So it's the port that the target machine will connect to once the backdoor is executed.

54
00:03:44,660 --> 00:03:46,970
So first we're going to set the L host.

55
00:03:46,970 --> 00:03:49,220
So we're going to go back to our command.

56
00:03:49,220 --> 00:03:54,260
And we're going to remove the dash dash list options because we don't want to see the options anymore.

57
00:03:54,260 --> 00:04:00,050
But we actually want to set the L host value, which is going to be the IP of this current computer,

58
00:04:00,050 --> 00:04:03,290
because I want to receive connections on this computer.

59
00:04:03,290 --> 00:04:05,960
So I'm going to split the screen in here.

60
00:04:05,960 --> 00:04:11,240
And I'm simply going to run ifconfig to get the IP of my current computer.

61
00:04:12,000 --> 00:04:15,450
And as you can see, this is the IP of this computer right now.

62
00:04:15,450 --> 00:04:18,390
You might have a different IP that's completely normal.

63
00:04:18,390 --> 00:04:20,610
Just make sure you use the correct IP.

64
00:04:20,610 --> 00:04:26,130
So it's 192.1680 26.

65
00:04:26,490 --> 00:04:30,330
Next we're going to set the next required option.

66
00:04:30,330 --> 00:04:33,270
So if we scroll up that is the L port.

67
00:04:33,270 --> 00:04:37,320
And like I said this is the port that we're going to receive the connection on.

68
00:04:37,320 --> 00:04:39,480
So we're going to follow the same syntax.

69
00:04:39,480 --> 00:04:44,430
We're going to type L port, which is the name of the option that we want to set equal to the value

70
00:04:44,430 --> 00:04:45,210
that we want.

71
00:04:45,210 --> 00:04:48,540
And the value that we want is going to be 8080.

72
00:04:48,540 --> 00:04:55,020
So I'm choosing the 8080 port because that is a common port that is used by web servers, port 80 and

73
00:04:55,020 --> 00:04:55,770
8080.

74
00:04:55,890 --> 00:05:02,460
And this makes perfect sense because we are using a backdoor that's going to use Https, which is a

75
00:05:02,460 --> 00:05:07,230
protocol that is usually used by web servers to serve websites over Https.

76
00:05:07,230 --> 00:05:09,960
And the port that we're using is 8080.

77
00:05:09,960 --> 00:05:16,950
So when this backdoor gets executed on the target computer, it's going to connect to us on port 8080.

78
00:05:16,950 --> 00:05:20,460
And it's going to use the Https protocol to do that.

79
00:05:20,460 --> 00:05:27,870
And this is great because the target computer will do that every time it loads a website that uses Https,

80
00:05:27,870 --> 00:05:30,330
which is pretty much all websites these days.

81
00:05:30,330 --> 00:05:36,930
That's why the traffic is going to look very similar to traffic that is generated when the target computer

82
00:05:36,930 --> 00:05:38,580
loads normal websites.

83
00:05:38,970 --> 00:05:46,440
So now that we have the basic options set, the last thing that we need to do is actually export this

84
00:05:46,440 --> 00:05:48,300
into an actual file.

85
00:05:48,820 --> 00:05:55,810
And to do that, you can see here in the help menu, we can use the dash F to specify the format of

86
00:05:55,810 --> 00:05:57,160
the payload.

87
00:05:57,160 --> 00:06:00,490
So we're going to do dash dash format.

88
00:06:00,490 --> 00:06:03,250
And that's going to be an executable.

89
00:06:03,250 --> 00:06:04,720
So we're doing x e.

90
00:06:05,320 --> 00:06:11,590
And finally we can use the dash o or dash dash out to specify the file name.

91
00:06:11,920 --> 00:06:16,780
So we're going to do dash dash out followed by the file name that we want.

92
00:06:16,780 --> 00:06:23,230
And I'm going to call it rev https at 80 dot e x e.

93
00:06:23,800 --> 00:06:29,860
And I'm just using a meaningful name here, just so that I know this is a reverse Https backdoor that

94
00:06:29,860 --> 00:06:32,080
uses the 880 port.

95
00:06:32,350 --> 00:06:32,980
And that's it.

96
00:06:32,980 --> 00:06:36,790
Our command is done and it's ready to be used to generate a backdoor.

97
00:06:36,790 --> 00:06:39,880
But let's go through it one more time before we hit enter.

98
00:06:39,880 --> 00:06:44,740
So we're using MSF venom, which is the tool that we're using to generate the backdoor.

99
00:06:44,740 --> 00:06:49,060
We're using the dash dash payload argument to specify the payload.

100
00:06:49,060 --> 00:06:51,790
And this is the payload that we are choosing.

101
00:06:51,790 --> 00:06:54,310
So it runs on windows platforms.

102
00:06:54,310 --> 00:07:02,290
The type of payload is a payload and it uses reverse connection over the Https protocol.

103
00:07:02,860 --> 00:07:06,040
Then we're setting the basic options of this payload.

104
00:07:06,040 --> 00:07:11,980
So the first option is the El host, which is the IP of the computer that will receive the connection

105
00:07:11,980 --> 00:07:13,570
from the target computer.

106
00:07:13,570 --> 00:07:15,400
And I put my current IP.

107
00:07:15,520 --> 00:07:19,630
And then we specified the port that we want to receive the connection on.

108
00:07:19,630 --> 00:07:21,400
And we set that to 8080.

109
00:07:21,700 --> 00:07:26,590
We're using the dash dash format argument to specify the format of the back door.

110
00:07:26,590 --> 00:07:28,780
And we want it to be an executable.

111
00:07:28,780 --> 00:07:34,780
And then we're using the dash dash out argument to specify the output file name of the back door, which

112
00:07:34,780 --> 00:07:39,280
is going to be called rev https at ATX.

113
00:07:39,820 --> 00:07:40,900
If we hit enter.

114
00:07:41,530 --> 00:07:47,140
This will take some time and it will generate the backdoor for us and the current working directory.

115
00:07:47,140 --> 00:07:52,570
So as you know, if you want to get the current working directory, you can use the PWD command.

116
00:07:52,570 --> 00:07:55,090
And you can see that I am currently in root.

117
00:07:55,090 --> 00:07:57,670
So if I go to my file manager.

118
00:07:58,700 --> 00:08:01,550
It will load the root directory by default.

119
00:08:01,550 --> 00:08:06,380
And if we look down you'll see that we have my backdoor generated in here.

120
00:08:06,650 --> 00:08:12,530
So next I'm going to show you how to use this backdoor in order to hack a windows computer and gain

121
00:08:12,530 --> 00:08:14,390
full remote control over it.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,080 --> 00:00:06,860
Now that we created the backdoor that when executed on a target computer, it will send a reverse connection

2
00:00:06,860 --> 00:00:08,720
to our computer.

3
00:00:09,080 --> 00:00:15,710
Before we do that, and before we go ahead and execute it, we need to go ahead and listen for incoming

4
00:00:15,710 --> 00:00:16,370
connections.

5
00:00:16,370 --> 00:00:23,180
So we need to create this socket, if you may, so that when the backdoor is executed on the target

6
00:00:23,180 --> 00:00:30,920
computer, it can actually go ahead and connect to the port or to the socket that we have open.

7
00:00:30,920 --> 00:00:37,790
So in this lecture I'm going to show you how to open up a port and listen for incoming connections on

8
00:00:37,790 --> 00:00:43,760
our current computer, so that when the backdoor is executed on the target computer, it will send that

9
00:00:43,760 --> 00:00:46,640
connection back to our computer.

10
00:00:47,390 --> 00:00:50,030
To do that, we're actually going to use Metasploit.

11
00:00:50,060 --> 00:00:55,430
So first of all we need to launch the console by doing MSF console as you know.

12
00:00:56,080 --> 00:00:57,580
Give it a moment to boot.

13
00:00:57,580 --> 00:01:05,410
And when it starts we're going to use a module that is called exploit multi handler.

14
00:01:05,680 --> 00:01:12,160
So basically this is a module that is designed to help you open up a port in your computer so that you

15
00:01:12,160 --> 00:01:15,370
can receive connections from computers that you hack.

16
00:01:15,400 --> 00:01:17,800
So again hit enter to load this module.

17
00:01:17,800 --> 00:01:24,190
And because we don't know how to use this module, we can simply do show options to see all of the options

18
00:01:24,190 --> 00:01:25,690
that we can set for it.

19
00:01:25,690 --> 00:01:31,570
And as you can see, you get a nice message showing you the module name, showing you the payload that

20
00:01:31,570 --> 00:01:35,020
this module is using by default and its options.

21
00:01:35,260 --> 00:01:41,380
Now if you look at this payload, you'll notice that this is not the payload that we used in our backdoor.

22
00:01:41,380 --> 00:01:43,990
And that's why we're going to have to modify this.

23
00:01:43,990 --> 00:01:50,200
And you can modify this value exactly the same way you modify any value in Metasploit.

24
00:01:50,200 --> 00:01:57,250
So we're going to do set followed by the option name that we want to modify which is the payload.

25
00:01:57,250 --> 00:02:03,520
The main thing you want to make sure is that you use the exact same payload that you used in the backdoor.

26
00:02:03,520 --> 00:02:09,280
So in the backdoor we use windows meterpreter reverse https.

27
00:02:11,120 --> 00:02:18,950
If we do show options again, you'll see that we'll actually going to see different options because

28
00:02:18,950 --> 00:02:20,780
the payload is different.

29
00:02:20,780 --> 00:02:26,660
So you can see that the options here are again very similar to the options that we set when we created

30
00:02:26,660 --> 00:02:27,470
the backdoor.

31
00:02:27,470 --> 00:02:33,200
So the main two options that we need to modify are the L host and the L port.

32
00:02:33,200 --> 00:02:38,060
And the L host is the IP of the computer that will receive the connection.

33
00:02:38,060 --> 00:02:40,970
And in our case it's this computer right here.

34
00:02:40,970 --> 00:02:45,200
And the L port is the port that you want to receive the connection on.

35
00:02:45,200 --> 00:02:51,770
And again both of these values need to match the exact same values that you set in the backdoor.

36
00:02:52,010 --> 00:02:58,250
So to get our current IP, we saw that when we run the backdoor we can do ifconfig.

37
00:02:58,250 --> 00:03:01,760
And we can see my IP is right here.

38
00:03:01,760 --> 00:03:03,320
So I'm going to copy it.

39
00:03:03,320 --> 00:03:08,390
And again we're going to set its value exactly the same way that you set values in Metasploit.

40
00:03:08,390 --> 00:03:14,150
So you do set followed by the option name which is l host followed by the value.

41
00:03:14,150 --> 00:03:16,670
And I'm going to paste my value which is my IP.

42
00:03:17,700 --> 00:03:19,320
Next we're going to set the port.

43
00:03:19,320 --> 00:03:20,880
So we're going to do set.

44
00:03:21,460 --> 00:03:26,530
Option name El Port, and the port that we used in the backdoor was 8080.

45
00:03:27,580 --> 00:03:33,490
Now that we set everything we can do, show options one more time to make sure that the options were

46
00:03:33,490 --> 00:03:35,650
set correctly and perfect.

47
00:03:35,650 --> 00:03:40,690
As you can see, we can see that the El host is set to my IP, which is the same value that I used in

48
00:03:40,690 --> 00:03:41,440
the backdoor.

49
00:03:41,440 --> 00:03:43,600
And same goes for the El port.

50
00:03:43,960 --> 00:03:47,560
So now that we're happy with everything we can do exploit.

51
00:03:49,550 --> 00:03:51,710
And this will basically just sit.

52
00:03:51,740 --> 00:03:57,230
It'll literally just open up the socket, open up this port, port 8080.

53
00:03:57,230 --> 00:04:03,530
And it's going to wait for incoming connections from any computer that executes the backdoor.

54
00:04:03,560 --> 00:04:08,960
So in the next lecture, we're going to go ahead and execute the backdoor on a windows computer and

55
00:04:08,960 --> 00:04:14,600
see if we're actually going to be able to get a reverse connection and be able to remotely control that

56
00:04:14,600 --> 00:04:15,230
computer.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,080 --> 00:00:06,020
So now that we are listening for incoming connections on our hacker machine, we are ready to go ahead

2
00:00:06,020 --> 00:00:12,470
and execute the backdoor at the target machine and receive a reverse connection from it and remotely

3
00:00:12,470 --> 00:00:13,970
control that computer.

4
00:00:13,970 --> 00:00:18,770
So first of all, we're going to have to transfer the backdoor to the target machine.

5
00:00:18,770 --> 00:00:24,410
And the next lectures I'm going to cover smart methods of delivering backdoors and malware to the target

6
00:00:24,410 --> 00:00:25,250
computers.

7
00:00:25,250 --> 00:00:30,950
But for now, just because we're testing, I'm trying to keep everything as simple as possible, and

8
00:00:30,950 --> 00:00:34,670
we just want to make sure that the backdoor that we created works.

9
00:00:34,670 --> 00:00:41,000
That's why we're going to use the web server that is built in within Kali Linux to upload the backdoor

10
00:00:41,000 --> 00:00:44,300
and download it from the target computer in here.

11
00:00:45,080 --> 00:00:51,560
So first of all, I'm going to go ahead and copy the backdoor to the location that the web server uses

12
00:00:51,560 --> 00:00:52,730
to serve files.

13
00:00:52,730 --> 00:00:57,230
So as you saw previously the backdoor was placed in my home directory.

14
00:00:57,230 --> 00:00:59,840
So I'm simply going to copy it.

15
00:00:59,840 --> 00:01:03,590
And then I'm going to click the title bar in here.

16
00:01:03,590 --> 00:01:08,900
Or you can do control L in order to open the path bar as you can see in here.

17
00:01:08,900 --> 00:01:12,500
And then type the location that you want to go to.

18
00:01:12,500 --> 00:01:18,740
And we want to go to var w w w h HTML.

19
00:01:19,040 --> 00:01:24,200
Again, this is the default location that the web server uses to serve files.

20
00:01:24,200 --> 00:01:28,130
So in here I'm going to create a new directory or a new folder.

21
00:01:28,130 --> 00:01:31,070
And I'm going to call it evil files.

22
00:01:31,070 --> 00:01:37,070
And I'm going to use this in the future as well to store my evil files so that I can easily share them

23
00:01:37,070 --> 00:01:43,250
with testing computers and test these evil files, because it's very important to make sure that your

24
00:01:43,250 --> 00:01:49,280
malware or backdoor or keylogger is working properly before you go ahead and use it in the wild.

25
00:01:49,280 --> 00:01:54,590
So now that I'm here, I'm simply going to right click and paste in order to paste my backdoor.

26
00:01:54,590 --> 00:01:56,480
And we have it in here now.

27
00:01:56,480 --> 00:02:00,320
And next we're going to go ahead and start this web server.

28
00:02:00,320 --> 00:02:02,390
So I'm going to clear my screen.

29
00:02:03,260 --> 00:02:05,780
And they start the built in web server in Cali.

30
00:02:05,810 --> 00:02:08,030
We're simply going to do service.

31
00:02:08,810 --> 00:02:10,970
Apache to start.

32
00:02:11,390 --> 00:02:17,090
So we're basically saying we want to start a service that is called Apache two.

33
00:02:17,090 --> 00:02:21,530
And Apache two is the name of the built in web server in Cali.

34
00:02:22,010 --> 00:02:23,270
I'm going to hit enter.

35
00:02:23,270 --> 00:02:28,250
And if you don't see any error messages it means that the web server is running properly.

36
00:02:28,250 --> 00:02:34,520
So to access this web server, all you're going to need is the IP of the Cali machine, and we can get

37
00:02:34,520 --> 00:02:38,150
the IP of the Cali machine, as you know, by doing ifconfig.

38
00:02:38,150 --> 00:02:45,560
And it's the same IP that we used in the backdoor as well, which is 1921680 26.

39
00:02:46,170 --> 00:02:49,710
So I'm going to go to the target windows computer right here.

40
00:02:49,710 --> 00:02:52,110
And I'm going to open up my web browser.

41
00:02:52,730 --> 00:02:59,540
And I'm simply going to navigate to the IP 1921680 26.

42
00:02:59,540 --> 00:03:05,120
And if you load it as is, you're going to get the default welcome screen of the web server.

43
00:03:05,120 --> 00:03:09,680
But our file is stored in a directory that is called evil files.

44
00:03:09,680 --> 00:03:16,550
So I'm going to do forward slash evil dash files to load the evil files directory.

45
00:03:16,550 --> 00:03:21,440
And as you can see in here we have the backdoor that I generated.

46
00:03:21,440 --> 00:03:25,430
And like I said this is not a very smart method of delivering backdoors.

47
00:03:25,430 --> 00:03:27,800
But we are just testing at the moment.

48
00:03:27,800 --> 00:03:30,320
And we just want to make sure that the backdoor works.

49
00:03:30,320 --> 00:03:35,750
And before we download this backdoor, I'm going to go ahead and disable the security on windows.

50
00:03:35,750 --> 00:03:41,720
This is important because we didn't implement any techniques to bypass antivirus programs or Windows

51
00:03:41,720 --> 00:03:47,480
Defender in this backdoor, because at the moment we're focusing on building a very simple backdoor.

52
00:03:47,480 --> 00:03:51,770
It is very important to break the big problem into simple problems.

53
00:03:51,770 --> 00:03:57,410
First, learn how to create a backdoor, make sure it's working properly, and only then you can go

54
00:03:57,410 --> 00:04:02,810
ahead and build up on this knowledge and try to make that backdoor, bypass antivirus programs and so

55
00:04:02,810 --> 00:04:03,260
on.

56
00:04:03,260 --> 00:04:08,390
So at the moment, we just want to make sure that we can create a basic backdoor that actually allows

57
00:04:08,390 --> 00:04:12,500
us to remotely control the computers that it gets executed on.

58
00:04:12,500 --> 00:04:17,000
So before downloading this, we're going to go ahead and disable Windows Defender.

59
00:04:17,000 --> 00:04:19,580
So we're simply going to go to the start menu.

60
00:04:19,610 --> 00:04:21,590
We're going to look for security.

61
00:04:22,190 --> 00:04:23,750
We have it right here.

62
00:04:24,500 --> 00:04:27,470
And we're going to click on the virus and threat protection.

63
00:04:27,470 --> 00:04:29,840
We're going to click on Manage Settings.

64
00:04:29,840 --> 00:04:33,530
And you want to make sure that everything is set to off in here.

65
00:04:33,530 --> 00:04:34,610
As you can see.

66
00:04:34,610 --> 00:04:38,480
Otherwise the backdoor will be detected and it will simply not work.

67
00:04:39,020 --> 00:04:41,780
So we're going to click it in here in order to download it.

68
00:04:41,780 --> 00:04:43,550
And that's download it for me.

69
00:04:43,550 --> 00:04:46,940
And we can see it here in our downloads directory.

70
00:04:47,450 --> 00:04:50,570
And also as you can see this file does look a bit scary.

71
00:04:50,570 --> 00:04:56,180
It's simply an executable we will cover how to make this look and function like any other file, like

72
00:04:56,180 --> 00:04:58,580
an image or a PDF or anything else.

73
00:04:58,580 --> 00:05:03,980
Again, we're breaking the big problem into simpler problems and we're focusing on a very simple goal,

74
00:05:03,980 --> 00:05:06,920
which is to get a backdoor working on windows.

75
00:05:06,920 --> 00:05:11,120
So right now just for testing, we're going to double click this file to run it.

76
00:05:11,120 --> 00:05:13,730
And we're simply going to say run anyway.

77
00:05:13,730 --> 00:05:16,400
And then we're going to go to the Kali machine.

78
00:05:16,580 --> 00:05:23,750
And perfect as you can see at the window where we started our multi handler, you can see that we received

79
00:05:23,750 --> 00:05:25,190
a connection back.

80
00:05:25,190 --> 00:05:32,930
And the prompt now says Meterpreter instead of MSF, which means that we actually received a connection

81
00:05:32,930 --> 00:05:38,900
back from the windows computer through the interpreter payload that we created.

82
00:05:38,900 --> 00:05:45,200
So just quickly we can do sysinfo to get information about the system that we just hacked.

83
00:05:46,200 --> 00:05:52,320
And as you can see, we can get the computer name, the operating system, the architecture.

84
00:05:52,320 --> 00:05:58,170
So now we can go ahead and execute any command that the interpreter allows us to execute.

85
00:05:58,170 --> 00:06:02,040
And you can do help to get a full list of all of these commands.

86
00:06:02,040 --> 00:06:03,990
But don't worry about this now.

87
00:06:03,990 --> 00:06:08,550
We will cover this in details in the Post-exploitation section later on.

88
00:06:08,550 --> 00:06:15,510
But for now, we've basically managed to hack this computer and gain full remote control over it.

89
00:06:15,510 --> 00:06:22,380
So right now we can do anything that the actual user of this computer can do on their computer, but

90
00:06:22,380 --> 00:06:27,090
we can do it remotely from within our meterpreter session in here.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:01,040  -->  00:00:06,780
Now in this lecture, I'd like to show you another tool that allow us to generate undetectable backdoors.

2

00:00:07,480  -->  00:00:13,460
This tool is called TheFatRat and you can think of it as a tool that's similar to Veil-Evasion.

3

00:00:13,860  -->  00:00:19,120
It doesn't use the same techniques as Veil-Evasion but it allow us to generate backdoors,

4

00:00:19,120  -->  00:00:24,560
that if executed on the target computer, they give us full access to that computer.

5

00:00:25,320  -->  00:00:30,780
Now, you might think why am I teaching you a tool that does exactly the same thing as Veil-Evasion.

6

00:00:31,140  -->  00:00:38,320
Well, the reason is as I said before, when it comes to antivirus 'Evasion', we are going in a loop,

7

00:00:38,520  -->  00:00:45,080
so antivirus programs release an update that render Veil and other programs maybe, detectable.

8

00:00:45,300  -->  00:00:49,780
So you might have to modify the options of the payloads, like I said before

9

00:00:50,140  -->  00:00:52,740
or you could just try to use a different tool.

10

00:00:53,700  -->  00:00:58,540
So I'm showing you a different tool, just in case you're trying to generate a backdoor using 'Veil'

11

00:00:58,540  -->  00:01:02,600
and it's getting detected then you can just come in and try a different tool.

12

00:01:02,600  -->  00:01:05,160
And in this example, we're gonna be trying TheFatRat

13

00:01:05,520  -->  00:01:09,020
and we'll see how that generates undetectable backdoors as well.

14

00:01:09,460  -->  00:01:13,240
The case could be the opposite, you might be generating something using TheFatRat

15

00:01:13,240  -->  00:01:16,850
and it gets detected and in that case you can go and try with Veil-Evasion.

16

00:01:17,800  -->  00:01:22,620
Now, always you need to keep in mind that you want to make sure that these tools are always updated.

17

00:01:22,620  -->  00:01:24,540
So you wanna make sure you have the latest update

18

00:01:25,040  -->  00:01:29,300
and you should be able to bypass most, if not all antivirus programs.

19

00:01:30,260  -->  00:01:34,940
The Fat Rat also has options to generate
backdoors for other operating systems

20

00:01:34,940  -->  00:01:40,420
such as Mac OS, Linux and Android and we'll talk about that later on in the course.

21

00:01:41,100  -->  00:01:43,600
For now, we're gonna talk about installing Fat Rat

22

00:01:43,600  -->  00:01:46,220
because it doesn't come pre-installed in Kali.

23

00:01:47,040  -->  00:01:50,040
So the first thing you want to do is go to their website

24

00:01:51,080  -->  00:01:54,020
and I'm gonna put it in the resources of the course

25

00:01:54,580  -->  00:01:59,340
or you can just literally, go to google and search for The Fat Rat.

26

00:02:03,540  -->  00:02:06,600
So to download something from 'Git' you an use the 'Git' command

27

00:02:06,940  -->  00:02:11,740
and the first thing you want to do is click here and 'get the URL for the project.

28

00:02:12,300  -->  00:02:17,560
So I'm going to copy this and that's all I need for now.

29

00:02:17,600  -->  00:02:18,760
So I'm gonna close this window.

30

00:02:22,860  -->  00:02:24,060
I'm going to open my terminal

31

00:02:28,240  -->  00:02:32,260
and I'm going to install the fat rat in OPT, in the OPT path.

32

00:02:32,580  -->  00:02:38,940
So to do that I'm going to use 'cd' to open and then I'm gonna go to '/opt'.

33

00:02:40,140  -->  00:02:45,440
Now I'm gonna download or clone The Fat Rat and I've already copied its URL.

34

00:02:45,900  -->  00:02:53,080
Now, to clone or download something from 'GitHub', all you have to do is just do 'git clone'

35

00:02:54,540  -->  00:02:57,760
and then put the URL of that project that
I just copied.

36

00:02:59,640  -->  00:03:04,192
So I'm gonna hit Enter and that's gonna create a new directory for me.

37

00:03:05,340  -->  00:03:10,480
Okay, now that the download is done, I'm gonna do 'Ls' to list the files or directories

38

00:03:10,860  -->  00:03:13,520
and as you can see again, we have The Fat Rat right here.

39

00:03:13,880  -->  00:03:19,300
So I'm gonna navigate into that and I'm going to do 'cd' to navigate into a directory

40

00:03:20,000  -->  00:03:22,940
and then we're going to put the directory name, which is 'TheFatRat'.

41

00:03:24,040  -->  00:03:30,720
If we do 'Ls' to list again, you can see all the files have been downloaded.

42

00:03:31,520  -->  00:03:34,960
Now we need to set up the tool before we can use it.

43

00:03:34,980  -->  00:03:37,140
So we have to run this setup script.

44

00:03:37,720  -->  00:03:41,760
To do that we have to first change its permissions to executable.

45

00:03:42,440  -->  00:03:48,180
You can do that using the 'chmod' command and then we're going to do '+x'

46

00:03:50,060  -->  00:03:54,440
and put the filename that we want to change its permission and it's 'setup.sh'.

47

00:03:56,540  -->  00:04:00,700
So the reason why we're doing this is because we want to execute that file,

48

00:04:00,760  -->  00:04:06,020
we want to execute 'setup.sh' and right now, it's not executable.

49

00:04:06,600  -->  00:04:09,220
So we're using the 'chmod' command to change its permission

50

00:04:09,220  -->  00:04:13,100
and this is the permission that we want to use, which is the executable permission.

51

00:04:13,100  -->  00:04:15,020
And we're doing it to this file.

52

00:04:15,560  -->  00:04:23,140
Now, if I hit Enter and do 'Ls', you'll see that the file color has changed to green,

53

00:04:23,140  -->  00:04:25,600
which means that it's an executable now.

54

00:04:26,360  -->  00:04:32,360
So to run this file I'm just gonna do './' and I'm gonna put the file name.

55

00:04:33,900  -->  00:04:37,440
So now, if I hit Enter that's gonna run the installer for me

56

00:04:38,000  -->  00:04:42,100
and the Installer is first checking if I have, if there is anything missing

57

00:04:42,100  -->  00:04:45,580
and as you can see so far, everything is installed by default.

58

00:04:46,140  -->  00:04:51,140
And if there was anything missing, it will tell us that this is missing and I'm gonna install it for you.

59

00:04:51,780  -->  00:04:55,660
Again, just like Veil-Evasion, it's checking for libraries and packages

60

00:04:55,660  -->  00:04:59,260
that it's gonna need to use when generating the backdoors

61

00:04:59,260  -->  00:05:04,000
and you can see, it just discovered that there is a dependency in Ruby, that's not met.

62

00:05:04,160  -->  00:05:06,980
And it's downloading it and installing it automatically.

63

00:05:09,380  -->  00:05:15,838
Okay, now it's finished everything and it's asking me if I want to create a shortcut for FatRat.

64

00:05:16,360  -->  00:05:21,840
Basically, what this does is, it'll allow us to run TheFatRat command from anywhere within the system.

65

00:05:21,840  -->  00:05:24,900
So we don't have to navigate into its directory.

66

00:05:24,900  -->  00:05:28,274
We won't have to do 'cd/opt/TheFatRat' and then run The Fat Rat.

67

00:05:28,280  -->  00:05:30,100
We'll be able to run this from anywhere.

68

00:05:30,660  -->  00:05:36,840
So I'm just gonna say, 'Yes' and that's telling me that it's done and we can use it now from anywhere.

69

00:05:38,560  -->  00:05:39,360
And that's it.

70

00:05:39,360  -->  00:05:40,440
The installation is complete.

71

00:05:40,960  -->  00:05:48,820
Now, if we run TheFatRat and
hit Enter, you'll see the program starting up

72

00:05:50,800  -->  00:05:55,380
and it's just giving us a note, not to upload the viruses or the backdoors

73

00:05:55,380  -->  00:05:58,820
that we create to 'VirusTotal', upload them to 'NoDistribute'.

74

00:05:59,120  -->  00:06:00,140
We're already doing that.

75

00:06:00,380  -->  00:06:03,640
So we're gonna say yes, no problem and that's it.

76

00:06:03,840  -->  00:06:09,340
Now the tool is ready to be used and we're going to talk about it in future videos.

77

00:06:09,680  -->  00:06:12,500
For now we're just, we just had a look on how to install it

78

00:06:12,720  -->  00:06:15,760
and as we did that, we actually learned a few Linux commands,

79

00:06:15,760  -->  00:06:17,820
that's gonna come in handy on the long run.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━


1

00:00:00,600  -->  00:00:06,940
Okay, now that we have TheFatRat loaded, let's see how we can use it to generate an undetectable backdoor.

2

00:00:07,660  -->  00:00:13,520
Now you can see the tool can be used to do many more things, like it creates an a backdoor for Android

3

00:00:13,520  -->  00:00:18,620
and combining a backdoor with another file but we're not going to be talking about that now.

4

00:00:18,620  -->  00:00:20,800
We're gonna go over that later on in the course.

5

00:00:21,020  -->  00:00:25,460
But for now we're interested into creating an undetectable backdoor.

6

00:00:26,240  -->  00:00:32,280
And the one I'm gonna use is Creating one with PwnWinds which actually uses PowerShell.

7

00:00:33,020  -->  00:00:38,840
So this is number '06' right here, so all I have to do is just enter '06' or '6'

8

00:00:40,640  -->  00:00:46,320
and that will take me to PwnWinds, which gives me even even more options to generate the backdoor.

9

00:00:48,240  -->  00:00:51,200
Now the backdoor generated with this uses PowerShell

10

00:00:51,600  -->  00:00:55,080
and PowerShell is basically, a framework made by Windows,

11

00:00:55,400  -->  00:00:59,480
which a kind of expands on to the basic DOS commands.

12

00:00:59,740  -->  00:01:04,580
So it basically, gives you more Shell commands that can be used to automate tasks

13

00:01:04,580  -->  00:01:07,460
and run things on Windows Operating Systems.

14

00:01:08,060  -->  00:01:12,680
Powershell comes in pre-installed with all Windows machines after Windows 7.

15

00:01:13,120  -->  00:01:17,100
So you can guarantee that it's going to work on all Windows computers,

16

00:01:17,100  -->  00:01:19,160
if they're running Windows 7 and up.

17

00:01:21,080  -->  00:01:24,760
I'm gonna 'Create a bat', which is an executable type

18

00:01:25,100  -->  00:01:30,680
and that's gonna use PowerShell to inject the backdoor and give us the Reverse Shell.

19

00:01:31,200  -->  00:01:36,480
The other options will still use PowerShell but we'll create a slightly different backdoor.

20

00:01:36,500  -->  00:01:41,540
For example, you can see this one will attempt to embed the backdoor with a PDF

21

00:01:41,540  -->  00:01:43,320
but it won't work against all systems.

22

00:01:43,740  -->  00:01:47,560
You'll see that this one will use 'C', this one will use 'Apache',

23

00:01:47,820  -->  00:01:53,960
this one will use 'C#' but for our one, we're just going to use a basic BAT file with PowerShell

24

00:01:53,960  -->  00:01:57,180
and it should be able to bypass antivirus programs.

25

00:01:58,040  -->  00:02:02,200
So I'm gonna go with number '1' which uses a bat file with PowerShell

26

00:02:02,460  -->  00:02:06,500
and the reason why I'm going with it because I've actually already tested the others

27

00:02:06,500  -->  00:02:12,560
and they get detected by at least an antivirus programs, so I'm gonna go with number '1'.

28

00:02:13,240  -->  00:02:17,160
And then you can see that the tool is really nice because it already tells us

29

00:02:17,160  -->  00:02:24,500
our local IP, our real IP and our Hostname, now we'll talk about the real IP and all of that later on.

30

00:02:24,820  -->  00:02:30,300
For now all that, we're interested in is our local IP and when we were using Veil,

31

00:02:30,300  -->  00:02:34,020
we had to run 'ifconfig' to see what's our local IP.

32

00:02:34,660  -->  00:02:40,060
So remember we had to split the screen and do 'ifconfig'

33

00:02:41,680  -->  00:02:46,020
and then we can see that our local IP is '10.20.14.213'.

34

00:02:46,700  -->  00:02:51,180

35

00:02:51,560  -->  00:02:53,909

36

00:02:53,909  -->  00:02:56,580
we had to say set something equals something and all that.

00:02:56,860  -->  00:03:00,140
For now we can just give it the IP straight away.

38

00:03:00,720  -->  00:03:07,020
So it's asking for the 'LHOST', which is my local IP and that's '10.20.14.213'

39

00:03:09,060  -->  00:03:13,160
and then it's asking me for the Port to
be used for the reverse connection

40

00:03:13,500  -->  00:03:15,540
and I'm going to set that to 8080

41

00:03:17,620  -->  00:03:27,560
and then it's asking me what do I want to call the file and I'm just gonna call it 'rev_https_8080_'

42

00:03:28,040  -->  00:03:33,340
and I'm gonna put 'fr' at the end for FatRat so we know that this was generated using TheFatRat.

43

00:03:34,300  -->  00:03:35,580
So I'm gonna hit 'Enter'

44

00:03:36,580  -->  00:03:40,520
and then it's gonna ask me what type of Payload do I want to use.

45

00:03:41,060  -->  00:03:46,660
So if you look at these, you'll see that they're actually kind of similar to the Payloads in Veil-Evasion.

46

00:03:47,120  -->  00:03:50,480
Especially, the last parts, so you can see that we have 'Meterpreter payloads'

47

00:03:51,000  -->  00:03:58,120
and you can see that we have a 'reverse_tcp' and we have a 'reverse_http' and if we scroll down,

48

00:03:58,120  -->  00:04:04,760
we have the 'meterpreterreverse_https', which is the same payload that we used with Veil-Evasion.

49

00:04:06,200  -->  00:04:11,320
So again, essentially, we're generating the exact same payload which is gonna give us

50

00:04:11,320  -->  00:04:13,580
the exact same access to the computer.

51

00:04:14,000  -->  00:04:17,840
The only difference is the way that this payload is going to be delivered

52

00:04:17,840  -->  00:04:20,360
and executed on the target computer.

53

00:04:22,300  -->  00:04:26,300
So I'm gonna click on 'OK' and again just like Veil-Evasion,

54

00:04:26,300  -->  00:04:31,060
TheFatRat will actually use 'Metasploit' framework to generate the payload.

55

00:04:33,740  -->  00:04:41,200
And now it's telling me that the payload is saved to the output folder and that's it it's telling me do I want to 'Exit'

56

00:04:41,200  -->  00:04:43,280
and I'm gonna say 'Yes' I'm done with you thank you.

Now what I wanna do is I wanna just check

00:05:01,680  -->  00:05:03,060
I'm gonna upload a file

62

00:05:04,800  -->  00:05:11,600
and the file is going to be stored in the output of TheFatRat.

63

00:05:11,600  -->  00:05:18,880

64

00:05:18,940  -->  00:05:23,880

65

00:05:24,280  -->  00:05:28,740
and it start in the same location, where we create-- Where we installed TheFatRat.

66

00:05:28,960  -->  00:05:34,560

67

and you can see that we have all the files of TheFatRat right here.

68

00:05:38,260  -->  00:05:44,160
Now if we go to 'output', there should be a file called 'output' and that's it right here.

69

00:05:44,980  -->  00:05:52,420
And in it, we have our backdoor, which is called the 'rev_https_8080_fr.bat'

70

00:05:53,520  -->  00:05:57,260
