71

00:05:59,340  -->  00:06:06,120
and as you can see the scan is done and it's only being detected by one antivirus program.

72

00:06:06,760  -->  00:06:12,700
So it's bypassing actually, pretty much all the famous antivirus programs, it's bypassing McAfee,

73

00:06:12,960  -->  00:06:21,400
it's bypassing Kaspersky, it's bypassing AVG, it's bypassing Norton right here.

74

00:06:21,760  -->  00:06:27,780
It's pretty much bypassing everything, the only one that's detecting it, is IKARUS security.

75

00:06:29,120  -->  00:06:33,520
So out of all the antivirus programs, we'll be able to bypass them all,

76

00:06:33,520  -->  00:06:37,820
except for this particular antivirus program, which is a really, really good result.

77

00:06:39,200  -->  00:06:45,920
So I'm gonna go back and we're gonna test the backdoor exactly the same way we tested our Veil backdoor.

78

00:06:46,280  -->  00:06:51,080
So the first thing we're gonna do is we're gonna listen for incoming connections using 'msfconsole'

79

00:06:52,540  -->  00:06:56,980
and we're gonna use the multi handler exactly the same way that we used it before.

80

00:06:57,560  -->  00:07:01,880
Keep in mind that doesn't matter how we created the payload both of these payloads use

81

00:07:01,880  -->  00:07:04,520
a 'Meterpreter_reverse_http' connection.

82

00:07:05,020  -->  00:07:10,140
Therefore, when we want to listen for incoming connections, we're gonna use the same module

83

00:07:10,140  -->  00:07:11,280
and the same payload.

84

00:07:11,940  -->  00:07:15,500
So I'm gonna do this a bit quickly because we have done it and explained it before.

85

00:07:15,920  -->  00:07:19,100
So the first thing I'm gonna do is 'use exploit/multi/handler'.

86

00:07:21,280  -->  00:07:22,880
Then I'm gonna set the payload

87

00:07:23,880  -->  00:07:29,340
and I have to set this exactly to the same payload that I created that I want to receive connections from.

88

00:07:29,820  -->  00:07:33,200
And we created a 'meterpreter_reverse_http payload'.

89

00:07:33,600  -->  00:07:41,680
So that's what we're gonna do to 'windows/meterpreter/reverse_https'

90

00:07:43,520  -->  00:07:47,300
and then we're gonna set the 'LHOST' to the IP address of my machine.

91

00:07:47,300  -->  00:07:50,880
Again, the same 'LHOST' that we set when we created the backdoor.

92

00:07:52,360  -->  00:07:57,240
And we're gonna set the 'LPORT' to the part that we want to receive the connection from

93

00:07:57,240  -->  00:08:02,060
and again the same 'LPORT' that we used when we created the backdoor, so it's 8080.

94

00:08:03,280  -->  00:08:08,600
And now all is done, I'm just gonna do 'show options' and just to make sure everything is okay

95

00:08:08,600  -->  00:08:12,060
and we can see that we were using a 'windows/meterpreter/reverse_https'.

96

00:08:12,780  -->  00:08:17,600
We have the right IP and we have the right port, so everything is perfect.

97

00:08:17,940  -->  00:08:21,400
Now I'm actually just gonna run one command before I run this exploit.

98

00:08:21,400  -->  00:08:27,320
I'm gonna do 'save' and what this will do is it's gonna save all this config automatically,

99

00:08:27,320  -->  00:08:33,660
so that the next time, I run 'msfconsole', I'll have the multi headler, Handler loaded already

100

00:08:33,820  -->  00:08:38,960
and it will have all these options pre configured automatically so I won't have to do it again.

101

00:08:39,940  -->  00:08:48,660
and now I'm just gonna do 'exploit' to run this module and wait for incoming connections on 8080.

102

00:08:48,960  -->  00:08:53,520
The last step to do now is to just run the backdoor on my Windows machine

103

00:08:53,980  -->  00:08:57,220
and I'm gonna deliver it using our very basic delivery method

104

00:08:58,000  -->  00:09:04,320
and that's basically by placing our backdoor in our Web server

105

00:09:04,680  -->  00:09:07,860
and then we're going to download it from the Windows machine.

106

00:09:08,780  -->  00:09:17,220
So my backdoor is right here, I'm gonna 'copy' it and I'm gonna click on the address bar again,

107

00:09:17,220  -->  00:09:28,580
put forward slash and then put var/www/html/' and we created a directory called 'evil-files'

108

00:09:30,160  -->  00:09:35,140
that we stored our previous backdoor in and I'm gonna paste this backdoor in it

109

00:09:37,020  -->  00:09:38,732
and that's it, we're good to go.

110

00:09:38,740  -->  00:09:43,380
So we're just going to download it from the Windows machine, like we did in the previous video.

111

00:09:43,800  -->  00:09:48,480
And before I can download it, I need to start the Apache server, the web server in Kali

112

00:09:48,660  -->  00:09:52,200
and we're gonna do that using the command service.

113

00:09:52,740  -->  00:09:54,280
Apache to start.

114

00:09:58,060  -->  00:09:59,880
So I'm gonna go to my Windows machine

115

00:10:02,040  -->  00:10:09,460
and I'm gonna navigate to my website, which is '10.20.14.213' the same IP as the Kali IP

116

00:10:09,460  -->  00:10:14,300
and we're gonna go to the directory that contains the evil files which is called 'evil-files'.

117

00:10:15,900  -->  00:10:19,300
And we can see our two backdoors, we see the one that we used before

118

00:10:19,680  -->  00:10:24,540
and we have our new backdoor here which is called 'rev_https_8080_fr',

119

00:10:25,420  -->  00:10:31,600
I'm going to click that, 'Download' it and then I'm gonna run it

120

00:10:33,280  -->  00:10:37,980
and again, Windows is just telling, telling me be careful this is an executable,

121

00:10:37,980  -->  00:10:42,200
so be careful when you're on files like this, it's not telling me that it's a virus

122

00:10:42,580  -->  00:10:47,040
and it's not telling me that it contains malware, so I'm gonna run it anyway.

123

00:10:50,600  -->  00:10:52,680
And if we come back here,

125

and the connection came back again from the target computer, which is at '10.20.14. 206',

126

came back to us on Port 8080.

127

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,410 --> 00:00:04,160
In this lecture, I'm going to talk about a tool called Empire.

2
00:00:04,820 --> 00:00:10,580
Empire allows us to generate backdoors that are not detectable by antivirus programs.

3
00:00:10,790 --> 00:00:14,480
Again, very similar to veal evasion and the fat rat.

4
00:00:14,870 --> 00:00:20,540
The reason why I'm showing you so many tools to do the same thing is all of these tools achieve the

5
00:00:20,540 --> 00:00:21,170
same goal.

6
00:00:21,170 --> 00:00:27,990
Yes, they all create undetectable backdoors, but they achieve this goal in different ways.

7
00:00:28,010 --> 00:00:34,100
So the backdoors generated by them use different techniques to bypass antivirus programs.

8
00:00:35,230 --> 00:00:42,970
Therefore, if for some reason, while evasion or the fat rat or empire became less effective, then

9
00:00:42,970 --> 00:00:47,410
you know, two other tools on how to generate undetectable backdoors.

10
00:00:47,620 --> 00:00:51,070
Antivirus programs will always try to stay up to date.

11
00:00:51,070 --> 00:00:55,150
And the three of these tools will also always try to stay up to date.

12
00:00:55,150 --> 00:01:00,040
But sometimes at certain times some of these tools will be less effective than others.

13
00:01:00,040 --> 00:01:06,490
That's why it's better to know all of these tools and have the option to pick which one that works better

14
00:01:06,490 --> 00:01:07,480
in your case.

15
00:01:08,130 --> 00:01:15,090
Unlike vile evasion and the fat rat empire does not use Metasploit to generate its backdoors.

16
00:01:15,120 --> 00:01:19,710
It actually generates backdoors that work using PowerShell.

17
00:01:19,710 --> 00:01:26,520
And I said PowerShell is a framework made by Microsoft that basically expands the basic command prompt

18
00:01:26,520 --> 00:01:31,860
that's used in Microsoft in Windows, and it comes in pre-installed from Windows 7 and up.

19
00:01:32,790 --> 00:01:39,810
Empire also generates backdoors that bypass antivirus programs and use secure communications.

20
00:01:39,810 --> 00:01:45,660
They actually use as encryption, which is known to be a very powerful encryption method.

21
00:01:45,720 --> 00:01:53,850
It also uses an encryption exchange protocol called encryption Key exchange, which basically results

22
00:01:53,850 --> 00:02:00,360
in backdoors that bypass antivirus protections and use secure and private communications.

23
00:02:00,990 --> 00:02:06,660
So before we start talking about how to use this tool and how this tool works, let's first download

24
00:02:06,660 --> 00:02:09,930
and install it just like any other package.

25
00:02:09,930 --> 00:02:13,170
We're going to have to first update the sources by running APT.

26
00:02:13,170 --> 00:02:14,250
Get update.

27
00:02:16,650 --> 00:02:23,460
And then we're going to install the package by saying apt, get install the package name, which in

28
00:02:23,460 --> 00:02:26,100
our case is PowerShell Empire.

29
00:02:28,310 --> 00:02:34,760
So very simple command apt get to use the package manager installed to install a package called PowerShell

30
00:02:34,760 --> 00:02:35,510
Empire.

31
00:02:35,540 --> 00:02:37,010
We're going to hit enter.

32
00:02:37,010 --> 00:02:40,670
It might ask you if you want to do this, you're going to have to hit enter again.

33
00:02:40,670 --> 00:02:45,440
But since I've already installed it, it's already installing and configuring everything for me.

34
00:02:45,440 --> 00:02:50,210
And now that everything is done, we can clear the screen and start the tool.

35
00:02:50,600 --> 00:02:54,230
Empire comes in two parts the server and the client.

36
00:02:54,260 --> 00:03:00,800
The server is what pretty much handles the back end and serving the back doors or the stagers as what

37
00:03:00,800 --> 00:03:01,850
Empire calls them.

38
00:03:01,850 --> 00:03:08,180
And the client is what we actually use to run commands and interact with the computers that we have

39
00:03:08,180 --> 00:03:08,870
hacked.

40
00:03:08,870 --> 00:03:15,410
So we're going to first of all, run the server by typing PowerShell empire, followed by the word server.

41
00:03:15,620 --> 00:03:21,080
So PowerShell Empire is the name of the program that we want to run and we want to run the server.

42
00:03:21,290 --> 00:03:24,440
We're going to hit Enter and let that run.

43
00:03:26,180 --> 00:03:26,900
And perfect.

44
00:03:26,900 --> 00:03:32,570
As you can see, everything is running normally and at the bottom is showing us the number of agents.

45
00:03:32,570 --> 00:03:37,060
So that's the number of connections that we have, the number of computers that we hacked.

46
00:03:37,070 --> 00:03:42,350
You'll see that later on once we actually get to use this tool to hack into other computers, you can

47
00:03:42,350 --> 00:03:43,970
see the number of listeners.

48
00:03:43,970 --> 00:03:47,450
So right now we have zero listeners because we haven't started any.

49
00:03:47,480 --> 00:03:52,670
You can think of listeners like the multi handler that we used in Metasploit.

50
00:03:52,670 --> 00:03:56,180
It basically is a process that listens for incoming connections.

51
00:03:56,180 --> 00:03:59,960
So obviously it's set to zero at this stage because we haven't done anything.

52
00:03:59,960 --> 00:04:06,170
And like I said, the server, what we're running right now in here is the back end side of Empire.

53
00:04:06,170 --> 00:04:08,840
So we're not actually going to be interacting with it much.

54
00:04:08,840 --> 00:04:14,300
That's why I'm going to put it all the way up in here and then we're actually going to run the client,

55
00:04:14,300 --> 00:04:20,840
which is the part that we interact with from Empire to generate our backdoors and even communicate with

56
00:04:20,840 --> 00:04:22,520
the computers that we hacked.

57
00:04:22,730 --> 00:04:24,260
So to run the client.

58
00:04:24,260 --> 00:04:29,430
So we're simply going to type PowerShell empire again, So similar to the previous command, but this

59
00:04:29,430 --> 00:04:33,360
time we're going to say we want to run the client, we're going to hit Enter.

60
00:04:33,360 --> 00:04:38,550
And this client right here is actually going to connect to the running server and then we'll be able

61
00:04:38,550 --> 00:04:42,450
to use the functionality in the server from the client in here.

62
00:04:42,960 --> 00:04:45,930
So as you can see, the client is running with no issues.

63
00:04:45,930 --> 00:04:50,940
And right now we can actually go ahead and start generating our backdoors and listeners.

64
00:04:51,570 --> 00:04:56,820
So before we actually generate a backdoor, let's go ahead and generate a listener.

65
00:04:56,850 --> 00:05:01,950
Like I said, you can think of a listener like the multi handler that we use with Metasploit, which

66
00:05:01,950 --> 00:05:07,110
is basically a process that is simply listening for incoming connections from the backdoors that we

67
00:05:07,140 --> 00:05:08,760
send to our targets.

68
00:05:09,180 --> 00:05:14,880
So as usual, you can always just type help to see all of the possible commands that you can use.

69
00:05:14,880 --> 00:05:16,800
But I already know what I want to use.

70
00:05:16,800 --> 00:05:21,720
I want to create a listener and therefore we actually can see we have a command to do that, which is

71
00:05:21,720 --> 00:05:23,580
basically called use listeners.

72
00:05:23,580 --> 00:05:29,280
So I'm going to type that command and as you can see, as you type with empire, it automatically tries

73
00:05:29,280 --> 00:05:32,400
to autocorrect you or autocomplete what you're typing.

74
00:05:32,400 --> 00:05:38,580
So we're basically trying to use listener so I can use the down arrow to select it and it automatically

75
00:05:38,580 --> 00:05:39,690
fills it for me.

76
00:05:40,050 --> 00:05:45,930
Then once I press space, you can see it automatically lists all of the possible listeners that we can

77
00:05:45,930 --> 00:05:46,530
use.

78
00:05:46,560 --> 00:05:53,250
Now I want to create a Http backdoor in the future and therefore I'm going to be using a Http listener.

79
00:05:53,400 --> 00:06:00,750
So if I hit enter, as you can see, it selects the Http listener and it automatically lists all of

80
00:06:00,750 --> 00:06:03,900
the possible options that I can set for this listener.

81
00:06:04,170 --> 00:06:06,810
Now, you don't really need to modify anything.

82
00:06:06,810 --> 00:06:11,910
Most of these come pre configured well, as you can see it automatically got my correct IP.

83
00:06:12,120 --> 00:06:18,840
So if I just split the screen real quick for you and type ifconfig eth0, you can see that this is my

84
00:06:18,870 --> 00:06:19,230
IP.

85
00:06:19,500 --> 00:06:23,370
I know it's slightly different than the previous lectures, but that doesn't really matter.

86
00:06:23,370 --> 00:06:27,000
The IP can change, so you might have a different IP yourself.

87
00:06:27,000 --> 00:06:31,290
Just make sure you use the right value when you're generating the listener and the backdoor.

88
00:06:31,470 --> 00:06:36,480
And the main point in here is that everything is set up correctly except for the port.

89
00:06:36,480 --> 00:06:38,880
So you can see that the port is empty.

90
00:06:38,910 --> 00:06:42,390
We haven't set anything for it, but it is a required field.

91
00:06:42,390 --> 00:06:44,940
So true in here means that it is required.

92
00:06:45,090 --> 00:06:49,770
So therefore we have to set the port which the listener is going to listen on.

93
00:06:49,770 --> 00:06:53,580
So that's the port that our backdoors are going to try to connect to.

94
00:06:53,610 --> 00:07:00,300
Now you can use the set command exactly like we did with Metasploit before, followed by the option

95
00:07:00,300 --> 00:07:01,680
name that you want to change.

96
00:07:01,680 --> 00:07:05,760
So for example, if you want to change the user agent, you can simply type user agent.

97
00:07:05,760 --> 00:07:07,920
But in our case we want to change the port.

98
00:07:07,920 --> 00:07:12,570
So we're going to type port and we want to set that to 8080.

99
00:07:12,990 --> 00:07:17,910
So the format of changing options in here is very similar to what we're used to so far.

100
00:07:17,910 --> 00:07:24,720
You basically use set to set the option followed by the option name, followed by the option value I'm

101
00:07:24,720 --> 00:07:25,590
going to hit enter.

102
00:07:25,590 --> 00:07:28,830
And as you can see, this should have been executed correctly.

103
00:07:28,830 --> 00:07:31,110
To double check, we can do info.

104
00:07:31,380 --> 00:07:35,040
We should actually use options to double check the options, not info.

105
00:07:35,040 --> 00:07:38,700
And that will list all of the options set for this listener.

106
00:07:38,700 --> 00:07:43,530
And as you can see right now, the port is set to 8080, so that is perfect.

107
00:07:43,560 --> 00:07:49,770
Once you're happy with all of the options set for the listener, you can either type, generate or execute

108
00:07:49,770 --> 00:07:51,990
and that will create the listener.

109
00:07:52,290 --> 00:07:57,240
So as you can see right now, it's saying listener Http is successfully started.

110
00:07:57,240 --> 00:08:03,270
And if you look at the server now, you can see that we have one listener ready and is listening for

111
00:08:03,270 --> 00:08:07,650
incoming connections on Port 8080 as we set it in here.

112
00:08:07,830 --> 00:08:14,250
So you can think of this step as a very similar step to starting a multi handler with Metasploit.

113
00:08:14,280 --> 00:08:19,950
And in the next lecture we're going to generate our backdoor, send it to the target and test this listener

114
00:08:19,950 --> 00:08:25,110
and test the backdoor to see if we can remotely control the target using an Empire backdoor.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,230 --> 00:00:05,630
Now that we generated an Empire listener to listen for incoming connections from the backdoors that

2
00:00:05,630 --> 00:00:11,600
we send, let's actually go ahead and generate a backdoor and test it against a Windows computer.

3
00:00:11,810 --> 00:00:18,710
So I'm here at the client terminal and we're going to do back to go back to the original menu outside

4
00:00:18,710 --> 00:00:19,970
of the listeners menu.

5
00:00:19,970 --> 00:00:23,510
And you can do help as usual to see what you can do.

6
00:00:23,510 --> 00:00:29,540
But we're looking to generate a backdoor or a stager as referred to by Empire.

7
00:00:29,930 --> 00:00:34,540
So to do that, we're going to use the use stager command from Empire.

8
00:00:34,550 --> 00:00:38,840
So we're simply going to type use S and it'll autocomplete for us.

9
00:00:38,840 --> 00:00:45,440
So all I have to do is press the down arrow or tab to autocomplete and then once I press space again,

10
00:00:45,440 --> 00:00:51,230
as you can see, we have all of the options that we can use with stagers or with backdoors.

11
00:00:51,380 --> 00:00:57,380
And what we actually want to use is a Windows launcher and that's going to be in a bad format.

12
00:00:57,380 --> 00:01:02,100
So it's going to be file name dot, but we're going to hit enter to select that.

13
00:01:02,100 --> 00:01:07,530
And as you can see now, Empire is set to use stager Windows launcher.

14
00:01:07,530 --> 00:01:13,740
But again, similar to the listener, it automatically lists all of the options that you can set for

15
00:01:13,740 --> 00:01:14,730
this stager.

16
00:01:14,730 --> 00:01:19,490
And similar to the listener, you actually don't need to modify much in here.

17
00:01:19,500 --> 00:01:25,080
Now, modifying these could be good to make the backdoor or the stager more unique, which will help

18
00:01:25,080 --> 00:01:29,040
you bypass more antivirus programs so the same concepts apply.

19
00:01:29,070 --> 00:01:30,960
He can see the file name in here.

20
00:01:30,960 --> 00:01:34,740
It's called launcher dot bat, so you can modify that if you want to.

21
00:01:34,740 --> 00:01:39,420
But the main thing that we actually have to set is the listener value in here.

22
00:01:39,420 --> 00:01:41,130
As you can see, it's required.

23
00:01:41,130 --> 00:01:42,660
It's not set to anything.

24
00:01:42,660 --> 00:01:47,070
And if you remember from the previous example, our listener was called Http.

25
00:01:47,280 --> 00:01:52,890
Therefore we're going to set the value of this option to Http to the name of our listener.

26
00:01:53,010 --> 00:01:56,490
And setting the options is very similar to what we're used to.

27
00:01:56,520 --> 00:02:02,160
We use the set command followed by the option name, which is listener in our case, followed by the

28
00:02:02,160 --> 00:02:03,840
value which is Http.

29
00:02:03,840 --> 00:02:08,340
In this case we're going to hit enter and if we run options again.

30
00:02:09,230 --> 00:02:12,000
You'll see that the listener is set to Http.

31
00:02:12,020 --> 00:02:18,710
Now I'm also going to change the output file name to something that is more meaningful instead of launcher

32
00:02:18,710 --> 00:02:19,490
dot pat.

33
00:02:20,590 --> 00:02:26,110
So again, I'm using the set command followed by the option, which is Outfile, followed by the name

34
00:02:26,110 --> 00:02:27,400
that I want to set it to.

35
00:02:27,400 --> 00:02:36,610
And we're just going to call it Empire http 8080 dot, but I'm going to hit enter and run options one

36
00:02:36,610 --> 00:02:40,060
more time to make sure that everything is configured as I want.

37
00:02:40,090 --> 00:02:44,620
I have the right name in here and we have the right listener name in here.

38
00:02:44,620 --> 00:02:45,880
So ready to go.

39
00:02:45,880 --> 00:02:52,060
And again, similar to the listener, you can do, execute or generate to actually generate this backdoor

40
00:02:52,060 --> 00:02:55,780
or stager we're going to hit enter and perfect.

41
00:02:55,780 --> 00:02:59,770
As you can see, it's telling us that it is stored in the following location.

42
00:02:59,770 --> 00:03:05,650
So you can simply copy this and paste it in your file manager to get there.

43
00:03:05,650 --> 00:03:12,730
But I actually want to copy that to my evil files directory, so I'm going to do CP to copy and I want

44
00:03:12,730 --> 00:03:19,750
to copy this file to var WW HTML evil files.

45
00:03:19,750 --> 00:03:22,730
So CP command is to copy files.

46
00:03:22,730 --> 00:03:28,190
We're giving it the location of the source followed by the location of the destination.

47
00:03:28,430 --> 00:03:33,170
So we're going to hit enter and that should be copied now to my evil files directory.

48
00:03:33,170 --> 00:03:39,800
And finally, I'm going to run my Apache web server so I can actually go ahead and download this stager

49
00:03:39,800 --> 00:03:46,760
from the Target Windows machine So we can do that by doing service Apache to start similar to what we

50
00:03:46,760 --> 00:03:48,170
have been doing so far.

51
00:03:48,170 --> 00:03:50,480
Now the Apache web server is running.

52
00:03:50,480 --> 00:03:56,930
So if we go to the Windows machine and simply just refresh this, as you can see, I'm getting the home

53
00:03:56,930 --> 00:03:59,720
page, which basically just says it works.

54
00:03:59,720 --> 00:04:10,160
And if I go to the evil files directory, we can see we have our file empire http 80 and if I click

55
00:04:10,160 --> 00:04:12,260
on it, it will download it for me.

56
00:04:12,260 --> 00:04:16,490
Now keep in mind that this is not the smartest way of delivering backdoors.

57
00:04:16,490 --> 00:04:20,480
We will talk about delivery methods in the delivery methods section.

58
00:04:20,480 --> 00:04:24,350
It's always best to break down bigger problems into simpler ones.

59
00:04:24,350 --> 00:04:30,320
So right now the goal is to generate a backdoor and then we will talk about smart ways of delivering

60
00:04:30,320 --> 00:04:32,660
it later on when we get to that section.

61
00:04:32,660 --> 00:04:38,240
So right now we just want to generate a backdoor that actually works and bypasses as many antivirus

62
00:04:38,240 --> 00:04:39,680
programs as possible.

63
00:04:39,860 --> 00:04:43,700
Empire backdoors usually bypass antivirus programs very well.

64
00:04:43,700 --> 00:04:48,440
If Empire has recently been updated and Empire has been recently updated.

65
00:04:48,440 --> 00:04:54,920
But if it wasn't, if your backdoor is being detected by antivirus programs for now, make sure you

66
00:04:54,920 --> 00:05:00,560
turn off Windows Defender by going to the start menu, look for Windows Defender.

67
00:05:01,820 --> 00:05:02,870
Click on it.

68
00:05:03,860 --> 00:05:06,320
Click on virus and threat protection.

69
00:05:06,680 --> 00:05:09,140
Scroll down to the Threat protection.

70
00:05:09,140 --> 00:05:13,040
Click on Manage Settings and make sure you set it to off.

71
00:05:13,400 --> 00:05:19,340
We're doing this because in the next lectures I'm actually going to show you how to enhance empire stagers

72
00:05:19,340 --> 00:05:21,990
and make them bypass more antivirus programs.

73
00:05:22,010 --> 00:05:28,220
Therefore, just like I said earlier, it's always best to fix one problem at a time or tackle one challenge

74
00:05:28,220 --> 00:05:28,970
at a time.

75
00:05:28,970 --> 00:05:35,360
And at the moment we're trying to keep things as simple as possible and just testing an unmodified version

76
00:05:35,360 --> 00:05:37,490
of the Empire Stager.

77
00:05:37,490 --> 00:05:42,860
And then later on we're going to be modifying it to make it bypass more antivirus programs.

78
00:05:43,010 --> 00:05:49,250
So once you have this done, we're going to close this, close this come here and we're going to click

79
00:05:49,250 --> 00:05:51,050
on the back door to download it.

80
00:05:51,080 --> 00:05:52,750
We're going to save the file.

81
00:05:52,760 --> 00:05:57,410
It's going to go into our downloads and then we're going to click on it to run it.

82
00:05:57,440 --> 00:06:02,600
You're going to get a notification from Windows telling you that this file is an executable.

83
00:06:02,600 --> 00:06:03,570
That's normal.

84
00:06:03,570 --> 00:06:08,490
You get this with any executable that is not made by a big company like Microsoft.

85
00:06:08,490 --> 00:06:11,880
So we're going to say that's fine and we're going to run it.

86
00:06:12,090 --> 00:06:17,820
And now that the executable got executed, let's go back to our client terminal.

87
00:06:17,820 --> 00:06:20,310
And as you can see, there's already a few things happening.

88
00:06:20,310 --> 00:06:22,950
You can see that it's saying there is a new agent.

89
00:06:22,950 --> 00:06:27,450
And if we look in here in the server, you can see the number of agents is one.

90
00:06:27,450 --> 00:06:34,110
And basically what that means is we managed to get a reverse connection from a Target computer.

91
00:06:34,260 --> 00:06:40,440
Now, to see all of the possible agents, we can simply type agents in our client and that's going to

92
00:06:40,440 --> 00:06:43,290
list all of the connections that we have at the moment.

93
00:06:43,320 --> 00:06:46,130
So you can see this is the new agent that we got.

94
00:06:46,140 --> 00:06:47,310
You can see it's typed.

95
00:06:47,310 --> 00:06:52,980
The language is PowerShell, you can see the IP, you can see the username on that computer.

96
00:06:52,980 --> 00:06:56,880
It's the MSA computer with the username, i.e. user.

97
00:06:56,880 --> 00:07:01,410
And at the end you can see the listener type as well, which is the Http listener.

98
00:07:01,800 --> 00:07:07,590
Now to interact with this agent, with Metasploit, we used to do sessions I for interact.

99
00:07:07,590 --> 00:07:12,090
In this case we just have to do interact followed by the agent name.

100
00:07:12,090 --> 00:07:15,900
And as you can see, it's automatically suggesting the agent that we have.

101
00:07:15,930 --> 00:07:23,490
So we're simply going to type nine and tap to select it, hit enter, and now we're inside that agent.

102
00:07:23,490 --> 00:07:29,190
So now we can remotely control this Windows computer from within this terminal.

103
00:07:29,280 --> 00:07:35,280
We will cover everything that we can do from this access in the post exploitation section.

104
00:07:35,280 --> 00:07:40,140
But for now, just to verify that we actually have access to that computer and that we can remotely

105
00:07:40,140 --> 00:07:45,720
control it, I'm simply going to run the info command to get information about that computer.

106
00:07:45,720 --> 00:07:50,580
And as you can see, again, you get the computer name, the username, all that kind of stuff.

107
00:07:50,610 --> 00:07:54,330
You can do help to see all of the possible commands that you can run.

108
00:07:54,330 --> 00:07:56,760
But like I said, don't worry about it for now.

109
00:07:56,760 --> 00:08:01,710
We will talk about it in details later on in the post exploitation section.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,590 --> 00:00:01,100
Okay.

2
00:00:01,100 --> 00:00:09,110
So so far we've seen how we can bypass pretty much all antivirus programs using Empire and the fat rat.

3
00:00:09,140 --> 00:00:16,550
The only problem is we were always getting detected by 1 or 2 antivirus programs, so the result wasn't

4
00:00:16,550 --> 00:00:17,330
perfect.

5
00:00:17,510 --> 00:00:23,900
Now, the cool thing about the bat backdoor is generated with Empire and the fat rat is that the code

6
00:00:23,900 --> 00:00:26,330
in them is Dos and PowerShell code.

7
00:00:26,330 --> 00:00:32,810
So we can literally just open them using a normal text editor and we'll be able to modify that code

8
00:00:32,810 --> 00:00:40,550
so that the backdoors are a bit more unique than the, than the default backdoors that get generated

9
00:00:40,550 --> 00:00:41,780
using these programs.

10
00:00:41,780 --> 00:00:47,960
And then hopefully their signature will be different than the other backdoors that are generated using

11
00:00:47,960 --> 00:00:49,340
Empire and the fat rat.

12
00:00:49,340 --> 00:00:53,810
And therefore we should be able to bypass more antivirus programs.

13
00:00:54,110 --> 00:01:02,170
Now we can't open the backdoor generated with Veil using a text editor because as veil generates exe

14
00:01:02,250 --> 00:01:06,540
backdoors and if we open them using a text editor, we'll just get gibberish.

15
00:01:07,290 --> 00:01:13,290
So what I'm going to do now is I'm just going to go to where I stored my backdoor, which is I'm just

16
00:01:13,290 --> 00:01:19,680
going to click on the navigation bar and go to var ww w HTML.

17
00:01:20,650 --> 00:01:22,000
Evil files.

18
00:01:24,480 --> 00:01:27,930
And we have our empire and our patriot back.

19
00:01:28,950 --> 00:01:32,910
Now I'm going to do this example on Empire, so I'm going to right click it.

20
00:01:34,060 --> 00:01:36,250
And open it with text editor.

21
00:01:36,250 --> 00:01:43,510
As you can see, if you don't see this, then just go on open with other application and then open it

22
00:01:43,510 --> 00:01:44,410
with leafpad.

23
00:01:46,300 --> 00:01:49,480
And as you can see, we have code that's actually readable.

24
00:01:49,480 --> 00:01:53,500
And the code in here is literally Windows commands.

25
00:01:53,500 --> 00:01:59,710
And then in here we have a PowerShell command which is going to run our shell code.

26
00:01:59,800 --> 00:02:05,200
So this is the shell code and as you can see, it's actually encrypted and this is the part of the code

27
00:02:05,200 --> 00:02:11,770
that will give us a reverse connection and it will allow us to gain access or hack the target computer.

28
00:02:13,050 --> 00:02:14,850
So I'm not a genius.

29
00:02:14,880 --> 00:02:21,870
What I did is I came to this file and the first thing I did is I removed everything from the file,

30
00:02:21,870 --> 00:02:26,430
and I literally only kept this this particular line, the shell code.

31
00:02:26,460 --> 00:02:28,590
So I removed all of this.

32
00:02:30,070 --> 00:02:31,720
And I removed all of this.

33
00:02:33,230 --> 00:02:38,700
And then I uploaded this to not distribute just to see if the shellcode is getting detected.

34
00:02:38,720 --> 00:02:44,600
If the shellcode is getting detected, then you need to play around with the parameters, with the ports,

35
00:02:44,600 --> 00:02:47,180
with the stager that you're using.

36
00:02:47,180 --> 00:02:50,060
So until you reach a shellcode that's not detected.

37
00:02:50,060 --> 00:02:55,850
So you can't really just modify the shellcode here from the text editor because as you can see, it's

38
00:02:55,850 --> 00:03:02,030
already encrypted and decrypting it and then modifying the parameters will probably just break the whole

39
00:03:02,030 --> 00:03:02,750
backdoor.

40
00:03:02,750 --> 00:03:04,790
So I removed everything.

41
00:03:04,790 --> 00:03:08,420
I tested it in no distribute, I just uploaded the file as is.

42
00:03:08,420 --> 00:03:13,880
I know this file is not going to work like this, but I uploaded it like this to no distribute and I

43
00:03:13,910 --> 00:03:17,690
bypassed all antiviruses so I was like, okay, this is cool.

44
00:03:17,720 --> 00:03:22,280
My shellcode is not getting detected, so I undo everything like I just did now.

45
00:03:22,280 --> 00:03:26,600
And then I started playing around with the parameters one by one.

46
00:03:26,600 --> 00:03:30,410
So after I did that, I just literally removed this whole line.

47
00:03:30,830 --> 00:03:36,570
And then I tested with no distribute again, I saved the file, upload it to not distribute.

48
00:03:36,570 --> 00:03:38,100
I bypassed everything.

49
00:03:38,100 --> 00:03:40,440
So I was like, okay, this is pretty cool.

50
00:03:40,770 --> 00:03:41,670
Undo.

51
00:03:41,670 --> 00:03:46,590
And I kept these two and I removed the rest again.

52
00:03:46,590 --> 00:03:51,330
Like sometimes literally just the word of PowerShell triggers some antivirus programs.

53
00:03:51,330 --> 00:03:58,800
So what I was trying to do is I wanted to isolate the part of the code that's triggering antivirus programs.

54
00:03:59,310 --> 00:04:01,110
So again, I deleted this.

55
00:04:01,110 --> 00:04:02,610
I tested it again.

56
00:04:02,610 --> 00:04:04,650
I still bypassed everything.

57
00:04:04,680 --> 00:04:13,020
So again, I added what I had and I kept doing this until I literally reached this point where I just

58
00:04:13,020 --> 00:04:18,090
put my PowerShell like this and I upload it and it still works.

59
00:04:18,090 --> 00:04:20,550
It still bypasses all antivirus programs.

60
00:04:20,550 --> 00:04:26,790
And I was like, okay, so the only difference here is the forward slash B, so this is just an argument

61
00:04:26,790 --> 00:04:27,750
for the command.

62
00:04:27,870 --> 00:04:33,630
I delete that, upload it to node distribute and I bypassed everything.

63
00:04:33,630 --> 00:04:34,340
Again.

64
00:04:34,340 --> 00:04:40,010
So literally if you actually upload this code right here to not distribute, it's going to bypass everything.

65
00:04:40,010 --> 00:04:46,070
And if you add the forward slash B to it, it's going to get detected by two antivirus programs like

66
00:04:46,070 --> 00:04:47,030
we've seen before.

67
00:04:47,720 --> 00:04:53,570
So what I did is I actually kept this and I just added another argument just to mix it up and make the

68
00:04:53,570 --> 00:04:54,530
file look different.

69
00:04:54,530 --> 00:05:01,010
So I added forward slash min, which basically tells Windows to minimize the pop up window so it not

70
00:05:01,010 --> 00:05:02,930
to even show it on screen.

71
00:05:03,050 --> 00:05:08,870
The point of this beer here actually is to prevent the window from showing on screen as well.

72
00:05:08,870 --> 00:05:12,980
So you can literally just remove this B or add another argument here.

73
00:05:13,340 --> 00:05:17,330
And what this is going to do, it's going to change the source code of the file.

74
00:05:17,330 --> 00:05:22,490
It's going to change the signature of the file, and it's actually going to bypass all antivirus programs

75
00:05:22,490 --> 00:05:27,290
by literally just adding one argument that doesn't really affect the whole code.

76
00:05:28,170 --> 00:05:34,140
So let's upload this file now again, to not distribute just to prove to you that it's actually bypassing

77
00:05:34,140 --> 00:05:34,890
everything.

78
00:05:36,280 --> 00:05:37,840
And this is my file right here.

79
00:05:37,840 --> 00:05:41,500
As you can see, it just got modified and I'm going to open it.

80
00:05:42,840 --> 00:05:44,130
And scan it.

81
00:05:49,180 --> 00:05:56,020
And as you can see, it's getting detected by zero antivirus programs and it's bypassing everything.

82
00:05:58,630 --> 00:06:04,570
And again, we literally did that by only adding one argument to the command, which is forward slash

83
00:06:04,570 --> 00:06:05,050
min.

84
00:06:06,330 --> 00:06:12,450
Now when you're testing, depending on the time that you're testing, your default backdoor might actually

85
00:06:12,450 --> 00:06:13,320
bypass everything.

86
00:06:13,320 --> 00:06:19,410
So maybe the backdoor that you're creating with the latest version of Empire just literally bypasses

87
00:06:19,410 --> 00:06:20,130
everything.

88
00:06:20,340 --> 00:06:28,440
Now, the main point to take from this is if your backdoor is getting detected by 3 or 2 antivirus programs,

89
00:06:28,440 --> 00:06:31,650
then open it and play around with the arguments.

90
00:06:31,650 --> 00:06:37,500
But make sure the first thing you do is you test, You remove everything and test if this shellcode

91
00:06:37,500 --> 00:06:38,580
is getting detected.

92
00:06:38,610 --> 00:06:42,840
If the shellcode is not getting detected, then play around with these arguments.

93
00:06:42,840 --> 00:06:49,050
Play around with by adding arguments, removing arguments and literally just remove them all and add

94
00:06:49,050 --> 00:06:49,890
them one by one.

95
00:06:49,890 --> 00:06:52,290
Like I said, like the method I followed.

96
00:06:52,320 --> 00:06:56,490
And each time you add something, upload it to not distribute and see.

97
00:06:57,200 --> 00:07:03,440
By doing that, you'll be able to isolate which part of the code is triggering the antivirus program,

98
00:07:03,440 --> 00:07:10,850
and then you'll be able to remove it or modify it or manipulate it in a way so that it produces a different

99
00:07:10,850 --> 00:07:14,060
signature and therefore bypass antivirus programs.

100
00:07:14,480 --> 00:07:19,430
Now, let's test this backdoor because we actually modified its code and we're not sure if this is going

101
00:07:19,430 --> 00:07:20,150
to work now.

102
00:07:20,150 --> 00:07:22,250
So I'm going to go to my Windows machine.

103
00:07:23,040 --> 00:07:25,860
And I'm just going to download my file right here.

104
00:07:28,000 --> 00:07:29,380
And I'm going to save it.

105
00:07:30,730 --> 00:07:32,470
And then I'm going to open it.

106
00:07:40,850 --> 00:07:43,200
And as you can see, we got our agent.

107
00:07:43,220 --> 00:07:44,930
So if we go to agents.

108
00:07:45,840 --> 00:07:50,310
And as you can see, we have a connection so we can interact with it.

109
00:07:51,980 --> 00:07:53,750
Just put CP and tab.

110
00:07:53,780 --> 00:07:55,680
Hit enter and we're inside.

111
00:07:55,700 --> 00:07:59,210
We have full access, full control over the Target computer.

112
00:07:59,210 --> 00:08:02,180
And we bypassed all antivirus programs.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================

# Section 9: Windows Malware - Spying

1
00:00:00,110 --> 00:00:03,230
In this lecture we're going to talk about keyloggers.

2
00:00:03,650 --> 00:00:11,150
A keylogger is a program that runs in the background of the target computer, and basically records

3
00:00:11,150 --> 00:00:15,050
all keystrokes that the target person enters on their computer.

4
00:00:15,050 --> 00:00:20,060
So it doesn't matter where they use the keyboard, whether they use it to log in to a website, whether

5
00:00:20,060 --> 00:00:24,020
they use it in a chat, whether they use it to write stuff in a word document.

6
00:00:24,020 --> 00:00:30,770
This program will literally just record every keystroke that the person types on their keyboard, and

7
00:00:30,770 --> 00:00:33,800
then send it to you to your email to analyze.

8
00:00:34,790 --> 00:00:37,460
Now you might think, why would I want to do that?

9
00:00:37,490 --> 00:00:43,460
You already know how to create a backdoor, and the backdoor will allow you to gain full control over

10
00:00:43,460 --> 00:00:44,420
the target computer.

11
00:00:44,420 --> 00:00:49,730
So you'll be able to record all the key strikes, and you'll also be able to do much more, because

12
00:00:49,730 --> 00:00:53,960
the backdoor basically allows you to do whatever the target person can do.

13
00:00:54,260 --> 00:00:59,960
Well, the reason why you might need a keylogger is, first of all, the keyloggers that come with Empire

14
00:00:59,960 --> 00:01:03,530
and with Meterpreter store the data on file.

15
00:01:03,530 --> 00:01:05,930
They store the data on the computer.

16
00:01:05,930 --> 00:01:12,830
Also, for you to be able to read this data, you'll need to come back after a while and connect to

17
00:01:12,830 --> 00:01:19,280
this computer through your backdoor, and then read the data that these keyloggers have gathered.

18
00:01:19,490 --> 00:01:24,950
Whereas when it comes to the keylogger that we're going to have a look on now, it'll actually run in

19
00:01:24,950 --> 00:01:28,490
the background and send the data to you through your email.

20
00:01:28,490 --> 00:01:30,380
So it's always going to be working there.

21
00:01:30,380 --> 00:01:33,110
And it's also always going to be sending the data to you.

22
00:01:33,110 --> 00:01:35,840
So you won't have to connect back to the target computer.

23
00:01:35,840 --> 00:01:41,780
There won't be any connections between you and the target computer, so it's less likely to be detected.

24
00:01:42,560 --> 00:01:49,040
Another reason why you might use a keylogger is you might not be interested into hacking the target's

25
00:01:49,040 --> 00:01:49,850
computer.

26
00:01:50,630 --> 00:01:57,110
So your target could be a website that the target person uses, or a Facebook account or a Twitter account.

27
00:01:57,110 --> 00:02:00,650
So the target computer is really not useful for you.

28
00:02:00,650 --> 00:02:07,070
So there is no point of sending them a backdoor and having connections coming back from that computer

29
00:02:07,070 --> 00:02:13,070
to your computer and have all that data getting transferred between you and the target computer and

30
00:02:13,070 --> 00:02:13,310
you.

31
00:02:13,310 --> 00:02:16,340
Really, there's really nothing useful in that computer.

32
00:02:16,460 --> 00:02:19,460
So a smarter thing to do is to send them a backdoor.

33
00:02:19,490 --> 00:02:24,740
The backdoor is going to run in the background and send you the data or the stuff that the person types

34
00:02:24,740 --> 00:02:31,130
to your email, and then you can analyze them and log in as the account owner, whether it's a server,

35
00:02:31,130 --> 00:02:36,470
whether it's a Facebook or any other type of account, it will literally record everything the target

36
00:02:36,470 --> 00:02:38,270
person types on their keyboard.

37
00:02:38,900 --> 00:02:46,100
Now, since this is not a programming course, we're going to use a program called Z logger to generate

38
00:02:46,100 --> 00:02:47,810
the key loggers for us.

39
00:02:47,810 --> 00:02:50,960
I've actually made this program myself.

40
00:02:50,960 --> 00:02:57,260
If you want to learn how to program your own key logger, then have a look on my Python course.

41
00:02:57,680 --> 00:03:04,550
In that course, you'll learn Python programming from scratch by writing hacking programs, and one

42
00:03:04,550 --> 00:03:10,340
of the programs that you'll actually build in that course is Z logger.

43
00:03:11,200 --> 00:03:13,750
Now the last lecture of this course.

44
00:03:13,750 --> 00:03:20,140
The bonus lecture contains all of the other courses taught by me and a comparison between them.

45
00:03:20,140 --> 00:03:23,350
So you can find the Python course in their.

46
00:03:24,280 --> 00:03:28,660
Now, in order to use the logger, we're going to have to download it first.

47
00:03:28,660 --> 00:03:33,670
And I'm going to include the link to its git repo in the resources of this lecture.

48
00:03:33,670 --> 00:03:36,250
But I have it already opened in here.

49
00:03:36,250 --> 00:03:41,680
And as usual, to download something from git, we're going to first get the clone URL.

50
00:03:41,680 --> 00:03:44,590
From here we're going to copy this.

51
00:03:44,590 --> 00:03:47,020
And then I'm going to go to my terminal.

52
00:03:47,230 --> 00:03:52,600
I'm going to go to my opt directory by doing CD forward slash opt.

53
00:03:52,720 --> 00:03:56,260
This is where we've been installing our optional programs.

54
00:03:56,260 --> 00:04:04,990
And then we're going to do git clone and give it the URL of the repo that I want to clone or download.

55
00:04:05,230 --> 00:04:07,450
So we've done this command before.

56
00:04:07,450 --> 00:04:09,550
All we're doing is we're calling git.

57
00:04:09,550 --> 00:04:12,370
We're telling it that we want to clone or download.

58
00:04:12,370 --> 00:04:16,300
And this is the URL of the repo that we want to download.

59
00:04:17,080 --> 00:04:18,580
I'm going to hit enter.

60
00:04:18,580 --> 00:04:20,830
This will download all the files.

61
00:04:21,490 --> 00:04:26,830
And now, if I do, LZ, you'll see I have a new directory called Z logger.

62
00:04:26,830 --> 00:04:30,670
So I can navigate into that using the CD command.

63
00:04:31,000 --> 00:04:37,030
And then if we LZ in here you'll see all the files that come with this key logger.

64
00:04:37,570 --> 00:04:41,380
Now the main file for the key logger is z logger.py.

65
00:04:41,380 --> 00:04:44,590
And I'm going to teach you how to use that in the next lecture.

66
00:04:44,590 --> 00:04:51,820
But before we run this file we need to install all the requirements required by this key logger.

67
00:04:52,440 --> 00:04:57,660
Now I've made this easy and I included a script called Install.sh.

68
00:04:57,660 --> 00:05:03,720
So this script will automatically install all the needed packages for the keylogger.

69
00:05:04,290 --> 00:05:11,250
So all we have to do is type bash because the script is written in bash, followed by the file name

70
00:05:11,250 --> 00:05:14,130
which is install dot ssh.

71
00:05:15,140 --> 00:05:22,880
I'm going to hit enter, and this will automatically update my sources and download all the needed packages.

72
00:05:23,390 --> 00:05:25,370
Please be patient with this as well.

73
00:05:25,370 --> 00:05:32,240
It might take some time because it's going to download all the windows libraries needed to compile Python

74
00:05:32,240 --> 00:05:35,090
files to windows executables.

75
00:05:36,440 --> 00:05:42,350
If you ever get a prompt for installing a program such as what we're getting here for installing Python,

76
00:05:42,350 --> 00:05:47,030
just keep going through the prompt and just keep clicking next, next next next.

77
00:05:51,910 --> 00:05:59,950
Okay, so now as you can see, everything is installed properly and if I clear the screen and do LZ

78
00:05:59,980 --> 00:06:04,330
again like I said the main program is z logger.py.

79
00:06:04,330 --> 00:06:15,430
So right now just to test it we can do python z logger.py and you'll see a quick usage message telling

80
00:06:15,430 --> 00:06:17,320
you how to use this program.

81
00:06:17,530 --> 00:06:19,360
Now don't be scared of this.

82
00:06:19,360 --> 00:06:22,330
I will walk you through all of this in the next lecture.

83
00:06:22,330 --> 00:06:27,250
But for now we have the program properly installed and ready to be used.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,110 --> 00:00:00,500
Okay.

2
00:00:00,500 --> 00:00:06,860
So now that we have Z logger installed, let's see how we can use it to generate a remote key logger.

3
00:00:06,860 --> 00:00:15,590
So when executed on a system, it will allow us to spy on all the key strikes entered on that computer

4
00:00:15,590 --> 00:00:16,580
remotely.

5
00:00:17,310 --> 00:00:22,650
So first of all, I'm going to change my working directory to the location where we installed z logger.

6
00:00:22,650 --> 00:00:30,150
So I'm going to do CD followed by the path where we installed it which is opt z logger.

7
00:00:31,220 --> 00:00:34,850
Now, if we do, LZ, we'll see all the files in this directory.

8
00:00:34,850 --> 00:00:39,590
And the main file for the program is z logger.py.

9
00:00:40,330 --> 00:00:48,070
Now, since this is a Python program, we have to type Python followed by the program name in order

10
00:00:48,070 --> 00:00:48,910
to run it.

11
00:00:49,560 --> 00:00:55,800
And because this is the first time that we run this program, it's a really good idea to type dash,

12
00:00:55,800 --> 00:01:02,070
dash, help to see how we can use this program and all the arguments that we can set for it.

13
00:01:02,780 --> 00:01:04,190
So I'm going to hit enter.

14
00:01:04,190 --> 00:01:09,440
And as you can see I'll get a very helpful message showing me how to use this program.

15
00:01:10,130 --> 00:01:16,820
So we can see first of all the usage is going to be Python followed by the program name followed by

16
00:01:16,820 --> 00:01:17,660
the options.

17
00:01:18,110 --> 00:01:19,970
So let's do the easy bit first.

18
00:01:19,970 --> 00:01:23,300
And we're just going to do Python followed by the program name.

19
00:01:24,050 --> 00:01:28,400
And then let's look at the options one by one and set their values.

20
00:01:29,150 --> 00:01:29,990
So we can see.

21
00:01:29,990 --> 00:01:33,620
The first argument is the help argument which we actually used.

22
00:01:33,620 --> 00:01:38,000
And the description for this is it'll literally show us this message right here.

23
00:01:38,660 --> 00:01:42,320
The next argument that we can use is the interval.

24
00:01:42,320 --> 00:01:44,330
And this takes the value.

25
00:01:44,330 --> 00:01:49,910
And the description tells us that this is the time between reports in seconds.

26
00:01:50,590 --> 00:01:55,300
So if we set this to 60, we're going to get a report every 60s.

27
00:01:55,330 --> 00:02:02,560
If you set it for 3600 seconds, which is an hour, then you're going to get a report every hour and

28
00:02:02,560 --> 00:02:03,280
so on.

29
00:02:03,730 --> 00:02:07,720
Now, because we're only testing, I'm going to set this to a small number.

30
00:02:07,720 --> 00:02:09,340
So I'm going to set it to 60.

31
00:02:09,370 --> 00:02:17,140
So I'm going to do dash I 60 so that the program will send me a report every minute every 60s.

32
00:02:17,260 --> 00:02:23,440
Now this won't be useful in a real life scenario because you're going to get a lot of reports, but

33
00:02:23,440 --> 00:02:28,990
because we're only testing, it's fine, because I actually don't want to wait for a long time before

34
00:02:28,990 --> 00:02:30,370
I can see the results.

35
00:02:31,290 --> 00:02:36,780
The next two arguments in here specify the target operating system.

36
00:02:37,280 --> 00:02:43,820
So if my target runs a windows operating system, we're going to use W or Dash dash windows.

37
00:02:43,820 --> 00:02:48,500
If it uses Linux then we're going to do dash L or Dash dash Linux.

38
00:02:49,250 --> 00:02:56,240
This will allow Z logger to know how to compile the key logger so that it runs on the target operating

39
00:02:56,240 --> 00:02:56,870
system.

40
00:02:57,230 --> 00:02:59,420
So for now we're targeting windows.

41
00:02:59,420 --> 00:03:01,370
So I'm going to do dash w.

42
00:03:02,010 --> 00:03:05,430
And then we're going to go to the required arguments here.

43
00:03:05,430 --> 00:03:09,540
And as you can see, the first one is dash e or dash dash email.

44
00:03:09,540 --> 00:03:12,330
And this takes a value of the email.

45
00:03:12,330 --> 00:03:14,880
And I think this is self-explanatory.

46
00:03:14,880 --> 00:03:19,890
Basically this is the email that the program will send the reports to.

47
00:03:20,040 --> 00:03:27,420
So I'm going to do dash e and put my email which is John Wick 70 at gmail.com.

48
00:03:28,220 --> 00:03:29,510
And the next argument.

49
00:03:29,510 --> 00:03:31,070
You probably guessed it by now.

50
00:03:31,070 --> 00:03:32,330
It's the password.

51
00:03:32,330 --> 00:03:39,530
And it's the password for this email so that the program can log in and send emails to myself of the

52
00:03:39,530 --> 00:03:40,340
reports.

53
00:03:41,150 --> 00:03:48,290
So the password is going to be a, b, C12A, BC12.

54
00:03:49,070 --> 00:03:49,940
Finally.

55
00:03:49,940 --> 00:03:56,360
And the last argument is the dash o argument, which allows us to specify the name of the file that

56
00:03:56,360 --> 00:03:58,850
will be generated by the keylogger.

57
00:03:59,480 --> 00:04:00,980
So I'm going to do dash.

58
00:04:00,980 --> 00:04:04,910
Oh and I'm going to call this just keylogger.

59
00:04:07,140 --> 00:04:08,280
And we're done.

60
00:04:08,970 --> 00:04:11,310
So very, very simple command.

61
00:04:11,310 --> 00:04:16,740
All we did is we're doing Python followed by the program name which is Z logger dot pi.

62
00:04:16,740 --> 00:04:20,640
Because this is a Python program, we're giving it the interval.

63
00:04:20,640 --> 00:04:24,270
So we're going to receive reports every 60s.

64
00:04:24,270 --> 00:04:28,770
We're using dash W to generate a keylogger for windows.

65
00:04:28,770 --> 00:04:31,800
We're given the email after the dash argument.

66
00:04:31,800 --> 00:04:34,590
This is the email that I'm going to receive the reports on.

67
00:04:34,590 --> 00:04:37,170
I'm giving the password for this email.

68
00:04:37,740 --> 00:04:44,280
And finally we're just telling the program to name the file that it's going to generate keylogger.

69
00:04:45,220 --> 00:04:53,080
Now, the account password that you usually use to log in to your Gmail account cannot be used from

70
00:04:53,080 --> 00:04:58,090
programs such as this program right here for security reasons.

71
00:04:58,330 --> 00:05:05,140
Therefore, we have to create a special app password to be used with this application.

72
00:05:05,410 --> 00:05:07,870
Doing this is very, very simple.

73
00:05:07,870 --> 00:05:17,230
All you have to do is simply go to my account dot google.com, go to the security section on the left

74
00:05:17,230 --> 00:05:20,950
and scroll down to the signing in to Google section.

75
00:05:20,950 --> 00:05:26,980
You want to make sure that two step verification is enabled, and you should see another option that

76
00:05:26,980 --> 00:05:29,170
is called app passwords in here.

77
00:05:29,170 --> 00:05:34,930
As you can see, it's saying none because I don't have an app password created to create one.

78
00:05:34,930 --> 00:05:36,820
You simply need to click in here.

79
00:05:37,650 --> 00:05:40,200
Login again with your normal password.

80
00:05:42,440 --> 00:05:45,800
Select the application that will be using the password.

81
00:05:45,800 --> 00:05:50,330
And our case we're going to see other because we're creating our own application.

82
00:05:50,330 --> 00:05:54,260
You can call it anything you want I like to use meaningful names.

83
00:05:54,260 --> 00:05:56,570
So I'm going to call it reporter.

84
00:05:57,370 --> 00:05:59,020
And click on generate.

85
00:06:00,120 --> 00:06:00,990
Right now.

86
00:06:00,990 --> 00:06:03,660
As you can see, it's given us a new password.

87
00:06:03,900 --> 00:06:11,130
And this will be the password that we will use to authenticate with our Google account from our application.

88
00:06:11,130 --> 00:06:18,060
So we're simply going to copy this and paste it in our program in the password field.

89
00:06:18,540 --> 00:06:19,950
And you're good to go.

90
00:06:20,460 --> 00:06:22,140
So I'm going to hit enter.

91
00:06:23,100 --> 00:06:27,930
And as you can see, it's telling me that the X has been generated successfully.

92
00:06:28,260 --> 00:06:33,600
The file that this program generated is stored in a directory called dist.

93
00:06:33,870 --> 00:06:40,590
So let me show you if I just open my file manager here and go to opt Z logger.

94
00:06:42,940 --> 00:06:43,660
You'll see.

95
00:06:43,660 --> 00:06:51,730
We have a new directory called dist and inside it we have the file which is called keylogger dot exe.

96
00:06:52,570 --> 00:07:01,060
Now you can copy this and paste it in var HTML like I showed you before, but I think you're more comfortable

97
00:07:01,060 --> 00:07:02,530
now with using the terminal.

98
00:07:02,530 --> 00:07:06,010
So I'm going to show you how to copy files through the terminal.

99
00:07:06,980 --> 00:07:12,680
So if I just go back here and do LZ, you'll see we have the dist directory right here.

100
00:07:12,680 --> 00:07:18,920
And if we do LZ dist to see what's inside it, we're going to see our backdoor.

101
00:07:18,920 --> 00:07:22,400
So this is exactly the same file that we're seeing in here.

102
00:07:23,210 --> 00:07:30,560
Now to copy this from the terminal we're going to do CP followed by the current location of the file.

103
00:07:30,560 --> 00:07:37,940
So it's in dist keylogger followed by the location where I want to copy it to.

104
00:07:37,940 --> 00:07:45,110
So I want to copy it to var w w w HTML eval files.

105
00:07:45,930 --> 00:07:47,430
So very simple command.

106
00:07:47,430 --> 00:07:51,270
Again CP is the command to copy files in Linux.

107
00:07:51,270 --> 00:07:57,210
I'm giving it the location of the file that I want to copy, followed by the location where I want to

108
00:07:57,210 --> 00:07:58,710
copy the file to.

109
00:07:59,160 --> 00:08:00,690
I'm going to hit enter.

110
00:08:00,690 --> 00:08:06,090
And if this runs without errors, this means that the command got executed properly.

111
00:08:06,570 --> 00:08:09,690
And I've already started my web server right now.

112
00:08:09,690 --> 00:08:13,200
So you can start it by doing service apache2 start.

113
00:08:13,200 --> 00:08:19,110
Once this is all running, we can go to the windows machine and download the file.

114
00:08:19,580 --> 00:08:27,920
So as usual, we're going to go to 1020 14 to 13 evil files, followed by the file name which is keylogger

115
00:08:27,920 --> 00:08:28,940
dot exe.

116
00:08:32,180 --> 00:08:33,950
We're going to save the file.

117
00:08:33,950 --> 00:08:38,270
And keep in mind my antivirus program is turned off at the moment.

118
00:08:38,270 --> 00:08:44,480
The keylogger actually bypasses all antivirus programs right now, but I'm sure by the time all the

119
00:08:44,480 --> 00:08:47,510
students download it and use it, it will be detected.

120
00:08:47,510 --> 00:08:49,190
But don't worry about that.

121
00:08:49,190 --> 00:08:54,320
We're going to talk about antivirus programs and how to bypass them later on in the course.

122
00:08:54,320 --> 00:08:59,060
So for now, let's focus on just trying to get the keylogger to work properly.

123
00:08:59,540 --> 00:09:01,730
So let's go to the downloads.

124
00:09:01,730 --> 00:09:05,300
And as you can see we have the keylogger downloaded in here.

125
00:09:05,870 --> 00:09:09,500
And you probably noticed that this keylogger looks suspicious.

126
00:09:09,500 --> 00:09:12,260
So you can't really send it to your target like this.

127
00:09:12,260 --> 00:09:14,180
But again, don't worry about this.

128
00:09:14,210 --> 00:09:17,450
We're going to talk about delivery methods later on in the course.

129
00:09:17,450 --> 00:09:22,430
And I'm going to show you how to make this look and function just like any other file type, like an

130
00:09:22,430 --> 00:09:24,020
image or a PDF.

131
00:09:24,470 --> 00:09:30,530
So now we can run this keylogger by double clicking it, but I'm going to leave that to the next lecture

132
00:09:30,530 --> 00:09:35,540
because there is a number of points that I want to highlight when we run it.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:00,420  -->  00:00:05,840
So, in the previous lecture we used ZLogger to generate this Keylogger.

2

00:00:06,480  -->  00:00:09,480
Now, let's go ahead and double click it to run it

3

00:00:09,720  -->  00:00:14,820
and to see if this Keylogger actually works as expected

4

00:00:16,120  -->  00:00:18,140
and again, I know this looks suspicious.

5

00:00:18,840  -->  00:00:22,020
Don't worry about that, we're gonna talk
about delivery methods later on

6

00:00:22,240  -->  00:00:28,920
and we'll talk about how to make this look and function just like any other file type, like an image or a PDF.

7

00:00:30,320  -->  00:00:33,980
So for now, the Keylogger is working and it should be running in the background.

8

00:00:34,640  -->  00:00:37,920
Now, before I do anything, if I go to the Kali machine,

9

00:00:39,220  -->  00:00:43,112
you'll see that I already got a report from my Keylogger

10

00:00:43,720  -->  00:00:48,740
and if we go in, you can see we have some basic information about the target.

11

00:00:49,120  -->  00:00:51,680
So we can see that it's running Windows 10.

12

00:00:52,940  -->  00:00:58,700
We can see the computer name is Zaids-PC and we can see the user is called Zaid.

13

00:01:00,020  -->  00:01:04,760
Now, in the logs, all we're seeing is Keylogger started

14

00:01:04,760  -->  00:01:08,440
because the user didn't really type anything using their keyboard.

15

00:01:09,540  -->  00:01:17,240
So let's go back here and let's go to Facebook for example and let's login.

16

00:01:17,260  -->  00:01:26,300
So let's put my username as zayedzayedzayed22@facebook.com.

17

00:01:26,300  -->  00:01:33,980
This is a fake user and then let's put the password as '12345678, doesn't really matter

18

00:01:34,400  -->  00:01:37,420
and the security in this website doesn't really matter either

19

00:01:37,420  -->  00:01:41,540
you can see that it's loading in HTTPS, everything is perfect.

20

00:01:42,160  -->  00:01:47,780
The problem is the Keylogger is registering every key strike entered on the keyboard,

21

00:01:47,780  -->  00:01:53,320
regardless of what type of security is used on the application thus, the user's using.

22

00:01:54,120  -->  00:01:58,100
So whether they're typing messages through WhatsApp, through Facebook, through Viber,

23

00:01:58,200  -->  00:02:03,260
if they're using their keyboard, this Keylogger will register everything that they type.

24

00:02:04,670  -->  00:02:08,360
So, let's go back to our machine and let's go to the inbox

25

00:02:08,360  -->  00:02:11,120
and again, you can see we've got two new emails.

26

00:02:11,600  -->  00:02:14,780
If we look in the first one, this one is empty.

27

00:02:14,780  -->  00:02:17,700
This is probably while I was just showing your stuff

28

00:02:18,000  -->  00:02:22,580
and if we go to the most recent one, you'll see we have all the key strikes.

29

00:02:22,960  -->  00:02:26,600
So you can see that I typed zid because I made a mistake.

30

00:02:26,900  -->  00:02:34,838
You can see that I write backspace to delete all of that and then I put my username as zaidzaid22.

31

00:02:35,680  -->  00:02:39,960
I use the Shift to type the ad and I actually messed this up

32

00:02:39,960  -->  00:02:43,299
because the layout of my Windows machine is different.

33

00:02:43,300  -->  00:02:45,300
So I typed the Shift multiple times.

34

00:02:45,700  -->  00:02:48,440
Finally, I got it and it was at facebook.com

35

00:02:48,820  -->  00:02:54,360
and then I hit Tab and then I put the password, which is '12345678'.

36

00:02:55,800  -->  00:03:02,160
So that's perfect and the Keylogger will continue doing this and sending me a report every 60 seconds.

37

00:03:02,560  -->  00:03:05,800
Again, this all depends on the interval that you pick.

38

00:03:07,260  -->  00:03:11,680
Now, another really cool feature for this Keylogger is that it's persistent.

39

00:03:12,140  -->  00:03:19,080
So if I close everything now and restart my computer,

40

00:03:20,760  -->  00:03:24,960
you'll see that the Keylogger will start again with the system start up

41

00:03:25,040  -->  00:03:29,120
and it will continue sending me reports every 60 seconds.

42

00:03:30,420  -->  00:03:36,740
So I'm just gonna login and let's go ahead and open FireFox

43

00:03:38,340  -->  00:03:41,300
and for this time let's go to twitter.com

44

00:03:41,880  -->  00:03:47,200
and keep in mind the only type 'tw' now and that's login.

45

00:03:47,200  -->  00:03:56,180
Put a username, we're gonna call it 'twitterusername' and put a password as '123456' again.

46

00:03:56,800  -->  00:03:59,120
And let's go and see if we're gonna
get a report.

47

00:04:00,300  -->  00:04:03,060
I'm gonna look at the most recent report

48

00:04:04,120  -->  00:04:09,960
and as you can see I typed tw, Enter, put the username as 'twitterusername'

49

00:04:10,380  -->  00:04:13,460
and then put the password as '123456'.

50

00:04:14,400  -->  00:04:18,420
So as you can see right now, once this is executed on a target computer,

51

00:04:18,840  -->  00:04:24,100
it will allow you to spy on every keystroke they enter on their keyboard

52

00:04:24,940  -->  00:04:27,820
even if they Restart or Shut down their computer.

53

00:04:28,760  -->  00:04:29,980
So that's very, very cool.

54

00:04:31,280  -->  00:04:36,140
Now, like I said, if you're interested in learning how to write programs like ZLogger

55

00:04:36,580  -->  00:04:39,280
then check out my Python programming course

56

00:04:39,460  -->  00:04:43,580
where I teach Python programming by writing hacking programs.

57

00:04:44,180  -->  00:04:50,980
And one of the example programs that I show you how to program in that course is actually, ZLogger.

58

00:04:52,800  -->  00:04:57,936
Now one last thing that I want to show you is once you're done with testing this, it will get annoying

59

00:04:57,936  -->  00:05:01,060
if the key, if the Keylogger keeps sending new reports.

60

00:05:01,600  -->  00:05:05,860
So I'm gonna show you how to delete it and stop it from running with the system startup.

61

00:05:07,040  -->  00:05:15,920
So first of all you want to go to start and type regedit, so regedit.

62

00:05:17,020  -->  00:05:18,860
This will open the Registry Editor.

63

00:05:19,500  -->  00:05:20,400
We're gonna say, yes.

64

00:05:20,420  -->  00:05:24,580
We're going to open it and you want to go to this path right here.

65

00:05:25,220  -->  00:05:30,360
So you can type it yourself or you can just use this Tree structure

66

00:05:30,640  -->  00:05:34,940
to go into each one of these directories until you're in the run.

67

00:05:36,000  -->  00:05:39,360
Once you're inside that, yeah, once you enter, in the run in here.

68

00:05:39,600  -->  00:05:44,660
You will see that there is an entry here and this entry is called winexplorer

69

00:05:45,500  -->  00:05:48,880
and you'll see the path for entry like so.

70

00:05:49,740  -->  00:05:54,000
Now, this is the Keylogger and that's why it's starting with the system startup.

71

00:05:54,440  -->  00:05:58,640
So to stop this you wanna right-click this and delete

72

00:06:00,100  -->  00:06:04,320
but before we do that, what I'm actually gonna do is, I'm gonna click on modify

73

00:06:05,160  -->  00:06:10,200
and I'm gonna copy this path because this is the path where the actual file is stored.

74

00:06:11,040  -->  00:06:12,719
So I'm gonna copy that.

75

00:06:13,260  -->  00:06:14,480
I'm gonna delete this.

76

00:06:15,900  -->  00:06:18,140
Gonna say Yes, so this is gone.

77

00:06:18,540  -->  00:06:25,140
And then, I'm gonna open a File Manager and paste the path that I just copied

78

00:06:25,580  -->  00:06:28,700
because I wanna also delete the actual executable.

79

00:06:30,060  -->  00:06:33,600
So I'm gonna remove Windows Explorer.exe from the end.

80

00:06:33,920  -->  00:06:38,100
I'm gonna hit Enter and you will see that I have the file right here.

81

00:06:39,000  -->  00:06:41,580
So I'm just going to view it as larger icons.

82

00:06:41,960  -->  00:06:43,960
You'll see I have the file right here.

83

00:06:44,300  -->  00:06:46,100
This is a copy of the Keylogger.

84

00:06:46,740  -->  00:06:48,560
So we're gonna delete this one as well

85

00:06:49,900  -->  00:06:53,360
and it's not deleting because it's actually running at the moment.

86

00:06:53,500  -->  00:06:56,880
So we're actually going to need to go to start again.

87

00:06:57,540  -->  00:07:00,740
Use the Task Manager, so I'm going to type Task Manager

88

00:07:02,480  -->  00:07:08,020
and if you look in here, you'll see all the programs running in the background at the moment

89

00:07:08,520  -->  00:07:12,720
and if you scroll down, you'll see it shown up here as Windows Explorer.

90

00:07:13,520  -->  00:07:16,440
Again, right-click, End Task to stop it.

91

00:07:17,080  -->  00:07:22,980
Once, it stopped, we can come back here and delete this and it's gone.

92

00:07:24,020  -->  00:07:30,000
Now, you can restart the computer and the Keylogger will not start with the system startup.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:01,860  -->  00:00:05,440
In this lecture, we're gonna have a look
on a tool called LaZagne.

2

00:00:06,000  -->  00:00:12,240
LaZagne is a password recovery tool that works on Windows, Linux and OS X.

3

00:00:13,300  -->  00:00:17,840
So far we've seen how we can use a keylogger to spy on the target computer

4

00:00:18,240  -->  00:00:22,520
and that'll send us everything that the target person types on the keyboard,

5

00:00:22,680  -->  00:00:27,160
including usernames and passwords, if they use the keyboard to type their password.

6

00:00:28,260  -->  00:00:33,660
Now, there is one problem with this is if the person have their password saved on the browser,

7

00:00:33,660  -->  00:00:38,040
so if they log in to their websites to their accounts without actually typing

8

00:00:38,040  -->  00:00:39,700
the password using the keyboard.

9

00:00:40,520  -->  00:00:46,600
Another problem is if the person runs the keylogger after they log into their account,

10

00:00:46,600  -->  00:00:50,735
again in both of these cases, the person will not be using the keyboard to log in

11

00:00:50,740  -->  00:00:53,000
and we won't be able to get their password.

12

00:00:53,720  -->  00:00:57,860
Therefore, in these situations using a keylogger will not be ideal.

13

00:00:58,300  -->  00:01:03,140
A better solution would be to use a password recovery tool like LaZagne.

14

00:01:04,580  -->  00:01:09,380
Now, I have the website of their GitHub in here and I'm gonna include that in the resources

15

00:01:10,080  -->  00:01:14,580
and if you scroll down, you'll see that this tool supports a large number of programs.

16

00:01:15,000  -->  00:01:20,000
It supports pretty much all browsers, so Chrome, Firefox, IE and Opera.

17

00:01:20,740  -->  00:01:24,700
It supports Wi-Fi passwords, it gets you the Windows passwords,

18

00:01:24,700  -->  00:01:29,060
it gets you the saved passwords of a large number of programs.

19

00:01:29,840  -->  00:01:34,340
Not only that, but it will also give you the passwords stored in memory.

20

00:01:35,080  -->  00:01:37,660
Now all of this will become clearer, when we run it.

21

00:01:39,080  -->  00:01:43,920
Now you can do 'Git-clone' from here and then compile the project to an executable

22

00:01:43,920  -->  00:01:48,114
and then send it to your target Windows Device, I'm not gonna do that

23

00:01:48,120  -->  00:01:53,240
because there is a detailed step-by-step explanation on how to do that in the wiki

24

00:01:53,520  -->  00:01:56,160
in here, if you just go on wiki and How to Build.

25

00:01:57,540  -->  00:02:01,020
What I'm gonna do is, there is already ready releases for this,

26

00:02:01,020  -->  00:02:04,200
so we can just download them without having to compile anything.

27

00:02:04,800  -->  00:02:10,980
The only drawback to that is the ready releases are detectable by antivirus programs,

28

00:02:11,160  -->  00:02:17,260
but we'll see how we can bypass that later on in the course, so for now just turn off your antivirus program

29

00:02:17,520  -->  00:02:20,040
and we're just gonna have a basic look on this tool.

30

00:02:20,200  -->  00:02:25,020
So, we're just gonna go on the ready releases that already have executables for us.

31

00:02:26,400  -->  00:02:29,520
So I'm just gonna go down and download the Windows version,

32

00:02:29,520  -->  00:02:31,940
as you can see we have the WinZip version right here.

33

00:02:32,580  -->  00:02:33,760
I'm gonna download it.

34

00:02:34,780  -->  00:02:37,740
And I'm gonna open the file, now this is gonna be a zip file,

35

00:02:37,740  -->  00:02:40,680
so if you have WinRAR installed, it's going to open like this,

36

00:02:41,000  -->  00:02:47,700
if you have-- if you don't have anything installed to open zip files, it'll literally just open the windows explorer,

37

00:02:47,700  -->  00:02:53,440
it'll just browse to the file for you and you can just drag drop the file to the location that you want.

38

00:02:53,680  -->  00:02:58,960
I'm gonna put it on my desktop and I'm gonna close this and that's it.

39

00:02:59,580  -->  00:03:04,640
Now you probably noticed that I'm doing this on my Windows machine and I'm not doing it on my Kali

40

00:03:05,260  -->  00:03:10,180
and the reason for this is LaZagne is designed to be a post exploitation tool.

41

00:03:10,520  -->  00:03:14,700
So it's designed to be used after you hack into the target computer,

42

00:03:15,540  -->  00:03:20,100
but I think it's a very handy tool to actually gain access to computers.

43

00:03:20,380  -->  00:03:24,420
So, it can be used in other situations other than post exploitation.

44

00:03:24,760  -->  00:03:29,960
So like I said before, sometimes you don't really want to hack into the target computer,

45

00:03:30,420  -->  00:03:35,360
sometimes you are interested into an account that the target person log is-- logs into

46

00:03:35,820  -->  00:03:42,200
and it will be better to just send them a file and that file somehow will give you the passwords

47

00:03:42,200  -->  00:03:44,500
that the target person uses.

48

00:03:44,500  -->  00:03:46,320
We have seen how we can do that with the keyloggers

49

00:03:46,580  -->  00:03:51,220
and I wanna show you how to do that with LaZagne, but for now for today's lecture,

50

00:03:51,400  -->  00:03:56,280
we're gonna be running LaZagne from within windows, just to see how we can use the tool.

51

00:03:56,500  -->  00:04:00,860
And then I'm gonna show you how to weaponize this and send it as an executable

52

00:04:01,060  -->  00:04:05,340
that if the target person runs, it will send you the passwords on your email.

53

00:04:06,620  -->  00:04:09,140
For now, we're just gonna run it from the command prompt

54

00:04:09,260  -->  00:04:11,700
and we're going to be displaying the results on screen.

55

00:04:12,320  -->  00:04:17,020
So I'm just gonna go to my browser to start and we're going to go to CMD.

56

64

67

68

00:05:29,320  -->  00:05:35,100
you can run 'wifi' to get the Wi-Fi password and you can run 'browsers' to get the browser passwords.

69

70

71

74

75

76

78

83

00:06:52,460  -->  00:06:57,760
so you can do' - oN' to store the passwords in a readable file

84

85
