00:06:04,330 --> 00:06:06,490
Now, these are just the basics of empire.

87
00:06:06,520 --> 00:06:09,040
Empire can be used to do so much, as I said.

88
00:06:09,040 --> 00:06:12,940
So I highly recommend you spend a lot of time with it, but don't do it now.

89
00:06:12,970 --> 00:06:17,960
Do it at the end of this section because we're actually going to be using Empire a lot as we go through

90
00:06:17,960 --> 00:06:24,530
this section to show you what we can do with it once we get an empire shell on a Target computer.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,140 --> 00:00:05,180
In this lecture, I want to show you some basic file system commands that will allow you to remotely

2
00:00:05,180 --> 00:00:11,300
navigate the file system of the hacked computers, read files, and even download them if you want to.

3
00:00:11,540 --> 00:00:18,650
Now, I'm already at the agents menu in my empire client and if I just do list or type agents, it will

4
00:00:18,650 --> 00:00:26,780
list all of the available agents and if you pay attention you'll notice that the Ubuntu agent is highlighted

5
00:00:26,780 --> 00:00:28,100
in red at the moment.

6
00:00:28,100 --> 00:00:31,790
And the reason for that is because I've actually lost connection to it.

7
00:00:31,790 --> 00:00:37,670
I've turned off the Ubuntu machine and that's why it's red indicating that at the moment I do not have

8
00:00:37,670 --> 00:00:41,180
access to this agent and I lost my access five minutes ago.

9
00:00:41,420 --> 00:00:46,940
We can see that the windows one is still green, indicating that I still have access to this agent,

10
00:00:47,180 --> 00:00:49,160
so we're going to interact with it.

11
00:00:50,690 --> 00:00:57,710
And previously we seen that we can type shell followed by any command in order to execute system commands

12
00:00:57,710 --> 00:00:59,150
on the target computer.

13
00:00:59,150 --> 00:01:06,050
So we can use the file system commands of whatever target computer it is to navigate within it.

14
00:01:06,050 --> 00:01:12,920
So for example, we can do Shell PWD and then it will execute the PWD command, which lists the current

15
00:01:12,920 --> 00:01:13,940
working directory.

16
00:01:13,940 --> 00:01:16,820
And as you can see, we get the current working directory.

17
00:01:17,090 --> 00:01:23,030
Now, luckily the file system commands are identical on Windows, Linux and OSX.

18
00:01:23,060 --> 00:01:29,120
Therefore you can actually use the exact same commands to navigate a file system regardless of what

19
00:01:29,150 --> 00:01:31,880
operating system it is now.

20
00:01:31,880 --> 00:01:38,000
You can always just do shell followed by whatever system command you want in order to navigate within

21
00:01:38,000 --> 00:01:38,990
the file system.

22
00:01:38,990 --> 00:01:46,640
Or you can simply just run the shell command and that will actually drop you into an interactive shell

23
00:01:46,640 --> 00:01:50,720
that is very similar to a file system command shell.

24
00:01:50,720 --> 00:01:55,910
And you can see automatically right now we have the current working directory in here in red.

25
00:01:55,910 --> 00:02:02,660
And now I can go ahead and navigate within the file system using normal file system commands.

26
00:02:02,660 --> 00:02:08,060
As we seen at the start of the course when we were interacting within our own current computer.

27
00:02:08,060 --> 00:02:15,890
So we can simply do CD dot dot to go back one directory and we can do PWD to see our current working

28
00:02:15,890 --> 00:02:17,690
directory or updated.

29
00:02:18,690 --> 00:02:21,360
And as you can see right now, we are in the user.

30
00:02:21,360 --> 00:02:28,380
We went back one working directory we can do to list all of the files and directories within the current

31
00:02:28,380 --> 00:02:29,610
working directory.

32
00:02:29,610 --> 00:02:32,610
And as you can see, we have them all in here.

33
00:02:32,730 --> 00:02:38,970
Note that we have the permissions in here and the permissions that start with a D indicate that the

34
00:02:38,970 --> 00:02:41,030
current entry is a directory.

35
00:02:41,040 --> 00:02:46,200
So you can see all of these right here are directories and all of these right here are files.

36
00:02:46,410 --> 00:02:50,790
Now, let's assume that I want to navigate to the documents directory.

37
00:02:50,790 --> 00:02:55,080
So all I have to do is simply type CD documents.

38
00:02:56,410 --> 00:03:02,370
And if I do PWD again, I'll get my current working directory and this should update.

39
00:03:02,380 --> 00:03:05,370
And as you can see right now, I'm in the documents.

40
00:03:05,380 --> 00:03:07,900
I can do ls to list again.

41
00:03:09,160 --> 00:03:11,890
And as you can see, this time we have a file.

42
00:03:11,890 --> 00:03:17,560
We know that this is a file because we know that it does not start with a D in here and the permission.

43
00:03:17,560 --> 00:03:21,790
And you can see that the name of this file is passwords dot txt.

44
00:03:21,970 --> 00:03:24,820
So that file is probably very, very interesting.

45
00:03:24,820 --> 00:03:30,310
Now obviously I put this there myself just as an example for you so that we have something to read.

46
00:03:30,430 --> 00:03:36,190
Assuming that we want to read this file, we can simply type cat followed by the file name, which is

47
00:03:36,190 --> 00:03:38,410
passwords, dot txt.

48
00:03:38,680 --> 00:03:43,990
And as I mentioned, these commands work on all operating systems regardless of what operating system

49
00:03:43,990 --> 00:03:44,530
it is.

50
00:03:44,530 --> 00:03:49,870
So if I hit enter, we will see the contents of this file that we specified here.

51
00:03:49,870 --> 00:03:54,460
And as you can see, assuming that these are passwords, we manage to read them in here.

52
00:03:54,640 --> 00:03:58,770
Now you might see a file that you actually want to download, not just read.

53
00:03:58,780 --> 00:04:06,670
In that case, you can simply press control and C to exit the shell, the interactive shell, and then

54
00:04:06,670 --> 00:04:11,990
you can actually use the download command followed by the file name that you want to download.

55
00:04:11,990 --> 00:04:14,840
And in this case it's called passwords dot txt.

56
00:04:15,530 --> 00:04:18,770
And if we hit, enter and give it a second.

57
00:04:19,990 --> 00:04:23,110
As you can see, it tells us that the download is successful.

58
00:04:23,110 --> 00:04:28,810
So right now we actually have a copy of this file on our own storage on the Kali machine.

59
00:04:29,260 --> 00:04:35,050
Now to access that, we're going to go and open the file manager and we're going to press in here to

60
00:04:35,050 --> 00:04:42,100
access the path bar and press a forward slash or press Ctrl and L to open up the path bar.

61
00:04:42,100 --> 00:04:48,250
And then we're going to go to var Lib PowerShell Empire.

62
00:04:49,550 --> 00:04:52,530
And in here we have a directory called downloads.

63
00:04:52,550 --> 00:05:00,530
We're going to open it and inside it you're going to have a directory for each agent session you get.

64
00:05:00,560 --> 00:05:07,710
So if we go up, we can see that the name of our session that the one that we're interested in is y

65
00:05:07,730 --> 00:05:08,410
whatever.

66
00:05:08,420 --> 00:05:15,560
So if we go down and open the directory that we're interested in, which is this one, you'll see that

67
00:05:15,560 --> 00:05:20,870
we have another directory in here, the C directory, because the file that we downloaded was in C,

68
00:05:20,900 --> 00:05:26,000
if you double click it, you'll see users, you'll see user and documents.

69
00:05:26,000 --> 00:05:33,320
So you have the file that we downloaded stored in the exact same hierarchy as the target computer because

70
00:05:33,320 --> 00:05:39,050
the file that we downloaded from the target from the Windows computer was called Passwords dot TXT and

71
00:05:39,050 --> 00:05:42,860
it was stored inside C users user documents.

72
00:05:42,860 --> 00:05:49,440
So Empire replicates that hierarchy and then stores the file that we downloaded inside it.

73
00:05:49,440 --> 00:05:55,500
Now if we open up this file, sure enough, you'll see that it contains the same passwords so it has

74
00:05:55,500 --> 00:05:58,410
the same content as the target computer.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:01,240  -->  00:00:05,960
Now what I also wanna show you is the 'upload' command, so we've seen how to download stuff.

2

00:00:06,360  -->  00:00:09,340
Let's see how we can upload stuff and this is very useful

3

00:00:09,600  -->  00:00:13,980
because we can upload things like Keyloggers or other backdoors.

4

00:00:13,980  -->  00:00:20,020
For example, let's say, you wanted to get a Meterpreter shell instead of the agent backdoor that we have here

5

00:00:20,380  -->  00:00:24,280
or you can upload your Credential Harvesters, you can upload LaZange

6

00:00:24,280  -->  00:00:27,280
because like I said LaZange is actually designed for this.

7

00:00:27,280  -->  00:00:32,500
It's designed as a Post Exploitation tool, not a, not a gaining access tool

8

00:00:32,500  -->  00:00:34,560
but we actually use it in gaining access.

9

00:00:35,280  -->  00:00:39,400
So, the 'upload' command can be very useful and I'm just gonna show you how to do that.

10

00:00:40,740  -->  00:00:47,660
What I'm gonna upload, I'm gonna be uploading my, a backdoor that I created with 'Veil'

11

00:00:47,820  -->  00:00:52,140
and this is it, it's a 'reserve_https Meterpreter backdoor'.

12

00:00:52,600  -->  00:00:58,440
I'm not using the one that we created in the previous lectures because it uses port 8080

13

00:00:58,760  -->  00:01:02,180
and 8080 is already in use by Empire.

14

00:01:02,480  -->  00:01:08,320
So, I created one that uses port 8888 and I'm gonna upload that to the target.

15

00:01:08,960  -->  00:01:13,900
This is also stored in 'usr, share, veil-output, compiled'.

16

00:01:14,340  -->  00:01:17,440
So, you know, we wanna keep this in mind when you're uploading stuff

17

00:01:17,440  -->  00:01:20,840
because we have to give the full path to the location of the file.

18

00:01:22,180  -->  00:01:26,300
So the 'upload' command is very simple, all you have to do is just type in 'upload',

19

00:01:27,300  -->  00:01:31,940
followed by the full path of the location where the file is stored

20

00:01:32,240  -->  00:01:38,640
and my file is stored in 'usr/share/veil-output/compiled/'

21

00:01:38,640  -->  00:01:45,080
and the name of the file is 'rev_https_8888.exe'.

22

00:01:45,700  -->  00:01:48,860
Very simple upload, followed by the location of the file,

23

00:01:49,480  -->  00:01:50,520
we're going to hit 'Enter'

24

00:01:51,920  -->  00:01:57,020
and that has uploaded the file for us, now now if we do 'ls' to see the file.

25

00:02:00,160  -->  00:02:08,820
You'll see that we have a new file in there and that file is called 'rev_https_8888.exe'.

26

00:02:10,240  -->  00:02:14,980
Now before we execute this file, I'm just gonna start my Metasploit very quickly

27

00:02:15,060  -->  00:02:18,960
and listen for incoming connections on port 8888.

28

00:02:20,200  -->  00:02:21,859
Now we covered how to do that before

29

00:02:21,859  -->  00:02:25,500
and we covered how to use the multi/handler to listen for incoming connections.

30

00:02:25,960  -->  00:02:31,400
So I actually have it, have all the options already stored, so all I'm gonna do is just 'exploit'.

31

00:02:33,000  -->  00:02:36,380
And that's gonna have the right options configured for me.

32

00:02:36,440  -->  00:02:41,480
If you don't remember how to do this, please go back to the listening for incoming connections lecture

33

00:02:41,480  -->  00:02:42,840
and see how to do that.

34

00:02:44,500  -->  00:02:49,320
Now, let's execute the file that we uploaded and see if that's gonna work.

35

00:02:49,820  -->  00:02:55,760
To execute any 'shell' command, any native OS command, you can do 'Shell',

36

00:02:57,660  -->  00:03:00,040
followed by the command that you want to execute.

37

00:03:00,360  -->  00:03:04,320
You just want to make sure that you use a Windows command, if your target is Windows

38

00:03:04,540  -->  00:03:11,000
or Unix command, if your target is a Linux-- is a Unix-like operating system, like OS X or Linux.

39

00:03:11,600  -->  00:03:16,000
So for example, let's say you just want to list the directories in your target,

40

00:03:16,160  -->  00:03:19,040
you can just do 'shell' followed by 'dir'

41

00:03:20,540  -->  00:03:25,080
and that'll execute a normal 'dir' command that Windows supports.

42

00:03:26,120  -->  00:03:31,480
We don't want to do that though, we want to do, to run our backdoor, so we're gonna do 'shell'

43

00:03:32,040  -->  00:03:36,880
followed by './' and we put the name of the backdoor that we uploaded

44

00:03:37,380  -->  00:03:46,560
and our backdoor was called 'rev_https_8888.exe'.

45

00:03:46,980  -->  00:03:48,040
We're gonna hit 'Enter'.

46

00:03:50,820  -->  00:03:54,520
And as you can see, we got our Meterpreter session here

47

00:03:54,660  -->  00:03:59,740
and now we can use the-- this Meterpreter session to do whatever you want to do.

48

00:04:02,060  -->  00:04:07,500
Now like I said, you can use this method of upload to upload any other file you want,

49

00:04:07,500  -->  00:04:12,480
you can use it to upload a backdoor, a download-and-execute file or anything that you want.

50

00:04:13,620  -->  00:04:16,000
You can also use this 'shell' command,

51

00:04:16,000  -->  00:04:21,440
like I said to execute any native command supported by the target operating system,

52

00:04:21,560  -->  00:04:26,180
just make sure you use a command that the target computer can run.

53

00:04:26,780  -->  00:04:32,320
All you have to do is like we did here, just type in 'shell' followed by the command that you want to run.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:01,000  -->  00:00:05,560
In this lecture, I'd like to give you an overview of Empire modules.

2

00:00:06,040  -->  00:00:10,220
Now every time we start Empire, we see three main things,

3

00:00:10,220  -->  00:00:13,940
it tell us the number of agents and we know what agents are.

4

00:00:14,300  -->  00:00:17,300
It tell us the number of listeners and we spoke about that

5

00:00:17,740  -->  00:00:25,200
and the only thing that we never really spoke about is the modules and you can see that we have 255 modules.

6

00:00:26,680  -->  00:00:29,820
These modules are Post Exploitation tools

7

00:00:29,820  -->  00:00:34,940
that allow you to do certain things on the target, on the hacked computer.

8

00:00:35,480  -->  00:00:41,800
They can allow you to inject Keyloggers, get screenshots, elevate your privileges and so on.

9

00:00:42,540  -->  00:00:47,780
These modules are also specific to each agent and each operating system.

10

00:00:48,060  -->  00:00:52,640
So unlike all the commands that we done so far which worked on all operating systems,

11

00:00:52,640  -->  00:00:56,320
these modules are specific to the target that you hacked.

12

00:00:58,840  -->  00:01:04,080
As you can see there is 255 modules, so there is no way for me to cover them all.

13

00:01:04,500  -->  00:01:10,100
Well, I'm gonna do though, I'm going to show you how to list the modules that work with the current agent,

14

00:01:10,320  -->  00:01:14,200
how to use these modules, configure them and execute them.

15

00:01:15,120  -->  00:01:17,640
Then the process is going to be very repetitive,

16

00:01:17,640  -->  00:01:21,540
so you can replicate it to any module that you want to use.

17

00:01:22,240  -->  00:01:26,640
Now, the module that I want to show you an example of, is a Windows module

18

00:01:26,640  -->  00:01:32,440
and it's a module that'll allow us to run code, from an existing process,

19

00:01:32,700  -->  00:01:36,640
something similar to the' migrate' command that Meterpreter has.

20

00:01:37,940  -->  00:01:43,500
So basically, you can see that we have our agent, is running from a PowerShell process

21

00:01:44,000  -->  00:01:48,760
and the PID or the process ID is 5008.

22

00:01:49,900  -->  00:01:54,640
Now, if we go to the Windows machine and run our System Monitor.

23

00:01:59,000  -->  00:02:04,920
If you look at the network here, you'll see that there is a connection going from a PowerShell process

24

00:02:05,400  -->  00:02:08,740
to IP number '10.20.14.213'.

25

00:02:09,280  -->  00:02:14,220
Now, this is very suspicious and the target might just terminate this process if they see it.

26

00:02:15,020  -->  00:02:21,820
So what we want to do is, we want to have our code running from a trusted process something like Explorer.

27

00:02:22,800  -->  00:02:25,500
We're going to use a Windows module to do that

28

00:02:25,960  -->  00:02:28,260
and before we can even list the modulee,

29

00:02:28,400  -->  00:02:31,320
we have to go inside the agent and 'interact' with it.

30

00:02:33,320  -->  00:02:39,800
Now once you're inside your target agent, you can list the modules that you can use with this agent.

31

00:02:40,220  -->  00:02:42,840
So, the modules that you're gonna see when you list them,

32

00:02:42,840  -->  00:02:46,400
all work with this operating system, with this agent,

33

00:02:46,600  -->  00:02:52,420
so when I list the agents, now for windows are going to be different than the ones that you're gonna get

34

00:02:52,420  -->  00:02:55,800
if you run the same command on Linux or OS X.

35

00:02:56,260  -->  00:03:01,147
But the command is the same, using the modules is the same, setting the options

36

00:03:01,147  -->  00:03:03,560
and executing them is the same as well.

37

00:03:03,740  -->  00:03:06,780
The only difference is the actual modules.

38

00:03:08,360  -->  00:03:10,080
So, I'm gonna do use modules,

39

00:03:10,860  -->  00:03:13,420
which is a command that work in all agents

40

00:03:13,420  -->  00:03:15,220
and then we're going to do double-tab,

41

00:03:16,320  -->  00:03:22,320
to list all the modules that we can use with this agent on this operating system.

42

00:03:24,240  -->  00:03:29,300
So, you can see the modules are categorized in categories that represent what they do.

43

00:03:29,820  -->  00:03:33,040
For example, we can see we have the collection modules here,

44

00:03:33,040  -->  00:03:35,200
which allow you to collect data,

45

00:03:35,200  -->  00:03:39,620
so you can collect passwords, inject the Keylogger, get screenshots and so on.

46

00:03:40,440  -->  00:03:43,380
We can see we have the code execution modules here,

47

00:03:43,380  -->  00:03:47,280
which will basically, allow you to execute code and so on.

48

00:03:47,960  -->  00:03:50,540
The module that I want to show you an example of,

49

00:03:50,540  -->  00:03:55,240
is a management module and it's called 'psIiject'.

50

00:03:55,920  -->  00:03:59,540
This module allow us to inject code into a process

51

00:03:59,720  -->  00:04:03,520
and then we'll have our connection coming back from that process.

52

00:04:04,120  -->  00:04:10,040
But before we use this module, we need to list all the processes on the target computer.

53

00:04:11,000  -->  00:04:16,620
So to do that, we're going to remove everything and just do 'ps'.

54

00:04:19,000  -->  00:04:21,120
Now, you can see all the processes

55

00:04:21,120  -->  00:04:23,580
and their process ID in here

56

00:04:23,580  -->  00:04:25,720
and we're looking for Explorer,

57

00:04:28,180  -->  00:04:29,680
which is right here

58

00:04:30,120  -->  00:04:32,760
and the process ID is 240,

59

00:04:33,280  -->  00:04:35,000
so you want to keep that in mind.

60

00:04:35,540  -->  00:04:40,660
Now, we're gonna use our module using the same command that we did before, so you need to do

61

00:04:40,820  -->  00:04:44,580
'usemodule', double-tab to list all the modules

62

00:04:44,580  -->  00:04:47,920
and like I said, this is the same for all agents in operating systems.

63

00:04:48,340  -->  00:04:55,340
Then we're gonna put the model-- module name which is 'management/psinject',

64

00:04:56,040  -->  00:04:57,040
hit Enter.

65

00:04:57,840  -->  00:05:01,400
Now to display information about this specific module,

66

00:05:01,520  -->  00:05:03,160
we're just gonna do 'info'.

67

00:05:04,740  -->  00:05:08,680
So you can see the pattern here with the way that they made their commands in Empire.

68

00:05:09,680  -->  00:05:13,260
In here, you're gonna get specifications about this module.

69

00:05:13,620  -->  00:05:18,080
You're gonna get a description about what this module does, which is very handy,

70

00:05:18,080  -->  00:05:22,700
so you can just go on your own time, use, use modules, use different modules

71

00:05:22,880  -->  00:05:25,840
and just type 'info' to see what these modules do.

72

00:05:28,020  -->  00:05:32,380
Now, you can see this module is telling us that it will execute the process from memory

73

00:05:32,840  -->  00:05:34,040
and this is what we want.

74

00:05:34,580  -->  00:05:37,700
We can also see all the options that we can set,

75

00:05:38,160  -->  00:05:40,720
with the description about these options.

76

00:05:41,720  -->  00:05:44,940
Now, we can change these options exactly the same way

77

00:05:44,940  -->  00:05:49,400
that we were changing options with the listeners and with the stagers.

78

00:05:49,700  -->  00:05:54,100
So all you have to do is do 'set', option name and put the option value .

79

00:05:55,380  -->  00:05:58,680
So, the first thing that we want to change is the process ID.

80

00:05:58,840  -->  00:06:02,740
This is the process that Empire will inject the code in.

81

00:06:03,620  -->  00:06:08,560
So we're going to do 'set ProcId'

82

00:06:08,560  -->  00:06:14,640
and we're gonna put the process ID of Explorer, which was 240.

83

00:06:15,180  -->  00:06:19,860
Then the next important option that you need to set is the 'Listener'.

84

00:06:19,940  -->  00:06:26,280
This is the listener that you want it's code to be injected in that process, so that you get your connection back

85

00:06:26,900  -->  00:06:31,300
and this is already set to the right listener that I want to use which is 'http'

86

00:06:31,480  -->  00:06:35,700
but I'm just gonna change this option, just so that you know how to do that.

87

00:06:35,700  -->  00:06:37,880
Again all you have to do is just do 'set',

88

00:06:38,680  -->  00:06:40,320
option name,

89

00:06:40,820  -->  00:06:45,080
option value, which is 'http', I actually want to keep it 'http'.

90

00:06:46,100  -->  00:06:48,500
Now once you're done with everything, you can do 'info',

91

00:06:48,840  -->  00:06:51,440
to make sure that everything is set correctly

92

00:06:51,760  -->  00:06:54,060
and once you're ready, you can do 'execute'.

93

00:06:55,160  -->  00:06:58,580
So, you can see the pattern in the commands that we're using

94

00:06:58,920  -->  00:07:04,800
and these commands are going to be exactly the same regardless of what module you are trying to use,

95

00:07:04,960  -->  00:07:08,300
so whether it's a Windows module, OS X or Linux

96

00:07:08,300  -->  00:07:11,860
or even if it's in a different agent the, process is the same.

97

00:07:12,020  -->  00:07:15,860
You can do 'use module', double-tab to list all the modules.

98

00:07:16,080  -->  00:07:21,320
Then do 'use module, module name' to enter that module, do 'info' to see the options,

99

00:07:21,500  -->  00:07:25,840
do 'set, option name, option value',  the same way that we've always been doing it

100

00:07:26,040  -->  00:07:28,960
and then do 'execute' to execute this module.

101

00:07:29,860  -->  00:07:30,900
Now I'm going to hit Enter

102

00:07:31,640  -->  00:07:36,880
and that's going to execute the code for the http listener on the target computer

103

00:07:37,140  -->  00:07:42,140
and then once it does that, I should get a new connection with a new agent.

104

00:07:42,380  -->  00:07:44,700
And as you can see now I got another agent,

105

00:07:45,000  -->  00:07:47,000
so if I just go back

106

00:07:48,260  -->  00:07:49,560
and go back again

107

00:07:50,840  -->  00:07:52,360
and list the agents,

108

00:07:54,600  -->  00:07:56,700
you'll see, I have three agents now.

109

00:07:57,860  -->  00:08:01,660
This is my original agent that we, that we were using before

110

00:08:02,240  -->  00:08:03,920
and this is the new one.

111

00:08:04,420  -->  00:08:09,500
And notice the difference, this one is running from a powershell process 5008

112

00:08:09,840  -->  00:08:14,140
and this one is running from an explorer process 240.

113

00:08:14,760  -->  00:08:19,060
So, this is working from a process that's not suspicious at all.

114

00:08:19,400  -->  00:08:21,220
If we go to the Windows machine,

115

00:08:21,540  -->  00:08:22,900
look at the processes,

116

00:08:24,180  -->  00:08:29,520
you'll see that explorer has a connection now to '10.20.14.213'.

117

00:08:31,040  -->  00:08:33,440
Now you can do this with a lot of processes,

118

00:08:33,440  -->  00:08:40,080
a very good idea to inject your code from a Firefox or a Chrome process or Internet Explorer

119

00:08:40,080  -->  00:08:42,700
that way, it's not going to look suspicious at all

120

00:08:43,020  -->  00:08:46,160
because these processes show up under the network anyway

121

00:08:46,160  -->  00:08:48,240
because they make a lot of connections.

122

00:08:48,660  -->  00:08:54,820
And if you are listening on port 80 or 8080, then that's perfect because all websites use these ports.

123

00:08:55,040  -->  00:09:00,620
So, you're going to look-- it's going to look as if this target computer is just browsing a normal website.

124

00:09:01,700  -->  00:09:07,380
Now like I said, it is very important to know that these modules are specific to each agent

125

00:09:07,380  -->  00:09:09,940
and each operating system, they're not generic,

126

00:09:10,280  -->  00:09:13,800
but the process of using them is very generic.

127

00:09:13,800  -->  00:09:16,240
You can just do 'use modules', double-tab to list,

128

00:09:16,240  -->  00:09:19,440
use module, module name to use, set options

129

00:09:19,440  -->  00:09:20,640
and then execute.

130

00:09:22,060  -->  00:09:26,580
It's actually a very good idea for you now to just go and do 'use modules double-tab',

131

00:09:26,800  -->  00:09:30,520
see all the available modules and start experimenting with them.

132

00:09:30,680  -->  00:09:35,120
When you're inside the module, you can just do 'info' and you'll see what that module does

133

00:09:35,260  -->  00:09:37,920
and then just 'execute' it and see how it works.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:00,440  -->  00:00:07,740
In this lecture, I'm gonna show you how to escalate your privileges in an OS X machine using Empire modules.

2

00:00:08,720  -->  00:00:13,740
Now there is a number of modules that allow you to do that in Empire and in Meterpreter.

3

00:00:14,120  -->  00:00:16,380
Most of them rely on exploits.

4

00:00:17,180  -->  00:00:23,720
What I wanna show you today is a way of doing this without relying on any programs or on any exploits.

5

00:00:23,960  -->  00:00:28,300
It's a kind of a Social-Engineering way but it's gonna always work.

6

00:00:28,480  -->  00:00:32,720
It's also gonna be a nice lecture to have more examples on using modules

7

00:00:32,720  -->  00:00:35,941
and it's also nice to do this on OS X for a change

8

00:00:35,941  -->  00:00:39,420
because we haven't to use Empire modules much on OS X.

9

00:00:40,240  -->  00:00:43,340
So first of all, I have my agent in here

10

00:00:44,360  -->  00:00:47,340
and I'm gonna interact with it by doing 'interact',

11

00:00:49,280  -->  00:00:56,500
so to list all the modules just like any other operating system, we have to do 'usemodule' space double tab.

12

00:00:56,960  -->  00:01:02,280
So 'usemodule' and then space and then and then I'm gonna hit the tab button twice.

13

00:01:03,980  -->  00:01:06,280
And we have all the modules in here.

14

00:01:07,100  -->  00:01:12,220
Now, usually the private escalation modules start with 'privesc'.

15

00:01:14,160  -->  00:01:18,580
Now you can see these ones start with Linux, so they only work on Linux systems.

16

00:01:19,060  -->  00:01:27,000
We also have 'multi' ones which work with multi which means OS X and Linux and Unix-like operating systems

17

00:01:27,500  -->  00:01:34,040
and then we have an OS X one, which actually relies on an existing sudo sessions,

18

00:01:34,040  -->  00:01:38,200
so if there is an existing logged in admin, this will allow you to become an admin.

19

00:01:38,500  -->  00:01:44,260
But it won't work, if there is no logged in admin, which is our case, the user is logged in as a normal user.

20

00:01:45,140  -->  00:01:50,280
So the one I wanna use is 'privesc/multi/sudo_spawn'

21

00:01:51,000  -->  00:01:56,500
and basically what this does, it just executes another stager with admin privileges.

22

00:01:57,460  -->  00:02:02,780
So for this to work, you need to know the password but we actually don't know the password,

23

00:02:03,160  -->  00:02:07,200
so to get the password, now again there is ways relying on exploits and all that

24

00:02:07,420  -->  00:02:10,400
but we're gonna have a look on a Social-Engineering way

25

00:02:10,900  -->  00:02:19,320
and to do that, we're gonna use another module and that module is gonna be 'collection/osx/prompt'.

26

00:02:20,640  -->  00:02:23,420
And what's this gonna do, it's gonna start the app store

27

00:02:23,980  -->  00:02:26,420
and then it's gonna ask the user for their password

28

00:02:27,160  -->  00:02:30,820
when the user enters the password, we're gonna get it, then we're gonna launch the

29

00:02:30,820  -->  00:02:34,000
'privesc/sudo_spawn', which will make us admin.

30

00:02:35,380  -->  00:02:38,480
So it's actually very simple, it might sound complicated but it's very simple.

31

00:02:38,720  -->  00:02:43,840
The first module we're gonna be using is this one, so I'm just gonna copy it and paste it

32

00:02:45,560  -->  00:02:46,920
and if we do 'info',

33

00:02:48,260  -->  00:02:50,380
you'll see the options are very simple here

34

00:02:50,620  -->  00:02:54,920
and you don't really need to configure anything here basically, the description says it all,

35

00:02:54,920  -->  00:02:58,620
it'll show a prompt and it will ask the user for their password,

36

00:02:59,000  -->  00:03:00,660
so we're just gonna do 'execute'.

37

00:03:01,880  -->  00:03:09,840
And this is telling us that this 'module is not opsec safe' which basically means is that it's gonna touch the disk.

38

00:03:10,280  -->  00:03:17,320
So we will believe in traces of ourself in the system, so if a forensics person comes in and scans the disk,

39

00:03:17,560  -->  00:03:20,800
they might be able to identify that there was a hacker in there.

40

00:03:21,580  -->  00:03:23,160
So we're gonna say, 'that's okay'.

41

00:03:24,680  -->  00:03:29,100
And if we come here, you'll see that the app store will automatically open

42

00:03:29,320  -->  00:03:31,400
and it's gonna ask me for my password.

43

00:03:32,160  -->  00:03:36,560
Now if you're a Linux user, you're used to put in your password when using the app store,

44

00:03:36,560  -->  00:03:39,860
it often asks you for your password, for your Apple account password

45

00:03:40,080  -->  00:03:42,940
or for your root password, we're installing stuff.

46

00:03:43,520  -->  00:03:48,780
So I'm gonna put my password which is actually just '6 zaid', so 'zaidzaidzaidzaidzaidzaid',

47

00:03:49,500  -->  00:03:50,640
I'm gonna hit 'OK'.

48

00:03:51,640  -->  00:03:52,960
And if we come here,

49

00:03:53,640  -->  00:03:56,000
you'll see that we got our password.

50

00:03:57,760  -->  00:04:02,520
So that's pretty cool, now we know the password but if we do 'sysinfo'

51

00:04:03,700  -->  00:04:05,260
actually I need to do 'back' first,

52

00:04:06,160  -->  00:04:08,520
to get out of this module and then do 'sysinfo'.

53

00:04:10,880  -->  00:04:18,460
You'll see that my high integrity is still '0' which means I'm not admin, I'm still a normal user, so if I do 'whoami'.

54

00:04:20,640  -->  00:04:21,980
I'm a normal user.

55

00:04:22,940  -->  00:04:30,560
So, what we want to do is we want to become an admin, we want to become the administrator of the system

56

00:04:30,900  -->  00:04:34,180
and to do that like I said, we're gonna use another module.

57

00:04:34,460  -->  00:04:36,080
So, we're gonna do 'usemodule'.

58

00:04:38,640  -->  00:04:43,320
And that module was in the privesc category, so I'm just gonna type 'privesc'

59

00:04:44,000  -->  00:04:49,860
and then it was a 'multi' and then if we double-Tab to show us all the options

60

00:04:50,240  -->  00:04:53,340
and the one that we want to use a 'sudo_spawn'.

61

00:04:55,020  -->  00:04:57,620
We're gonna do 'info' to see all the information

62

00:04:58,600  -->  00:05:02,140
and in here, you can see that there is a number of options that we need to set.

63

00:05:02,620  -->  00:05:08,100
The first one is, we need to set the Listener and we're going to set that to 'http1'

64

00:05:08,100  -->  00:05:10,860
because this is the listener that I'm using for this network.

65

00:05:13,160  -->  00:05:17,320
So, we're setting the options exactly the same way that we always set the options,

66

00:05:17,320  -->  00:05:19,540
set option name, option value.

67

00:05:20,980  -->  00:05:23,780
The next thing that we want to set is the password

68

00:05:23,780  -->  00:05:27,940
and you can see the description here, it says, the 'password for sudo'.

69

00:05:28,620  -->  00:05:32,680
Now we already got this from the previous module, so we're gonna set that.

70

00:05:34,320  -->  00:05:36,140
And that was six z's ('zzzzzz').

71

00:05:37,660  -->  00:05:41,280
Now, I'm gonna do 'info' to make sure that my information is correct

72

00:05:42,820  -->  00:05:44,600
and everything is set up properly.

73

00:05:44,740  -->  00:05:50,520
Now to execute this, same way that we always execute modules, you just have to type 'execute'.

74

00:05:52,200  -->  00:05:59,760
Now what this is gonna do, it's gonna create another connection with root access, with the sudo access.

75

00:06:00,400  -->  00:06:06,320
So as you can see, now we got an agent from the target computer, so if I just go back

76

00:06:08,280  -->  00:06:11,180
and back and 'list' my agents.

77

00:06:12,740  -->  00:06:17,620
You'll see that I have a new connection in here, so that's my-- that was my original connection

78

00:06:18,060  -->  00:06:24,620
and now I have a new connection and the username in here is 'root' it's not 'zOOz', its 'root'.

79

00:06:25,780  -->  00:06:27,300
So if I interact with this

80

00:06:30,520  -->  00:06:31,860
and do 'sysinfo',

81

00:06:34,220  -->  00:06:37,080
you will see that I'm running in high integrity,

82

00:06:37,820  -->  00:06:42,140
which means that I am root at the moment, so if I do 'whoami'.

83

00:06:44,960  -->  00:06:49,740
As you can see, I am root and that means I can do anything I want on the system.

84

00:06:49,940  -->  00:06:53,580
I can access all the components, I can access all the file system,

85

00:06:53,580  -->  00:06:57,300
I can access all the user accounts, I'm not limited to my user

86

00:06:57,300  -->  00:07:02,160
and I can basically, do anything I want or execute any command I want.

87

00:07:03,080  -->  00:07:07,920
Now like I said, this is not the only method to escalate your privileges and become root

88

00:07:08,320  -->  00:07:15,380
but this method does not rely on any programs, it does not rely on any exploits, therefore it will always work.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:00,640  -->  00:00:06,480
In this lecture I'd like to show you how to maintain your access on an OS X machine.

2

00:00:07,400  -->  00:00:15,060
And it's going to be a nice example to show how useful it is to be running in high integrity or as root.

3

00:00:16,320  -->  00:00:18,280
Now, if we just do use modules.

4

00:00:20,040  -->  00:00:21,260
And double-tap.

5

00:00:23,100  -->  00:00:28,440
You'll see that there is a number of modules in here that start with 'persistence'.

6

00:00:30,040  -->  00:00:36,380
Now, all these modules allow you or should allow you to maintain your access on the target computer,

7

00:00:36,380  -->  00:00:39,740
so even if it restarts or if they terminate your agent,

8

00:00:40,040  -->  00:00:42,460
you'll still have access to that computer.

9

00:00:43,200  -->  00:00:46,220
Now, not all of these require you to be admin

10

00:00:46,580  -->  00:00:53,580
but the most reliable one is the one that require you to be admin which is the 'launchdaemonexecutable'.

11

00:00:54,420  -->  00:00:59,720
Other modules rely on vulnerabilities and some of them are just not very reliable.

12

00:01:00,180  -->  00:01:04,500
Also we have the 'mail' one in here which doesn't require you to be admin

13

00:01:04,720  -->  00:01:10,180
but it only works using, if the target person uses the native Mail app.

14

00:01:11,000  -->  00:01:17,820
The idea behind, it is very cool though because you can trigger the backdoor by sending an email to the target.

15

00:01:18,280  -->  00:01:21,520
So, once you send an email with a specific subject,

16

00:01:21,740  -->  00:01:25,280
if the target uses up the native Apple Mail app,

17

00:01:25,500  -->  00:01:29,080
this will trigger your backdoor and then the target will connect back to you

18

00:01:29,560  -->  00:01:36,300
but if the target uses a normal web browser to access their email, then this is just not going to work.

19

00:01:36,760  -->  00:01:41,280
That's why and because it's going to be a very good example for us in this situation.

20

00:01:41,640  -->  00:01:44,900
I'm gonna show you how to use the 'launchdaemonexecutable'.

21

00:01:45,740  -->  00:01:47,120
So, I'm going to copy  this.

22

00:01:47,740  -->  00:01:49,480
And I'm going to paste it.

23

00:01:51,920  -->  00:01:55,700
Then, we're going to do 'info' to see the information and the options that we can set.

24

00:01:57,040  -->  00:02:00,680
Now the first thing that I'd like to note is, if you look in here,

25

00:02:01,020  -->  00:02:03,520
you'll see 'NeedsAdmin' is set to 'True',

26

00:02:03,980  -->  00:02:06,760
so that means you need to be admin for this to work.

27

00:02:08,040  -->  00:02:10,600
You can also see the options in here

28

00:02:11,080  -->  00:02:15,700
and usually the two main options that you want to set is, the 'DaemonLocation'.

29

00:02:16,060  -->  00:02:18,960
This is the location where the daemon is going to be installed

30

00:02:19,360  -->  00:02:24,800
and I've already set it to '/Library/Application/Support' and I set the name to 'Test'.

31

00:02:25,460  -->  00:02:29,120
Now, I'm just going to set this in front of you, just for the sake of completion.

32

00:02:29,340  -->  00:02:36,880
So, I'm going to do 'set DaemonLocation /Library/Application Support/'

33

00:02:36,880  -->  00:02:41,140
and I'm gonna call my launcher 'QuickTimeDamon'.

34

00:02:45,240  -->  00:02:48,920
So, it's just like any other command. We're doing 'set' to set an option,

35

00:02:49,260  -->  00:02:53,500
the option name is 'DamonLocation' and then we specified the location.

36

00:02:54,680  -->  00:02:56,080
I'm gonna hit Enter

37

00:02:56,500  -->  00:02:59,900
and the next thing that you'd want to do is set the Listener.

38

00:03:00,980  -->  00:03:04,700
Now again, this is set to my right listener which is 'http1'.

39

00:03:05,000  -->  00:03:07,600
This is the listener that you want to get the connection on.

40

00:03:08,220  -->  00:03:11,680
So, all you have to do is just do 'set Listener http1'.

41

00:03:13,660  -->  00:03:16,280
Once you're done with everything, you can do 'info'

42

00:03:17,040  -->  00:03:19,640
and you'll have all the information in front of you.

43

00:03:20,560  -->  00:03:26,340
Now, you can also set the daemon name and let's set this to something that corresponds to our launcher.

44

00:03:26,540  -->  00:03:28,500
So, I'm gonna do 'set DaemonName'

45

00:03:29,260  -->  00:03:34,400
and let's, let's just call it 'com.apple.QuickTime'.

46

00:03:36,500  -->  00:03:43,140
Now, I'm setting these names to make them look a little bit like the native names that Apple use,

47

00:03:43,380  -->  00:03:47,060
so that the target person doesn't get very suspicious.

48

00:03:47,840  -->  00:03:50,600
Once we're done with everything, we can do 'execute'.

49

00:03:52,360  -->  00:03:54,920
And then it's telling us that this is
not opsec safe

50

00:03:55,200  -->  00:03:57,660
because it's going to touch the hard disk,

51

00:03:57,780  -->  00:04:04,500
so if someone analyzes this hard disk forensically, they'll be able to identify that there was a hacker here.

52

00:04:04,940  -->  00:04:06,300
I'm gonna say, "Yeah, that's fine."

53

00:04:07,860  -->  00:04:12,500
And remember this is just a normal module so we're setting the options and run execute,

54

00:04:12,940  -->  00:04:15,480
exactly the same way that we run it with other modules.

55

00:04:16,040  -->  00:04:19,900
And as you can see now, I've got my connection already from my agent

56

00:04:20,400  -->  00:04:22,320
but I'm just going to go to the agents.

57

00:04:23,740  -->  00:04:26,440
And I just got my most recent connection.

58

00:04:26,920  -->  00:04:29,540
Now, this is cool but this is not what we are looking for,

59

00:04:29,740  -->  00:04:34,040
we want the connection to come back to us every time the computer restarts.

60

00:04:34,580  -->  00:04:38,840
So, this is what I'm gonna do. I'm gonna come here and I'm just going to restart the computer.

61

00:04:46,260  -->  00:04:50,660
Now, even before the target person logged in because we are running as root.

62

00:04:51,040  -->  00:04:54,300
As you can see, we get a new connection straight away.

63

00:04:56,420  -->  00:04:58,520
So, if I do 'agents' now, if I do 'list',

64

00:04:59,000  -->  00:05:01,160
you'll see I have my new connection

65

00:05:01,640  -->  00:05:03,200
and I can just interact with this.

66

00:05:08,920  -->  00:05:10,800
And do whatever I want on the system.

67

00:05:12,480  -->  00:05:16,460
So basically, now I can connect to the system, anytime I want,

68

00:05:16,460  -->  00:05:22,380
as soon as I launch my Empire, I'll have a connection coming back to me from the target computer.

69

00:05:22,640  -->  00:05:26,800
There is a 'persistent' agent now installed in the target computer.

70

00:05:27,220  -->  00:05:33,020
So, even if the target tries to terminate this connection, it will automatically just connect back to me.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================

# Section 19: Security

1

00:00:00,940  -->  00:00:08,740
We've seen before how easy it is to send an email and pretend to be any person or any company that we want.

2

00:00:09,880  -->  00:00:12,500
So, you have an example here that we used before

3

00:00:12,680  -->  00:00:18,440
and I use this to send an e-mail to pretend to be a friend to be Mohammad Askar,

4

00:00:18,780  -->  00:00:21,840
even though that's Mohamed Askar didn't actually send this e-mail.

5

00:00:23,020  -->  00:00:25,600
Now, you can see that we have the picture of Mohammad coming up.

6

00:00:26,080  -->  00:00:29,660
We can see that it's saying that the e-mail came from Mohammad Askar,

7

00:00:30,060  -->  00:00:32,200
if we click on the arrow here.

8

00:00:34,180  -->  00:00:38,420
You'll see that the e-mails coming from that 'm.askar@isecur1ty.org'.

9

00:00:39,060  -->  00:00:43,840
And if we click on this, we'll actually go to his Google Plus page

10

00:00:44,200  -->  00:00:49,380
which is very, very, very convincing and at the same time it's very scary

11

00:00:49,380  -->  00:00:55,300
because anyone can do this and target you and social engineer you to do something that they want

12

00:00:55,620  -->  00:00:59,060
and then they'll be able to hack into your computer or into your company

13

00:00:59,340  -->  00:01:01,220
or use you to hack into other people.

14

00:01:03,440  -->  00:01:07,600
Now, in this lecture I want to show you how to spot fake e-mail

15

00:01:07,840  -->  00:01:12,380
and know that this person or this e-mail is not actually sent from Mohammad Askar.

16

00:01:13,920  -->  00:01:18,252
Now, the first thing that you should spot is after the name it says,

17

00:01:18,260  -->  00:01:27,340
that this was sent via 'orbit.eternalimpact.info', so it's being sent from a strange domain name.

18

00:01:28,060  -->  00:01:32,660
Now, this domain name could be different depending on the domain that the hacker is using.

19

00:01:33,120  -->  00:01:36,640
But you'll also see this 'via something', coming up in here

20

00:01:36,980  -->  00:01:39,420
which should make you feel a bit suspicious

21

00:01:39,420  -->  00:01:43,000
because if you look at the normal emails that I get from Mohammad,

22

00:01:43,500  -->  00:01:45,300
I'll see that they come normally

23

00:01:45,520  -->  00:01:49,860
and I don't see this extra message in here saying that this came 'via something else'.

24

00:01:51,880  -->  00:01:55,140
Now, also if we click on this little arrow here

25

00:01:55,760  -->  00:01:58,140
and show the original, right here.

26

00:01:59,580  -->  00:02:01,540
Now, if you're on Hotmail or on a different mail,

27

00:02:02,000  -->  00:02:04,700
I think you right click and view 'Message Source'

28

00:02:04,980  -->  00:02:06,060
but you get the idea,

29

00:02:06,480  -->  00:02:12,980
in here, this shows you information about the headers, about the servers, used to send the e-mail

30

00:02:13,320  -->  00:02:15,640
and the source of the e-mail.

31

00:02:16,820  -->  00:02:22,300
Now again, this can be different depending on the way that the target sent the fake e-mail

32

00:02:22,300  -->  00:02:24,960
because there is a number of ways to send fake e-mails.

33

00:02:25,220  -->  00:02:28,880
But the bottom line is when you look at this in here,

34

00:02:29,160  -->  00:02:33,880
you should be able to see that this e-mail is not coming from 'google.com'

35

00:02:34,180  -->  00:02:37,200
or from 'isecur1ty.org' which is in my example.

36

00:02:38,860  -->  00:02:43,460
Looking at this, we can see that this is coming from 'anonymousemail.me'.

37

00:02:44,060  -->  00:02:50,140
And we can see this coming up in a different place as you can see in here, the Return-Path is anonymous email

38

00:02:50,580  -->  00:02:56,780
and you can see in here, it's being sent from 'anonymousemail.me:/public_html".

39

00:02:57,800  -->  00:03:01,380
Now, there is a number of indications in the source code in here

40

00:03:01,700  -->  00:03:08,540
that tell us that this message was sent from a fake domain or a domain that we shouldn't be trusting.

41

00:03:08,540  -->  00:03:11,980
So again in here, you can see that this is sent from 'anonymous.me'.

42

00:03:12,340  -->  00:03:16,380
You can see the domain names that they're using are not very trustable domains.

43

00:03:16,660  -->  00:03:18,520
They're pretending to be iSecur1ty,

44

00:03:18,840  -->  00:03:25,140
but the message isn't coming from iSecur1ty and it's not coming from Google or Hotmail or a trusted mail server.

45

00:03:25,860  -->  00:03:29,380
So looking at this, should tell you that this is a fake email

46

00:03:29,640  -->  00:03:32,360
and you shouldn't really trust anything, that's said in here.

47

00:03:33,460  -->  00:03:38,040
Now again, the information here could be different depending on the method that the target used,

48

00:03:38,240  -->  00:03:42,600
to send the fake email and depending on the domains and the services that they used.

49

00:03:43,160  -->  00:03:44,980
So, you might see some domains that you think

50

00:03:45,520  -->  00:03:50,920
that you can trust them, they might look trustworthy or they might look, not very suspicious,

51

00:03:50,960  -->  00:03:53,600
they, they might not say anonymous email like this.

52

00:03:54,120  -->  00:03:59,900
But again, if it's not coming from the domain that it's claiming to be, so in my case it's 'isecur1ty.org',

53

00:04:00,360  -->  00:04:04,540
and if it's not coming from Google or one of our trusted mail server,

54

00:04:04,820  -->  00:04:07,540
then there is a very high chance that this is a fake email.

55

00:04:09,040  -->  00:04:15,300
Now, the best way to actually make sure that this is a right or a fake e-mail is to reply to this person.

56

00:04:17,200  -->  00:04:19,180
So, we can just type a message to Mohammad

57

00:04:19,460  -->  00:04:25,540
and just say here, "I think someone is trying to pretend to be you. I wasn't sure that you want me to open this file.

58

00:04:25,740  -->  00:04:28,720
Can you please confirm, it's you that's trying to do this?"

59

00:04:28,860  -->  00:04:32,420
And then this letter is, if we look here at the reply,

60

00:04:32,840  -->  00:04:37,180
you'll see that this is actually going to go to 'm.askar@isecur1ty.org'.

61

00:04:37,620  -->  00:04:39,660
So, it's not going to go to the fake e-mail.

62

00:04:40,300  -->  00:04:42,100
This way Mohammad is going to receive this.

63

00:04:42,480  -->  00:04:47,120
He's going to know that he didn't send me this and he's going to tell me here, "Be careful! I didn't send this.

64

00:04:47,400  -->  00:04:50,100
This is probably someone trying to hack into your account."

65

00:04:52,180  -->  00:04:53,680
Now, that's the first scenario.

66

00:04:54,320  -->  00:04:58,520
The second scenario; the hacker can actually configure the reply to e-mail,

67

00:04:58,760  -->  00:05:01,280
to send to an e-mail that they have access to.

68

00:05:02,400  -->  00:05:05,080
Now in that case, if we click on the reply in here,

69

00:05:05,620  -->  00:05:08,560
you'll actually see that this won't go to Mohammad Askar

70

00:05:08,860  -->  00:05:13,180
and it's going to go to a fake or two or just a different e-mail than Mohammad,

71

00:05:13,180  -->  00:05:15,880
just might be called 'mohammadaskar@gmail.com'

72

00:05:16,280  -->  00:05:19,280
but it's just not going to be the one that sent the messages,

73

00:05:19,280  -->  00:05:22,200
it's not going to be that 'askar@isecur1ty.org'.

74

00:05:22,680  -->  00:05:27,580
And that way, you'll be able to know that again this message was not sent by your friend

75

00:05:27,820  -->  00:05:30,480
but it's sent by someone who is trying to be them.

76

00:05:31,980  -->  00:05:35,120
So, there's two outcomes of sending a message to a person.

77

00:05:35,480  -->  00:05:42,200
The first one is you see the reply to address in here and it's going to be a weird reply address,

78

00:05:42,400  -->  00:05:45,740
so you are going to know that this is going to go to a person that's not your friend

79

00:05:46,620  -->  00:05:49,760
or you will see the address of your friend.

80

00:05:50,040  -->  00:05:52,000
And when you ask them if that's them,

81

00:05:52,440  -->  00:05:53,820
they will get that message.

82

00:05:54,120  -->  00:05:56,540
And obviously, because they're your friend, they're going to tell you,

83

00:05:56,540  -->  00:05:59,420
" You know, I didn't send you this. Be careful! This is a hacker."

84

00:06:00,220  -->  00:06:04,460
This way you'll be able to know for sure whether this message was sent from your friend or not.

85

00:06:04,700  -->  00:06:07,300
And then, you'll know if you should trust it or not.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:00,880  -->  00:00:06,020
In this lecture, I'd like to show you how to protect yourself from 'browser exploits'.

2

00:00:06,700  -->  00:00:12,680
Now, most browser exploits unless they exploit a vulnerability in your browser.

3

00:00:13,000  -->  00:00:19,460
They will rely on some JavaScript code to do stuff on your browser to inject the Keylogger,

4

00:00:19,540  -->  00:00:23,860
to execute code, to show you fake updates and so on.

5

00:00:24,500  -->  00:00:27,180
Now if your browser has a vulnerability,

6

00:00:27,200  -->  00:00:32,900
if the actual browser is vulnerable to a buffer overflow or a code execution vulnerability,

7

00:00:33,040  -->  00:00:35,940
then what I'm going to show you now, is not really going to protect you

8

00:00:36,040  -->  00:00:41,880
and the only way to protect against that is to stay up to date with the latest version of the browser.

9

00:00:42,380  -->  00:00:46,600
What I want to show you in this lecture, is how to protect yourself from things

10

00:00:46,600  -->  00:00:50,880
like BeEF framework and other browser exploitation frameworks

11

00:00:50,880  -->  00:00:54,220
that will execute JavaScript code on your browser.

12

00:00:55,740  -->  00:00:57,740
So, the solution to this is very simple,

13

00:00:57,740  -->  00:01:04,760
all you have to do is just prevent JavaScript from working on all websites except the ones

14

00:01:04,760  -->  00:01:08,220
that you really trust like Facebook, Google and all of that.

15

00:01:08,720  -->  00:01:15,740
So to do that, we're going to go to Google and look for an-- a plugin called 'no javascript'.

16

00:01:16,060  -->  00:01:21,980
Now there is versions for Firefox and for Chrome, so just install the one that's related to your browser.

17

00:01:23,040  -->  00:01:28,720
And you can see, I have here the first result is an Add-on that will prevent JavaScript from working.

18

00:01:29,420  -->  00:01:31,880
I'm going to add this Add-on to my Firefox

19

00:01:33,480  -->  00:01:34,740
and I'm going to install it

20

00:01:37,120  -->  00:01:39,940
and then I'm just going to restart my Firefox.

21

00:01:42,260  -->  00:01:43,620
Now I'm gonna close this

22

00:01:43,960  -->  00:01:51,080
and as you can see, it's already stopping JavaScript code from getting executed on-- even on Mozilla's page.

23

00:01:51,760  -->  00:01:54,440
Now obviously, might not want this to always happen

24

00:01:54,440  -->  00:01:58,420
and I'll show you how to allow JavaScript on certain pages.

25

00:01:59,000  -->  00:02:04,560
For now, let's go to the evil URL that contains my BeEF hook code

26

00:02:04,660  -->  00:02:06,280
and see, if beEF is gonna work.

27

00:02:06,860  -->  00:02:13,320
So that was at '10.20.14.213' and it was just at the index, so I can just go now

28

00:02:14,680  -->  00:02:18,800
and as you can see my, my webpage works, the fake webpage,

29

00:02:19,220  -->  00:02:23,700
but in here at the bottom, you see that there is scripts that are forbidden.

30

00:02:24,000  -->  00:02:29,000
And if we go here on the Kali machine, you'll see that the browser didn't come online,

31

00:02:29,000  -->  00:02:34,580
so the browser didn't get hooked to BeEF, even though they're browsing the BeEF hook page.

32

00:02:35,200  -->  00:02:39,540
Now in here, if we go to YouTube for example,

33

00:02:44,580  -->  00:02:47,500
you'll see that it's still blocking JavaScript code,

34

00:02:47,780  -->  00:02:52,820
but the website is working normal so you can just use the website the way it is

35

00:02:52,820  -->  00:02:58,760
but if the website isn't functioning the way that you expect it, you can just go to Options

36

00:02:59,220  -->  00:03:00,880
and allow this page.

37

00:03:02,060  -->  00:03:06,380
Now obviously, you want to only do this to web pages that you trust,

38

00:03:06,700  -->  00:03:11,780
that you're sure that they want execute bad or dangerous code on your browser

39

00:03:12,180  -->  00:03:14,760
and then keep this on for all other websites.

40

00:03:14,960  -->  00:03:19,280
So, if you get social engineered or if you click on a website by mistake,

41

00:03:19,560  -->  00:03:24,440
then any JavaScript code in that page will not be executed on your browser

42

00:03:24,640  -->  00:03:26,500
because you're using this plugin.

43

00:03:26,880  -->  00:03:31,760
Now like I said, most browser exploitation frameworks use JavaScript

44

00:03:32,000  -->  00:03:36,520
and using this method, you'll be able to protect yourself from these frameworks.

45

00:03:36,800  -->  00:03:43,980
The only way that you'll be-- that people will be able to hack into your computer through the browser,

46

00:03:44,220  -->  00:03:50,860
is if they exploit a vulnerability in your actual browser, like a code execution or a buffer overflow.

47

00:03:51,160  -->  00:03:54,408
And like I said, if  you want to protect yourself from that,

48

00:03:54,408  -->  00:03:58,240
then just make sure you're always using the latest update of the browser,

49

00:03:58,520  -->  00:04:03,100
don't use Internet Explorer or Edge and stick with Firefox and Chrome.

50

00:04:07,420  -->  00:04:11,180
Also, always be careful when downloading stuff and downloading files

51

00:04:11,440  -->  00:04:16,200
from untrusted websites or untrusted sources or untrusted emails.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:01,880  -->  00:00:08,180
So the Trojans that we created so far are very cool, they can bypass antivirus programs.

2

00:00:09,680  -->  00:00:15,680
They run two pieces of code; the first one runs in the background which runs our own code,

3

00:00:15,680  -->  00:00:21,540
it-- which does what we want to do, for example, open a port or connect back to us and give us a Shell.

4

00:00:22,160  -->  00:00:25,700
And it runs another piece of code that the user expects,

5

00:00:25,700  -->  00:00:30,980
so it could display an image, it could play an MP3 or display a PDF file.

6

00:00:32,740  -->  00:00:36,800
This functionality makes it very difficult to detect.

7

00:00:38,120  -->  00:00:43,220
So the best thing to do, is the first thing is check the properties of the file

8

00:00:43,380  -->  00:00:46,380
and make sure that it is, what it's claiming to be.

9

00:00:49,860  -->  00:00:55,860
So, we have our GTR picture here and we can see that it's JPG, so it looks like a picture

10

00:00:55,980  -->  00:01:01,140
and has an icon and if I run it, I'll get a picture like we've seen in previous videos.

11

00:01:01,620  -->  00:01:05,440
Well, let's go right-click and go on Properties.

12

00:01:07,380  -->  00:01:11,400
And when we go to the properties, you'll see that this is an application, it's not a picture.

13

00:01:11,900  -->  00:01:19,280
So, same goes with PDF, same goes with MP3, it should say MP3, if it was on MP3,

14

00:01:19,380  -->  00:01:25,100
if it should see a-- say a PDF, if, if it was a PDF and it should say a JPG if it is a JPG.

15

00:01:25,460  -->  00:01:30,000
But in this case, we can see that it's saying and it's telling us that it's an executable.

16

00:01:31,440  -->  00:01:34,720
Going on the details, you'll see that it is an application,

17

00:01:35,540  -->  00:01:39,500
so it's not a picture, if it was a picture, it should tell you that it's a picture.

18

00:01:40,140  -->  00:01:43,600
So from here, we'll know that we're being fooled by this.

19

00:01:43,980  -->  00:01:46,480
Another thing, you can play with the file name

20

00:01:46,560  -->  00:01:50,560
and you'll be able to reset it, if you just rename the file to anything else,

21

00:01:50,800  -->  00:01:57,500
you'll see that it's an 'exe' file and it's not a JPG, so if I just do it, change it to 'test',

22

00:01:57,820  -->  00:02:00,780
you'll see that the name has been changed to test.

23

00:02:02,040  -->  00:02:07,220
Now let's assume, this Trojan was combined with an executable,

24

00:02:07,620  -->  00:02:13,760
so if you run it, it was-- you were expecting to get an 'exe' and you were expecting an application.

25

00:02:14,140  -->  00:02:18,400
So if it's-- let's assume that it's combined with Download Accelerator Plus

26

00:02:18,400  -->  00:02:22,020
instead of being combined with a picture li-- like so.

27

00:02:22,600  -->  00:02:27,480
So the task is gonna be more difficult because you are expecting an application anyway.

28

00:02:28,320  -->  00:02:30,000
So, let's try to run this.

29

00:02:32,360  -->  00:02:35,480
Now obviously, again with the picture and the-- with the PDF,

30

00:02:35,480  -->  00:02:38,800
Windows will tell you that you're trying to run an executable

31

00:02:39,320  -->  00:02:44,040
but if you're running-- if you're expecting an executable, then you're gonna run it anyway.

32

00:02:44,220  -->  00:02:45,800
Such as the case with DEB

33

00:02:46,140  -->  00:02:52,760
and this will obviously, play the executable that you're looking for and send a reverse session to Kali.

34

00:02:58,840  -->  00:03:03,900
So what I'm going to do is, I'm gonna go to us-- a tool called Resource Manager.

35

00:03:08,400  -->  00:03:13,460
And from this tool, if you go to the Network tab, I'm already in the Network tab,

36

00:03:13,980  -->  00:03:17,660
you'll be able to see all the open ports in your machine.

37

00:03:19,560  -->  00:03:28,970
And we can see here, we have port 8080 and it's connecting to an IP address which is '10.20.14.203'.

38

00:03:29,440  -->  00:03:36,000
Now obviously 88 is not very suspicious, so even if it was on 80 then it could look not as sus-- suspicious

39

00:03:36,380  -->  00:03:42,220
and also it's coming from a process called 'Browser', which again is not very suspicious.

40

00:03:44,180  -->  00:03:46,500
The suspicious part is the Remote Address,

41

00:03:46,760  -->  00:03:50,560
so it's accessing an address of '10.20.14.203'

42

00:03:50,620  -->  00:03:52,840
that we just don't even know what it is,

43

00:03:52,840  -->  00:03:58,760
if it was a website, then putting this in the browser should take you to a br-- to a website

44

00:03:58,820  -->  00:04:00,820
or to a server of that website.

45

00:04:01,300  -->  00:04:07,140
And in most cases, if this was a hacker computer, it will not take you to a website or anything

46

00:04:07,140  -->  00:04:10,400
and then you'll know that this person is an attacker.

47

00:04:12,640  -->  00:04:17,720
To verify this, you can use tools called 'Reverse DNS Lookup'.

48

00:04:18,300  -->  00:04:25,540
What that does is, is basically, you give it an IP and it should tell you what website this IP belongs to

49

00:04:25,540  -->  00:04:28,320
or which domain the IP belongs to.

50

00:04:29,000  -->  00:04:30,760
So let's have an example on Facebook,

51

00:04:30,760  -->  00:04:35,400
so let's say in your Resource Manager you see an IP and you're suspicious about it.

52

00:04:36,000  -->  00:04:39,920
So, I'm actually gonna get you a proper IP address for Facebook.

53

00:04:48,120  -->  00:04:53,360
So let's say for example you see in this IP, there is a connection on port 80 going to this IP,

54

00:04:53,840  -->  00:04:57,060
so if you copy this and go on Google

55

00:04:59,820  -->  00:05:01,780
and search for 'reverse dns'.

56

00:05:08,360  -->  00:05:12,320
So I'm gonna put the IP in here, the IP that you see in your resources

57

00:05:12,880  -->  00:05:17,220
and you look it up, if it's for a proper website then there is nothing to be concerned about,

58

00:05:17,540  -->  00:05:22,260
if it looks suspicious then you'll know that this is going to a suspicious person.

59

00:05:22,640  -->  00:05:26,600
Now, if you see something like this and it's going to Facebook and you're browsing Facebook,

60

00:05:26,820  -->  00:05:31,260
then this is normal, you're using Facebook so there's a connection between, between you and Facebook.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

00:00:01,260  -->  00:00:07,580
Another way to discover malicious files is to use a Sandbox.

2

00:00:08,080  -->  00:00:14,480
A Sandbox is basically, a place where your file will be executed and analyzed.

3

00:00:14,480  -->  00:00:17,420
They will check if the-- any of-- any ports will be opened,

4

00:00:17,580  -->  00:00:19,980
if it's gonna modify registry entries,

5

00:00:20,120  -->  00:00:24,280
if it's gonna do any suspicious stuff, so it's not an anti-virus program.

6

00:00:24,540  -->  00:00:31,080
The your-- your Trojan might pass antivirus programs like we did, our Trojan passes all antivirus programs

7

00:00:31,300  -->  00:00:35,380
but the Sandbox applications or the Sandbox environments

8

00:00:35,680  -->  00:00:38,820
will run this into a place, into a controlled environment

9

00:00:38,960  -->  00:00:44,220
and try to see, if it does anything suspicious and give you a report to that.

10

00:00:45,280  -->  00:00:47,740
You can Google against Sandbox, online.

11

00:00:49,200  -->  00:00:53,200
And an example of it is a website called 'Hybrid Analysis'.

12

00:00:53,860  -->  00:00:58,700
Using the website is very simple, all you have to do is just go to the URL.

13

00:01:04,520  -->  00:01:07,280
And select your file and upload it.

14

00:01:09,660  -->  00:01:11,180
Now, I've done this already.

15

00:01:11,760  -->  00:01:13,160
So I'm just gonna show you the report

16

00:01:13,160  -->  00:01:17,460
because analyzing the file and doing the report might-- might take some time

17

00:01:18,000  -->  00:01:19,380
so once you get the report,

18

00:01:20,360  -->  00:01:22,240
you'll see some basic information,

19

00:01:22,980  -->  00:01:26,360
you'll see that there was malicious indicators, have been found.

20

00:01:26,540  -->  00:01:32,560
Now, it's hiding it from you and you have to use the full version and pay for it to see them

21

00:01:32,680  -->  00:01:35,880
but you don't really need to see them, if you read the whole report

22

00:01:36,140  -->  00:01:41,460
you'll be able to know that this file is malicious and it's gonna do something bad on your computer.

23

00:01:42,120  -->  00:01:46,980
First of all, you can see that the file suppresses error boxes, so it doesn't display error boxes.

24

00:01:48,020  -->  00:01:49,780
It also modifies registry.

25

00:01:51,680  -->  00:01:55,220
And you can see the registry parameters that it's modifying.

26

00:01:56,520  -->  00:01:59,220
You can see that it's playing with the Internet Settings

27

00:02:00,180  -->  00:02:01,660
and with the connections.

28

00:02:03,560  -->  00:02:07,420
You can also see that it's using Windows Socket service,

29

00:02:07,640  -->  00:02:09,560
so it's trying to create connections.

30

00:02:11,400  -->  00:02:15,000
You can also see that it's playing with the address of the process.

31

00:02:17,180  -->  00:02:21,140
Scrolling down, you'll see one of the most important indicators.

32

00:02:21,140  -->  00:02:23,380
Now obviously, there is more information in here.

33

00:02:27,420  -->  00:02:32,480
Right here, on the Network place, you'll see that it tries to connect to this IP

34

00:02:33,280  -->  00:02:34,740
on port 8080.

35

00:02:35,840  -->  00:02:38,920
Now again, you can go on this IP and do a 'Reverse DNS Lookup'

36

00:02:39,040  -->  00:02:41,680
and see that this IP is not related to any website.

37

00:02:42,360  -->  00:02:43,180
Also...

38

00:02:43,900  -->  00:02:49,820
this, when you upload the payload to this place, it's never gonna be executed on your computer,

39

00:02:49,820  -->  00:02:54,640
it's gonna be executed on that server in a Sandbox environment.

40

00:02:54,900  -->  00:02:59,100
Now obviously, the method I showed you, you should always do it in a VirtualBox,

41

00:02:59,100  -->  00:03:02,120
when you are executing it here on your Windows.

42

00:03:02,120  -->  00:03:05,680
Always do it in a Virtual machine, don't do, do it on your main machine

43

00:03:06,080  -->  00:03:10,260
or you can use this method, where you upload it into a Sandbox environment

44

00:03:10,400  -->  00:03:13,780
and it will be analyzed for you and then you can read the report.
